topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress Strain Diagram (Ductile & Brittle Materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stress-Strain Diagram?

A **Stress-Strain Diagram** (also known as a **Tensile Test Diagram** or **Stress-Strain Curve**) is a graphical representation that depicts the relationship between the **axial normal stress** ($\sigma$) applied to a material specimen and the resulting **axial normal strain** ($\varepsilon$) produced, when the specimen is subjected to a gradually increasing uniaxial tensile load until fracture occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A stress-strain diagram is a plot of stress ($\sigma$) along the Y-axis versus strain ($\varepsilon$) along the X-axis, obtained from a standardized **Tensile Test** (per IS 1601 / ASTM E8) on a specimen of standard dimensions. The shape of this curve is the **fingerprint of a material's mechanical behavior** under load.

### The Universal Tensile Test Specimen

A standard tensile test specimen has three distinct regions:
- **Gauge Length ($L_0$):** The central, uniform cross-section region where elongation is measured.
- **Grip Sections:** The wider ends held by the testing machine's jaws.
- **Fillets / Transition Zones:** Smooth curves connecting the gauge section to the grip sections (to prevent stress concentration at the shoulders).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chewing Gum Experiment"**
>
> Imagine stretching a piece of **chewing gum** (a ductile material analog) versus a piece of **dry biscuit/cookie** (a brittle material analog).
>
> - When you pull the **chewing gum** slowly: it first resists a little (elastic region), then begins to stretch a LOT without snapping ŌĆö you can see "necking" form, the cross-section visibly shrinks. It warns you *before* it breaks. **This is ductile behavior.**
> - When you pull the **dry biscuit**: it gives almost no warning, no stretching, no thinning ŌĆö it simply **SNAPS** suddenly with a loud "crack!" **This is brittle behavior.**
>
> The stress-strain curve is essentially a **graphical medical record** of how a material "behaves" under load ŌĆö it tells engineers everything: how much load it can take, how much it stretches, when it yields, and how/when it fails.

## 1.3 Why is the Stress-Strain Diagram Important in Engineering?

| Engineering Application | Information Extracted from Curve |
|---|---|
| **Bridges, Buildings, Pressure Vessels** | Elastic Modulus ($E$), Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$) ŌĆö prevents permanent deformation |
| **Crankshafts, Axles, Cables** | Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$) ŌĆö prevents sudden failure |
| **Springs, Shock Absorbers** | Resilience, Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) |
| **Car Crash Design, Earthquakes** | Ductility, Toughness, Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$) |
| **Brittle Material Selection (Concrete, Cast Iron)** | Modulus of Rupture, Fracture Stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The stress-strain curve is **NOT** a property of the material alone ŌĆö it is also a function of **temperature**, **strain rate**, and **specimen geometry**. However, for standard room-temperature tests on standard specimens, the curve is **unique** to a given material and is treated as a fundamental engineering property.

## 1.4 Engineering Stress vs. True Stress

| Quantity | Engineering Stress | True Stress |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | $\sigma_{eng}$ | $\sigma_{true}$ |
| **Definition** | Load divided by **original** cross-sectional area | Load divided by **instantaneous** cross-sectional area |
| **Formula** | $\sigma_{eng} = P / A_0$ | $\sigma_{true} = P / A_i$ |
| **Used Up To** | Ultimate Stress Point | Beyond Ultimate Stress (post-necking) |
| **Curve Behavior** | Drops after ultimate point | Continues to rise until fracture |

Where $A_0$ = original area, $A_i$ = instantaneous (actual) area during deformation, $P$ = applied axial load.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress-Strain Curve for a Ductile Material (Mild Steel ŌĆö The Reference Material)

A ductile material (e.g., **Mild Steel**, low-carbon steel) exhibits the most complete and educationally important stress-strain curve. The KTU syllabus demands mastery of **EVERY labeled point and region**. We will walk through each one in order.

### 2.1.1 The Distinct Points on the Ductile Material Curve

| Point | Name | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **O** | Origin | ŌĆö | Zero stress, zero strain ŌĆö unloaded state |
| **A** | Proportional Limit | $\sigma_p$ | Highest stress at which **Hooke's Law is strictly obeyed** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$). The curve is a perfect straight line OA. |
| **B** | Elastic Limit | $\sigma_{el}$ | Maximum stress up to which the material returns to its original shape **completely on unloading** (no permanent deformation). For many metals, $\sigma_p \approx \sigma_{el}$. |
| **C** | Upper Yield Point | $\sigma_{yu}$ | Stress at which material begins to deform plastically. There is a **sudden drop** in load. |
| **C'** | Lower Yield Point | $\sigma_{yl}$ | Lower stress value at which plastic deformation continues at **constant stress**. The "yield plateau" is between C and D. |
| **D** | Strain Hardening Region Begins | ŌĆö | After the yield plateau, the curve rises again because the material has undergone **strain hardening** (dislocation pile-up). |
| **E** | Ultimate Stress Point | $\sigma_u$ | **Maximum stress** the material can withstand. After this, **necking** begins. |
| **F** | Fracture / Breaking Point | $\sigma_f$ | Stress at which the specimen **physically breaks** into two pieces. |

### 2.1.2 The Distinct Regions on the Ductile Material Curve

1. **Region OA ŌĆö Linear Elastic Region (Hooke's Law Region):**
   - The relationship is **linear and reversible**.
   - $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus of Elasticity** (slope of line OA).
   - The deformation is purely elastic ŌĆö fully recoverable on unloading.

2. **Region AB ŌĆö Non-Linear Elastic Region:**
   - Curve is non-linear but deformation is still **recoverable**.
   - Hooke's Law is **not strictly valid** here.

3. **Region BC-CD ŌĆö Perfectly Plastic Region (Yield Plateau):**
   - Material yields plastically at **nearly constant stress**.
   - The phenomenon of sudden yield followed by a flat plateau is characteristic of **mild steel** (due to interactions between dislocations and interstitial carbon/nitrogen atoms ŌĆö the **L├╝ders Band** phenomenon).

4. **Region DE ŌĆö Strain Hardening (Work Hardening) Region:**
   - Material regains load-carrying capacity due to increased dislocation density.
   - The curve rises up to the **Ultimate Point E**.

5. **Region EF ŌĆö Necking Region (Local Plastic Instability):**
   - The cross-section **localizes** at one point and rapidly decreases.
   - Engineering stress *appears* to drop because we divide by original area $A_0$, but **true stress is still increasing**.
   - The specimen finally **fractures at F**.

### 2.1.3 The Stress-Strain Curve for a Brittle Material (e.g., Cast Iron, Concrete, Glass, Ceramics)

Brittle materials exhibit a **fundamentally different** curve:

- **No yielding** ŌĆö the material fails before any significant plastic deformation.
- **Very small strain at fracture** (typically $< 0.1$).
- The curve is **linear up to fracture** ŌĆö Hooke's Law is valid almost until failure.
- **Fracture stress = Ultimate stress** (point B and F coincide).
- The fracture in **tension** occurs perpendicular to the load (a **flat, granular fracture**).
- The fracture in **compression** occurs at roughly **45┬░** to the load (a **shear-type fracture**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Fact:** Brittle materials are **much stronger in compression than in tension** (e.g., cast iron tensile strength $\approx 200$ MPa but compressive strength $\approx 800$ MPa). This is why concrete columns are reinforced with steel ŌĆö concrete takes compression, steel takes tension.

## 2.2 Engineering Properties Derived from the Stress-Strain Curve

| Property | Definition | Formula (Area-based) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | Slope of linear elastic region | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Stiffness ŌĆö resistance to elastic deformation |
| **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** | Stress at which plastic deformation begins | Read from curve (at 0.2% offset for non-ferrous metals) | Maximum allowable working stress |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$)** | Maximum stress on the curve | $\sigma_u = P_{max} / A_0$ | Maximum load-carrying capacity |
| **Factor of Safety (FoS)** | Ratio of strength to working stress | $FoS = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{working}$ | Safety margin against failure |
| **Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$)** | Energy absorbed per unit volume in elastic region | $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | Capacity to absorb energy **elastically** |
| **Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$)** | Total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Capacity to absorb energy **plastically** before fracture |
| **Percentage Elongation (%EL)** | Ductility measure | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | How much the material stretches before breaking |
| **Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA)** | Ductility measure | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | How much cross-section shrinks before breaking |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = P / A$ | Engineering stress |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (valid in elastic region) |
| 4 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus (slope of linear region) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | True stress in terms of engineering stress (assuming constant volume) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | True strain in terms of engineering strain |
| 7 | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | Modulus of resilience (triangular area under elastic region) |
| 8 | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Modulus of toughness (total area under curve) |
| 9 | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Percentage elongation |
| 10 | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in area |
| 11 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \sigma_{yield} / FoS$ | Allowable working stress |
| 12 | $Poisson\ Ratio\ \nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Lateral strain ratio (related to E and G) |
| 13 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Relation between E, G, and $\nu$ |
| 14 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Relation between E, K, and $\nu$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Stress $\rightarrow$ **N/m┬▓ = Pa** (MPa = N/mm┬▓ for engineering); Strain $\rightarrow$ **dimensionless**; $E$ $\rightarrow$ **GPa** (typically).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Stress-Strain Curves Matter |
|---|---|
| **Civil / Structural** | Design of steel reinforcement bars (rebars) ŌĆö must have a definite yield point for ductile failure warning |
| **Aerospace** | Aluminum alloys chosen for high **toughness** (area under curve) ŌĆö energy absorption during impact |
| **Automotive** | Crash zones made of **ductile** steel ŌĆö controlled collapse absorbs crash energy |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Materials chosen for high **resilience** ŌĆö energy storage in elastic deformation |
| **Bridges** | Brittle materials (cast iron) ŌåÆ **avoided** in modern bridges due to sudden failure (e.g., 1907 Quebec Bridge collapse) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modulus of Resilience from the Stress-Strain Curve

> **Goal:** Prove that $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ for a linear elastic material.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition of Strain Energy Density

Strain energy density ($U$) is the strain energy stored per unit volume of the material. It is the **area under the stress-strain curve** up to the point of interest:

$$U = \int_0^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

This is because stress is force per unit area, strain is displacement per unit length, and their product is energy per unit volume:

$$U = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} \times \frac{\text{Displacement}}{\text{Length}} = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Hooke's Law in the Elastic Region

In the linear elastic region, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. Substitute this into the integral:

$$U = \int_0^{\varepsilon} (E \varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Perform the Integration

The integral of $\varepsilon$ with respect to $\varepsilon$ is $\dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{2}$:

$$U = E \left[ \frac{\varepsilon^2}{2} \right]_0^{\varepsilon} = \frac{E \varepsilon^2}{2}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Express in Terms of Stress Instead of Strain

From Hooke's Law, $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$. Substituting:

$$U = \frac{E}{2} \left( \frac{\sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \frac{E}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{E^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Specialize to the Yield Point

The modulus of resilience is the strain energy density up to the **yield point** (the end of the elastic region):

$$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation

This expression has a beautiful geometric meaning. In the linear elastic region, the area under the curve is a **right triangle** with base $\varepsilon_y$ and height $\sigma_y$:

$$U_r = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \varepsilon_y \cdot \sigma_y = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \sigma_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

This confirms that the formula is just the **area of the triangle OAB** on the stress-strain diagram.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between True Stress and Engineering Stress

> **Goal:** Derive $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ assuming **incompressibility** (volume constant during plastic deformation).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of Engineering Stress

Engineering stress uses the **original** cross-sectional area $A_0$:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{P}{A_0}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Definition of True Stress

True stress uses the **instantaneous** cross-sectional area $A_i$ at the current load:

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Incompressibility Assumption

For plastic deformation, the material volume is conserved (no porosity forms until very late):

$$V_0 = V_i \implies A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for the Ratio of Areas

Rearranging the volume conservation equation:

$$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Express in Terms of Engineering Strain

By definition, engineering strain is $\varepsilon_{eng} = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$, which gives $L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$. Substituting:

$$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute into True Stress Equation

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{P}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_i} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \, (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Range:** This relationship is valid **only up to the point of necking** (Ultimate Stress Point). Beyond necking, the stress distribution becomes triaxial at the neck, and the simple incompressibility assumption breaks down.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| Feature | Ductile Material (Mild Steel) | Brittle Material (Cast Iron) |
|---|---|---|
| **Example Materials** | Mild steel, copper, aluminum | Cast iron, concrete, glass, ceramic |
| **Yield Point** | **Clearly defined** (upper and lower yield points) | **No yield point** ŌĆö fails before yielding |
| **Plastic Region** | Large, well-defined | Negligible / almost zero |
| **Strain at Fracture** | Large (0.1 to 0.4 for steel) | Very small (< 0.01) |
| **Fracture Type** | Cup-and-cone (ductile) | Flat / granular (cleavage) |
| **Warning Before Failure** | **Yes** (deforms visibly) | **No** (sudden, catastrophic) |
| **Ultimate Stress vs Fracture Stress** | $\sigma_u > \sigma_f$ (they are different) | $\sigma_u = \sigma_f$ (they coincide) |
| **Tensile vs Compressive Strength** | Approximately **equal** | **Compressive >> Tensile** (often 4├Ś to 10├Ś) |
| **Energy Absorbed (Toughness)** | **High** (large area under curve) | **Low** (tiny area under curve) |
| **Young's Modulus** | Lower (e.g., steel $E \approx 200$ GPa) | Higher in some (cast iron $E \approx 100$ŌĆō170 GPa) |
| **Preferred Engineering Use** | Structural frames, bridges, cars, pressure vessels | Compressive members, machine beds, grinding wheels |
| **Failure Strain** | 10% ŌĆō 40% elongation | < 1% elongation |
| **Shape of Curve** | S-shaped with yield plateau | Linear up to fracture |
| **L├╝ders Bands** | Yes (visible during yielding) | No |
| **Necking** | Yes ŌĆö clearly visible | No |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu \approx 0.3$ | $\nu \approx 0.2$ to $0.3$ |
| **Failure Mode in Tension** | Shear at 45┬░ to axis (cup-and-cone) | Cleavage, fracture normal to axis |
| **Failure Mode in Compression** | Barrel formation + shear cracks at 45┬░ | 45┬░ shear fracture |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Stress-Strain Curve Regions for Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startO[Origin O: Unloaded State] --> propA[Point A: Proportional Limit]
    propA -->|"E = slope of OA"| elastB[Point B: Elastic Limit]
    elastB --> yldUp[Point C: Upper Yield Point]
    yldUp -->|"Sudden load drop"| yldLow[Point C: Lower Yield Point]
    yldLow -->|"Constant stress, plastic flow"| pltD[Point D: End of Yield Plateau]
    pltD -->|"Strain hardening"| ultE[Point E: Ultimate Stress Point]
    ultE -->|"Necking begins, area shrinks"| fracF[Point F: Fracture Point]
    fracF --> done[Specimen Splits into Two]
    
    propA -.->|"Region 1: Linear Elastic, Žā = E╬Ą"| region1[Hooke's Law Valid]
    elastB -.->|"Region 2: Non-linear Elastic"| region2[Recoverable but not linear]
    yldUp -.->|"Region 3: Perfectly Plastic"| region3[Yield Plateau]
    pltD -.->|"Region 4: Strain Hardening"| region4[Dislocation Pile-up]
    ultE -.->|"Region 5: Necking"| region5[Triaxial Stress State]
```

```mermaid
    q1 -->|"Strain at fracture < 5%"| brit[Brittle Material]
    
    duct --> dEx["Examples: Mild Steel, Copper, Aluminum, Gold"]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence ŌĆö Ductile vs Brittle Behavior Under Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph s1["Ductile Material - Mild Steel"]
        d1[Apply Load] --> d2[Elastic Stretch]
        d2 --> d3[Yield Point Reached]

    end

    b4 --> bRes["NO Warning - Catastrophic"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram ŌĆö Energy Absorption Zones on the Curve

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    ElasticRegion : Elastic Region O to A
    ElasticRegion : Energy Stored = Modulus of Resilience

    ElasticRegion --> PlasticRegion : Yield Point Crossed
    PlasticRegion : Plastic Region D to F
    PlasticRegion : Energy Absorbed = Modulus of Toughness
    PlasticRegion : Formula: Ut = Ōł½Žā d╬Ą from 0 to ╬Ąf

    FractureState --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Past Year Style ŌĆö PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style, Module 1]**

### **Question A1 (3 Marks):**
**Distinguish clearly between a ductile material and a brittle material, citing two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **ductile material** is one that undergoes **significant plastic deformation** before fracture, providing visible warning before failure. The stress-strain curve shows a clearly defined **yield point, strain hardening, necking, and cup-and-cone fracture**. Examples: *mild steel, copper, aluminum*.

A **brittle material** is one that fractures with **little or no plastic deformation**. The stress-strain curve is essentially **linear up to fracture**, with no yield point. Examples: *cast iron, concrete, glass, ceramics*.

> **Key distinguishing parameters:** Percentage elongation at fracture ŌĆö ductile: > 5%, brittle: < 5%. Fracture energy (toughness) ŌĆö ductile: high, brittle: very low.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks):**
**Define the following terms with reference to a stress-strain diagram: (i) Proportional Limit, (ii) Ultimate Stress, (iii) Modulus of Resilience.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Proportional Limit ($\sigma_p$):** It is the maximum stress up to which the stress-strain relationship is **strictly linear**, i.e., the material obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) exactly. Beyond this point, the curve becomes non-linear.

**(ii) Ultimate Stress ($\sigma_u$):** It is the **maximum engineering stress** the material can withstand. Beyond this point, **necking** begins and the engineering stress decreases, but the true stress continues to rise until fracture.

**(iii) Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$):** It is the **strain energy absorbed per unit volume** when the material is loaded up to its **elastic limit** (or yield point). It is equal to the area under the linear portion of the stress-strain curve and is given by:

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

A high modulus of resilience indicates a material that can absorb significant energy elastically ŌĆö ideal for **springs**.

---

## 5.2 KTU Past Year Style ŌĆö PART B Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö December 2023, Module 1, Question Pattern]**

### **OPTION A ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labeled stress-strain diagram for a **mild steel specimen** under uniaxial tension, explain the significance of the following points clearly: *Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Upper and Lower Yield Points, Ultimate Stress Point, and Fracture Point*. Mention the salient regions of the curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel bar of diameter **20 mm** and gauge length **200 mm** is subjected to a tensile test. The bar yields at a load of **80 kN** and fractures at a load of **120 kN**. The final diameter at fracture is **15 mm** and the final gauge length is **240 mm**. Determine: **(i)** Yield Stress, **(ii)** Ultimate Tensile Stress, **(iii)** Percentage Elongation, (iv) Percentage Reduction in Area. **(7 Marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a):**

The stress-strain diagram of mild steel is the canonical example of a **ductile material** response. The points are:

- **Proportional Limit (Point A):** The stress up to which strain is **directly proportional** to stress, with slope equal to Young's Modulus $E$.
- **Elastic Limit (Point B):** The maximum stress up to which the material returns to its **original dimensions on complete unloading** (zero permanent deformation).
- **Upper Yield Point (Point C):** The stress at which **plastic deformation initiates** with a sudden drop in load.
- **Lower Yield Point (Point C'):** The lower, constant stress at which plastic flow continues (the **yield plateau**, characterized by **L├╝ders bands**).
- **Ultimate Stress Point (Point E):** The **maximum engineering stress** the material can carry, beyond which **necking** begins.
- **Fracture Point (Point F):** The stress at which the specimen **physically breaks** (cup-and-cone fracture for ductile materials).

**Regions:** Linear elastic (OŌĆōA), Non-linear elastic (AŌĆōB), Perfectly plastic / yield plateau (BŌĆōD), Strain hardening (DŌĆōE), Necking (EŌĆōF).

#### **MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d_0 = 20$ mm $\implies A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.159$ mm┬▓
- Gauge length $L_0 = 200$ mm
- Yield load $P_y = 80$ kN = $80{,}000$ N
- Fracture load $P_f = 120$ kN = $120{,}000$ N
- Final diameter at fracture $d_f = 15$ mm
- Final gauge length $L_f = 240$ mm

**Step 1 ŌĆö Yield Stress:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000}{314.159} = 254.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Ultimate Tensile Stress:**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{120{,}000}{314.159} = 381.97 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Percentage Elongation:**

$$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{240 - 200}{200} \times 100 = \frac{40}{200} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Percentage Reduction in Area:**

Final area $A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.71$ mm┬▓

$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.159 - 176.71}{314.159} \times 100 = \frac{137.45}{314.159} \times 100 = 43.75\%$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y = 254.65$ MPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Stress | $\sigma_u = 381.97$ MPa |
| Percentage Elongation | $20\%$ |
| Percentage Reduction in Area | $43.75\%$ |

---

### **OPTION B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Draw the stress-strain curves for (i) a **ductile material** and (ii) a **brittle material** on the same axis, and bring out **six points of difference** between their mechanical behavior. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel specimen of gauge length **100 mm** and diameter **10 mm** is tested in tension. The specimen yields at a load of **30 kN** and the yield plateau continues up to a strain of **0.02**. The Young's Modulus is **200 GPa**. Calculate: **(i)** Yield Stress, **(ii)** Strain at the Proportional Limit (assuming $\sigma_p \approx \sigma_y$), (iii) **Modulus of Resilience**, (iv) Strain Energy stored in the specimen at yield (in Joules). **(7 Marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Sketch required:** Two curves on the same set of axes ŌĆö one for mild steel (showing yielding, strain hardening, necking) and one for cast iron (linear up to fracture). The student must label both curves clearly.

**Six Points of Difference:**

| # | Feature | Ductile | Brittle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plastic deformation | Large | Negligible |
| 2 | Yield point | Clearly defined | Absent |
| 3 | Strain at fracture | High | Very low |
| 4 | Fracture type | Cup-and-cone (fibrous) | Flat / granular (cleavage) |
| 5 | Energy absorbed (toughness) | High | Low |
| 6 | Strength in tension vs compression | Nearly equal | Compressive strength >> tensile strength |

#### **MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Gauge length $L_0 = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m
- Diameter $d_0 = 10$ mm $\implies A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(10)^2 = 78.54$ mm┬▓
- Yield load $P_y = 30$ kN = $30{,}000$ N
- Young's Modulus $E = 200$ GPa = $200 \times 10^3$ N/mm┬▓ = $200{,}000$ MPa

**Step 1 ŌĆö Yield Stress:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{30{,}000}{78.54} = 381.97 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Strain at Proportional Limit (Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon_p = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{381.97}{200{,}000} = 1.9098 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00191$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Modulus of Resilience:**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(381.97)^2}{2 \times 200{,}000} = \frac{145{,}901}{400{,}000} = 0.3648 \text{ N┬Ęmm/mm}^3 = 0.3648 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total Strain Energy in the Specimen at Yield:**

Volume of gauge section: $V = A_0 \times L_0 = 78.54 \times 100 = 7854$ mm┬│

$$U_{total} = U_r \times V = 0.3648 \times 7854 = 2865.0 \text{ N┬Ęmm} = 2.865 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y = 381.97$ MPa |
| Strain at Proportional Limit | $\varepsilon_p = 0.00191$ |
| Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = 0.3648$ MPa |
| Total Strain Energy at Yield | $U_{total} = 2.865$ J |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing engineering stress and true stress:** Many students write $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}$ for the entire curve. This is **wrong** beyond the ultimate point. Use $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ only up to necking.
> 2. **Using fracture area instead of original area for UTS:** UTS = $P_{max}/A_0$, **not** $P_{max}/A_f$. This is a 1-mark deduction mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** Stress answers should ideally be in MPa, not N/mm┬▓ (although dimensionally equivalent, the convention matters). Also, $E = 200$ GPa must be converted to MPa (= $200{,}000$ MPa) when $\sigma_y$ is in MPa.
> 4. **Not sketching the diagram properly in theory questions:** A stress-strain question without a labeled sketch will lose **at least 2 marks**. Always draw and label.
> 5. **Mixing up elastic limit and proportional limit:** These are **close but distinct** ŌĆö the proportional limit is the end of the linear region; the elastic limit is the maximum stress for complete recovery. They coincide for some materials but not all.
> 6. **Calculation errors in %RA and %EL:** Compute $A_f$ correctly from $d_f$ using $\pi/4 \cdot d_f^2$, not $d_f^2$ alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤Ä» KTU 60-Second Rapid Revision ŌĆö Stress-Strain Diagram**

Ō£ģ The **stress-strain diagram is the "fingerprint"** of a material's mechanical behavior under tensile load.

Ō£ģ **Ductile material curve** (Mild Steel) ŌĆö has **5 regions**: Linear Elastic ŌåÆ Non-linear Elastic ŌåÆ Yield Plateau ŌåÆ Strain Hardening ŌåÆ Necking.

Ō£ģ **Key Points (in order):** Origin O ŌåÆ Proportional Limit A ŌåÆ Elastic Limit B ŌåÆ Upper Yield C ŌåÆ Lower Yield C' ŌåÆ End of Plateau D ŌåÆ Ultimate E ŌåÆ Fracture F.

Ō£ģ **Brittle material curve** (Cast Iron) ŌĆö **linear up to fracture**, **no yield point**, **no plastic region**, **no necking**.

Ō£ģ **Young's Modulus $E$** = slope of linear elastic region = $\sigma/\varepsilon$ in MPa or GPa.

Ō£ģ **Modulus of Resilience** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ = area of triangle under elastic region = energy stored **elastically**.

Ō£ģ **Modulus of Toughness** $U_t = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ = total area under the curve = energy absorbed **plastically**.

Ō£ģ **Engineering stress** uses **original** area $A_0$. **True stress** uses **instantaneous** area $A_i$.

Ō£ģ **True stress formula:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ (incompressibility assumption).

Ō£ģ **Ductility measures:** $\%EL = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ and $\%RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$.

Ō£ģ **Brittle materials are much stronger in compression than tension** ŌĆö this is why reinforced concrete is used.

Ō£ģ **Fracture of ductile material** = **cup-and-cone** (45┬░ shear). **Fracture of brittle material** = **flat / cleavage** (normal to load in tension).

Ō£ģ **Factor of Safety (FoS):** $FoS = \sigma_{yield}/\sigma_{working}$ ŌĆö use yield stress, not ultimate, for ductile materials.

Ō£ģ **Units Convention:** $\sigma$ in MPa (= N/mm┬▓), $E$ in GPa (= $10^3$ MPa), strain is dimensionless, energy in Joules.

Ō£ģ **A tensile test is governed by IS 1601 / ASTM E8** ŌĆö standard specimen dimensions are mandatory for reproducible curves.

Ō£ģ **Remember:** Stress-strain curves are temperature-dependent and strain-rate dependent ŌĆö the curves shift with these parameters.

Ō£ģ **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always draw the diagram, label all 6 points, and show all calculation steps with units ŌĆö partial marks are awarded generously for correct methodology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Definition of Material Properties in Solid Mechanics

**Material Properties** are the intrinsic mechanical characteristics of a solid that quantify its response (deformation) when subjected to external forces or loads. They form the foundational language of Solid Mechanics and govern every analytical calculation from a simple tie-rod to a massive dam.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT302 ŌĆō Solid Mechanics)**, material properties are categorized into two broad families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> 1. **Elastic Constants** ŌĆö Quantify *stiffness* and *deformability* (E, G, K, ╬Į).
> 2. **Strength Properties** ŌĆö Quantify *failure limits* and *energy absorption* (Yield stress, Ultimate stress, Modulus of Resilience, Modulus of Toughness).

The four primary elastic constants are:
- **Young's Modulus (E)** ŌĆö resistance to uniaxial tension/compression
- **Shear Modulus (G)** ŌĆö resistance to shear deformation
- **Bulk Modulus (K)** ŌĆö resistance to volumetric (hydrostatic) compression
- **Poisson's Ratio (╬Į)** ŌĆö lateral strain response to longitudinal strain

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are comparing **two springs** held between your hands:

- A **stiff steel spring** barely stretches when you pull ŌåÆ high **Young's Modulus (E)**.
- A **soft rubber band** stretches easily ŌåÆ low **E**.
- If you twist a **metal rod** vs a **plastic rod**, the metal resists more ŌåÆ high **Shear Modulus (G)**.
- If you push a **sponge** underwater vs a **marble block**, the marble barely compresses ŌåÆ high **Bulk Modulus (K)**.
- When you stretch a **rubber band**, its width *shrinks* ŌåÆ this lateral contraction per unit longitudinal stretch is **Poisson's ratio (╬Į)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Memory Hook)**
> Think of a material as a "personality":
> - **E** = How stubborn the material is when pulled (stiffness)
> - **G** = How stubborn when twisted (rigidity)
> - **K** = How stubborn when squeezed from all sides (incompressibility)
> - **╬Į** = How much it "leans sideways" while being pulled sideways in
> - These four traits are not independent ŌĆö they are mathematically linked! (See Section 2)

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

Every structural design ŌĆö bridges, buildings, aircraft, pressure vessels, machine components ŌĆö begins with the question: *"How will this material behave under load?"* Without accurate knowledge of material properties, the engineer cannot predict deformation, buckling, fatigue, or failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units & Constants (must memorize)**
> - **Stress (Žā, Žä):** N/m┬▓ or **Pascal (Pa)**. Practical unit: **MPa = 10ŌüČ Pa** or **GPa = 10Ōü╣ Pa**.
> - **Strain (╬Ą, ╬│):** Dimensionless (m/m).
> - **Elastic Moduli (E, G, K):** Same units as stress ŌĆö **Pa, MPa, or GPa**.
> - **Poisson's Ratio (╬Į):** Dimensionless.
> - **Standard reference values:**
>   - **Steel:** E Ōēł 200 GPa, G Ōēł 80 GPa, ╬Į Ōēł 0.30
>   - **Aluminium:** E Ōēł 70 GPa, ╬Į Ōēł 0.33
>   - **Concrete:** E Ōēł 30 GPa, ╬Į Ōēł 0.20
>   - **Rubber:** E Ōēł 0.01 GPa, ╬Į Ōēł 0.49

---

### 1.4 Stress and Strain ŌĆö The Two Primary Kinematic Quantities

| Quantity | Definition | Symbol | Unit |
|----------|------------|--------|------|
| Normal Stress | Force per unit area acting perpendicular to a cross-section | **Žā** | **N/m┬▓ (Pa)** |
| Shear Stress | Force per unit area acting parallel (tangential) to a cross-section | **Žä** | **N/m┬▓ (Pa)** |
| Normal Strain | Change in length per unit original length | **╬Ą** | Dimensionless |
| Shear Strain | Change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular lines | **╬│** or **╬”** | Radians (dimensionless) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Stress is the *cause*, strain is the *effect*. The relationship between them defines the material itself. A linear relationship ŌåÆ **Hookean material**; non-linear ŌåÆ **non-linear elastic** or **plastic** material.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Four Elastic Constants ŌĆö Theoretical Foundation

#### (a) Young's Modulus (E) ŌĆö Modulus of Elasticity
- **Definition:** The ratio of longitudinal (normal) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
- **Physical meaning:** Slope of the stressŌĆōstrain curve in the linear elastic region.
- **Governing Equation:** Žā = E ┬Ę ╬Ą
- **Engineering utility:** Determines axial elongation, beam bending, and column shortening.

#### (b) Shear Modulus (G) ŌĆö Modulus of Rigidity
- **Definition:** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
- **Physical meaning:** Resistance to shape distortion (angular change).
- **Governing Equation:** Žä = G ┬Ę ╬│
- **Engineering utility:** Critical in torsion of shafts, shear in bolts, and fastener design.

#### (c) Bulk Modulus (K) ŌĆö Modulus of Volumetric Elasticity
- **Definition:** The ratio of hydrostatic (volumetric) stress to volumetric strain.
- **Physical meaning:** Resistance to uniform compression from all sides.
- **Governing Equation:** K = Žā_v / ╬Ą_v = -p / (╬öV/V)
- **Engineering utility:** Essential in pressure vessel design, deep-sea structures, and fluidŌĆōstructure interaction problems.

#### (d) Poisson's Ratio (╬Į)
- **Definition:** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain.
- **Physical meaning:** How much a material "squeezes sideways" when stretched.
- **Governing Equation:** ╬Į = -╬Ą_lateral / ╬Ą_longitudinal
- **Theoretical range:** **-1.0 Ōēż ╬Į Ōēż 0.5** for stable, isotropic, linear-elastic materials.
- **Common range for engineering materials:** 0.20 to 0.45.
- **Incompressible material (rubber, water):** ╬Į Ōēł 0.5.

---

### 2.2 Relationships Between Elastic Constants

For **homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic materials**, only **two** of the four constants are independent. The other two are derivable:

| Relationship | Formula | Use |
|--------------|---------|-----|
| E, G, and ╬Į | **E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)** | Most commonly used; converts between E and G |
| E, K, and ╬Į | **E = 3K(1 ŌłÆ 2╬Į)** | Pressure-vessel and confined-flow problems |
| E, K, and G | **E = 9KG / (3K + G)** | When only K and G are known |
| K, G, and ╬Į | **K = 2G(1 + ╬Į) / 3(1 ŌłÆ 2╬Į)** | Rarely used directly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Memorize the first two relations. They appear in nearly every board exam paper.

---

### 2.3 Mechanical / Strength Properties (From the StressŌĆōStrain Diagram)

Reading from a **tensile stressŌĆōstrain diagram for a ductile material** (e.g., mild steel), the following points and zones are defined:

| Property | Symbol | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Proportional Limit | Žā_p | Up to this stress, Žā ŌłØ ╬Ą (Hooke's Law valid) |
| Elastic Limit | Žā_e | Maximum stress up to which the material returns to original shape on unloading |
| Yield Stress | Žā_y (or Žā_{0.2}) | Stress at which plastic deformation begins |
| Ultimate Tensile Stress | Žā_u | Maximum stress the material can withstand |
| Breaking / Fracture Stress | Žā_b | Stress at which the specimen actually fractures |
| Modulus of Resilience | U_r | Strain energy per unit volume up to elastic limit = Žā_y┬▓ / (2E) |
| Modulus of Toughness | U_t | Total strain energy per unit volume up to fracture (area under full ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve) |

### 2.4 Other Engineering Material Properties

- **Elasticity:** Ability to regain original shape after removal of load.
- **Plasticity:** Ability to undergo permanent deformation without rupture.
- **Ductility:** Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture (e.g., mild steel, copper).
- **Brittleness:** Negligible plastic deformation before fracture (e.g., glass, cast iron).
- **Toughness:** Ability to absorb energy up to fracture (area under ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve).
- **Hardness:** Resistance to localized plastic deformation (scratch, indentation).
- **Fatigue Resistance:** Ability to withstand cyclic loading.
- **Creep Resistance:** Ability to resist time-dependent deformation at constant stress (important at high temperatures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why Each Property Matters**
> - For a **bridge cable**: high E and high Žā_y (so it stretches little and won't yield).
> - For an **earthquake damper**: high toughness (to absorb seismic energy).
> - For a **chassis panel**: moderate E with good ductility (to absorb crash energy).
> - For a **turbine blade**: high creep resistance (to survive high temperatures).

---

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU board exams**. We will derive it using a unit cube subjected to pure shear, expressed as the superposition of two principal stresses.

**Setup ŌĆö Consider a unit cube** of side 'a' subjected to pure shear stress **Žä** on its faces. The shear strain is **╬│**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decomposition of Pure Shear**
A state of pure shear is equivalent to a biaxial state of normal stresses: tension ŽāŌéü = Žä along one diagonal and compression ŽāŌéā = -Žä along the perpendicular diagonal (both acting at 45┬░ to the cube face).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Hooke's Law in Two Perpendicular Directions**
Let us denote the two principal directions as 1 (tension) and 3 (compression). For an isotropic material:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_1 - \nu (\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \right]$$

In our case, the third direction is stress-free: ŽāŌéé = 0. So:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \tau - \nu (0 - \tau) \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \tau + \nu \tau \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Shear Strain ╬│**
The total change in the right angle of the cube (which was originally 90┬░) equals the difference between the principal strains:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3$$

By symmetry, ╬ĄŌéā = -╬ĄŌéü (the compression direction contracts equally as the tension direction elongates):

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - (-\varepsilon_1)$$

$$\gamma = 2 \varepsilon_1 = \frac{2 \tau (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Shear Hooke's Law**
By definition: Žä = G ┬Ę ╬│

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{2 \tau (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for E**
Cancelling Žä from both sides:

$$1 = \frac{2G (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The factor (1 + ╬Į) accounts for the lateral "squeezing" that makes a material stiffer in shear than it would otherwise be if ╬Į were zero.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship E = 3K(1 ŌłÆ 2╬Į)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Uniform Hydrostatic Pressure p**
A cube submerged in a fluid experiences equal normal compressive stress Žā on all six faces:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Volumetric Stress**
The volumetric stress is the average of the three normal stresses:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3} = -p$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Volumetric Strain**
The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains (valid for small strains):

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

Using the generalized Hooke's law for ╬Ą_x:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z) \right] = \frac{1}{E} \left[ -p - \nu(-p - p) \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left[ -p + 2\nu p \right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry, ╬Ą_y = ╬Ą_z = ╬Ą_x, so:

$$\varepsilon_v = 3 \varepsilon_x = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus**
By definition, K = Žā_v / ╬Ą_v. Since Žā_v = -p:

$$K = \frac{-p}{\frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}}$$

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** When ╬Į ŌåÆ 0.5, E ŌåÆ Ōł× for a finite K. This is the perfectly incompressible limit (e.g., rubber). When ╬Į ŌåÆ 0, E = 3K ŌĆö i.e., the material is "stiffer" in hydrostatic compression than in uniaxial tension by a factor of 3.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Style)

#### Example 1 ŌĆö Finding All Elastic Constants
**Question:** A material has Young's modulus E = 200 GPa and Poisson's ratio ╬Į = 0.30. Find G, K, and verify E = 9KG/(3K + G).

**Solution:**

Using **E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)**:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{200}{2(1.30)} = \frac{200}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

Using **E = 3K(1 ŌłÆ 2╬Į)**:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{200}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200}{1.20}$$

$$\boxed{K = 166.67 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Verification using E = 9KG / (3K + G):**

$$E = \frac{9 \times 166.67 \times 76.92}{3(166.67) + 76.92} = \frac{115{,}380}{576.93} = 200 \text{ GPa} \checkmark$$

---

#### Example 2 ŌĆö Lateral Strain and Volumetric Strain
**Question:** A steel rod of length 2 m and diameter 30 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 100 kN. Given E = 200 GPa and ╬Į = 0.30, find: (a) longitudinal strain, (b) lateral strain, (c) change in diameter, (d) change in volume.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.030)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Axial stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 141.47 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Longitudinal strain (a):**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{141.47 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_L = 7.074 \times 10^{-4} = 0.7074 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Lateral strain (b):**

$$\varepsilon_d = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L = -0.30 \times 7.074 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_d = -2.122 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Change in diameter (c):**

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_d \cdot d = -2.122 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.030 = -6.37 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta d = -6.37 \text{ \mu m (diameter decreases)}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Volumetric strain and change in volume (d):**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L + 2 \varepsilon_d = 7.074 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-2.122 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 7.074 \times 10^{-4} - 4.244 \times 10^{-4} = 2.830 \times 10^{-4}$$

Original volume:

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot L = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.4137 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Change in volume:

$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 2.830 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.4137 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\delta V = 4.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 = 0.400 \text{ cm}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the result:** Even though the rod got *thinner*, the volume *increased* because ╬Į < 0.5. For a perfectly incompressible material (╬Į = 0.5), ╬Ą_v would be exactly zero.
