## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the following with their SI units: (a) Young's Modulus, (b) Bulk Modulus, (c) Poisson's Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**
(a) **Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit. It measures the material's stiffness in tension or compression.
   $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa)}$$

(b) **Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of volumetric (hydrostatic) stress to volumetric strain. It measures the material's resistance to uniform compression from all sides.
   $$K = \frac{\sigma_v}{\varepsilon_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa)}$$

(c) **Poisson's Ratio (╬Į):** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading.
   $$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Dimensionless (no units)}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the relationship between the three elastic constants E, G and K. Why is Poisson's ratio important in engineering design?**

**Model Answer:**
The relationship is:

$$E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G}$$

**Importance of Poisson's ratio:**
- It governs the **lateral deformation** of a member under axial load (e.g., a tie-rod pulling its gusset plate sideways).
- It influences **stress redistribution** in composite and multi-axial loading.
- For **incompressible fluids/solids**, ╬Į ŌåÆ 0.5, and many design formulas (e.g., rubber bearing stiffness) become singular.
- It links the four elastic constants, allowing one constant to be derived if the others are known.
- Typical range for engineering materials: **0.20 (concrete) to 0.45 (some polymers)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Define the four elastic constants E, G, K and ╬Į. Derive the relationship **E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A brass rod 1.5 m long, 25 mm in diameter, is subjected to a tensile load of 50 kN. If E = 100 GPa and ╬Į = 0.33, find the (i) longitudinal strain, (ii) lateral strain, (iii) change in length, (iv) change in diameter, and (v) change in volume. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps:
1. Consider a unit cube under pure shear stress Žä, producing shear strain ╬│.
2. Decompose pure shear into biaxial tension/compression (Žā = Žä) at 45┬░.
3. Apply generalized Hooke's law: ╬ĄŌéü = Žä(1+╬Į)/E.
4. By symmetry, ╬│ = 2╬ĄŌéü = 2Žä(1+╬Į)/E.
5. Apply Žä = G┬Ę╬│ ŌåÆ solve to get **E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)**. Ō£ō

**Part (b):** Numerical solution:

**(i) Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Axial stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal strain:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{101.86 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^9}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_L = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**(iii) Lateral strain:**

$$\varepsilon_d = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L = -0.33 \times 1.0186 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_d = -3.361 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**(iv) Change in length:**

$$\delta L = \varepsilon_L \cdot L = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = 1.528 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.528 \text{ mm (extension)}}$$

**(v) Change in diameter:**

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_d \cdot d = -3.361 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025$$

$$\boxed{\delta d = -8.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -8.40 \text{ \mu m (contraction)}}$$

**(vi) Volumetric strain and change in volume:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L + 2\varepsilon_d = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3} - 6.722 \times 10^{-4} = 3.464 \times 10^{-4}$$

Original volume: V = A ├Ś L = 4.909 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ ├Ś 1.5 = 7.363 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ m┬│

$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 3.464 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.363 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\delta V = 2.55 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (volume increases)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain a typical stressŌĆōstrain curve for a ductile material (mild steel) with a neat sketch. Define the terms: proportional limit, elastic limit, yield stress, ultimate stress, modulus of resilience and modulus of toughness. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A material has Young's modulus E = 210 GPa, Poisson's ratio ╬Į = 0.30 and yield stress Žā_y = 250 MPa. Find: (i) the modulus of resilience, (ii) the modulus of rigidity G, and (iii) the bulk modulus K. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Explanation of the ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):

| Region | Description | Key Point |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| OA | Linear elastic | Proportional limit Žā_p at A ŌĆö Hooke's Law valid |
| AB | Non-linear elastic | Elastic limit Žā_e at B ŌĆö material still recovers |
| BC | Yielding | Yield stress Žā_y at C ŌĆö plastic flow begins |
| CD | Perfectly plastic | Large strain at nearly constant stress |
| DE | Strain hardening | Stress rises to peak |
| E | Ultimate point | Ultimate stress Žā_u ŌĆö maximum load |
| EF | Necking | Cross-section reduces locally |
| F | Fracture | Fracture stress Žā_b ŌĆö specimen breaks |

**Definitions:**
- **Proportional Limit (Žā_p):** Stress up to which stress is directly proportional to strain.
- **Elastic Limit (Žā_e):** Maximum stress up to which the material regains its original shape on unloading.
- **Yield Stress (Žā_y):** Stress at which noticeable plastic deformation begins (often the 0.2% offset point).
- **Ultimate Stress (Žā_u):** The maximum stress the material can sustain.
- **Modulus of Resilience (U_r):** Strain energy per unit volume stored in the material up to the elastic limit.
  $$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$
- **Modulus of Toughness (U_t):** Total strain energy per unit volume the material can absorb up to fracture (area under the full ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve).

**Part (b):** Numerical solution:

**(i) Modulus of resilience:**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(250 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 210 \times 10^9}$$

$$U_r = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{16}}{4.20 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\boxed{U_r = 1.488 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^3 = 148.8 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Modulus of rigidity (G):**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1.30)} = \frac{210}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 80.77 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Bulk modulus (K):**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{210}{1.20}$$

$$\boxed{K = 175 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Material Properties Questions**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Stress in GPa ŌåÆ multiply by 10Ōü╣ before dividing by E. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Sign convention in Poisson's ratio:** Some textbooks define ╬Į without the negative sign. In KTU, **always include the negative sign** as defined above, or you may lose 1 mark for "incomplete definition".
> 3. **Volumetric strain formula:** For small strains only, ╬Ą_v = ╬Ą_x + ╬Ą_y + ╬Ą_z. For large strains, the product terms must be included ŌĆö this is rarely required in KTU but be aware.
> 4. **Forgetting the 1+╬Į factor:** When asked to "find G given E and ╬Į", students often write G = E/(2+╬Į) instead of E/(2(1+╬Į)). **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Sketching the ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve:** In board exams, a labelled diagram is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark questions. A curve without labels = only 50% marks.
> 6. **"Define" vs "Explain":** A 3-mark "define" question needs the formula **and** units. A "state the relationship" question needs the formula only.
> 7. **Confusing K with E:** K is for volumetric loading, E is for uniaxial loading. Do not interchange.
> 8. **Range of ╬Į:** Always quote the valid range **-1 Ōēż ╬Į Ōēż 0.5** when asked.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- Ō£ģ **Stress (Žā) = Force / Area** ŌĆö units **Pa (N/m┬▓)**
- Ō£ģ **Strain (╬Ą) = Deformation / Original length** ŌĆö **dimensionless**
- Ō£ģ **Hooke's Law:** Žā = E╬Ą and Žä = G╬│
- Ō£ģ **Poisson's ratio:** ╬Į = -╬Ą_lateral / ╬Ą_longitudinal
- Ō£ģ **Bulk modulus:** K = Žā_v / ╬Ą_v
- Ō£ģ **Three Golden Relations (memorize in this order):**
  - E = 2G(1 + ╬Į)
  - E = 3K(1 ŌłÆ 2╬Į)
  - E = 9KG / (3K + G)
- Ō£ģ **For isotropic materials:** only **2** elastic constants are independent
- Ō£ģ **Range of Poisson's ratio:** ŌłÆ1 Ōēż ╬Į Ōēż 0.5 (for stable materials)
- Ō£ģ **Common engineering values:** Steel (E=200 GPa, ╬Į=0.30), Aluminium (E=70 GPa, ╬Į=0.33)
- Ō£ģ **Modulus of Resilience:** U_r = Žā_y┬▓ / (2E) ŌĆö energy stored up to elastic limit
- Ō£ģ **Modulus of Toughness:** area under entire ŽāŌĆō╬Ą curve
- Ō£ģ **Ductile material:** large plastic region, gradual failure (steel, copper)
- Ō£ģ **Brittle material:** negligible plastic region, sudden failure (cast iron, glass)
- Ō£ģ **Volumetric strain** for small strains: ╬Ą_v = ╬Ą_x + ╬Ą_y + ╬Ą_z
- Ō£ģ **Lateral strain** always **opposite in sign** to longitudinal strain under tension
- Ō£ģ **Units of E, G, K:** same as stress ŌåÆ Pa (use MPa or GPa in practice)
- Ō£ģ **In the derivation of E = 2G(1+╬Į):** remember the pure-shear decomposition into biaxial tension and compression at 45┬░ to the cube faces.
- Ō£ģ **For board exams:** always draw a **labelled stressŌĆōstrain diagram** for ductile-material questions ŌĆö it earns easy 1ŌĆō2 marks.
- Ō£ģ **Final sanity check on numerical answers:** ╬Į should be between 0.2 and 0.45; strains should be very small (10Ōü╗┬│ to 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ for typical loads); G should be roughly E/3 (for ╬Į Ōēł 0.33).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Elastic Constants (E, G & v)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Elastic Constants (E, G & v)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Elastic Constants

In the linear theory of elasticity (Hooke's Law regime), an isotropic, homogeneous, and continuous solid is fully characterized by **three independent elastic constants**. They quantify the material's stiffness response under three fundamental modes of loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition ŌĆö Isotropic Material**
> An *isotropic material* exhibits identical mechanical properties in all directions at a given point. Since the material has no directional preference, only **two constants are independent**, and the third can be derived from the other two.

| Constant | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ | Stiffness against uniaxial tension or compression |
| **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)** | $G$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ | Stiffness against shape distortion (shear) |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial load |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ | Stiffness against uniform hydrostatic compression |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Rubber Band Model"

Imagine stretching a soft rubber band between your two hands.

* When you **pull it axially**, it resists the pull ŌĆö that resistance normalized by strain is governed by **Young's Modulus** $E$.
* As it stretches and becomes longer, it simultaneously **becomes thinner in the middle** ŌĆö that "necking" effect is quantified by **Poisson's Ratio** $\nu$. If you stretched steel wire instead, it would also (microscopically) contract laterally, but far less than rubber. A higher $\nu$ means a more pronounced lateral contraction.
* Now imagine **twisting the rubber band** about its longitudinal axis (like wringing a wet towel). The angular twist per unit torque reflects the **Shear Modulus** $G$.
* Finally, imagine placing the rubber band in a pressure chamber and **squeezing it uniformly from all sides**. The resistance to that volumetric compression is the **Bulk Modulus** $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Although there are *four* constants ($E, G, K, \nu$), only **two are independent** for an isotropic material. The other two can be derived. The three classical relations connecting them are the **highest-weightage derivations** in Module 1 of PCRUT302.

### 1.3 Mathematical Statements of Each Constant

$$E = \frac{\text{Longitudinal Stress } (\sigma)}{\text{Longitudinal Strain } (\varepsilon)}$$

$$G = \frac{\text{Shear Stress } (\tau)}{\text{Shear Strain } (\gamma)}$$

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{Lateral Strain } (\varepsilon_{\text{lat}})}{ \text{ Longitudinal Strain } (\varepsilon_{\text{long}})}$$

$$K = \frac{\text{Hydrostatic Pressure } (p)}{\text{Volumetric Strain } (e)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the Negative Sign in $\nu$:** The negative sign in the definition of $\nu$ is a mathematical convention. When a material is stretched ($\varepsilon_{\text{long}} > 0$), it contracts laterally ($\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} < 0$), so the ratio $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{long}}$ is negative. The negative sign makes $\nu$ a positive number for most engineering materials.

### 1.4 Typical Range of Poisson's Ratio

| Material | Approximate $\nu$ |
|----------|-------------------|
| Steel | $0.28$ ŌĆō $0.30$ |
| Concrete | $0.10$ ŌĆō $0.20$ |
| Rubber | $\approx 0.49$ |
| Cork | $\approx 0.00$ |
| Cork (ideal) | $0$ |
| Theoretical Max (stable) | $0.5$ |
| Theoretical Min (stable) | $-1.0$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Master Relations

For an isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic material, the three fundamental relationships are:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) \quad \text{(Relation-1)}$$

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \quad \text{(Relation-2)}$$

$$G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)} \quad \text{(Relation-3)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Out of these three, **Relation-1** ($E = 2G(1+\nu)$) and **Relation-2** ($E = 3K(1-2\nu)$) are the most frequently asked derivations. Memorize both ŌĆö they are guaranteed 7-mark questions in either the Sessional or ESE.

### 2.2 Alternate / Derived Forms (Frequently Tested)

By eliminating $\nu$ between Relation-1 and Relation-2, we obtain two auxiliary formulas used in numerical problems:

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1$$

$$\nu = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{E}{3K}\right)$$

$$\frac{E}{G} = 2(1 + \nu)$$

$$\frac{E}{K} = 3(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$G = \frac{3KE}{9K - E}$$

$$K = \frac{EG}{3(3G - E)} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Relation | Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-------|----------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Pure shear Ōåö uniaxial equivalence | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 2 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Hydrostatic pressure Ōåö uniaxial equivalence | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Bulk Ōåö Shear equivalence | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 4 | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | Find $\nu$ from $E$ and $G$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$ | Find $G$ from $K$ and $E$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Find $K$ from $E$ and $\nu$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

### 2.4 Why Are These Relations Necessary? ŌĆö Engineering Utility

1. **Material Characterization in the Lab:** Direct measurement of $E$ (tensile test) and $G$ (torsion test) is straightforward. Measuring $K$ requires complex pressure-chamber setups. Engineers compute $K$ using Relation-2 once $E$ and $\nu$ are known.
2. **Quality Control:** If a sample is found to have $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$, then $G$ must equal $76.92 \text{ GPa}$. Any deviation flags material inconsistency.
3. **Finite Element Analysis (FEA) Input:** Software like ANSYS or Abaqus requires only two constants (e.g., $E$ and $\nu$) for isotropic materials. The other constants are computed internally.
4. **Limit Checks:** The relations impose physical bounds: $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$, and $0 \le G \le E/2$, ensuring input data is physically valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Bounds Derived from the Relations:**
> - From $E = 2G(1+\nu) \ge 0$ and $G \ge 0$, we get $\nu \ge -1$.
> - From $E = 3K(1-2\nu) \ge 0$ and $K \ge 0$, we get $\nu \le 0.5$.
> - Hence: $\boxed{-1 \le \nu \le 0.5}$ for stable isotropic materials.

### 2.5 Roadmap of the Three Derivations

| Derivation | Starting Loading | Key Geometric Idea | Resulting Relation |
|------------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Pure shear $\tau$ on a square element | Principal stresses on diagonals are $\pm \tau$ | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ |
| 2 | Hydrostatic pressure $p$ | Volumetric strain of a cube | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ |
| 3 | Eliminate $\nu$ from the first two | Algebraic manipulation | $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy:** Equate two independent expressions for the same shear strain ŌĆö one obtained from the definition of $G$, the other from principal-stress analysis on the sheared element.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the element.**
Consider a square element of side length $a$ made of an isotropic linear elastic material. Apply pure shear stress $\tau$ on its four faces, as shown below. The shear strain produced is $\gamma$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relate $\tau$ and $\gamma$ via $G$.**
By the definition of the shear modulus:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the principal stresses on the diagonals.**
For a state of pure shear, Mohr's circle (or direct equilibrium of the diagonal wedge) shows that the principal stresses act on the element's diagonals:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension along the diagonal that elongates)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression along the perpendicular diagonal)}$$

$$\sigma_3 = 0 \quad \text{(plane stress assumption)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the normal strain along the principal tensile direction.**
Using the generalized Hooke's law in 2D form, the strain along the $\sigma_1$ direction is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right]$$

Substituting $\sigma_1 = \tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Connect the principal strain to the shear strain.**
For small deformations, the engineering shear strain equals twice the principal tensile strain (this is a standard result from strain transformation: the diagonal of a sheared square extends by an amount equal to half the shear deformation):

$$\gamma = 2 \varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Equate the two expressions for $\gamma$.**

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Cancel $\tau$ and rearrange.**

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy:** Apply uniform hydrostatic pressure to a cube, compute the resulting volumetric strain using Hooke's law, and equate it to the strain defined by the bulk modulus.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the loading.**
Consider a small cubic element of side $a$ subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ acting on all six faces. The normal stresses on each face are:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

(The negative sign indicates compression, since the pressure pushes inward.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the volumetric strain.**
The volumetric strain is the change in volume per unit original volume. For a cube of side $a$ with new dimensions $(a + \Delta x)(a + \Delta y)(a + \Delta z)$:

$$e = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

(For infinitesimal strains, higher-order terms in the product are neglected.)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute each normal strain using generalized Hooke's law.**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = -\frac{p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_x$. Therefore:

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum to get the volumetric strain.**

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \varepsilon_x = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the definition of Bulk Modulus.**
The bulk modulus is defined by:

$$K = -\frac{p}{e} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad e = -\frac{p}{K}$$

(The negative sign makes $K$ positive under compression.)

**Step 6 ŌĆö Equate the two expressions for $e$.**

$$-\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Cancel $-p$ and rearrange.**

$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy:** Eliminate $\nu$ algebraically between Relation-1 and Relation-2.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express $\nu$ from Relation-1.**

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express $\nu$ from Relation-2.**

$$1 - 2\nu = \frac{E}{3K} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2\nu = 1 - \frac{E}{3K} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nu = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{E}{6K}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equate the two expressions for $\nu$.**

$$\frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{E}{6K}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Add 1 to both sides.**

$$\frac{E}{2G} = \frac{3}{2} - \frac{E}{6K}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Combine the right side over a common denominator.**

$$\frac{E}{2G} = \frac{9K - E}{6K}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Cross-multiply.**

$$6K \cdot E = 2G \cdot (9K - E)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for $G$.**

$$6KE = 18GK - 2GE$$

$$6KE + 2GE = 18GK$$

$$2E(3K + G) = 18GK$$

After cross-multiplying correctly and rearranging:

$$G = \frac{3KE}{9K - E}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{3KE}{9K - E}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** For steel, $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $K = 163 \text{ GPa}$ (typical). Then $G = \dfrac{3 \times 163 \times 200}{9 \times 163 - 200} = \dfrac{97800}{1267} \approx 77.2 \text{ GPa}$, which matches the standard textbook value. Ō£ō

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For a given material, Young's modulus $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$. Calculate the shear modulus $G$ and bulk modulus $K$.

**Solution:**

Using Relation-1:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1.30)} = \frac{210}{2.60} = 80.77 \text{ GPa}$$

Using Relation-2:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{210}{1.20} = 175.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**Cross-check using Relation-3:**

$$G = \frac{3 \times 175 \times 210}{9 \times 175 - 210} = \frac{110250}{1365} = 80.77 \text{ GPa} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Elastic Constant Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Isotropic Linear Elastic Material] --> B1[Uniaxial Tension Test]
    A0 --> B2[Torsion Test on Shaft]
    A0 --> B3[Hydrostatic Pressure Chamber]
    B1 --> C1[Young's Modulus E]
    B2 --> C2[Shear Modulus G]
    B3 --> C3[Bulk Modulus K]
    B1 --> C4[Poisson Ratio nu]
    C1 --> D1{E = 2G times 1 plus nu}
    C2 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C1 --> D2{E = 3K times 1 minus 2nu}
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2
    C2 --> D3{G = 3KE over 9K minus E}
    C3 --> D3
    C1 --> D3
    D1 --> E1[Verified Result 1]
    D2 --> E2[Verified Result 2]
    D3 --> E3[Verified Result 3]
    E1 --> F1[Engineering Design Inputs]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[ANSYS FEA]
    F1 --> G2[Material Certification]
    F1 --> G3[Structural Safety Check]
```

### 4.2 Schematic ŌĆö Pure Shear Element and Principal Stress Directions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Square Element side a under pure shear tau] --> S1[Face A and C carry tau in opposite directions]
    S0 --> S2[Face B and D carry tau in opposite directions]
    S1 --> P1[Principal stress sigma1 equals plus tau along diagonal BD]
    S2 --> P2[Principal stress sigma2 equals minus tau along diagonal AC]
    P1 --> R1[Strain along BD: epsilon1 equals tau times 1 plus nu over E]
    P2 --> R2[Strain along AC: epsilon2 equals minus tau times 1 plus nu over E]
    R1 --> F1[Shear strain gamma equals 2 times epsilon1]
    F1 --> F2[Equate with tau over G]
    F2 --> F3[Final: E equals 2G times 1 plus nu]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mind Map ŌĆö Three Derivations and Their Starting Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Goal: Relate E, G, K, and nu] --> Path1[Path 1: Pure Shear]
    Start --> Path2[Path 2: Hydrostatic Pressure]
    Start --> Path3[Path 3: Algebraic Elimination]
    Path1 --> Out1[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu]
    Path2 --> Out2[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu]
    Path3 --> Out3[G equals 3KE over 9K minus E]
    Out1 --> Final[All Three Constants Connected]
    Out2 --> Final
    Out3 --> Final
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents an *input quantity* or an *intermediate result*. The arrows represent the *direction of derivation*. The diamond blocks contain the *master relations* derived in Section 3. This visualizes how the three derivations interconnect to fully characterize an isotropic material.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define Poisson's ratio. For a perfectly incompressible material, what is its value?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Poisson's ratio $\nu$ is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a material subjected to uniaxial loading.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity. For a perfectly incompressible material, the volumetric strain is zero ($e = 0$). Using the relation $e = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu) = 0$ (for a uniaxial stress state), we obtain $\nu = 0.5$. Physically, an incompressible material (like rubber at low strains) does not change in volume when stretched, so the lateral contraction must exactly compensate the longitudinal extension. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU Sessional Exam ŌĆö Sample Paper, Module 1]**
*State the three relations connecting the elastic constants $E$, $G$, $K$, and $\nu$ for a homogeneous isotropic material.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The three relations are:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

$$G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{3KE}{9K - E} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

For an isotropic material, only **two** of the four constants are independent; the other two can be determined from these relations. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ for a homogeneous isotropic material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a material, Young's modulus $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$. Determine the shear modulus $G$ and bulk modulus $K$. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1:** Consider a square element of side $a$ subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces. Let the resulting shear strain be $\gamma$.

**Step 2:** By definition of shear modulus:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3:** For pure shear, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (along the elongating diagonal) and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (along the contracting diagonal), with $\sigma_3 = 0$.

**Step 4:** The normal strain along $\sigma_1$ direction using generalized Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** The shear strain relates to the principal strain by $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1$ (for small strains, from geometry of the deformed square):

$$\gamma = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 6:** Equating (i) and (ii):

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)} \quad \text{ŌĆö Proved}$$

**(7 Marks ŌĆö 1 for setup, 2 for principal stresses, 2 for Hooke's law application, 1 for strain relation, 1 for final result)**

##### **Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $G$:**
Using $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2.60}$$

$$G = 7.692 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 7.69 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $K$:**
Using $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{1.20}$$

$$K = 1.667 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cross-verification using Relation-3:**

$$G = \frac{3KE}{9K - E} = \frac{3 \times 1.667 \times 10^5 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{9 \times 1.667 \times 10^5 - 2.0 \times 10^5}$$

$$= \frac{1.000 \times 10^{11}}{1.300 \times 10^6} = 7.692 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:** $G = 7.69 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $K = 1.667 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ **(7 Marks ŌĆö 3 for $G$, 3 for $K$, 1 for verification)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ for a homogeneous isotropic material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A material has $E = 70 \text{ GPa}$ and $G = 26.92 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate Poisson's ratio $\nu$ and bulk modulus $K$. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1:** Consider a small cubic element of side $a$ subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all six faces. The normal stresses are:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2:** The volumetric strain for a cube is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 3:** Using generalized Hooke's law for $\varepsilon_x$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = -\frac{p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_x$. Therefore:

$$e = 3\varepsilon_x = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** The bulk modulus is defined by $K = -p/e$, so:

$$e = -\frac{p}{K}$$

**Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for $e$:

$$-\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)} \quad \text{ŌĆö Proved}$$

**(7 Marks ŌĆö 1 for setup, 2 for Hooke's law, 1 for volumetric strain, 1 for bulk modulus definition, 1 for equating, 1 for final result)**

##### **Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate Poisson's ratio $\nu$:**
Using $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$:

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{70}{2 \times 26.92} - 1 = \frac{70}{53.84} - 1$$

$$= 1.300 - 1 = 0.30$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate bulk modulus $K$:**
Using $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{70}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{70}{1.20} = 58.33 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification using auxiliary formula:**

$$\nu = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{E}{3K}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{70}{3 \times 58.33}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - 0.40\right) = 0.30 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:** $\nu = 0.30$, $K = 58.33 \text{ GPa}$ **(7 Marks ŌĆö 3 for $\nu$, 3 for $K$, 1 for verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Poisson's ratio definition** ŌåÆ -1 mark
> 2. **Writing $\gamma = \varepsilon_1$ instead of $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1$** in the shear derivation ŌåÆ This is the single most common error, costing up to -3 marks
> 3. **Mixing up $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ with $E = 3K(1+\nu)$** ŌåÆ -2 marks
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of small strains / infinitesimal deformation** in the geometry step ŌåÆ -1 mark
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** (e.g., reporting $G$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ when the question gives $E$ in $\text{GPa}$) ŌåÆ -1 mark
> 6. **Skipping the cross-verification step** using Relation-3 in numerical answers ŌåÆ -1 mark
> 7. **Not mentioning "isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic" assumptions** at the start of the derivation ŌåÆ -1 mark

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four constants, two independent:** For an isotropic material, only **two** of $\{E, G, K, \nu\}$ are independent. The other two follow from the three master relations.
- **The three master relations to memorize verbatim:**
  * $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  * $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  * $G = \dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$ (or equivalently $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$)
- **Two key derivations:** Derivation of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ via pure shear, and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ via hydrostatic pressure. These are 7-mark questions in every KTU ESE.
- **Crucial formula:** $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{\text{principal}}$ in pure shear ŌĆö this geometric relation is the heart of the first derivation.
- **Volumetric strain:** $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ (additive for small strains).
- **Physical bounds on Poisson's ratio:** $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$ (derived from the relations themselves).
- **Incompressibility limit:** $\nu \to 0.5$ corresponds to $K \to \infty$ and $G \to E/3$.
- **Engineering materials typically have:** $0.25 \le \nu \le 0.35$ (steel, aluminum, copper) and rubber is the only common material approaching $\nu = 0.5$.
- **Units reminder:** $E, G, K$ are in $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$). $\nu$ is dimensionless.
- **Exam tip:** Always **state assumptions** (isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic, small strains) at the start of derivations ŌĆö this fetches easy marks and signals conceptual clarity to the examiner.
- **Numerical problem strategy:** When two constants are given, immediately identify the unknown from the relation that involves only those two. Cross-verify with the third relation for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Stress, Strain and Change in Length in Axially Loaded Members with Single and Composite Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Stress, Strain and Change in Length in Axially Loaded Members with Single and Composite Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Axially Loaded Member:** A structural member (prismatic bar) in which the resultant internal force acts along its longitudinal centroidal axis, producing only **normal stress** $\sigma$ (tensile or compressive) uniformly distributed over the cross-section, provided the section is far from the points of load application (Saint-Venant's Principle).

When an axial force $P$ is applied to a bar of cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

The resulting **axial (linear/normal) strain** $\varepsilon$ is defined as the change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

where $\delta L$ is the change in length (elongation if positive, contraction if negative).

For materials obeying **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)**, expressed in **GPa (Gigapascals)** for metals.

A **Composite Bar** is a member made of two or more different materials bonded together, loaded either:
- **In Series (Concatenated):** Materials are joined end-to-end; the same axial force $P$ passes through each.
- **In Parallel (Co-axial / Compound):** Materials share the load side-by-side over the same length; elongations must be equal (compatibility).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Spring Telephone Analogy:**
> Imagine a heavy load (say, a bucket of water, $P = 100\,\text{N}$) being suspended by a thin rubber cord versus a thick steel wire of the same length.
> - The **rubber cord** (low $E$, small $A$) stretches a lot ŌĆö high $\delta L$.
> - The **steel wire** (high $E$, small $A$) stretches very little ŌĆö low $\delta L$.
> The governing equation $\delta L = \dfrac{P L}{A E}$ tells you that a stiffer material (high $E$) or a thicker cross-section (high $A$) resists elongation ŌĆö exactly like a thicker spring is harder to stretch.
> Now, if you glue a rubber cord and a steel wire **side-by-side** (parallel composite) and pull them, they must stretch by the **same amount** (they are bonded). The stiffer steel carries a larger share of the load ŌĆö this is the essence of the **compatibility condition** $\delta_1 = \delta_2$.

### 1.3 Important Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Standard value of $E$ for **mild steel** $\approx \mathbf{2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}}$ ($\mathbf{200\,\text{GPa}}$).
> - Standard value of $E$ for **copper** $\approx \mathbf{1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}}$ ($\mathbf{100\,\text{GPa}}$).
> - Standard value of $E$ for **brass** $\approx \mathbf{1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}}$ ($\mathbf{100\,\text{GPa}}$).
> - Standard value of $E$ for **aluminium** $\approx \mathbf{0.7 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}}$ ($\mathbf{70\,\text{GPa}}$).
> - Standard value of $E$ for **concrete** $\approx \mathbf{0.2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}}$ ($\mathbf{20\,\text{GPa}}$).
> - $1\,\text{N/mm}^{2} = 1\,\text{MPa}$, so $200\,\text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{MPa}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions (Saint-Venant & Linear Elasticity)

For the formulas in this module to be valid, the following conditions must hold:

1. The bar material is **homogeneous** (uniform properties at every point) and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
2. The bar material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic behaviour).
3. The bar is **initially straight** and the load is applied along the **centroidal axis** (no eccentric loading, no bending).
4. **Plane sections remain plane** after loading (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis for slender bars).
5. **Self-weight is neglected** unless explicitly given in the problem statement.
6. The bar is considered **prismatic** (constant $A$ in each segment) or analysed as a sequence of prismatic segments.

### 2.2 Conceptual Flow of Analysis

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Loading Type:**
- Determine if the member is in **tension** (force pulls outward) or **compression** (force pushes inward).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Internal Force Calculation:**
- Use the **method of sections**. Cut the bar at the location of interest. The internal axial force $N$ equals the algebraic sum of all external axial forces on either side of the cut.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Stress Calculation:**
- Compute normal stress $\sigma = N/A$ at the section of interest. Units: $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ or $\text{MPa}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Strain Calculation:**
- Apply Hooke's Law: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ (dimensionless, often expressed in micro-strain $\mu\varepsilon$).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Elongation (Change in Length) Calculation:**
- For a uniform segment of length $L$: $\delta L = \varepsilon L = \dfrac{N L}{A E}$.
- For multiple segments: $\delta L_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{N_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sign Convention:**
- **Tensile** forces and **elongations** are taken as **positive (+)**.
- **Compressive** forces and **shortenings** are taken as **negative (ŌĆō)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference ŌĆö Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Physical Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable To |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ or $\text{MPa}$ | Any uniform axially loaded member |
| 2 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) | Linear elastic region |
| 3 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | $E$ in $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ or $\text{GPa}$ | Linear elastic region |
| 4 | Elongation (Uniform Bar) | $\delta L = \dfrac{P L}{A E}$ | $\text{mm}$ (if lengths in $\text{mm}$, $P$ in $\text{N}$) | Single material, single section |
| 5 | Elongation (Stepped Bar) | $\delta L = \displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Single material, multiple cross-sections |
| 6 | Compatibility (Parallel Composite) | $\delta_1 = \delta_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \dfrac{\sigma_2}{E_2}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Composite bars in parallel |
| 7 | Stress Ratio (Parallel) | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Dimensionless | Rigidly bonded composite bars |
| 8 | Equilibrium (Parallel) | $P = P_1 + P_2 = \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2$ | $\text{N}$ | External load distribution |
| 9 | Equivalent Stiffness (Parallel) | $P = \delta L \cdot \dfrac{(A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)}{L}$ | $\text{N}$ | Shortcut for parallel composites |
| 10 | Elongation (Parallel Composite) | $\delta L = \dfrac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Direct formula for parallel |
| 11 | Elongation (Series Composite) | $\delta L = \displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{P L_i}{A_i E_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Same load $P$ through all segments |
| 12 | Strain Energy (Bonus) | $U = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2 A E}$ | $\text{N┬Ęmm}$ or $\text{Joules}$ | Often asked as sub-part |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Reminder:** In all of the above, **compressive** loads give **negative stress** and **negative strain** (shortening). KTU examiners deduct marks for forgetting the sign ŌĆö always state the nature of stress clearly.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Civil Engineering:** Sizing of high-rise building columns (often steel encased in concrete ŌĆö a parallel composite).
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of connecting rods, tie-rods in boilers, and tie-bars in bridges.
> - **Aerospace:** Fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite shafts, carbon-fibre control rods in aircraft.
> - **Pressure Vessels:** Multi-layer cylinders (shrunk-fit assemblies) are analysed using parallel composite theory.
> - **Civil Composite Columns:** Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) columns where steel rebars and concrete share the axial load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Elongation of a Uniform Prismatic Bar

**Statement:** A bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ is subjected to an axial tensile load $P$ at its free end. Derive an expression for the total elongation $\delta L$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Consider an elemental slice:**
Take an infinitesimal slice of the bar of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the free end. Let the cross-sectional area at this section be $A(x)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Internal force in the element:**
The internal axial force in this elemental slice equals the applied load $P$ (since the slice is in equilibrium and the only external load is $P$).

$$N(x) = P$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Stress on the element:**
The normal stress on the cross-section of the element is:

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{N(x)}{A(x)} = \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Strain in the element:**
Applying Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{\sigma(x)}{E} = \frac{P}{A E}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Elongation of the elemental slice:**
The infinitesimal elongation $d(\delta L)$ of this slice is strain times its original length:

$$d(\delta L) = \varepsilon(x)\, dx = \frac{P\, dx}{A E}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Total elongation by integration:**
Integrate over the full length of the bar. For a uniform bar ($A$ is constant):

$$\delta L = \int_0^L \frac{P}{A E} dx = \frac{P}{A E} \int_0^L dx = \frac{P L}{A E}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{P L}{A E}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Elongation is directly proportional to the load $P$ and the original length $L$, and inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$ and the modulus of elasticity $E$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Elongation of a Stepped Bar (Same Material)

**Statement:** A bar of length $L$ consists of $n$ prismatic segments of lengths $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ and cross-sectional areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$, all made of the same material ($E$ constant). An axial load $P$ is applied. Derive the total elongation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply equilibrium:**
Since the bar is loaded only at its ends and self-weight is neglected, the internal axial force in every section equals $P$:

$$N_i = P \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Elongation of each segment:**
From the uniform bar formula applied segment-wise:

$$\delta L_i = \frac{N_i L_i}{A_i E} = \frac{P L_i}{A_i E}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Superposition of elongations:**
The total elongation is the algebraic sum (segments in series add elongations because they are end-to-end):

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta L_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P L_i}{A_i E}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Factor out the constants:**
Since $P$ and $E$ are common to all terms:

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta L_{\text{total}} = \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Composite Bar in Series (Different Materials End-to-End)

**Statement:** A composite bar consists of $n$ segments of lengths $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$, areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$, and Young's moduli $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$, joined end-to-end. An axial load $P$ is applied. Derive the total elongation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Equilibrium:**
The same internal axial force $P$ is transmitted through every segment (no external load is applied at the interfaces):

$$N_i = P \quad \text{for all } i$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Elongation of each segment:**
Using $\delta L_i = N_i L_i / (A_i E_i)$ for each segment with its own $E_i$:

$$\delta L_i = \frac{P L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total elongation:**
Sum the individual elongations:

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P L_i}{A_i E_i} = P \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta L_{\text{total}} = P \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i E_i}}$$

**Note:** Unlike the stepped bar, here we cannot factor out $E$ because each segment has a different modulus.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Composite Bar in Parallel (Rigidly Bonded Side-by-Side)

**Statement:** Two prismatic bars of different materials are rigidly bonded along their length $L$ and share an axial load $P$. Material 1 has area $A_1$ and modulus $E_1$; material 2 has area $A_2$ and modulus $E_2$. Derive the stresses $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$ and the common elongation $\delta L$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compatibility (Geometric) Condition:**
Because the two materials are **rigidly bonded**, they must elongate by the same amount:

$$\delta L_1 = \delta L_2 = \delta L$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the compatibility using Hooke's Law:**

$$\delta L_1 = \frac{\sigma_1 L}{E_1}, \quad \delta L_2 = \frac{\sigma_2 L}{E_2}$$

Setting them equal:

$$\frac{\sigma_1 L}{E_1} = \frac{\sigma_2 L}{E_2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the compatibility equation:**
The length $L$ cancels from both sides:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_1 E_2 = \sigma_2 E_1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Equilibrium Condition:**
The total external load equals the sum of the loads carried by each material:

$$P = P_1 + P_2 = \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the two simultaneous equations:**
From the compatibility equation, express $\sigma_1$ in terms of $\sigma_2$:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 \cdot \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

Substitute into the equilibrium equation:

$$P = \left(\sigma_2 \cdot \frac{E_1}{E_2}\right) A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2 = \sigma_2 \left(\frac{A_1 E_1}{E_2} + A_2\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{\dfrac{A_1 E_1}{E_2} + A_2} = \frac{P \, E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

And by symmetry:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P \, E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Derive the elongation:**
Substitute $\sigma_2$ (or $\sigma_1$) into the elongation formula:

$$\delta L = \frac{\sigma_2 L}{E_2} = \frac{P \, E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot \frac{L}{E_2} = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Final Results (Parallel Composite):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = \frac{P \, E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}, \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{P \, E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}, \quad \delta L = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The product $A \cdot E$ is called the **axial stiffness**. The formula $\delta L = P L / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)$ shows that parallel members behave like **springs in parallel** ŌĆö their stiffnesses **add**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example ŌĆö Parallel Composite (Reference Solution for Question Bank)

**Problem:** A composite column consists of a solid steel core of $30\,\text{mm}$ diameter enclosed in a brass tube of outer diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $30\,\text{mm}$. The column carries an axial compressive load of $200\,\text{kN}$. Find: (i) the stresses in steel and brass, and (ii) the shortening of the column over a length of $500\,\text{mm}$. Take $E_s = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, $E_b = 1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d_s^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 900 = 706.858\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} (50^{2} - 30^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} (2500 - 900) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1600 = 1256.637\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the axial stiffness of each material:**

$$A_s E_s = 706.858 \times 2 \times 10^{5} = 1.4137 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

$$A_b E_b = 1256.637 \times 1 \times 10^{5} = 1.2566 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

$$A_s E_s + A_b E_b = (1.4137 + 1.2566) \times 10^{8} = 2.6704 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate the stress in steel:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P \, E_s}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \times 2 \times 10^{5}}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\sigma_s = 149.79\,\text{N/mm}^{2}\,(\text{compressive}) = 149.79\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate the stress in brass:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P \, E_b}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \times 1 \times 10^{5}}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\sigma_b = 74.90\,\text{N/mm}^{2}\,(\text{compressive}) = 74.90\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify the compatibility condition:**

$$\frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} = \frac{149.79}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 7.495 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_b}{E_b} = \frac{74.90}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 7.490 \times 10^{-4}$$

The values agree (small difference is rounding). Ō£ō

**Step 6 ŌĆö Calculate the shortening of the column:**

$$\delta L = \frac{\sigma_s L}{E_s} = \frac{149.79 \times 500}{2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{74{,}895}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 0.3745\,\text{mm}\,(\text{shortening})$$

**Alternative cross-check using the direct formula:**

$$\delta L = \frac{P L}{A_s E_s + A_b E_b} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \times 500}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{10^{8}}{2.6704 \times 10^{8}} = 0.3745\,\text{mm}\,Ō£ō$$

**Final Answers:**
- Stress in steel: $\sigma_s = 149.79\,\text{MPa}$ (compressive)
- Stress in brass: $\sigma_b = 74.90\,\text{MPa}$ (compressive)
- Shortening of column: $\delta L = 0.3745\,\text{mm}$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Classification of Axially Loaded Members

The following Mermaid flowchart maps out the analytical decision tree a student should follow when encountering any axially loaded member problem in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[AXIALLY LOADED MEMBER PROBLEM] --> Q1{SINGLE OR COMPOSITE MATERIAL}
    Q1 -- SINGLE --> Q2{UNIFORM OR STEPPED SECTION}
    Q1 -- COMPOSITE --> Q3{LOADING GEOMETRY}
    Q2 -- UNIFORM --> S1[APPLY DELTA L = PL BY AE]
    Q2 -- STEPPED --> S2[SUM DELTA L ACROSS SEGMENTS]
    Q3 -- SERIES --> S3[SAME FORCE P THROUGH ALL SEGMENTS]
    Q3 -- PARALLEL --> S4[COMPATIBILITY DELTA 1 = DELTA 2]
    S3 --> S5[DELTA L = P SUM L BY AE PER SEGMENT]
    S4 --> S6[EQUILIBRIUM P = SIGMA 1 A1 + SIGMA 2 A2]
    S6 --> S7[STRESS RATIO SIGMA 1 BY E1 = SIGMA 2 BY E2]
    S7 --> S8[SOLVE SIMULTANEOUS EQUATIONS]
    S8 --> S9[FIND SIGMA 1, SIGMA 2, DELTA L]
    S1 --> OUT[FINAL STRESS STRAIN ELONGATION]
    S2 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT
    S9 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Begin every problem at `START`. Decide whether you are dealing with a single-material or composite-material member. For composites, the **loading geometry** (series vs. parallel) determines whether you use **force-equality** or **elongation-equality** as your second governing equation.

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Solution Workflow for Parallel Composite Bars

This diagram illustrates the iterative step-by-step procedure to solve a two-material parallel composite problem, which is the most frequently asked configuration in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INPUT DATA P, L, A1, A2, E1, E2] --> P2[CALCULATE A1 E1 AND A2 E2]
    P2 --> P3[WRITE COMPATIBILITY: SIGMA 1 BY E1 = SIGMA 2 BY E2]
    P3 --> P4[WRITE EQUILIBRIUM: P = SIGMA 1 A1 + SIGMA 2 A2]
    P4 --> P5[SUBSTITUTE FROM COMPATIBILITY INTO EQUILIBRIUM]
    P5 --> P6[SOLVE FOR SIGMA 1 AND SIGMA 2]
    P6 --> P7[CALCULATE DELTA L USING DELTA L = SIGMA L BY E]
    P7 --> P8[VERIFY: SIGMA 1 A1 + SIGMA 2 A2 EQUALS P]
    P8 --> P9[OUTPUT: SIGMA 1, SIGMA 2, DELTA L]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation (Block Schematic)

Because Mermaid cannot easily render a scaled physical drawing, the following **block schematic** shows the conceptual layout of the two main composite bar configurations.

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    A["MATERIAL 1 L1 A1 E1"]:1
    B["MATERIAL 2 L2 A2 E2"]:1
    C["MATERIAL 3 L3 A3 E3"]:1
    D["SAME LOAD P PASSES THROUGH ALL"]:1
    E["SERIES CONFIGURATION"]:1
    F["MATERIAL 1 A1 E1"]:1
    G["MATERIAL 2 A2 E2"]:1
    H["BONDED ALONG LENGTH"]:1
    I["SHARED LOAD P = P1 + P2"]:1
    J["PARALLEL CONFIGURATION"]:1
```

| Configuration | Geometric Layout | Load Sharing | Elongation |
|---------------|-----------------|--------------|------------|
| **Series** | End-to-end (1 ŌåÆ 2 ŌåÆ 3) | Same $P$ in all | Elongations add: $\delta L = \Sigma \delta L_i$ |
| **Parallel** | Side-by-side (rigidly bonded) | Load splits: $P_1 + P_2 = P$ | Elongations are equal: $\delta L_1 = \delta L_2$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Define normal stress and normal strain in an axially loaded member. State Hooke's Law relating the two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** The intensity of internal resistive force per unit cross-sectional area developed at a section of an axially loaded member when subjected to an external axial force. Mathematically, $\sigma = P/A$, where $P$ is the internal axial force and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. Units: $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ or $\text{MPa}$.

> **Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of change in length ($\delta L$) of a member to its original length ($L$). Mathematically, $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$. It is a dimensionless quantity.

> **Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, normal stress is directly proportional to normal strain, and the constant of proportionality is the Young's Modulus ($E$) of the material. $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`
**What is a composite bar? State the compatibility condition for two materials rigidly bonded in parallel and loaded axially.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Composite Bar:** A structural member made up of two or more different materials of different Young's moduli, joined together to act as a single unit under axial loading.

> **Compatibility Condition (Parallel):** When two materials are rigidly bonded along their length and loaded axially, they must undergo the **same elongation** because the bond prevents any relative slip between them. Therefore, $\delta L_1 = \delta L_2$. Using Hooke's Law, this becomes $\sigma_1/E_1 = \sigma_2/E_2$, or equivalently $\sigma_1 E_2 = \sigma_2 E_1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the sign convention. Compressive loads give negative stress and negative elongation. Many students lose 1ŌĆō2 marks for not stating the nature (tensile/compressive).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Mixing up series and parallel logic. In **series**, force is the same; in **parallel**, elongation is the same. Drawing a free body diagram is the only way to avoid this trap.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using wrong units. If $P$ is in kN and $A$ in mm┬▓, the resulting stress is in **MPa**, not GPa. The factor is $10^{3}/10^{0} = 10^{3}$, i.e., kN/mm┬▓ = MPa.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** For composite problems, students often forget the **equilibrium equation** ($P = P_1 + P_2$) and only use compatibility. Both equations are mandatory for a 7-mark solution.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not verifying the final answer by substituting back into both governing equations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) A mild steel bar of cross-section 30 mm ├Ś 20 mm and length 1.5 m is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. Determine:**
- **(i) The normal stress in the bar.**
- **(ii) The total elongation of the bar.**

**Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Cross-section: $30\,\text{mm} \times 20\,\text{mm}$, so $A = 30 \times 20 = 600\,\text{mm}^{2}$
- Length: $L = 1.5\,\text{m} = 1500\,\text{mm}$
- Axial tensile load: $P = 90\,\text{kN} = 90{,}000\,\text{N}$
- Young's modulus: $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$

**(i) Normal Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{600} = 150\,\text{N/mm}^{2} = 150\,\text{MPa (tensile)}$$

**(ii) Total Elongation:**

Using $\delta L = P L / (A E)$:

$$\delta L = \frac{90{,}000 \times 1500}{600 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1.35 \times 10^{8}}{1.2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.125\,\text{mm (elongation)}$$

**Verification using strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{150}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\delta L = \varepsilon L = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1500 = 1.125\,\text{mm}\,Ō£ō$$

---

**(b) A composite bar in series consists of a copper section of length 400 mm and diameter 25 mm joined to a steel section of length 600 mm and diameter 25 mm. An axial tensile load of 60 kN is applied at the ends. Find:**
- **(i) The stresses in copper and steel.**
- **(ii) The total elongation of the bar.**

**Take $E_c = 1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, $E_s = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Copper: $L_c = 400\,\text{mm}$, $d_c = 25\,\text{mm}$, $E_c = 1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$
- Steel: $L_s = 600\,\text{mm}$, $d_s = 25\,\text{mm}$, $E_s = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$
- Load: $P = 60\,\text{kN} = 60{,}000\,\text{N}$ (tensile)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute areas (same diameter, so equal areas):**

$$A_c = A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} (25)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 625 = 490.874\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply equilibrium (series, same load):**

$$N_c = N_s = P = 60{,}000\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate stresses:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A_c} = \frac{60{,}000}{490.874} = 122.23\,\text{MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{60{,}000}{490.874} = 122.23\,\text{MPa (tensile)}$$

(Same stress because areas and forces are equal ŌĆö but the strains and elongations will differ due to different $E$.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate elongations:**

$$\delta L_c = \frac{P L_c}{A_c E_c} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 400}{490.874 \times 1 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{7}}{4.909 \times 10^{7}} = 0.4889\,\text{mm}$$

$$\delta L_s = \frac{P L_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 600}{490.874 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{7}}{9.817 \times 10^{7}} = 0.3667\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total elongation:**

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \delta L_c + \delta L_s = 0.4889 + 0.3667 = 0.8556\,\text{mm (elongation)}$$

**Verification using the series formula:**

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = P \left( \frac{L_c}{A_c E_c} + \frac{L_s}{A_s E_s} \right) = 60{,}000 \left( \frac{400}{490.874 \times 10^{5}} + \frac{600}{490.874 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} \right)$$

$$= 60{,}000 \left( 8.149 \times 10^{-6} + 6.112 \times 10^{-6} \right) = 60{,}000 \times 1.4261 \times 10^{-5} = 0.8556\,\text{mm}\,Ō£ō$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) A composite bar in parallel consists of a solid aluminium core of 40 mm diameter surrounded by a copper tube of outer diameter 60 mm and inner diameter 40 mm. An axial compressive load of 300 kN is applied. Find:**
- **(i) The stresses developed in aluminium and copper.**
- **(ii) The shortening of the composite bar over a length of 800 mm.**

**Take $E_{Al} = 0.7 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, $E_{Cu} = 1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Aluminium core: $d_{Al} = 40\,\text{mm}$
- Copper tube: $D_o = 60\,\text{mm}$, $D_i = 40\,\text{mm}$
- Load: $P = 300\,\text{kN} = 300{,}000\,\text{N}$ (compressive)
- Length: $L = 800\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_{Al} = \frac{\pi}{4} (40)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1600 = 1256.637\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{Cu} = \frac{\pi}{4} (60^2 - 40^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (3600 - 1600) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2000 = 1570.796\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate axial stiffnesses:**

$$A_{Al} E_{Al} = 1256.637 \times 0.7 \times 10^{5} = 0.8796 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

$$A_{Cu} E_{Cu} = 1570.796 \times 1.0 \times 10^{5} = 1.5708 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

$$\Sigma A E = 0.8796 \times 10^{8} + 1.5708 \times 10^{8} = 2.4504 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate stress in aluminium (compressive):**

$$\sigma_{Al} = \frac{P \cdot E_{Al}}{A_{Al} E_{Al} + A_{Cu} E_{Cu}} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 10^{5}}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\sigma_{Al} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{10}}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}} = 85.70\,\text{MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate stress in copper (compressive):**

$$\sigma_{Cu} = \frac{P \cdot E_{Cu}}{A_{Al} E_{Al} + A_{Cu} E_{Cu}} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{5}}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\sigma_{Cu} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{10}}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}} = 122.43\,\text{MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify compatibility:**

$$\frac{\sigma_{Al}}{E_{Al}} = \frac{85.70}{0.7 \times 10^{5}} = 12.243 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_{Cu}}{E_{Cu}} = \frac{122.43}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 12.243 \times 10^{-4}\,Ō£ō$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Calculate the shortening:**

$$\delta L = \frac{\sigma_{Al} L}{E_{Al}} = \frac{85.70 \times 800}{0.7 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{68{,}560}{70{,}000} = 0.9794\,\text{mm (shortening)}$$

**Cross-check:**

$$\delta L = \frac{P L}{A_{Al} E_{Al} + A_{Cu} E_{Cu}} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 800}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{8}}{2.4504 \times 10^{8}} = 0.9794\,\text{mm}\,Ō£ō$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\sigma_{Al} = 85.70\,\text{MPa (compressive)}$
- $\sigma_{Cu} = 122.43\,\text{MPa (compressive)}$
- $\delta L = 0.9794\,\text{mm (shortening)}$

---

**(b) A stepped steel bar is made up of three segments: Section 1 of 30 mm diameter and 200 mm length, Section 2 of 50 mm diameter and 300 mm length, and Section 3 of 40 mm diameter and 250 mm length. An axial tensile load of 80 kN is applied at one end. Calculate the total elongation of the bar.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Section 1: $d_1 = 30\,\text{mm}$, $L_1 = 200\,\text{mm}$
- Section 2: $d_2 = 50\,\text{mm}$, $L_2 = 300\,\text{mm}$
- Section 3: $d_3 = 40\,\text{mm}$, $L_3 = 250\,\text{mm}$
- Load: $P = 80\,\text{kN} = 80{,}000\,\text{N}$ (tensile, same throughout)
- Material: Steel, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate areas of each section:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 900 = 706.858\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (50)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2500 = 1963.495\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_3 = \frac{\pi}{4} (40)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1600 = 1256.637\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the stepped bar formula:**

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{L_i}{A_i}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $L_i/A_i$ for each section:**

$$\frac{L_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{706.858} = 0.2829\,\text{mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{L_2}{A_2} = \frac{300}{1963.495} = 0.1528\,\text{mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{L_3}{A_3} = \frac{250}{1256.637} = 0.1989\,\text{mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum the values:**

$$\sum \frac{L_i}{A_i} = 0.2829 + 0.1528 + 0.1989 = 0.6346\,\text{mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute total elongation:**

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = \frac{80{,}000}{2 \times 10^{5}} \times 0.6346 = 0.4 \times 0.6346 = 0.2538\,\text{mm (elongation)}$$

**Cross-check by computing each segment's elongation individually:**

$$\delta L_1 = \frac{80{,}000 \times 200}{706.858 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{1.4137 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1132\,\text{mm}$$

$$\delta L_2 = \frac{80{,}000 \times 300}{1963.495 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{7}}{3.927 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0611\,\text{mm}$$

$$\delta L_3 = \frac{80{,}000 \times 250}{1256.637 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{7}}{2.5133 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0796\,\text{mm}$$

$$\delta L_{\text{total}} = 0.1132 + 0.0611 + 0.0796 = 0.2539\,\text{mm}\,Ō£ō$$

**Final Answer:** $\delta L_{\text{total}} = 0.254\,\text{mm (elongation)}$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Axially Loaded Members**

**Conceptual Foundation:**
- ŌśÉ **Normal Stress:** $\sigma = P/A$ ŌĆö force per unit area on a cross-section.
- ŌśÉ **Normal Strain:** $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ ŌĆö change in length per unit original length.
- ŌśÉ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ ŌĆö valid only within the proportional (elastic) limit.

**Single Material ŌĆö Uniform Bar:**
- ŌśÉ **Elongation:** $\delta L = P L / (A E)$.
- ŌśÉ All of $P$, $L$, $A$, $E$ must be in **consistent units** (commonly N, mm, mm┬▓, N/mm┬▓).
- ŌśÉ A uniform bar has **uniform stress** along its length.

**Single Material ŌĆö Stepped Bar:**
- ŌśÉ Internal force is the same ($N_i = P$) in every section.
- ŌśÉ **Total elongation:** $\delta L_{\text{total}} = (P/E) \sum (L_i / A_i)$.
- ŌśÉ **Stress varies** between sections (highest stress in the section with the smallest area).

**Composite Bar ŌĆö Series Configuration:**
- ŌśÉ **Same axial force** $P$ passes through all segments.
- ŌśÉ **Total elongation:** $\delta L = P \sum (L_i / (A_i E_i))$.
- ŌśÉ Stresses differ between segments if areas differ; strains always differ if $E$ differs.

**Composite Bar ŌĆö Parallel Configuration:**
- ŌśÉ **Compatibility:** $\delta L_1 = \delta L_2 \Rightarrow \sigma_1 / E_1 = \sigma_2 / E_2$.
- ŌśÉ **Equilibrium:** $P = \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2$.
- ŌśÉ **Stress formulas:** $\sigma_1 = P E_1 / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)$ and $\sigma_2 = P E_2 / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)$.
- ŌśÉ **Elongation formula:** $\delta L = P L / (A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2)$.
- ŌśÉ The product $A \cdot E$ is the **axial stiffness**; parallel members add stiffnesses.

**Sign Convention (Frequently Tested):**
- ŌśÉ **Tensile loads** ŌåÆ positive stress, positive strain, positive elongation.
- ŌśÉ **Compressive loads** ŌåÆ negative stress, negative strain, shortening (negative elongation).
- ŌśÉ Always **state the nature** (tensile/compressive) in your final answer.

**Unit Conversion Traps:**
- ŌśÉ $1\,\text{kN} = 10^{3}\,\text{N}$; $1\,\text{N/mm}^{2} = 1\,\text{MPa}$.
- ŌśÉ $1\,\text{m} = 10^{3}\,\text{mm}$; $1\,\text{GPa} = 10^{3}\,\text{MPa} = 10^{3}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$.

**Common Engineering Values of E (Memorize):**
- ŌśÉ Steel: $2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (200 GPa).
- ŌśÉ Copper: $1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (100 GPa).
- ŌśÉ Brass: $1 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (100 GPa).
- ŌśÉ Aluminium: $0.7 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (70 GPa).

**Validation Strategy (Always Apply):**
- ŌśÉ Substitute the final answer back into **both** governing equations (compatibility + equilibrium).
- ŌśÉ Check the **dimensional consistency** of the final result.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Thermal Loading - Thermal Stress and Thermal Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effects of Thermal Loading - Thermal Stress and Thermal Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermal Strain?

When the temperature of a solid body changes, its dimensions change. If a uniform bar of original length $L$ experiences a uniform temperature change $\Delta T$, the small change in its length $\delta_T$ is called **thermal expansion** (or contraction if the body cools). The associated dimensionless deformation, normalized by the original length, is called **thermal strain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Thermal Strain):**
> Thermal strain is the strain produced in a body solely due to a change in temperature, in the absence of any external mechanical load. For a homogeneous, isotropic material, it is given by:
> $$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$$
> where $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of linear thermal expansion** (unit: per ┬░C or per K) and $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$ is the change in temperature.

## 1.2 What is Thermal Stress?

A material expands or contracts freely when heated or cooled ŌĆö **only if it is unconstrained**. The moment this free deformation is prevented (by external restraints, neighboring members, or the material's own internal continuity), internal forces are generated. These self-equilibrating internal forces, distributed over the cross-section, produce **thermal stress**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Thermal Stress):**
> Thermal stress is the internal stress induced in a body when its free thermal expansion (or contraction) is fully or partially prevented, either by external constraints or by continuity with other parts of the structure.
> For a fully constrained bar:
> $$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$
> where $E$ is the Young's modulus of the material.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a long steel rail lying on the ground on a cold winter morning.
- **Free condition (no stress):** The rail simply gets a tiny bit longer as the sun warms it. There is no internal force ŌĆö the rail happily stretches.
- **Constrained condition (stress develops):** Now imagine the same rail is rigidly bolted between two immovable concrete walls. When the temperature rises, the rail "wants" to expand but the walls refuse to let it. The walls push back, and the rail is squeezed in **compression**. That squeezing is *thermal stress* ŌĆö invisible, but capable of buckling railroad tracks or cracking bridge decks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three classical scenarios in KTU problems:**
> 1. **Free bar (no stress):** $\delta_T = L \alpha \Delta T$ freely accommodated.
> 2. **Fully constrained bar (maximum stress):** $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$, member is in pure compression (if heated) or tension (if cooled).
> 3. **Partially constrained bar (e.g., with a gap):** Stress develops only after the free expansion exceeds the available gap.

## 1.4 The Physical Constant ŌĆö Coefficient of Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)

The coefficient $\alpha$ is a **material property** indicating how much a unit length expands per degree of temperature rise. Standard values at room temperature (used in KTU exam problems):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical values of $\alpha$ (per ┬░C):**
> - Steel: $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
> - Brass: $\alpha \approx 19 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
> - Copper: $\alpha \approx 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
> - Aluminum: $\alpha \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
> - Concrete: $\alpha \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
>
> For steel, with $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$, **a temperature rise of just 50 ┬░C produces a thermal stress of about 120 MPa** ŌĆö comparable to the yield stress of mild steel. This is why thermal effects in steel structures are never ignored.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Logic of Thermal Stress Analysis

Step 1 ŌĆö **Free thermal deformation** of each member is calculated assuming no restraint:

$$\delta_T = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

Step 2 ŌĆö **Compatibility equation** is written by equating the actual deformation to the *geometrically possible* deformation. If the structure is restrained, the net deformation is the *difference* between free thermal expansion and the elastic compression caused by the restraint force $P$:

$$\delta_{actual} = \delta_{thermal} - \delta_{mechanical}$$

Step 3 ŌĆö **Equilibrium equation** is applied. For a statically indeterminate system with one redundant restraint, the redundant force $P$ is found by combining compatibility and equilibrium.

## 2.2 Why Thermal Stress Exists ŌĆö The Underlying Physics

- Solids are made of atoms held by interatomic bonds. As temperature rises, atomic vibrations increase in amplitude, and the average interatomic spacing increases ŌĆö this is the microscopic origin of thermal expansion.
- If a bar is free, the atoms simply spread out, and no force is needed.
- If the bar is clamped, the atoms *cannot* spread out. The constraint supplies an external compressive force that pushes the atoms back to their original average spacing. The bar is now in a state of **mechanical compression**, and the magnitude of this stress is exactly $E \alpha \Delta T$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table ŌĆö Topic: Thermal Stress and Strain**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Thermal strain | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ in /┬░C, $\Delta T$ in ┬░C |
| 2 | Free thermal elongation | $\delta_T = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $L$ in mm or m, $\delta_T$ in same unit |
| 3 | Fully constrained stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $E$ in MPa or GPa |
| 4 | Constrained strain (mechanical) | $\varepsilon_m = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | Elastic part only |
| 5 | Net deformation (constrained bar) | $\delta = L \alpha \Delta T - \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | $P$ is the restraint force |
| 6 | Bar with initial gap $g$ | Stress develops only if $L \alpha \Delta T > g$ | Otherwise $\sigma = 0$ |
| 7 | Composite bar (two materials) | Compatibility: $\delta_{T1} - \delta_{P1} = \delta_{T2} - \delta_{P2}$ | Same force $P$ in both |
| 8 | Compatibility equation (rigid walls) | $\alpha_1 L_1 \Delta T_1 + \alpha_2 L_2 \Delta T_2 = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$ | Both materials between rigid walls |
| 9 | Temperature for allowable stress | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{E \, \alpha}$ | Reverse of fully constrained case |
| 10 | Force in compound bar | $P = \dfrac{\alpha_1 L_1 \Delta T + \alpha_2 L_2 \Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}$ | Direct from compatibility |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Railway tracks:** Gaps (expansion joints) are deliberately left between successive rail segments. Modern *continuously welded rails* (CWR) manage thermal stress through concrete sleepers and fastenings.
- **Bridge expansion joints and bearings:** Allow decks to expand and contract without inducing damaging forces in piers.
- **Bimetallic strips:** Two metals of different $\alpha$ bonded together bend predictably with temperature ŌĆö used in thermostats and circuit breakers.
- **Pipelines and prestressed concrete:** Thermal effects are deliberately combined with prestress to control cracking.
- **Turbine rotors and pressure vessels:** Differential thermal expansion in thick sections creates secondary stresses that must be checked during start-up and shut-down.

## 2.5 Sign Convention Adopted in KTU Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> - **Heating ($\Delta T > 0$):** Material wants to elongate. If restrained, the bar is pushed by the supports, hence it is in **compression** ($\sigma$ negative if we follow tension-positive convention).
> - **Cooling ($\Delta T < 0$):** Material wants to contract. If restrained, the supports hold it back, hence the bar is in **tension** ($\sigma$ positive).
> - In exam answers, clearly state whether the bar is in tension or compression; this is a frequently marked step.
