# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Stress in a Fully Constrained Bar

**Setup:** A uniform bar of length $L$, cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient $\alpha$ is rigidly fixed between two immovable walls. The temperature is raised by $\Delta T$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Free Thermal Elongation

If the bar were free, it would elongate by:

$$\delta_{free} = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

This is the deformation the bar "wants" to undergo.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Mechanical Shortening Required by the Walls

The walls do not move, so the actual deformation must be zero. The walls therefore push the bar with a compressive force $P$ that compresses it by exactly the free elongation:

$$\delta_{mech} = \dfrac{P L}{A E}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compatibility (Geometric) Equation

The total deformation is the sum of the free thermal expansion and the mechanical shortening due to the reaction. Because the bar is rigidly held, the net deformation must equal zero:

$$\delta_{free} - \delta_{mech} = 0$$

$$L \, \alpha \, \Delta T - \dfrac{P L}{A E} = 0$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for the Reaction Force

$$\dfrac{P L}{A E} = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

$$P = A \, E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Thermal Stress

$$\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{P}{A} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

> This is the **fundamental thermal stress equation** for a fully constrained bar. The result is independent of the bar's length and cross-sectional area ŌĆö it depends only on the material properties $E$ and $\alpha$ and the temperature change $\Delta T$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bar With an Initial Gap

**Setup:** A bar of length $L$ is placed between two walls, with a small gap $g$ between the bar and one wall. The temperature rises by $\Delta T$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Free Thermal Elongation

$$\delta_{free} = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Two Possible Cases

**Case A:** If $\delta_{free} \le g$, the bar expands but does not touch the far wall. No stress develops:

$$\sigma_{th} = 0$$

**Case B:** If $\delta_{free} > g$, the bar contacts the far wall. The portion of the expansion that the wall now resists is the *excess* over the gap:

$$\delta_{mech} = \delta_{free} - g = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T - g$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compatibility Equation for Case B

$$\dfrac{P L}{A E} = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T - g$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Reaction Force

$$P = \dfrac{A E}{L} \left( L \, \alpha \, \Delta T - g \right)$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Thermal Stress

$$\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{P}{A} = E \left( \alpha \, \Delta T - \dfrac{g}{L} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the natural generalization of the fully-constrained result. Setting $g = 0$ recovers $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Composite Bar Between Two Rigid Walls

**Setup:** Two bars of different materials are joined end-to-end and placed between two rigid walls. Material 1 has length $L_1$, area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, coefficient $\alpha_1$. Material 2 has $L_2, A_2, E_2, \alpha_2$. The system temperature is raised by $\Delta T$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Free Thermal Expansions (Independent)

Material 1 wants to expand by $\delta_{T1} = L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T$.

Material 2 wants to expand by $\delta_{T2} = L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$.

If both were free, the total free expansion would be $\delta_{T1} + \delta_{T2}$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Reaction from Rigid Walls

The walls are immovable, so the actual total deformation must be zero. The walls push back with a common compressive force $P$ that compresses both segments. (Because the segments are in series between the same two walls, the internal force throughout the assembly is the same.)

### Step 3 ŌĆö Mechanical Compression of Each Segment

$$\delta_{P1} = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1}$$

$$\delta_{P2} = \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compatibility Equation

The total free thermal expansion is exactly cancelled by the total mechanical compression:

$$\delta_{T1} + \delta_{T2} = \delta_{P1} + \delta_{P2}$$

$$L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for the Reaction Force $P$

$$P = \dfrac{\Delta T \left( \alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2 \right)}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Thermal Stress in Each Segment

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{P}{A_1} = \dfrac{\Delta T \left( \alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2 \right)}{A_1 \left( \dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} \right)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{P}{A_2} = \dfrac{\Delta T \left( \alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2 \right)}{A_2 \left( \dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are *not* equal to $E_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T$ and $E_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$ respectively. They are coupled through the single force $P$. This is the most common source of mistakes in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Allowable Temperature Rise for a Given Permissible Stress

**Setup:** A bar is fully constrained. The maximum permissible stress is $\sigma_{allow}$. Find the maximum temperature rise the bar can safely undergo.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the Fully-Constrained Stress Equation

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Impose the Strength Condition

$$\sigma_{th} \le \sigma_{allow}$$

$$E \, \alpha \, \Delta T \le \sigma_{allow}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $\Delta T$

$$\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{E \, \alpha}$$

> **Quick numerical example:** For a steel bar with $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C, and $\sigma_{allow} = 120$ MPa:
> $$\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 12 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{120}{2400} = 50 \text{ ┬░C}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Single Material vs. Composite ŌĆö A Side-by-Side Snapshot**

| Feature | Single Bar (Fully Constrained) | Composite Bar (Two Materials) |
|---------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Number of unknown forces | 1 | 1 (common reaction) |
| Equations needed | 1 (compatibility) | 1 (compatibility) ŌĆö but coupled |
| Stress depends on $L$ and $A$? | No | Yes |
| Stress equal to $E \alpha \Delta T$? | Yes | No (only in the special limit $L_2 \to 0$) |
| Typical KTU question | Find $\sigma$ for a given $\Delta T$ | Find $P$ or $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ for a given $\Delta T$ |
| Difficulty level | Easy | Moderate to Hard |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free vs. Constrained Thermal Expansion ŌĆö Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bar Subjected to Temperature Change Delta T"] --> B{"Is the bar free to expand?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Free Thermal Elongation deltaT equals L alpha Delta T"]
    C --> D["Thermal Strain epsilon equals alpha Delta T"]
    C --> E["Thermal Stress sigma equals 0"]
    B -- No --> F{"Is the bar fully constrained or has a gap g?"}
    F -- Fully Constrained --> G["sigma equals E alpha Delta T, Compression if Heated"]
    F -- Gap g Exists --> H{"Is L alpha Delta T greater than g?"}
    H -- No --> I["Bar does not contact wall, sigma equals 0"]
    H -- Yes --> J["sigma equals E times alpha Delta T minus g over L, Compression"]
    G --> K["Compute Reaction Force P equals sigma times A"]
    J --> K
    I --> L["No Reaction Force, P equals 0"]
```

## 4.2 Composite Bar Between Rigid Walls ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Wall A: Rigid, Immovable"] --> M1["Material 1: Length L1, Area A1, Modulus E1, Coefficient alpha1"]
    M1 --> M2["Material 2: Length L2, Area A2, Modulus E2, Coefficient alpha2"]
    M2 --> R1["Wall B: Rigid, Immovable"]
    M1 -. "Internal Force P same in both" .-> M2
    L1 -. "Reaction RA equals P pushing left" .-> L1
    R1 -. "Reaction RB equals P pushing right" .-> R1
```

## 4.3 Three-State Deformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["State 1: Initial Geometry - Length L, Temperature T0"] --> S2["State 2: Free Expansion - Length L plus L alpha Delta T, Stress equals 0"]
    S2 --> S3["State 3: Mechanical Compression by Walls - Length returns to L, Stress equals E alpha Delta T Compression"]
    S1 -. "Skip State 2 if directly constrained" .-> S3
```

## 4.4 Problem-Solving Decision Tree (KTU Exam Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Read the problem and identify all members and supports"] --> Q2["Identify supports: Fixed-Fixed, Fixed with Gap, or Free"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Compute Free Thermal Elongation of each member using L alpha Delta T"]
    Q3 --> Q4{"Are supports fully restraining the system?"}
    Q4 -- No, gap exists --> Q5["Check if total free expansion exceeds gap, if no, stress is zero"]
    Q4 -- Yes, fully restrained --> Q6["Set up Compatibility Equation: Free Expansion equals Mechanical Compression"]
    Q5 --> Q7["If yes, write compatibility with gap included"]
    Q6 --> Q8["Solve for Reaction Force P"]
    Q7 --> Q8
    Q8 --> Q9["Compute Stress sigma equals P over A for each member"]
    Q9 --> Q10["Report Stress with sign: Tension positive, Compression negative"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define thermal strain and thermal stress. State the relation between them for a fully constrained bar.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal strain is the strain produced in a material solely due to a change in temperature, given by $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear thermal expansion and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature.

Thermal stress is the internal stress induced when the free thermal deformation of a body is prevented, either by external constraints or by continuity with other parts of the structure.

For a fully constrained uniform bar of Young's modulus $E$:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \varepsilon_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

The bar develops compressive stress when heated and tensile stress when cooled.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A steel rod of length 1.5 m is placed between two rigid walls with a gap of 1 mm. Find the temperature rise at which the rod will just touch the wall. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per ┬░C.**

**Model Answer:**

For the rod to just touch the wall, the free thermal elongation must equal the gap:

$$L \, \alpha \, \Delta T = g$$

$$1.5 \times 1000 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta T = 1$$

$$1500 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta T = 1$$

$$0.018 \, \Delta T = 1$$

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{1}{0.018} = 55.56 \text{ ┬░C}$$

The rod will just touch the wall when the temperature rises by approximately **55.56 ┬░C**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A composite bar consists of a steel section and a brass section placed between two rigid walls as shown. The steel portion has length 800 mm and cross-sectional area 1200 mm┬▓. The brass portion has length 600 mm and cross-sectional area 900 mm┬▓. The temperature of the assembly is raised by 40 ┬░C. Determine:**
**(a) the magnitude of the force developed in the bar, and**
**(b) the stresses induced in the steel and brass portions.**

**Given:**
- Steel: $E_s = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C
- Brass: $E_b = 100$ GPa, $\alpha_b = 19 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Free thermal expansions (both materials are between rigid walls).

Steel:

$$\delta_{Ts} = L_s \, \alpha_s \, \Delta T = 800 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 0.384 \text{ mm}$$

Brass:

$$\delta_{Tb} = L_b \, \alpha_b \, \Delta T = 600 \times 19 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 0.456 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Total free expansion:

$$\delta_{T,total} = 0.384 + 0.456 = 0.840 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Mechanical compression produced by the restraint force $P$ (in N):

For steel:

$$\delta_{Ps} = \dfrac{P L_s}{A_s E_s} = \dfrac{P \times 800}{1200 \times 200 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{P \times 800}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} \text{ mm}$$

For brass:

$$\delta_{Pb} = \dfrac{P L_b}{A_b E_b} = \dfrac{P \times 600}{900 \times 100 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{P \times 600}{9 \times 10^{7}} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Compatibility equation (total thermal expansion = total mechanical compression):

$$\delta_{T,total} = \delta_{Ps} + \delta_{Pb}$$

$$0.840 = \dfrac{800 \, P}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} + \dfrac{600 \, P}{9 \times 10^{7}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the coefficients:

$$\dfrac{800}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\dfrac{600}{9 \times 10^{7}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$0.840 = (3.333 \times 10^{-6} + 6.667 \times 10^{-6}) \, P$$

$$0.840 = 10.0 \times 10^{-6} \, P$$

$$P = \dfrac{0.840}{10.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 84{,}000 \text{ N} = 84 \text{ kN}$$

**The force developed in the bar is 84 kN.**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Stress in steel:

$$\sigma_s = \dfrac{P}{A_s} = \dfrac{84{,}000}{1200} = 70 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 2:** Stress in brass:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{84{,}000}{900} = 93.33 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

> Both members are in **compression** because the temperature has increased and the walls restrain the expansion.

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$P = \dfrac{\Delta T \left( \alpha_s L_s + \alpha_b L_b \right)}{\dfrac{L_s}{A_s E_s} + \dfrac{L_b}{A_b E_b}}$$

$$P = \dfrac{40 \times \left( 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 800 + 19 \times 10^{-6} \times 600 \right)}{\dfrac{800}{1200 \times 200 \times 10^{3}} + \dfrac{600}{900 \times 100 \times 10^{3}}}$$

Numerator:

$$40 \times \left( 9.6 \times 10^{-3} + 11.4 \times 10^{-3} \right) = 40 \times 21.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.84$$

Denominator:

$$\dfrac{800}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} + \dfrac{600}{9 \times 10^{7}} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} + 6.667 \times 10^{-6} = 10.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P = \dfrac{0.84}{10.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 84{,}000 \text{ N} = 84 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**A steel rod of 30 mm diameter and 2 m length is placed between two rigid supports. The temperature of the rod is then raised by 60 ┬░C. Find:**
**(a) the thermal stress developed in the rod, and**
**(b) the maximum temperature rise the rod can safely withstand if the allowable stress is 90 MPa.**

**Given:** $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /┬░C.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** The bar is fully constrained between rigid walls, so the thermal stress is given directly by:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$\sigma_{th} = (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 200 \times 12 \times 60 \times 10^{3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 144{,}000 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 144 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**The thermal stress developed in the rod is 144 MPa (compressive).**

**Optional verification via the force method:**

Free elongation:

$$\delta_{free} = L \, \alpha \, \Delta T = 2000 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 1.44 \text{ mm}$$

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

Force required to compress the bar by 1.44 mm:

$$P = \dfrac{\delta_{free} \, A \, E}{L} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 706.86 \times 200 \times 10^{3}}{2000} = 101{,}788 \text{ N} \approx 101.8 \text{ kN}$$

Stress:

$$\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{101{,}788}{706.86} = 144 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** The strength condition is $\sigma_{th} \le \sigma_{allow}$. Using the fully-constrained formula:

$$E \, \alpha \, \Delta T_{max} = \sigma_{allow}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\Delta T_{max}$:

$$\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{E \, \alpha}$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{90 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{90 \times 10^{6}}{2.4 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = 37.5 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**The maximum safe temperature rise is 37.5 ┬░C.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $\alpha$ is given in /┬░C, $E$ in GPa or MPa, $L$ in mm or m. Inconsistent units are the **#1 reason** for numerical errors in thermal stress problems. Always express everything in **N and mm** (so $E$ in MPa, $L$ in mm, $\delta$ in mm) or in **N and m** (so $E$ in Pa, $L$ in m, $\delta$ in m).
>
> 2. **Sign of stress:** When the bar is heated and fully constrained, the stress is **compressive** ŌĆö not tensile. Examiners specifically check for the correct sign. State clearly: "The bar is in compression because the supports prevent the thermal expansion."
>
> 3. **Confusing free elongation with final elongation:** Free elongation is the deformation the bar *wants* to undergo. The actual deformation is determined by the boundary conditions. Never equate free elongation directly to stress.
>
> 4. **Composite bar stress formula:** A common mistake is to write $\sigma_s = E_s \alpha_s \Delta T$ and $\sigma_b = E_b \alpha_b \Delta T$ *independently* for a composite bar. This is **wrong** when both materials share the same walls. The correct procedure is to use the compatibility equation to find the common force $P$ first, then divide by the respective areas.
>
> 5. **Gap problem oversight:** In problems with a gap, students often forget to check whether the temperature rise is large enough to close the gap. Always verify: $L \alpha \Delta T > g$?
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always write the assumption that the bar is homogeneous, isotropic, and that the temperature change is uniform throughout the length.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Thermal Stress and Strain**

- **Thermal strain** is the strain due to temperature change alone, $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$. It exists even in a free bar.
- **Thermal stress** exists **only when the free thermal deformation is prevented**, $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ for a fully constrained bar.
- A free bar has thermal strain but **no** thermal stress.
- The coefficient of linear thermal expansion $\alpha$ has units of **per ┬░C** (or per K, which is numerically identical for a temperature interval).
- The fully-constrained thermal stress is **independent** of the bar's length and cross-sectional area ŌĆö it depends only on $E$, $\alpha$, and $\Delta T$.
- For a bar with a gap $g$ between it and a wall, stress develops only if $L \alpha \Delta T > g$. The stress is then $\sigma_{th} = E \left( \alpha \Delta T - g/L \right)$.
- For a composite bar between rigid walls, the **same force** acts in both materials; use the compatibility equation to find $P$, then compute individual stresses as $\sigma = P/A$.
- **Heating ŌåÆ Compression; Cooling ŌåÆ Tension** (when the bar is constrained).
- The maximum safe temperature rise for a given allowable stress is $\Delta T_{max} = \sigma_{allow} / (E \alpha)$.
- For a steel bar, $E \alpha \approx 200 \times 10^9 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \text{ MPa/┬░C}$ ŌĆö a useful mental benchmark.
- Two classical KTU problem patterns: (1) *Find stress for given $\Delta T$* and (2) *Find $\Delta T$ for given allowable stress* ŌĆö both are direct applications of the same formula rearranged.
- Composite bar problems are statically indeterminate to the first degree; the redundant is the wall reaction $P$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Method of Sections** is a fundamental analytical procedure in plane truss analysis used to determine the internal axial forces in selected members of a statically determinate truss by passing an imaginary section cut through the truss that divides it into two separate free-body diagrams (FBDs), and then applying the three equations of static equilibrium to one of the resulting portions.

$$ \sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_O = 0 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The Method of Sections is classified as a *determinate analysis technique* for **pin-jointed plane frames (trusses)**. It is taught alongside the **Method of Joints** and **Method of Sections** as part of the analysis of statically determinate trusses. The governing principle is that a section cut **must sever a maximum of three members** whose forces are unknown, in order for the three equilibrium equations to solve them uniquely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology You Must Know:**
> - **Truss:** A structure composed of slender members connected at joints (assumed pin-connected).
> - **Section Plane / Cut:** An imaginary line (a-a, b-b, etc.) that passes through the truss, dividing it into two parts.
> - **Free-Body Diagram (FBD):** A sketch of one isolated portion of the cut truss showing all external loads, reactions, and the internal member forces exposed by the cut.
> - **Axial Force:** The internal force acting along the longitudinal axis of a member (tension $\;T\;$ or compression $\;C\;$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **chocolate bar** made of smaller square pieces glued together in a grid pattern. Now, suppose you want to know how strongly the chocolate square in the **middle row, third column** is being pulled or pushed.

Instead of checking every single square one-by-one (which would be like the **Method of Joints**), you simply take a **sharp imaginary knife and make one clean vertical cut** through the entire bar ŌĆö right through that middle row. The bar splits into two halves.

- The **left half** is now isolated. It is held in place by the surrounding chocolate (external reactions) and is being pulled by the remaining chocolate still attached to it.
- The internal glue forces of the three cut squares now appear on the cut face as **exposed forces** pointing outward.
- By balancing the forces on this single half, you can find the strength of those three cut squares directly, **in one shot**.

> [!TIP]
> This "one clean cut, one quick balance" idea is exactly what the **Method of Sections** does for a truss. It is the structural engineer's equivalent of "cut once, solve thrice" ŌĆö you sever three members with a single section plane, and solve for all three internal forces using three equilibrium equations.

**Why this method is preferred:**
When you need forces in only a **few specific members** (say, the central diagonal and two chords), the Method of Sections gives the answer **directly**, whereas the Method of Joints would require you to solve joint-by-joint across the entire truss ŌĆö wasteful and error-prone.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Standard Conventions)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ |
| Force (SI base unit) | $F$ | $\mathbf{Newtons \; (N)}$ |
| Distributed load | $w$ | $\mathbf{kN/m}$ |
| Moment | $M$ | $\mathbf{N \cdot m}$ or $\mathbf{kN \cdot m}$ |
| Sign Convention (Tension) | $T$ | $\mathbf{Positive \; (+)}$ (member pulls the joint) |
| Sign Convention (Compression) | $C$ | $\mathbf{Negative \; (-)}$ (member pushes the joint) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Members are **assumed in tension** (forces pull away from the joint, i.e., arrows point outward along the member at the cut). A negative result after calculation indicates **compression**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Theoretical Foundation

The Method of Sections is rooted in three classical pillars of engineering mechanics:

1. **Rigid-Body Static Equilibrium** ŌĆö A body at rest must have zero net force and zero net moment.
2. **Newton's Third Law** ŌĆö The force a cut member exerts on one FBD is equal, opposite, and collinear with the force it exerts on the other FBD.
3. **Statically Determinate Truss Criterion** ŌĆö A plane truss is determinate when:
   $$ m + r = 2j $$
   where $m$ = number of members, $r$ = number of reaction components, and $j$ = number of joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 2D truss, only **three equilibrium equations** are available. Therefore, a valid section cut must pass through **at most three members** with unknown forces. If the cut severs more than three, the system becomes statically indeterminate at that cut (or you must combine with the method of joints).

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Procedure

The following bullet list represents the **gold-standard KTU examiner-approved algorithm** for applying the Method of Sections:

1. **Verify Determinacy & Stability.** Compute $m + r$ versus $2j$. If $m + r > 2j$, the truss is statically indeterminate and the method (alone) is insufficient.
2. **Compute Support Reactions.** Solve the entire truss as a rigid body using global equilibrium to find all reaction components. *This is non-negotiable ŌĆö you cannot start section analysis without reactions.*
3. **Draw the FBD of the Whole Truss** with all external loads and reaction arrows clearly shown.
4. **Choose the Section Cut.** Select an imaginary line (a-a, b-b, etc.) that severs **at most three** members whose forces you wish to find. The cut can be straight, curved, or zig-zag, but should pass through as few members as possible.
5. **Separate into Two FBDs.** Mentally (or graphically) split the truss. Choose the **side with fewer external forces** (typically the side with fewer applied loads and reactions) for easier analysis.
6. **Expose Internal Forces.** At the cut, draw the assumed-tension arrows on each severed member ŌĆö arrows point **away from the FBD** along the member's axis.
7. **Apply the Three Equilibrium Equations.** Choose strategic equations to minimize unknowns per equation:
   - Take $\sum M = 0$ about a point where two of the three unknown member lines intersect. This isolates the **third** unknown force immediately.
   - Use $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$ to find the remaining two forces.
8. **Interpret Sign Convention.** A positive answer means the member is in **tension** (assumption correct); a negative answer means the member is in **compression** (opposite of assumed direction).

## 2.3 Strategic Selection of Moment Centers

The **most powerful technique** in the Method of Sections is picking the moment center wisely. Use the table below to select the optimal point:

| If you want to find force in member... | Take moments about the point where... | This is because... |
|---|---|---|
| Diagonal / inclined member | The other two cut members intersect | Their moment arms become zero; they drop out of the moment equation. |
| Top chord member | The intersection of the other two cut members | Two unknowns vanish from the moment sum. |
| Bottom chord member | The intersection of the other two cut members | Same reasoning ŌĆö isolate one unknown at a time. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pro Tip:** Always write down the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from your chosen moment center to the line of action of the unknown force. Substituting $F \cdot d$ directly into the moment equation saves time and reduces sign errors.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Sum of horizontal force components = 0 | Find horizontal member force |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Sum of vertical force components = 0 | Find vertical member force |
| 3 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Sum of moments about point $O$ = 0 | Isolate a single unknown force |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{M_O}{d}$ | Unknown force = Moment about $O$ divided by perpendicular distance $d$ | Direct computation |
| 5 | $F_{ij} \cos\theta = F_x$ | Horizontal component of inclined member | Resolve inclined force |
| 6 | $F_{ij} \sin\theta = F_y$ | Vertical component of inclined member | Resolve inclined force |
| 7 | $m + r = 2j$ | Determinacy check (2D truss) | Verify before analysis |
| 8 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{b}\right)$ | Inclination angle of member with slope $h$ over base $b$ | Geometry-based force resolution |
| 9 | $\sum M_A = \sum M_B$ | Two-moment equation (cantilever / overhang check) | Verify reactions |
| 10 | $d = L \sin\theta$ | Perpendicular distance from moment center to inclined member of length $L$ | Moment arm computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the formula sheet, the symbol $\theta$ denotes the angle the member makes with the **horizontal**. Be consistent ŌĆö do not mix it with the angle the member makes with the vertical.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Method of Sections is not just a textbook exercise. It is **actively used** in:

- **Roof Truss Design** ŌĆö Quick verification of forces in critical chord and web members of factory sheds, warehouses, and airport hangars.
- **Bridge Girder Analysis** ŌĆö Pratt, Howe, Warren, and K-truss bridges are routinely checked by section cuts through panel points.
- **Crane Jib and Boom Design** ŌĆö Determining compression in the top boom of a lattice jib under lifted loads.
- **Transmission / Telecommunication Towers** ŌĆö Identifying tension in the back-stay cables and compression in the main leg members.
- **Scaffolding Systems** ŌĆö Checking forces in standard couplers and ledgers under field loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Equilibrium System for a Section Cut

Consider a 2D plane truss cut by an imaginary section plane $a\text{-}a$ that severs three members with internal axial forces $F_{AB}$, $F_{BC}$, and $F_{CD}$. One FBD (the left portion) is isolated. Let the joint at the section be subjected to a representative external load $P$ at an angle $\alpha$ from the horizontal, and let the support reactions on this side be $R_{Ax}$ (horizontal) and $R_{Ay}$ (vertical).

The equilibrium of the left FBD requires:

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{Ax} - P\cos\alpha + F_{AB}\cos\theta_1 + F_{BC}\cos\theta_2 + F_{CD}\cos\theta_3 = 0 $$

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{Ay} - P\sin\alpha + F_{AB}\sin\theta_1 + F_{BC}\sin\theta_2 + F_{CD}\sin\theta_3 = 0 $$

$$ \sum M_O = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; M_O(P) + F_{AB}\,d_1 + F_{BC}\,d_2 + F_{CD}\,d_3 = 0 $$

where $\theta_i$ is the angle the $i^{th}$ member makes with the horizontal, and $d_i$ is the perpendicular distance from the moment center $O$ to the line of action of the $i^{th}$ member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** The three scalar equations contain exactly three unknowns ($F_{AB}$, $F_{BC}$, $F_{CD}$) ŌĆö making the system *algebraically determinate*. The system has a **unique solution** that can be found by direct substitution, matrix inversion, or Cramer's rule.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivation ŌĆö A Canonical Pratt Truss

**Problem Setup:** A simply-supported Pratt truss has a span $L = 8$ m, height $h = 2$ m, with 4 equal panels of $2$ m each. A vertical point load of $\mathbf{20 \; kN}$ is applied at the bottom joint **B** (one panel in from the left support **A**). Compute the force in members **CD** (top chord), **DE** (top chord), and **BD** (vertical web member) using the Method of Sections.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Support Reactions

By symmetry of geometry (not loading), apply equilibrium to the whole truss.

$$ \sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_E \cdot 8 - 20 \cdot 2 = 0 $$

$$ R_E = \dfrac{20 \cdot 2}{8} = 5 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A + R_E - 20 = 0 $$

$$ R_A = 20 - 5 = 15 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H_A = 0 $$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Identify Joint & Member Geometry

The truss has bottom joints **A, B, C, D, E** at $x = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8$ m, and top joints **F, G, H, I** at $x = 1, 3, 5, 7$ m (over the panel midpoints). Height $h = 2$ m.

The inclined diagonals **FG, GH, HI** each have a horizontal projection of $2$ m and vertical projection of $2$ m, so their length is:

$$ L_{FG} = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2} \;\text{m} $$

$$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{2}\right) = 45^\circ $$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Draw Section a-a Through Members CD, GH, and DH

The section passes vertically through the **third panel** of the truss.

- **Left FBD** contains joints A, B, F, G, the left portion of the cut, and the support reaction $R_A = 15$ kN.
- Three cut members are: **CD** (bottom chord, horizontal), **GH** (top chord, horizontal), and **DH** (diagonal, at $45^\circ$).

Assume all three in tension ŌĆö arrows point **away from the left FBD** along each member axis.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Apply $\sum M_G = 0$ to Find $F_{CD}$

Moment center **G** is the intersection of **GH** (line of action passes through G) and **DH** (line of action passes through G, since the diagonal DH has its top end at H and bottom at D, but the line of action crosses the vertical line through G at distance zero horizontally). Actually, **G** lies on both **GH** and the vertical through D and H. To eliminate both **GH** and **DH**, take moments about their intersection point.

The line of action of **GH** is the horizontal line through G. The line of action of **DH** passes through D $(6, 0)$ and H $(5, 2)$ ŌĆö extending this line, its intersection with the horizontal through G $(3, 2)$ is at infinity if the line is parallel, so the moment about G of $F_{DH}$ is **not zero** about G. We need a different center.

**Correct strategic moment center:** Take moments about the intersection of **CD** and **GH** ŌĆö both are horizontal. They are parallel, so they don't intersect at any finite point. Hence, take moments about a point that lies on **DH** and on one of the horizontals.

The cleanest approach: take $\sum M_H = 0$ ŌĆö point H lies on **GH** (drops $F_{GH}$) and on **DH** (drops $F_{DH}$), leaving only $F_{CD}$.

$$ \sum M_H = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A \cdot 5 - F_{CD} \cdot 2 = 0 $$

$$ F_{CD} = \dfrac{15 \cdot 5}{2} = 37.5 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Tension}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Check:** Positive ŌåÆ Tension. The member CD is being pulled at the cut face, consistent with the bottom chord in a simply-supported truss under downward load.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Apply $\sum F_y = 0$ to Find $F_{DH}$

The vertical components on the left FBD are: $R_A$ (up), and the vertical component of $F_{DH}$ acting at the cut. Since **DH** slopes from D $(6,0)$ up-and-to-the-left to H $(5,2)$, the angle with the horizontal is $45^\circ$. The vertical component of $F_{DH}$ on the left FBD acts **downward** (assuming tension ŌĆö the member pulls the FBD down-and-to-the-right at D, but we must be careful with direction).

Let us set up a sign convention. On the left FBD, at the cut, the assumed-tension arrow on **DH** points from D toward H, i.e., **up and to the left** at $45^\circ$. Therefore the vertical component on the left FBD is **upward** at D.

Wait ŌĆö careful. The member **DH** has its bottom end **D** on the left FBD and top end **H** on the right FBD. The assumed-tension arrow at the cut, on the left FBD side, points **into the member**, i.e., from D toward H. So on the left FBD, the arrow at D points **up-and-left at $45^\circ$**.

Vertical component (upward positive): $F_{DH} \sin 45^\circ$ is **upward** on the left FBD.

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A + F_{DH} \sin 45^\circ = 0 $$

$$ F_{DH} = \dfrac{-R_A}{\sin 45^\circ} = \dfrac{-15}{0.7071} = -21.21 \;\text{kN} $$

$$ F_{DH} = 21.21 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Compression}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Check:** Negative result ŌåÆ member is in **compression**. This makes physical sense: in a Pratt truss, the diagonal members carry compression under gravity loading.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply $\sum F_x = 0$ to Find $F_{GH}$

The horizontal components: $F_{CD}$ acts at the cut pointing **to the right** (tension, away from the FBD) on the left FBD at C. $F_{GH}$ acts at the cut pointing **to the right** (tension, away from the FBD) on the left FBD at G. The horizontal component of $F_{DH}$ at D points **to the left** (tension, toward the FBD interior, since D is on the left FBD and H is on the right FBD ŌĆö the arrow on the left FBD points from D to H, which is up-and-to-the-left, so horizontal component is to the left, magnitude $F_{DH}\cos 45^\circ$).

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{CD} + F_{GH} - F_{DH}\cos 45^\circ = 0 $$

$$ 37.5 + F_{GH} - (-21.21)(0.7071) = 0 $$

$$ 37.5 + F_{GH} + 15.0 = 0 $$

$$ F_{GH} = -52.5 \;\text{kN} $$

$$ F_{GH} = 52.5 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Compression}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The top chord **GH** is in compression (52.5 kN), the bottom chord **CD** is in tension (37.5 kN), and the diagonal **DH** is in compression (21.21 kN). This matches the classical bending behavior of a simply-supported beam: **top in compression, bottom in tension**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Summary of Computed Forces

| Member | Type | Computed Force | Nature | Physical Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CD** | Bottom chord | $37.5$ kN | Tension | Bottom fiber of beam analogy ŌåÆ stretches under sagging |
| **GH** | Top chord | $52.5$ kN | Compression | Top fiber of beam analogy ŌåÆ shortens under sagging |
| **DH** | Diagonal (Pratt) | $21.21$ kN | Compression | Pratt diagonals carry compression under gravity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Method of Sections

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the operational pipeline of the Method of Sections as a structured engineering decision flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Step 1: Receive Truss Geometry and Loads] --> checkA{Step 2: Determinacy Check m plus r equals 2j}
    checkA -- Indeterminate --> outA[Stop: Use Advanced Method]
    checkA -- Determinate --> react[Step 3: Compute Support Reactions via Global Equilibrium]
    react --> draw[Step 4: Draw Full Truss FBD with Reactions]
    draw --> sel{Step 5: Select Section Cut Through Maximum 3 Members}
    sel -- More than 3 --> sel[Step 5: Re-select a Different Section]
    sel -- At most 3 --> fbd[Step 6: Separate into Two FBDs and Choose Easier Side]
    fbd --> expose[Step 7: Expose Internal Forces Assume Tension Outward]
    expose --> strat{Step 8: Choose Strategic Moment Center}
    strat -- Two members intersect there --> mom[Step 9: Apply Sum M equals 0 to Isolate Third Unknown]
    strat -- All parallel or complex --> fy[Step 9: Apply Sum Fx equals 0 and Sum Fy equals 0]
    mom --> fx[Step 10: Apply Remaining Two Equilibrium Equations]
    fy --> fx
    fx --> interp{Step 11: Interpret Sign Convention}
    interp -- Positive --> tens[Member in Tension]
    interp -- Negative --> comp[Member in Compression]
    tens --> final[Step 12: Tabulate Results and Validate by Method of Joints]
    comp --> final
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Section Cut (Left FBD)

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes the isolated FBD of the left portion after section a-a is applied, showing all exposed internal forces:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    jointA[Joint A: Support Reaction R_A upward 15 kN] --> memberAB[Member AB: Bottom Chord]
    memberAB --> jointB[Joint B: Applied Load 20 kN downward]
    jointB --> memberBC[Member BC: Bottom Chord]
    memberBC --> jointC[Joint C]
    jointC -. Section Cut .-> memberCD[(Member CD: Exposed Force F_CD to the right, Tension)]
    memberCD -. Section Cut .-> memberDH[(Member DH: Exposed Force F_DH at 45 degrees up and left, Tension)]
    memberDH -. Section Cut .-> memberGH[(Member GH: Exposed Force F_GH to the right, Tension)]
    jointA --> memberAF[Member AF: Inclined]
    memberAF --> jointF[Joint F]
    jointF --> memberFG[Member FG: Top Chord]
    memberFG --> jointG[Joint G]
    jointG --> memberGH2[Member GH Top Chord continuation]
    memberGH2 --> jointH[Joint H]
    jointB --> memberBF[Member BF: Vertical]
    memberBF --> jointF
    jointC --> memberCG[Member CG: Vertical]
    memberCG --> jointG
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree ŌĆö Choosing the Optimal Moment Center

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Three Cut Members Exposed] --> q1{Do two of the three members intersect at a common point?}
    q1 -- Yes --> action1[Take Sum M equals 0 about that intersection point]
    action1 --> result1[Third member force isolated directly]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Are any two of the three members parallel?}
    q2 -- Yes --> action2[Take Sum M equals 0 about any point on the third member]
    action2 --> result2[Parallel members contribute zero moment arm]
    q2 -- No --> action3[Take Sum Fx equals 0 and Sum Fy equals 0 to solve simultaneously]
    action3 --> result3[Two equations two unknowns system]
    result1 --> final[Compute the unknown force and tabulate]
    result2 --> final
    result3 --> final
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent **block-level functional architecture** of the analysis pipeline. In an actual exam, the FBD must be drawn as a clean hand-sketched diagram with clear arrows, dimensions, and labeled angles, but the logical structure of the workflow is exactly as captured in the flowcharts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A consists of short-answer questions testing direct conceptual recall. Each carries **3 marks**, with a model answer of 3ŌĆō4 lines.

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the Method of Sections and explain the basic principle used in this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Method of Sections is a procedure for determining the internal axial forces in specific members of a statically determinate plane truss. The basic principle is to pass an imaginary section through the truss, dividing it into two portions such that the cut severs **at most three members** whose forces are unknown. One portion is then treated as a free body, and the three equations of static equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) are applied to solve for the three unknown member forces. The method is preferred over the Method of Joints when forces in only a few specific members are required.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Method of Joints and the Method of Sections.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Method of Joints | Method of Sections |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | A joint with at most 2 unknowns | A section cutting at most 3 members |
| Equation Basis | $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$ only (2 per joint) | All three equilibrium equations |
| Best For | Finding forces in all members | Finding forces in a few specific members |
| Speed | Slow for large trusses (joint-by-joint) | Fast ŌĆö one cut gives 3 answers |
| Preliminary Step | Reactions must be known | Reactions must be known |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B). Each question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A simply-supported Pratt truss of span 9 m and height 3 m consists of 3 equal panels of 3 m each. It is loaded with a vertical point load of 30 kN at the bottom joint located 3 m from the left support, and a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over the entire span acting on the top chord.**

**(a)** Determine the support reactions of the truss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Method of Sections, find the forces in the members cut by a vertical section passing through the middle panel of the truss. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Support Reactions

Convert the UDL to an equivalent point load: $W = 10 \times 9 = 90$ kN acting at midspan, i.e., $4.5$ m from the left support.

Total downward load on the truss: $30 + 90 = 120$ kN.

$$ \sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_B \cdot 9 - 30 \cdot 3 - 90 \cdot 4.5 = 0 $$

$$ R_B = \dfrac{30 \cdot 3 + 90 \cdot 4.5}{9} = \dfrac{90 + 405}{9} = \dfrac{495}{9} = 55 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A + R_B - 120 = 0 $$

$$ R_A = 120 - 55 = 65 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H_A = 0 $$

**Result:** $R_A = 65$ kN, $R_B = 55$ kN, $H_A = 0$. (7 marks)

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Method of Sections

The middle panel is between $x = 3$ m and $x = 6$ m. A vertical section through this panel cuts three members:
- **CD** (bottom chord, horizontal)
- **FG** (top chord, horizontal, mid-height)
- **DG** (diagonal, inclined at $45^\circ$ since panel is $3 \times 3$)

For the **left FBD**, the forces acting are:
- $R_A = 65$ kN upward at A $(0, 0)$
- Point load $30$ kN downward at B $(3, 0)$
- UDL on top chord: $10$ kN/m over $3$ m = $30$ kN total, acting at $x = 1.5$ m (downward)
- Three unknown member forces: $F_{CD}$, $F_{FG}$, $F_{DG}$ (assumed tension, arrows pointing away from the FBD)

Joint positions on left FBD: A $(0,0)$, B $(3,0)$, C $(6,0)$, F $(1.5, 3)$, G $(4.5, 3)$.

**Step 1: $\sum M_F = 0$ to find $F_{DG}$** (F is on the line of action of $F_{FG}$ and... wait, we need a center on both $F_{FG}$ and $F_{CD}$). Better: take $\sum M_G = 0$ ŌĆö G is on $F_{FG}$ line, and on $F_{DG}$ line, isolating $F_{CD}$.

Moments about G $(4.5, 3)$:

$$ \sum M_G = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A \cdot 4.5 - 30 \cdot 1.5 - 30 \cdot 3 - F_{CD} \cdot 3 = 0 $$

$$ 65 \cdot 4.5 - 30 \cdot 1.5 - 30 \cdot 3 - 3 F_{CD} = 0 $$

$$ 292.5 - 45 - 90 - 3 F_{CD} = 0 $$

$$ 157.5 - 3 F_{CD} = 0 $$

$$ F_{CD} = 52.5 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Tension}) $$

**Step 2: $\sum F_y = 0$ to find $F_{DG}$**

The diagonal DG slopes from D $(6, 0)$ to G $(4.5, 3)$. On the left FBD, the tension arrow at D points from D toward G, i.e., up-and-to-the-left at $45^\circ$. Vertical component: $F_{DG} \sin 45^\circ$ upward.

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A - 30 - 30 + F_{DG} \sin 45^\circ = 0 $$

$$ 65 - 30 - 30 + F_{DG} \cdot 0.7071 = 0 $$

$$ 5 + F_{DG} \cdot 0.7071 = 0 $$

$$ F_{DG} = -7.07 \;\text{kN} = 7.07 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Compression}) $$

**Step 3: $\sum F_x = 0$ to find $F_{FG}$**

Horizontal components on left FBD: $F_{CD}$ at C pointing right $(+)$, $F_{FG}$ at F pointing right $(+)$, $F_{DG}$ at D pointing left $(-)$, with magnitude $F_{DG} \cos 45^\circ$.

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{CD} + F_{FG} - F_{DG}\cos 45^\circ = 0 $$

$$ 52.5 + F_{FG} - (-7.07)(0.7071) = 0 $$

$$ 52.5 + F_{FG} + 5.0 = 0 $$

$$ F_{FG} = -57.5 \;\text{kN} = 57.5 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Compression}) $$

**Summary of Results (7 marks):**
- $F_{CD} = 52.5$ kN (Tension) ŌĆö bottom chord
- $F_{FG} = 57.5$ kN (Compression) ŌĆö top chord
- $F_{DG} = 7.07$ kN (Compression) ŌĆö diagonal

---

### Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**A simply-supported Warren truss of span 12 m and height 3 m has 4 equal panels of 3 m each (equilateral triangles). It carries a vertical point load of 40 kN at the apex of the second panel from the left support, and 40 kN at the apex of the third panel.**

**(a)** Compute the support reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Method of Sections, find the forces in the three members cut by a vertical section passing through the third panel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Support Reactions

Both point loads are 40 kN. By the geometry of the Warren truss, the apex joints are at $x = 1.5, 4.5, 7.5, 10.5$ m (the top chord is at height 3 m above the midpoints of each panel).

Loads at $x = 4.5$ m and $x = 7.5$ m, each 40 kN downward.

$$ \sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_B \cdot 12 - 40 \cdot 4.5 - 40 \cdot 7.5 = 0 $$

$$ R_B = \dfrac{180 + 300}{12} = \dfrac{480}{12} = 40 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A + R_B - 80 = 0 $$

$$ R_A = 40 \;\text{kN} \;(\uparrow) $$

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H_A = 0 $$

**Result:** $R_A = 40$ kN, $R_B = 40$ kN, $H_A = 0$. (7 marks)

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Method of Sections

The third panel spans $x = 6$ m to $x = 9$ m. A vertical section through this panel cuts three members:
- **Bottom chord** between the joint at $x = 6$ and the joint at $x = 9$
- **Top chord** between the apex at $x = 7.5$ and the next apex
- **Diagonal** connecting the bottom joint at $x = 9$ to the apex at $x = 7.5$ (or similar ŌĆö depends on configuration)

In a Warren truss with equilateral triangles, the diagonals make $60^\circ$ with the horizontal. The section through the third panel cuts:
- Bottom chord member at $y = 0$ (horizontal)
- Top chord member at $y = 3$ (horizontal, at apex of panel)
- Diagonal at $60^\circ$

For the **left FBD** (containing left support, $R_A = 40$ kN, and the load at $x = 4.5$ m of 40 kN):

$$ \sum M_{\text{top apex}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_A \cdot 7.5 - 40 \cdot 3 - F_{\text{bottom chord}} \cdot 3 = 0 $$

$$ 40 \cdot 7.5 - 120 - 3 F_{BC} = 0 $$

$$ 300 - 120 - 3 F_{BC} = 0 $$

$$ F_{BC} = 60 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Tension}) $$

$$ \sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 40 - 40 + F_{\text{diag}} \sin 60^\circ = 0 $$

$$ F_{\text{diag}} = 0 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Zero-Force Member}) $$

$$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{\text{TC}} + F_{BC} - 0 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{\text{TC}} = -60 \;\text{kN} $$

$$ F_{\text{TC}} = 60 \;\text{kN} \;(\text{Compression}) $$

**Summary (7 marks):**
- Bottom chord: $60$ kN (Tension)
- Top chord: $60$ kN (Compression)
- Diagonal: $0$ kN (Zero-Force Member)

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Insight:** The zero-force diagonal arises due to a specific loading configuration in a symmetric Warren truss ŌĆö when the panel cut is at the midspan, the diagonal carries no force. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute support reactions first.** Section analysis on a truss with unknown reactions leads to cascading errors. Always solve reactions *before* drawing the section cut.
> 2. **Cutting more than three members.** A section through four or more members leaves you with more unknowns than equations. The system becomes unsolvable with this method alone.
> 3. **Sign convention errors at the cut.** Drawing the assumed-tension arrow in the **wrong direction** (toward the FBD instead of away) flips the sign of the final answer. Always draw the arrow **away from the FBD** along the member axis.
> 4. **Mixing moment arms.** A moment arm is the **perpendicular distance** from the moment center to the **line of action** of the force ŌĆö *not* the horizontal or vertical distance. Using the wrong distance produces a numerically wrong answer.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency.** Loads in kN and distances in mm will give forces in kN ŌĆö but the numerical answer will be off by a factor of 1000. Always check units at the start of the problem.
> 6. **Not verifying with method of joints.** KTU examiners appreciate a cross-check. If time permits, verify one or two member forces using the method of joints on an adjacent joint.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Method of Sections**

- **Definition:** Imaginary cut through a truss, dividing it into two FBDs; solve using three equilibrium equations.
- **Determinacy Check:** Always verify $m + r = 2j$ before starting.
- **Reactions First:** Support reactions are mandatory prerequisites.
- **Three-Member Rule:** A 2D section cut must sever **at most three** members with unknown forces.
- **Assumed Tension:** Always assume members are in tension; arrows point **away** from the FBD.
- **Sign Convention:** Positive answer = tension; negative = compression.
- **Strategic Moment Center:** Pick the intersection of two cut members' lines of action to isolate the third.
- **Equation Choice:** Use $\sum M$ to isolate one unknown; use $\sum F_x$ and $\sum F_y$ for the remaining two.
- **Beam Analogy:** Top chord in compression, bottom chord in tension for a simply-supported truss under gravity loads (sagging analogy).
- **Zero-Force Members:** Diagonals in specific Warren truss configurations can carry zero force ŌĆö check carefully.
- **Diagonal Angle:** For a Pratt truss with square panels, $\theta = 45^\circ$; for a Warren truss with equilateral panels, $\theta = 60^\circ$.
- **Cross-Verification:** Method of Sections answers can be spot-checked with the Method of Joints on adjacent joints.
- **Draw Clean FBDs:** The single biggest determinant of full marks in KTU exams is a **neat, well-labeled free-body diagram** with all forces, dimensions, and angles clearly indicated.
- **Real-World Use:** Section analysis is used in roof trusses, bridge girders, crane jibs, transmission towers, and scaffolding systems.

---

**End of Module 2 ŌĆö Method of Sections Study Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCRUT302 ŌĆö Solid Mechanics*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Vectors on Cartesian Coordinate Planes Passing Through a Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Vectors on Cartesian Coordinate Planes Passing Through a Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cauchy's Stress Principle (The Foundation):** The state of stress at a point in a continuum body is completely described by a **second-order symmetric tensor** known as the **Cauchy Stress Tensor**, denoted by $[\sigma]$. It is a $3 \times 3$ matrix having **9 components** in three-dimensional Cartesian space.

The **stress vector** (or **traction vector**) acting on *any* plane passing through a point is obtained by the **Cauchy's Stress Formula**:

$$\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \cdot \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the **unit outward normal vector** to the plane on which the traction is sought, and $\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})}$ is the resulting traction vector.

The full stress tensor is written as:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case ŌĆō Cartesian Coordinate Planes:** When the plane passing through the point is one of the three coordinate planes (i.e., the $yz$-plane, $zx$-plane, or $xy$-plane), the unit normal coincides with one of the basis vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$. The traction vectors on these planes are simply the **columns of the stress tensor**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a small cubic sample of material under load (like a piece of rubber being squeezed). If you slice this cube with three imaginary knives along the $x$, $y$, and $z$ directions passing through a single interior point, each slice reveals a different "fingerprint" of internal force. The stress tensor is the **complete fingerprint** of all internal forces at that point. Each coordinate plane slice exposes a different "view" ŌĆö and these three views, taken together, give us complete information about how the material is being pushed, pulled, and sheared at that location.

Think of $[\sigma]$ as a **photograph of internal forces from all three sides simultaneously**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for KTU Boards)

- **Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$):** The component of traction perpendicular to the plane. Positive for **tension**, negative for **compression**.
- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The component of traction parallel (tangential) to the plane.
- **Stress Tensor Symmetry:** $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ (assumes no body couples / no couple-stresses).
- **Traction Vector:** Force per unit area acting on a specific plane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Setting Up the Stress Tensor
At any interior point of a deformable body, nine stress components completely define the stress state:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Convention:** The first subscript denotes the **direction of the outward normal** to the plane; the second subscript denotes the **direction of the stress component**.

### Step 2: Cauchy's Formula (General Plane)
For *any* plane with outward unit normal $\hat{n} = (l, m, n)^T$:

$$\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \cdot \hat{n} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} l + \sigma_{xy} m + \sigma_{xz} n \\ \sigma_{yx} l + \sigma_{yy} m + \sigma_{yz} n \\ \sigma_{zx} l + \sigma_{zy} m + \sigma_{zz} n \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Specialize to Cartesian Coordinate Planes

**Plane 1: Plane perpendicular to x-axis** ($\hat{n} = \hat{i} = (1, 0, 0)^T$):

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yx} \\ \sigma_{zx} \end{Bmatrix}$$

This is the **first column** of the stress tensor. The normal stress on this plane is $\sigma_{xx}$ and the shear stress magnitude is $\sqrt{\sigma_{yx}^2 + \sigma_{zx}^2}$.

**Plane 2: Plane perpendicular to y-axis** ($\hat{n} = \hat{j} = (0, 1, 0)^T$):

$$\vec{t}^{(y)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{yy} \\ \sigma_{zy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

This is the **second column** of the stress tensor. Normal stress: $\sigma_{yy}$. Shear magnitude: $\sqrt{\sigma_{xy}^2 + \sigma_{zy}^2}$.

**Plane 3: Plane perpendicular to z-axis** ($\hat{n} = \hat{k} = (0, 0, 1)^T$):

$$\vec{t}^{(z)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zz} \end{Bmatrix}$$

This is the **third column** of the stress tensor. Normal stress: $\sigma_{zz}$. Shear magnitude: $\sqrt{\sigma_{xz}^2 + \sigma_{yz}^2}$.

### Step 4: Decomposition of Traction
Each traction vector $\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})}$ is decomposed into:

- **Normal Component:** $\sigma_n = \vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} \cdot \hat{n} = \hat{n}^T [\sigma] \hat{n}$
- **Shear Component (vector):** $\vec{\tau} = \vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} - \sigma_n \hat{n}$
- **Shear Magnitude:** $\tau = \sqrt{\vert \vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** The 3 coordinate-plane tractions together form the **column representation** of the stress tensor. If you know the tractions on these 3 mutually perpendicular planes, you know the entire stress state at that point.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Applicable to |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \hat{n}$ | Cauchy's Traction Formula | Any plane |
| 2 | $\sigma_n = \hat{n}^T [\sigma] \hat{n}$ | Normal stress on plane with normal $\hat{n}$ | Any plane |
| 3 | $\tau = \sqrt{\vert \vec{t} \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2}$ | Shear stress magnitude | Any plane |
| 4 | $\vec{t}^{(x)} = (\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yx}, \sigma_{zx})^T$ | Traction on $yz$-plane | Cartesian plane (x-normal) |
| 5 | $\vec{t}^{(y)} = (\sigma_{xy}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zy})^T$ | Traction on $zx$-plane | Cartesian plane (y-normal) |
| 6 | $\vec{t}^{(z)} = (\sigma_{xz}, \sigma_{yz}, \sigma_{zz})^T$ | Traction on $xy$-plane | Cartesian plane (z-normal) |
| 7 | $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ | Symmetry of stress tensor | Conservative body, no couples |
| 8 | $\tau_x = \sqrt{\sigma_{yx}^2 + \sigma_{zx}^2}$ | Shear on $yz$-plane | Cartesian plane (x-normal) |
| 9 | $\tau_y = \sqrt{\sigma_{xy}^2 + \sigma_{zy}^2}$ | Shear on $zx$-plane | Cartesian plane (y-normal) |
| 10 | $\tau_z = \sqrt{\sigma_{xz}^2 + \sigma_{yz}^2}$ | Shear on $xy$-plane | Cartesian plane (z-normal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units (SI):** All stress components are in **Pascals (Pa)** or **N/m┬▓**. In engineering practice, **MPa (N/mm┬▓)** is heavily used.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessels:** Determining principal stresses to prevent rupture in boilers, storage tanks, and pipelines.
- **Bridges and Trusses:** Identifying critical stress points in steel girders and reinforced concrete.
- **Machine Design:** Shaft design under combined bending and torsion, where stress on any inclined plane must be known.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Analyzing soil and rock failure planes along Mohr's circle extrema.
- **Aerospace:** Composite laminate analysis where stress transformation between plies uses the same tensor logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Traction Vector on Cartesian Coordinate Planes

### Derivation A: Traction on the $yz$-plane (Normal to x-axis)

**Given:** A point $P$ inside a stressed continuum, with stress tensor $[\sigma]$.

**Step A.1:** Define the unit outward normal to the $yz$-plane.

$$\hat{n}_x = \hat{i} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step A.2:** Apply Cauchy's formula $\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \hat{n}$:

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step A.3:** Perform the matrix multiplication column by column. The result is the first column of $[\sigma]$:

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = \begin{Bmatrix} (\sigma_{xx})(1) + (\sigma_{xy})(0) + (\sigma_{xz})(0) \\ (\sigma_{yx})(1) + (\sigma_{yy})(0) + (\sigma_{yz})(0) \\ (\sigma_{zx})(1) + (\sigma_{zy})(0) + (\sigma_{zz})(0) \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yx} \\ \sigma_{zx} \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step A.4:** Compute the normal stress on this plane as the projection of $\vec{t}^{(x)}$ onto $\hat{n}_x$:

$$\sigma_n^{(x)} = \vec{t}^{(x)} \cdot \hat{n}_x = (\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yx}, \sigma_{zx}) \cdot (1, 0, 0) = \sigma_{xx}$$

**Step A.5:** Compute the shear stress magnitude (the tangential part):

$$\tau_x = \sqrt{\sigma_{yx}^2 + \sigma_{zx}^2}$$

---

### Derivation B: Traction on the $zx$-plane (Normal to y-axis)

**Step B.1:** Unit normal: $\hat{n}_y = \hat{j} = (0, 1, 0)^T$.

**Step B.2:** Apply Cauchy's formula:

$$\vec{t}^{(y)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{yy} \\ \sigma_{zy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step B.3:** Normal stress:

$$\sigma_n^{(y)} = \vec{t}^{(y)} \cdot \hat{n}_y = \sigma_{yy}$$

**Step B.4:** Shear magnitude:

$$\tau_y = \sqrt{\sigma_{xy}^2 + \sigma_{zy}^2}$$

---

### Derivation C: Traction on the $xy$-plane (Normal to z-axis)

**Step C.1:** Unit normal: $\hat{n}_z = \hat{k} = (0, 0, 1)^T$.

**Step C.2:** Apply Cauchy's formula:

$$\vec{t}^{(z)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zz} \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step C.3:** Normal stress:

$$\sigma_n^{(z)} = \vec{t}^{(z)} \cdot \hat{n}_z = \sigma_{zz}$$

**Step C.4:** Shear magnitude:

$$\tau_z = \sqrt{\sigma_{xz}^2 + \sigma_{yz}^2}$$

---

### Derivation D: Symmetry of the Stress Tensor (Moment Equilibrium)

Consider a small rectangular parallelepiped element of size $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ centered at point $P$. Take moments about the $x$-axis. The only stress components that produce a moment about the $x$-axis are $\sigma_{yz}$ and $\sigma_{zy}$ acting on opposite faces.

**Step D.1:** Moment produced by $\sigma_{yz}$ on the top $xy$-face (at $z = +dz/2$):

$$M_{\sigma_{yz}} = (\sigma_{yz} \cdot dx \cdot dy) \cdot \frac{dz}{2}$$

**Step D.2:** Moment produced by $\sigma_{zy}$ on the right $yz$-face (at $y = +dy/2$):

$$M_{\sigma_{zy}} = (\sigma_{zy} \cdot dx \cdot dz) \cdot \frac{dy}{2}$$

**Step D.3:** Sum of moments about the $x$-axis must equal zero (no body couples):

$$\begin{aligned} \sum M_x &= (\sigma_{yz} - \sigma_{zy}) \, dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0 \\ \Rightarrow \sigma_{yz} &= \sigma_{zy} \end{aligned}$$

**Step D.4:** Similarly for the other axes, yielding the symmetry conditions:

$$\sigma_{xy} = \sigma_{yx}, \quad \sigma_{xz} = \sigma_{zx}, \quad \sigma_{yz} = \sigma_{zy}$$

This reduces the **9 stress components to 6 independent components**.

---

### Derivation E: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Type]:** The stress tensor at a point is given by:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} 80 & 30 & -20 \\ 30 & 40 & 15 \\ -20 & 15 & 50 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**Find the stress vectors on the three Cartesian coordinate planes. Also find the resultant shear stress on each plane.**

**Step E.1: Traction on the $yz$-plane** ($\hat{n} = \hat{i}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 80 \\ 30 \\ -20 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

- Normal stress: $\sigma_{xx} = 80$ MPa (tensile)
- Shear magnitude: $\tau_x = \sqrt{30^2 + (-20)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 400} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06$ MPa

**Step E.2: Traction on the $zx$-plane** ($\hat{n} = \hat{j}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(y)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 30 \\ 40 \\ 15 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

- Normal stress: $\sigma_{yy} = 40$ MPa
- Shear magnitude: $\tau_y = \sqrt{30^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{900 + 225} = \sqrt{1125} \approx 33.54$ MPa

**Step E.3: Traction on the $xy$-plane** ($\hat{n} = \hat{k}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(z)} = \begin{Bmatrix} -20 \\ 15 \\ 50 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

- Normal stress: $\sigma_{zz} = 50$ MPa
- Shear magnitude: $\tau_z = \sqrt{(-20)^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{400 + 225} = \sqrt{625} = 25$ MPa

**Step E.4: Verify Symmetry** ŌĆö The given tensor is already symmetric ($\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$), confirming physical consistency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Tensor & Traction Vector Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STRESS TENSOR AT POINT P"] --> B["9 COMPONENTS IN 3x3 MATRIX"]
    B --> C["COLUMN 1 = TRACTION ON yz PLANE"]
    B --> D["COLUMN 2 = TRACTION ON zx PLANE"]
    B --> E["COLUMN 3 = TRACTION ON xy PLANE"]
    C --> F["NORMAL STRESS sigmaxx"]
    C --> G["SHEAR VECTOR sigmayx and sigmazx"]
    D --> H["NORMAL STRESS sigmayy"]
    D --> I["SHEAR VECTOR sigmaxy and sigmazy"]
    E --> J["NORMAL STRESS sigmazz"]
    E --> K["SHEAR VECTOR sigmaxz and sigmayz"]
    F --> L["RESOLVE INTO NORMAL AND SHEAR"]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["COMPLETE STRESS STATE AT POINT P"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Computational Procedure for Finding Stress Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: READ STRESS TENSOR"] --> S2["STEP 2: IDENTIFY UNIT NORMAL VECTOR"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: APPLY CAUCHY FORMULA t = sigma times n"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: EXTRACT NORMAL COMPONENT dot product"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: COMPUTE SHEAR MAGNITUDE"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: REPEAT FOR ALL THREE PLANES"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: VERIFY SYMMETRY AND SIGN CONVENTION"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decomposition of Traction Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TV["TRACTION VECTOR t superscript n"] --> DEC{"DECOMPOSE"}
    DEC --> NN["NORMAL COMPONENT parallel to n"]
    DEC --> SH["SHEAR COMPONENT perpendicular to n"]
    NN --> M1["MAGNITUDE sigman equals t dot n"]
    SH --> M2["MAGNITUDE tau equals sqrt of t squared minus sigman squared"]
    M1 --> RES["RESULTANT TRACTION COMPLETE"]
    M2 --> RES
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Cauchy's stress tensor. What is the physical significance of the symmetry of the stress tensor?**

**Model Answer:**
Cauchy's stress tensor is a second-order tensor represented as a $3 \times 3$ matrix of 9 components $[\sigma_{ij}]$ that completely describes the state of stress at a point in a continuum.

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

The symmetry $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ arises from **moment equilibrium of an infinitesimal element** in the absence of body couples. This reduces the independent stress components from 9 to **6**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Write down Cauchy's formula for the stress vector on an inclined plane. What are the normal and shear components of this traction?**

**Model Answer:**
Cauchy's formula states:

$$\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \cdot \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal. The normal stress is:

$$\sigma_n = \hat{n}^T [\sigma] \hat{n}$$

The shear stress magnitude is:

$$\tau = \sqrt{\vert \vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2}$$

---

## PART B Question (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

At a point in a stressed body, the stress tensor is given by:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} 60 & 40 & -30 \\ 40 & 20 & 25 \\ -30 & 25 & 45 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**(a)** Determine the stress vectors (tractions) on the three Cartesian coordinate planes passing through the point. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the normal and resultant shear stress on each of the three coordinate planes. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Traction Vectors**

**For $yz$-plane** (normal $\hat{i}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 60 \\ 40 \\ -30 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**For $zx$-plane** (normal $\hat{j}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(y)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 40 \\ 20 \\ 25 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**For $xy$-plane** (normal $\hat{k}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(z)} = [\sigma] \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -30 \\ 25 \\ 45 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b): Normal and Shear Stresses**

**On $yz$-plane:**

$$\sigma_n = 60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_x = \sqrt{40^2 + (-30)^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 900} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**On $zx$-plane:**

$$\sigma_n = 20 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_y = \sqrt{40^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 625} = \sqrt{2225} \approx 47.17 \text{ MPa}$$

**On $xy$-plane:**

$$\sigma_n = 45 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_z = \sqrt{(-30)^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{900 + 625} = \sqrt{1525} \approx 39.05 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

The stress state at a point inside a steel component is given by:

$$[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 50 & 0 \\ 50 & 80 & 30 \\ 0 & 30 & 60 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**(a)** Compute the traction vectors on the three coordinate planes and identify the normal stress on each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Verify the symmetry of the stress tensor and find the magnitudes of the shear stress on each coordinate plane. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Tractions and Normal Stresses**

**On $yz$-plane** (normal $\hat{i}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(x)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 100 \\ 50 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_n^{(x)} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**On $zx$-plane** (normal $\hat{j}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(y)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 50 \\ 80 \\ 30 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_n^{(y)} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**On $xy$-plane** (normal $\hat{k}$):

$$\vec{t}^{(z)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 30 \\ 60 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_n^{(z)} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b): Symmetry Check and Shear Magnitudes**

**Symmetry Check:**

$$\sigma_{xy} = 50 = \sigma_{yx} \checkmark$$
$$\sigma_{xz} = 0 = \sigma_{zx} \checkmark$$
$$\sigma_{yz} = 30 = \sigma_{zy} \checkmark$$

The stress tensor is **symmetric**. Ō£ō

**Shear Magnitudes:**

$$\tau_x = \sqrt{\sigma_{yx}^2 + \sigma_{zx}^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 0^2} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_y = \sqrt{\sigma_{xy}^2 + \sigma_{zy}^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 900} = \sqrt{3400} \approx 58.31 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_z = \sqrt{\sigma_{xz}^2 + \sigma_{yz}^2} = \sqrt{0^2 + 30^2} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Students often forget the sign convention (tension positive, compression negative). $\sigma_{xz} = -20$ MPa is compressive, not an error.
> 2. **Column vs. Row Confusion:** Many students write the traction as the *row* of the stress tensor. Remember: the traction on a plane with normal $\hat{n}$ is the **matrix product**, which gives the **column** of the tensor corresponding to that axis.
> 3. **Symmetry Marks:** If you do not verify symmetry of the given tensor, you lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Unit Omission:** Always write "MPa" or "N/mm┬▓" ŌĆö omitting units costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting $\sigma_n$ in Step E.4:** When asked for "stress vector on a plane," giving only the shear part loses marks. The full vector is required.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Cauchy's Stress Tensor:** $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix with **6 independent components**.
- **Traction Formula:** $\vec{t}^{(\hat{n})} = [\sigma] \hat{n}$ ŌĆö applies to **any** plane through a point.
- **Column Rule:** Traction on a plane with normal along axis $x$, $y$, or $z$ = **corresponding column** of $[\sigma]$.
- **Symmetry:** $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ (from moment equilibrium, no body couples).
- **Normal Stress on coordinate plane $i$:** $\sigma_{ii}$ (the diagonal element).
- **Shear Magnitude on plane with normal along axis $i$:** $\tau_i = \sqrt{\sigma_{ij}^2 + \sigma_{ik}^2}$ where $j \neq i, k \neq i, j \neq k$.
- **Sign Convention:** **Tensile stresses are POSITIVE**, compressive stresses are NEGATIVE.
- **Units:** **Pa (SI)** or **MPa (N/mm┬▓)** ŌĆö always annotate answers.
- **Physical Interpretation:** Diagonal elements = **direct/normal** stresses. Off-diagonal elements = **shear** stresses.
- **Equilibrium:** The given stress tensor must be symmetric for a physically valid (non-polar) continuum.
- **For any inclined plane:** Use Cauchy's formula, NOT just the column rule.
- **Resultant Traction Magnitude:** $\vert \vec{t} \vert = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + \tau^2}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress at a Point in the Form of a Matrix. Equality of Cross Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress at a Point in the Form of a Matrix. Equality of Cross Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **state of stress at a point** in a deformable body under load is completely described by a **second-order Cartesian tensor** known as the **Cauchy Stress Tensor**, denoted as $\sigma_{ij}$. In matrix form, this tensor is represented by a **3├Ś3 matrix** that lists the stresses acting on the three mutually perpendicular faces of an infinitesimal cubical element extracted at that point.

The stress matrix at a point is written as:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

where the **first subscript** denotes the **face** (face whose outward normal is along that axis) and the **second subscript** denotes the **direction** in which the stress component acts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT ŌĆö Stress Tensor Properties**
> - The stress tensor is a **second-order tensor** (not a vector or scalar).
> - The matrix has **9 components** in general, but only **6 are independent** because of symmetry.
> - The diagonal elements $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ are **normal stresses** (tensile positive by convention).
> - The off-diagonal elements $\tau_{ij}$ are **shear stresses** acting on the $i$-face in the $j$-direction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ­¤Ź░ ŌĆö "The Tiny Sugar Cube Inside a Cake"

Imagine you bake a cake (the solid body) and want to know what is happening at one microscopic point deep inside it. You "scoop out" a tiny imaginary **sugar cube** (the infinitesimal element) at that point. Now, the surrounding cake material pushes and pulls on **each of the six faces** of this cube.

- The **normal stresses** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ are like the cake material trying to **squeeze or stretch** the cube along each axis.
- The **shear stresses** $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yx}$, etc., are like the cake material trying to **slide one face of the cube** past the opposite face.

The **stress matrix** is simply the "complete report card" of these 9 force components. The **equality of cross shear** (also called the **theorem of complementary shear stresses** or **reciprocity of shear stresses**) states: *if the right face of the cube is pushed downward by a shear $\tau_{xy}$, then the top face of the cube is pushed sideways by the **same magnitude** $\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy}$ in the perpendicular direction.* This is the internal equivalent of **Newton's Third Law** (actionŌĆōreaction).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Sign Convention

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Sign Convention (Used in Board Exams):**
> - **Tensile normal stress** ŌåÆ **Positive (+)** ; **Compressive** ŌåÆ Negative (ŌłÆ)
> - **Shear stress** that produces a **clockwise couple** on the element face ŌåÆ **Positive (+)** when acting on a positive face.
> - **Units** in SI: **N/m┬▓** or **Pa** (Pascal). Larger values are expressed in **MPa = 10ŌüČ Pa** or **GPa = 10Ōü╣ Pa**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Stress Matrix ŌĆö What Each Entry Means

The stress matrix is built by **slicing an infinitesimal element** with edges parallel to the $x$, $y$, $z$ axes and recording the traction on each of the **three pairs of opposite faces**.

### (A) Diagonal Elements ŌĆö Normal Stresses
- $\sigma_x$ ŌåÆ Normal stress on the face perpendicular to the $x$-axis (acts in the $\pm x$ direction).
- $\sigma_y$ ŌåÆ Normal stress on the face perpendicular to the $y$-axis.
- $\sigma_z$ ŌåÆ Normal stress on the face perpendicular to the $z$-axis.

### (B) Off-Diagonal Elements ŌĆö Shear Stresses
- $\tau_{xy}$ ŌåÆ Shear stress on the **$x$-face**, acting in the **$y$-direction**.
- $\tau_{yx}$ ŌåÆ Shear stress on the **$y$-face**, acting in the **$x$-direction**.
- Similarly for $\tau_{xz}, \tau_{zx}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zy}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **9 components** can be visualized as: on each of the 3 faces, there is **1 normal + 2 shear** stresses = 3 components ├Ś 3 faces = 9 total. However, due to the **equality of cross shear**, the matrix becomes symmetric, and only **6 components are independent**.

## 2.2 Why is the Matrix Symmetric? ŌĆö The "Why" Behind It

If the stress matrix were **not symmetric**, an infinitesimal element under pure shear loading would experience a **net unbalanced moment** (a couple) and would start **rotating spontaneously**, which violates the fundamental assumption of **static equilibrium** of a continuum under zero body couple. The only way to satisfy moment equilibrium is to enforce:

$$
\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}, \quad \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}, \quad \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}
$$

This reduces the **9 unknowns to 6**, and the matrix becomes:

$$
