[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Symmetric Matrix)}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all the high-yield equations** required for Module 2 ŌĆö Stress at a Point, KTU 2024 Scheme Board Exams.

| # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stress Tensor (3D)** | $\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$ | Complete stress state at a point in 3D |
| 2 | **Symmetry of Stress Tensor** | $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$ | Equality of cross shear (complementary shear) |
| 3 | **Cauchy's Stress Formula** | $\vec{T}^{(n)} = [\sigma] \cdot \hat{n}$ | Traction vector on a plane with unit normal $\hat{n}$ |
| 4 | **Number of Independent Components** | $6$ (in 3D) | 3 normal + 3 shear |
| 5 | **2D Stress State (Plane Stress)** | $\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}$ | Reduced matrix for thin plates |
| 6 | **First Stress Invariant ($I_1$)** | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ | Sum of normal stresses (trace) |
| 7 | **Second Stress Invariant ($I_2$)** | $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$ | Used in Mohr's circle |
| 8 | **Third Stress Invariant ($I_3$)** | $I_3 = \det([\sigma])$ | Determinant of stress matrix |

> [!TIP]
> In the table above, $\hat{n} = (l, m, n)$ is the **unit outward normal** to a plane. The traction vector components are $T_x = \sigma_x l + \tau_{xy} m + \tau_{xz} n$, and similarly for $T_y$ and $T_z$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility ­¤ÅŚ’ĖÅ

- **Finite Element Analysis (FEM):** The stress tensor is the fundamental output of every structural FEM software (ANSYS, ABAQUS, SAP2000) at each Gauss/integration point.
- **Failure Theories:** Von-Mises, Tresca, Rankine ŌĆö all require the full $3 \times 3$ stress matrix to compute principal stresses.
- **Pressure Vessels & Shafts:** Principal stress calculation for design of cylinders, turbines, and pressure pipes.
- **Geomechanics:** Determining the in-situ stress state of rock/soil for tunnel design and foundation engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equality of Cross Shear Stresses (Theorem of Complementary Shear)

### Statement
> *"On any two mutually perpendicular planes passing through a point, the shear stresses acting in directions perpendicular to the line of intersection of the planes are equal in magnitude."*

In 2D form: $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$

### Setup ŌĆö Consider an Infinitesimal Rectangular Element

Let the element have:
- Width along $x$-axis: $dx$
- Height along $y$-axis: $dy$
- Unit depth along $z$-axis: $dz = 1$ (per unit thickness)

On the **right face** (outward normal in $+x$ direction):
- Normal stress $\sigma_x$ acts in $+x$ direction
- Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acts in $+y$ direction

On the **left face** (outward normal in $-x$ direction):
- Normal stress $\sigma_x$ acts in $-x$ direction
- Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acts in $-y$ direction (actionŌĆōreaction)

On the **top face** (outward normal in $+y$ direction):
- Normal stress $\sigma_y$ acts in $+y$ direction
- Shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ acts in $+x$ direction

On the **bottom face** (outward normal in $-y$ direction):
- Normal stress $\sigma_y$ acts in $-y$ direction
- Shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ acts in $-x$ direction

### Moment Equilibrium About the $z$-Axis (Out of Page)

For the element to be in static equilibrium, the **sum of moments about the center** must be zero.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Moment due to $\tau_{xy}$ on the right face:**
- Force on right face due to $\tau_{xy}$: $F_1 = \tau_{xy} \cdot (dy) \cdot 1 = \tau_{xy} \, dy$
- Acts in $+y$ direction at a perpendicular distance of $dx/2$ from center.
- Moment about $z$-axis: $M_1 = +\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2}$ (counterclockwise, positive)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment due to $\tau_{xy}$ on the left face:**
- Force on left face: $F_2 = \tau_{xy} \cdot dy$ acting in $-y$ direction.
- Acts at distance $dx/2$ on the negative side.
- Moment about $z$-axis: $M_2 = +\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2}$ (also counterclockwise)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Moment due to $\tau_{yx}$ on the top face:**
- Force on top face: $F_3 = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx$ acting in $+x$ direction.
- Acts at distance $dy/2$ above center.
- Moment about $z$-axis: $M_3 = -\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dy}{2}$ (clockwise, negative)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Moment due to $\tau_{yx}$ on the bottom face:**
- Force on bottom face: $F_4 = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx$ acting in $-x$ direction.
- Acts at distance $dy/2$ below center.
- Moment about $z$-axis: $M_4 = -\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dy}{2}$ (also clockwise)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply $\sum M_z = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 + M_2 + M_3 + M_4 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \frac{dx}{2} + \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \frac{dx}{2} - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dy}{2} - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dy}{2} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy &= 0 \\[4pt]
(\tau_{xy} - \tau_{yx}) \cdot dx \cdot dy &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclusion:**

Since $dx \neq 0$ and $dy \neq 0$, the only way the moment equation is satisfied is:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}
$$

By **analogous reasoning** on the other two pairs of faces, we also obtain:

$$
\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}
$$

### Why This Result is Powerful (The "How")

This single result is what makes the **stress matrix symmetric** and the stress a true **tensor**. Without this symmetry, the stress state would depend on the observer's reference frame, violating the objectivity principle of continuum mechanics. It also drastically reduces computational work ŌĆö from 9 unknowns down to **6 independent components**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Reduction of Independent Components

Starting from the full 9-component matrix:

$$
[\sigma]_{3 \times 3} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix} \longrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Independent components:** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$ ŌåÆ **6 components**
- **Dependent components:** $\tau_{yx}, \tau_{zy}, \tau_{xz}$ ŌåÆ **3 components** (equal to their cross counterparts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a 2D (plane stress / plane strain) problem**, the matrix reduces to:
> $$
> [\sigma]_{2D} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}
> $$
> Only **3 independent components** remain.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Architecture of the Stress Tensor & Proof Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Point in a Loaded Continuum] --> B[Extract Infinitesimal Cubical Element]
    B --> C[Identify Stresses on All 6 Faces]
    C --> D1[3 Normal Stresses: sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z]
    C --> D2[6 Shear Stresses: 3 Pairs]
    D2 --> E1[tau_xy on X-face, tau_yx on Y-face]
    D2 --> E2[tau_yz on Y-face, tau_zy on Z-face]
    D2 --> E3[tau_xz on X-face, tau_zx on Z-face]
    E1 --> F[Apply Moment Equilibrium about z-axis]
    E2 --> G[Apply Moment Equilibrium about x-axis]
    E3 --> H[Apply Moment Equilibrium about y-axis]
    F --> I[Result 1: tau_xy equals tau_yx]
    G --> J[Result 2: tau_yz equals tau_zy]
    H --> K[Result 3: tau_xz equals tau_zx]
    I --> L[Symmetric 3x3 Stress Tensor]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    D1 --> L
    L --> M[6 Independent Stress Components]
    M --> N[Foundation for Principal Stresses and Mohrs Circle]
```

## 4.2 ASCII Schematic of the 2D Infinitesimal Element (For Exam Sketch)

```
                Ōåæ +y direction
                |
        Žä_yx ŌåÆ  |ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆö Žā_y ŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ  ŌåÉ Žä_yx
                |   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                |   Ōöé  ŌĆó  O   Ōöé   O = Center (about which moments are taken)
                |   Ōöé         Ōöé
        Žä_yx ŌåÆ  |ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌöé  ELEMENTŌöéŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ  ŌåÉ Žä_yx
                |   Ōöé         Ōöé
                |   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
        Žä_xy Ōåæ  |   Ōåæ Žä_xy
                |
                Ōåō +x direction
```

- The element has **width $dx$** along $x$ and **height $dy$** along $y$.
- $\tau_{xy}$ acts on the **vertical (x) faces** in the **$y$-direction**.
- $\tau_{yx}$ acts on the **horizontal (y) faces** in the **$x$-direction**.
- Moment equilibrium of these 4 shear forces about the center proves $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always draw a clear, labeled free-body diagram of the element in 2D when proving the equality of cross shear. Examiners award **2ŌĆō3 marks** just for a clean sketch with proper direction arrows and face identification.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ŌØō Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the equality of cross shear stresses with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **theorem of complementary shear stresses** (or equality of cross shear) states that the shear stresses acting on two perpendicular planes at a point, and acting in directions perpendicular to the line of intersection of the planes, are **equal in magnitude**.

**Statement:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$

**Physical Reason:** If $\tau_{xy} \neq \tau_{yx}$, the infinitesimal element would experience a net couple and would start rotating, violating the **principle of moment equilibrium** of a static continuum.

**Sketch:** A 2D element of size $dx \times dy$ with $\tau_{xy}$ on vertical faces and $\tau_{yx}$ on horizontal faces, taking moments about the center $O$ gives $\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy \Rightarrow \tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$.

---

### ŌØō Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Write the matrix form of the stress tensor at a point in 3D and identify the independent components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **3D stress tensor** at a point is represented as a $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa)}
$$

- **Total components:** 9
- **Independent components:** **6** (since $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$)
- The 6 independent components are: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$, $\tau_{xy}$, $\tau_{yz}$, $\tau_{zx}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module 2 Internal Choice

### ŌØō Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the equality of cross shear stresses, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, for a 2D rectangular element under pure shear using the principle of moment equilibrium. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The state of stress at a point in a steel member is given by the matrix:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} 80 & 40 & 0 \\ 40 & 30 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 20 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}
$$

Determine the **principal stresses** and **maximum shear stress** in the $xy$-plane. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a 2D rectangular element of size $dx \times dy \times 1$ (unit depth). (1 mark for sketch)

**Step 2:** Mark all four shear forces on the element:
- $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy$ on right face (upward)
- $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy$ on left face (downward, actionŌĆōreaction)
- $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx$ on top face (rightward)
- $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx$ on bottom face (leftward) (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Take moments about the center $O$ (perpendicular to plane): (1 mark for setup)

$$
\sum M_O = \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \frac{dx}{2} + \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot \frac{dx}{2} - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dy}{2} - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dy}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify: (1 mark)

$$
\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $dx \cdot dy$ to get the result. (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}
$$

**Step 6:** State that by similar reasoning, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$ and $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$, making the stress tensor symmetric. (2 marks)

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Principal Stresses in $xy$-plane (7 Marks):**

**Given (in-plane):** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa

**Step 1:** The $xy$-plane principal stresses are obtained from the 2D characteristic equation:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the center and radius:

$$
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 30}{2} = 55 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 30}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{25^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{625 + 1600} = \sqrt{2225} = 47.17 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Principal stresses:

$$
\sigma_1 = 55 + 47.17 = 102.17 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_2 = 55 - 47.17 = 7.83 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_3 = 20 \text{ MPa (out-of-plane, given)}
$$

**Ordering:** $\sigma_1 = 102.17$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 20$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = 7.83$ MPa

**Step 4:** Maximum shear stress (in 3D):

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{102.17 - 7.83}{2} = 47.17 \text{ MPa}
$$

---

### ŌØō Question B (Alternative Choice ŌĆö 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define the stress tensor. With a neat 3D sketch of an infinitesimal element, show all **9 stress components** and explain how the symmetry condition reduces them to **6 independent components**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D stress state with $\sigma_x = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 20$ MPa, find the **principal stresses**, **maximum in-plane shear stress**, and the **orientation of principal planes**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Stress Tensor & Symmetry (7 Marks):**

- Definition of stress tensor: 1 mark
- 3D element sketch with all 9 components: 2 marks
- Explanation of equality of cross shear: 2 marks
- Reduction to 6 independent components with matrix: 2 marks

**Key points to write:**

1. The Cauchy stress tensor is a second-order tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ that completely describes the stress state at a point.
2. On each of the 3 faces (positive and negative $x, y, z$), there is 1 normal stress + 2 shear stresses = 3 stress components.
3. Total = 3 faces ├Ś 3 components = 9 components.
4. **Symmetry proof:** Moment equilibrium of an infinitesimal element gives $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$.
5. Therefore, the stress matrix is symmetric:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

with only **6 independent components**.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Principal Stresses (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute center:

$$
\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} = 10 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute radius:

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{50 - (-30)}{2}\right)^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Principal stresses:

$$
\sigma_1 = 10 + 44.72 = 54.72 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 10 - 44.72 = -34.72 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum in-plane shear stress:

$$
\tau_{max,\, in\text{-}plane} = R = 44.72 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Principal plane orientation:

$$
\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 20}{50 - (-30)} = \frac{40}{80} = 0.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = 13.28^\circ
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - ŌØī **Do not confuse** $\tau_{xy}$ (shear on $x$-face, in $y$-direction) with $\tau_{yx}$. The order of subscripts matters and is a frequent deduction point.
> - ŌØī **Do not forget the symmetry condition** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ while solving for principal stresses. Writing an unsymmetric matrix is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**.
> - ŌØī **Sign error** in moment direction (clockwise vs counterclockwise) is the most common mistake in the derivation. Always explicitly state the assumed positive sense (e.g., counterclockwise = positive) before summing moments.
> - ŌØī **Unit mismatch** ŌĆö stresses must be in **consistent units** (Pa, MPa, or GPa) throughout the calculation. Mixing kN/m┬▓ with N/mm┬▓ will lose a mark.
> - ŌØī **In the 2D problem**, students often compute in-plane principal stresses but forget to include the **out-of-plane $\sigma_z$** while ordering the three principal stresses and computing the absolute $\tau_{max}$.

---

## 5.3 ­¤ōØ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ The **state of stress at a point** is represented by a **$3 \times 3$ symmetric tensor** called the Cauchy stress tensor.
- Ō£ģ Matrix form: $[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$
- Ō£ģ **9 total components** but only **6 are independent** due to symmetry.
- Ō£ģ **Diagonal elements** = normal stresses; **off-diagonal elements** = shear stresses.
- Ō£ģ **Equality of cross shear (complementary shear theorem):** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$.
- Ō£ģ Proof: Take **moment equilibrium** of an infinitesimal element about a point. As $dx, dy \to 0$, the body forces and inertia terms vanish, leaving $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$.
- Ō£ģ The symmetry is what makes stress a true **tensor** (frame-indifferent quantity).
- Ō£ģ For **2D plane stress/plane strain**, the matrix reduces to $\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}$ with only **3 independent** components.
- Ō£ģ **Cauchy's formula:** Traction vector on a plane with unit normal $\hat{n}$ is $\vec{T} = [\sigma] \cdot \hat{n}$.
- Ō£ģ **Stress invariants** $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are independent of coordinate system rotation ŌĆö crucial for principal stress calculations.
- Ō£ģ **Sign convention:** Tensile = positive, compressive = negative; shear producing clockwise couple on positive face = positive.
- Ō£ģ **SI Units:** N/m┬▓ = Pa; commonly use **MPa = 10ŌüČ Pa** and **GPa = 10Ōü╣ Pa**.
- Ō£ģ **Engineering applications:** Foundation for Mohr's circle, principal stress analysis, failure theories (von Mises, Tresca), and FEM post-processing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cauchy's Equation (No Derivation). Principal Planes and Principal Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cauchy's Equation (No Derivation). Principal Planes and Principal Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cauchy's Equation** (also called the **stress transformation equation** on an inclined plane) is a fundamental relation in the theory of elasticity that expresses the **normal stress ($\sigma_n$)** and **tangential stress ($\tau$)** acting on any plane inclined at an angle $\theta$ to a reference axis, in terms of the stresses acting on a 2D rectangular element: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$.

**Principal Planes** are specific planes within a stressed material on which the **shear stress ($\tau$) is zero**. On these planes, the stress is entirely **normal (direct) stress**.

**Principal Stresses** are the **maximum ($\sigma_1$)** and **minimum ($\sigma_2$)** normal stresses acting on a material element, occurring on the principal planes. They represent the extreme values of normal stress in any orientation of the cutting plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This is a **core concept** in Module 2 of Solid Mechanics. Cauchy's equation is the mathematical backbone used in pressure vessels, shaft design, column buckling, and combined loading analysis. **Full marks questions are frequently asked** in Part B examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **rectangular bar** and pulling it horizontally ŌĆö this is the $\sigma_x$ force. Now imagine someone is also pulling it vertically ŌĆö this is $\sigma_y$. The bar's particles experience a **diagonal struggle** because the bar is being stretched in two directions simultaneously.

If you were to **slice this bar at an angle** (say 30┬░ to the horizontal), the internal forces on the cut surface are not purely horizontal or vertical ŌĆö they have both a **perpendicular (normal)** and a **parallel (shear/tangential)** component.

- **Cauchy's Equation** is the mathematical tool that tells you: *"If I tilt my cutting plane by angle $\theta$, what normal and shear stresses will I find on that plane?"*
- **Principal Planes** are the *special* tilt angles where the shear stress completely vanishes ŌĆö only pure tension or compression remains. Think of these as the *"resting positions"* of stress, where no sliding occurs.
- **Principal Stresses** are the *largest and smallest* pure pushes/pulls the material experiences, no matter how you slice it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A structural engineer must always know the **maximum stress** a component can experience. Principal stresses give precisely this ŌĆö the absolute peak values. This is critical for failure analysis (e.g., ductile vs. brittle fracture criteria).

### 1.3 Standard Notation and Sign Convention (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma_x$ | Normal stress on face perpendicular to X-axis | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\sigma_y$ | Normal stress on face perpendicular to Y-axis | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\tau_{xy}$ | Shear stress on face perpendicular to X-axis (acts in Y-direction) | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\sigma_n$ | Normal stress on an inclined plane | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\tau$ | Tangential (shear) stress on an inclined plane | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\theta$ | Angle of inclined plane measured from the face on which $\sigma_x$ acts | **Degrees (┬░)** or **Radians** |
| $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ | Major and minor principal stresses | **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** |
| $\theta_p$ | Principal angle (orientation of principal plane) | **Degrees (┬░)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile stresses are POSITIVE (+ve)**
> - **Compressive stresses are NEGATIVE (ŌłÆve)**
> - **Shear stress POSITIVE if it tends to rotate the element in the ANTI-CLOCKWISE direction**
> - **Angle $\theta$ is POSITIVE when measured ANTI-CLOCKWISE from the reference face**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö Cauchy's Equation on an Inclined Plane

Consider a **2D rectangular stress element** subjected to:
- $\sigma_x$ (normal stress on the face perpendicular to the X-axis)
- $\sigma_y$ (normal stress on the face perpendicular to the Y-axis)
- $\tau_{xy}$ (shear stress on the same faces)

Now, imagine a plane cutting the element at an **angle $\theta$** (measured anti-clockwise from the vertical face on which $\sigma_x$ acts). The inclined plane has:
- An **outward normal** making angle $\theta$ with the X-axis
- An **area** larger than the projected X-face by a factor of $\sec(\theta)$

The forces on the four faces of the element must be in **static equilibrium**. Resolving these forces along the inclined plane's normal and tangential directions yields **Cauchy's equations**.

### 2.2 The Two Cauchy's Equations (Final Form)

**Normal Stress on Inclined Plane:**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Tangential (Shear) Stress on Inclined Plane:**

$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Cauchy's Equation" is the "No Derivation" form:** As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, the derivation of these equations is **excluded** from the assessment. You are expected to **state, apply, and interpret** them ŌĆö not derive them. However, you **must memorize the final form** and the sign convention.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Principal Planes Derivation

The condition for a **principal plane** is that the **shear stress on the plane is zero**, i.e., $\tau = 0$.

Setting $\tau = 0$ in Cauchy's tangential equation:

$$-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) = \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)$$

Dividing both sides by $\cos(2\theta_p)$:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

> This gives **two values of $2\theta_p$** separated by **180┬░**, meaning **two values of $\theta_p$ separated by 90┬░** ŌĆö these are the two principal planes.

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic: Principal Stresses Derivation

Substituting the principal angle back into the normal stress equation (or by using standard trigonometric identities on the inclined plane equations), we obtain:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

By convention:
- $\sigma_1$ (Major Principal Stress) = larger value (use **+ sign**)
- $\sigma_2$ (Minor Principal Stress) = smaller value (use **ŌłÆ sign**)

### 2.5 Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress occurs on planes that are **oriented at 45┬░ to the principal planes**:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

> Equivalently, $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ ŌĆö this relation is **frequently tested** in KTU exams.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ | **Cauchy's Normal Stress Equation** |
| 2 | $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ | **Cauchy's Tangential Stress Equation** |
| 3 | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | **Principal Plane Angle** |
| 4 | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | **Principal Stresses** |
| 5 | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | **Maximum Shear Stress** |
| 6 | $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45┬░$ | **Plane of Maximum Shear Stress** |
| 7 | $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ | **Stress Invariant (Sum Rule)** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Cauchy's Equation & Principal Stress |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Hoop and longitudinal stresses in cylindrical shells |
| **Shaft Design** | Combined bending and torsion in rotating shafts |
| **Civil/Structural Engineering** | Combined loading in beams, columns, and trusses |
| **Mechanical Design** | Failure analysis in ductile (e.g., mild steel) and brittle (e.g., cast iron) materials |
| **Geotechnical Engineering** | Principal stress directions in soil mass under foundation loads |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Stress analysis in aircraft wings, fuselage skin panels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU examinations, students often confuse the **angle of the plane** with the **angle of the normal to the plane**. Remember: the angle $\theta$ in Cauchy's equation is measured to the **plane itself** (i.e., to its **normal vector**) measured anti-clockwise from the X-axis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Principal Stresses from Cauchy's Equation

We will derive the principal stress formula starting from Cauchy's normal stress equation, by finding the value of $\theta$ that maximizes (or minimizes) $\sigma_n$.

**Step 1: Start with Cauchy's normal stress equation.**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 2: To find the maximum or minimum value of $\sigma_n$, differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set the derivative to zero.**

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + 2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

**Step 3: Set the derivative to zero for extremum (maxima/minima).**

$$-(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin(2\theta) + 2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

**Step 4: Rearrange the equation.**

$$2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 5: Divide both sides by $\cos(2\theta)$ to get $\tan(2\theta)$.**

$$\tan(2\theta) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This is the **principal angle** equation. The value of $\theta$ that satisfies this is the **principal plane orientation** ($\theta_p$).

**Step 6: Recognize that $\tan(2\theta)$ gives a specific value of $2\theta_p$. Call this value $2\alpha$ where:**

$$\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Step 7: Substitute these cosine and sine values back into the normal stress equation.**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Step 8: Simplify by combining the last two terms (put over a common denominator).**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Step 9: Notice that the numerator inside the square root is the same as outside.**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}{2}$$

**Step 10: Final Result ŌĆö Principal Stress Formula.**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The `+` sign gives $\sigma_1$ (maximum) and the `ŌłÆ` sign gives $\sigma_2$ (minimum).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference Table for Problem Solving

| Given Condition | Principal Angle | Principal Stresses | Maximum Shear |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma_y = 0$ (uniaxial) | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x}$ | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ |
| $\tau_{xy} = 0$ (pure normal) | $\theta_p = 0┬░$ or $90┬░$ | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_y$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\vert\sigma_x - \sigma_y\vert}{2}$ |
| $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$ (hydrostatic) | Indeterminate (all planes have $\tau = 0$) | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$ | $\tau_{max} = \tau_{xy}$ |
| $\sigma_x = -\sigma_y$ (pure shear equivalent) | $\theta_p = 45┬░$ | $\sigma_{1,2} = \pm\tau_{xy}$ | $\tau_{max} = \tau_{xy}$ |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Complete Solution Following KTU Pattern

**Problem:** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are:
$\sigma_x = 80$ N/mm┬▓ (tensile), $\sigma_y = 40$ N/mm┬▓ (tensile), $\tau_{xy} = 30$ N/mm┬▓.

Find:
(a) The principal stresses and principal planes.
(b) The maximum shear stress and its orientation.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Principal Stresses**

Using the principal stress formula:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{120}{2} \pm \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{400 + 900}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{1300}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm 36.06 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Final Principal Stresses:**
- $\sigma_1 = 96.06$ N/mm┬▓ (Major)
- $\sigma_2 = 23.94$ N/mm┬▓ (Minor)

**Principal Plane Angle:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31┬░$$

$$\theta_p = 28.15┬░ \text{ (for major principal stress } \sigma_1)$$

The other principal plane is at $\theta_p + 90┬░ = 118.15┬░$.

**Part (b): Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{96.06 - 23.94}{2} = \frac{72.12}{2} = 36.06 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Orientation of Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45┬░ = 28.15┬░ + 45┬░ = 73.15┬░$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Major Principal Stress ($\sigma_1$) | **96.06 N/mm┬▓** |
| Minor Principal Stress ($\sigma_2$) | **23.94 N/mm┬▓** |
| Principal Plane Angle ($\theta_p$) | **28.15┬░** |
| Maximum Shear Stress ($\tau_{max}$) | **36.06 N/mm┬▓** |
| Plane of Max Shear ($\theta_s$) | **73.15┬░** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 2D Stress Element with All Stresses (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[2D Stress Element] --> B[X-Face Analysis]
    A --> C[Y-Face Analysis]
    A --> D[Inclined Plane Analysis]

    B --> B1[Normal stress sigma x acts outward]
    B --> B2[Shear stress tau xy acts downward on top face]

    C --> C1[Normal stress sigma y acts outward]
    C --> C2[Shear stress tau xy acts rightward on right face]

    D --> D1[Plane inclined at angle theta]
    D --> D2[Normal stress sigma n on plane]
    D --> D3[Shear stress tau on plane]
    D --> D4[Area of inclined plane greater by factor sec theta]

    B1 --> E[Equilibrium equations]
    B2 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    E --> F[Cauchy's Equation for sigma n and tau]
    F --> G[Apply boundary conditions]
    G --> H[Principal Plane: tau equals 0]
    G --> I[Maximum Shear Plane: d sigma n by d theta equals 0]
    H --> J[Principal Stress Formula]
    I --> K[Maximum Shear Stress Formula]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Stress Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input: sigma x, sigma y, tau xy] --> Step1[Step 1: Compute Center of Mohr Circle]
    Step1 --> Step1A[Center equals sigma x plus sigma y divided by 2]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Compute Radius of Mohr Circle]
    Step2 --> Step2A[Radius equals sqrt of sigma x minus sigma y by 2 squared plus tau xy squared]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compute Principal Angle]
    Step3 --> Step3A[Two theta p equals tan inverse of 2 tau xy divided by sigma x minus sigma y]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Compute Principal Stresses]
    Step4 --> Step4A[sigma 1 equals Center plus Radius]
    Step4 --> Step4B[sigma 2 equals Center minus Radius]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Compute Maximum Shear Stress]
    Step5 --> Step5A[Tau max equals Radius equals sigma 1 minus sigma 2 divided by 2]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Compute Orientation of Max Shear]
    Step6 --> Step6A[Theta s equals theta p plus or minus 45 degrees]
    Step6 --> Output[Output: sigma 1, sigma 2, theta p, tau max, theta s]
```

### 4.3 Mohr's Circle Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MohrCircle[MOHR CIRCLE FOR STRESS]
        MC1[Center C: Located on sigma axis at sigma x plus sigma y by 2]
        MC2[Radius R: Equals tau max equals sqrt of sigma x minus sigma y by 2 squared plus tau xy squared]
        MC3[Point A: Represents X-face with coordinates sigma x and minus tau xy]
        MC4[Point B: Represents Y-face with coordinates sigma y and plus tau xy]
        MC5[Point P1: Rightmost point, sigma 1 equals Center plus R]
        MC6[Point P2: Leftmost point, sigma 2 equals Center minus R]
        MC7[Point T: Top of circle, maximum positive shear stress]
        MC8[Point B2: Bottom of circle, maximum negative shear stress]
        MC1 --> MC3
        MC1 --> MC4
        MC1 --> MC5
        MC1 --> MC6
        MC3 --> MC4
        MC3 --> MC7
        MC4 --> MC8
    end

    MC3 --> Key[Key Relationships]
    MC4 --> Key
    MC5 --> Key
    MC6 --> Key
    MC7 --> Key
    Key --> K1[Angle 2 theta on Mohr circle equals physical angle theta on actual element]
    Key --> K2[Direction of rotation on Mohr circle: ANTI-CLOCKWISE on circle equals CLOCKWISE on actual element, and vice versa]
    Key --> K3[Stress at any point on circle equals stress on corresponding physical plane]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Problem Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Problem: Given sigma x, sigma y, tau xy] --> Q1[What is being asked?]
    Q1 --> Q1A[Normal or shear stress on inclined plane]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Principal stresses and planes]
    Q1 --> Q1C[Maximum shear stress and orientation]

    Q1A --> Path1[Use Cauchy's equations directly with given theta]
    Q1B --> Path2[Use principal stress formula and principal angle formula]
    Q1C --> Path3[Compute principal stresses first, then tau max equals sigma 1 minus sigma 2 by 2]

    Path1 --> Check1[Check sign convention: tensile positive, compressive negative]
    Path2 --> Check2[Check tan 2 theta sign: use atan2 function for correct quadrant]
    Path3 --> Check3[Check: max shear plane is 45 degrees from principal planes]

    Check1 --> Result[Final Answer with units N per mm squared and degrees]
    Check2 --> Result
    Check3 --> Result
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**State Cauchy's equations for normal and tangential stresses on an inclined plane in a 2D stress system.**

**Model Answer:**

Cauchy's equations express the normal stress ($\sigma_n$) and tangential stress ($\tau$) on an inclined plane making an angle $\theta$ with the reference axis, in terms of the stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ on the original element.

**Normal stress on inclined plane:**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Tangential (shear) stress on inclined plane:**

$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

These equations are valid for a 2D stress element following the standard sign convention where tensile stress is positive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, May 2023]**
**Define principal planes and principal stresses. Why are they important in engineering design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principal Planes** are specific planes within a stressed material on which the **shear stress is zero** ($\tau = 0$). The stresses acting on these planes are entirely **normal stresses**.

**Principal Stresses** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** acting on the principal planes. They are denoted as $\sigma_1$ (major) and $\sigma_2$ (minor) in 2D analysis.

**Engineering Importance:**
1. Principal stresses are the **extreme values of normal stress** that a material element can experience regardless of the orientation of the cutting plane.
2. **Failure theories** (like maximum normal stress theory, maximum shear stress theory, and von Mises criterion) are all based on principal stresses.
3. In design of shafts, pressure vessels, and beams under combined loading, principal stresses determine whether the material will yield or fracture.
4. For **ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel), failure is governed by maximum shear stress (= $\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$), and for **brittle materials** (e.g., cast iron), failure is governed by maximum normal stress (= $\sigma_1$).

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Marks Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (Option 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model 2024]**

At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are:
$\sigma_x = 100$ N/mm┬▓ (tensile), $\sigma_y = 50$ N/mm┬▓ (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 40$ N/mm┬▓ (as per KTU sign convention).

**(a) Determine the principal stresses and the orientation of the principal planes. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Find the maximum shear stress and the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a): Principal Stresses and Principal Planes**

**Step 1: Identify the given stresses.**

- $\sigma_x = +100$ N/mm┬▓ (tensile, positive)
- $\sigma_y = -50$ N/mm┬▓ (compressive, negative)
- $\tau_{xy} = +40$ N/mm┬▓

**Step 2: Apply the principal stress formula.**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{100 + (-50)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{100 - (-50)}{2}\right)^2 + (40)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{50}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{150}{2}\right)^2 + 1600}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 25 \pm \sqrt{(75)^2 + 1600}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 25 \pm \sqrt{5625 + 1600}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 25 \pm \sqrt{7225}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 25 \pm 85$$

**Principal Stresses:**
- $\sigma_1 = 110$ N/mm┬▓ (Major Principal Stress)
- $\sigma_2 = -60$ N/mm┬▓ (Minor Principal Stress)

**Step 3: Calculate the principal angle.**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 40}{100 - (-50)} = \frac{80}{150} = 0.5333$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5333) = 28.07┬░$$

$$\theta_p = 14.04┬░$$

**Verification of Quadrant:** Since $\sigma_x > \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy} > 0$, the principal plane for $\sigma_1$ lies between 0┬░ and 45┬░. Our value of 14.04┬░ satisfies this condition. Ō£ō

**Orientation of Principal Planes:**
- Plane for $\sigma_1$: $\theta_{p1} = 14.04┬░$
- Plane for $\sigma_2$: $\theta_{p2} = 14.04┬░ + 90┬░ = 104.04┬░$

**Part (b): Maximum Shear Stress and Normal Stress on its Plane**

**Step 1: Calculate the maximum shear stress.**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{110 - (-60)}{2} = \frac{170}{2} = 85 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the orientation of the maximum shear stress plane.**

The plane of maximum shear stress is at 45┬░ to the principal planes:

$$\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45┬░ = 14.04┬░ + 45┬░ = 59.04┬░$$

(or the complementary plane at $59.04┬░ + 90┬░ = 149.04┬░$)

**Step 3: Calculate the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear.**

On the plane of maximum shear, the **average normal stress** acts:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{110 + (-60)}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Therefore, the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear is **25 N/mm┬▓** (tensile).

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Major Principal Stress ($\sigma_1$) | **110 N/mm┬▓** |
| Minor Principal Stress ($\sigma_2$) | **ŌłÆ60 N/mm┬▓** |
| Principal Plane Angle ($\theta_p$) | **14.04┬░** |
| Maximum Shear Stress ($\tau_{max}$) | **85 N/mm┬▓** |
| Plane of Max Shear ($\theta_s$) | **59.04┬░** |
| Normal Stress on Max Shear Plane | **25 N/mm┬▓** |

---

**Question B (Option 2) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model 2024]**

A steel shaft is subjected to combined bending and torsion. At a critical point, the bending produces a tensile stress of **60 N/mm┬▓** along the X-axis, and the torsion produces a shear stress of **45 N/mm┬▓** on the same plane. The stress along the Y-axis is negligible.

**(a) Compute the magnitude and direction of the principal stresses at this point. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Determine the normal and tangential stresses on a plane inclined at 30┬░ to the X-axis. Verify your answer using the principal stress values. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a): Principal Stresses and Their Direction**

**Step 1: Identify the given stresses.**

- $\sigma_x = +60$ N/mm┬▓ (tensile, due to bending)
- $\sigma_y = 0$ N/mm┬▓ (negligible)
- $\tau_{xy} = +45$ N/mm┬▓ (due to torsion)

**Step 2: Apply the principal stress formula.**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{60 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{60 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (45)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 30 \pm \sqrt{(30)^2 + 2025}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 30 \pm \sqrt{900 + 2025}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 30 \pm \sqrt{2925}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 30 \pm 54.08$$

**Principal Stresses:**
- $\sigma_1 = 84.08$ N/mm┬▓ (Major Principal Stress, Tensile)
- $\sigma_2 = -24.08$ N/mm┬▓ (Minor Principal Stress, Compressive)

**Step 3: Calculate the principal angle.**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 45}{60 - 0} = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31┬░$$

$$\theta_p = 28.15┬░$$

**Orientation of Principal Planes:**
- $\theta_{p1} = 28.15┬░$ (for $\sigma_1$)
- $\theta_{p2} = 28.15┬░ + 90┬░ = 118.15┬░$ (for $\sigma_2$)

**Part (b): Stresses on a Plane at 30┬░**

**Method 1: Direct application of Cauchy's Equations**

Given: $\theta = 30┬░$, so $2\theta = 60┬░$.

**Normal stress on inclined plane:**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{60 + 0}{2} + \frac{60 - 0}{2}\cos(60┬░) + 45\sin(60┬░)$$

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 30 \times 0.5 + 45 \times 0.866$$

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 15 + 38.97$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_n = 83.97 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

**Tangential (shear) stress on inclined plane:**

$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

$$\tau = -\frac{60 - 0}{2}\sin(60┬░) + 45\cos(60┬░)$$

$$\tau = -30 \times 0.866 + 45 \times 0.5$$

$$\tau = -25.98 + 22.5$$

$$\boxed{\tau = -3.48 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

The negative sign indicates that the actual direction of shear is opposite to the assumed positive direction.

**Method 2: Verification using Principal Stresses**

The angle between the inclined plane (at 30┬░) and the principal plane (at 28.15┬░) is:
$$\phi = 30┬░ - 28.15┬░ = 1.85┬░$$

Using the transformation formula based on principal stresses:
$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}\cos(2\phi)$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{84.08 + (-24.08)}{2} + \frac{84.08 - (-24.08)}{2}\cos(2 \times 1.85┬░)$$

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 54.08 \times \cos(3.70┬░)$$

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 54.08 \times 0.9979$$

$$\sigma_n = 30 + 53.97 = 83.97 \text{ N/mm}^2 \checkmark$$

For the shear stress:
$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}\sin(2\phi) = -54.08 \times \sin(3.70┬░)$$

$$\tau = -54.08 \times 0.0645 = -3.49 \text{ N/mm}^2 \checkmark$$

The two methods give matching results, confirming the correctness of the calculation.

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Major Principal Stress ($\sigma_1$) | **84.08 N/mm┬▓** |
| Minor Principal Stress ($\sigma_2$) | **ŌłÆ24.08 N/mm┬▓** |
| Principal Plane Angle ($\theta_p$) | **28.15┬░** |
| Normal Stress at $\theta = 30┬░$ | **83.97 N/mm┬▓** |
| Shear Stress at $\theta = 30┬░$ | **ŌłÆ3.48 N/mm┬▓** |

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often write compressive stresses as positive in the formula. **Always** use the KTU sign convention: tensile = +, compressive = ŌłÆ. A single sign error can flip both principal stresses and the principal angle.
> 
> 2. **Quadrant Error in $\tan(2\theta_p)$:** The formula $\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ gives the **acute angle** of $2\theta_p$. You must verify which principal plane corresponds to $\sigma_1$ vs $\sigma_2$ by substitution. A common error is to assume $\theta_p$ is always for $\sigma_1$ ŌĆö sometimes it gives $\sigma_2$ depending on signs.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Plane Angle with Normal Angle:** The angle $\theta$ in Cauchy's equation is measured to the **outward normal of the plane**, not to the plane itself. Examiners specifically test this conceptual clarity.
> 
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always maintain $\sigma$ in **N/mm┬▓ (MPa)** and $\theta$ in **degrees (┬░)** OR $\theta$ in **radians** ŌĆö never mix. For trigonometric functions, ensure your calculator is in the correct mode.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting Complementary Principal Planes:** Students often forget that there are **two principal planes** at $\theta_p$ and $\theta_p + 90┬░$. **Both must be stated** for full marks.
> 
> 6. **Maximum Shear Plane Orientation:** The plane of maximum shear is at $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45┬░$, **not** $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45┬░$ only. There are two such planes.
> 
> 7. **Invariance Property Omission:** Examiners love to test the property $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$. This is a free 1-mark scoring point ŌĆö **always state and verify** it.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- Ō£ģ **Cauchy's Normal Stress Equation:** $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ ŌĆö memorize with sign convention.

- Ō£ģ **Cauchy's Tangential Stress Equation:** $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ ŌĆö note the **negative sign** in the first term.

- Ō£ģ **Principal Plane Condition:** Set $\tau = 0$ in Cauchy's equation to get $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.

- Ō£ģ **Principal Stresses:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ ŌĆö `+` gives $\sigma_1$, `ŌłÆ` gives $\sigma_2$.

- Ō£ģ **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.

- Ō£ģ **Orientation of Max Shear:** $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45┬░$ ŌĆö always 45┬░ offset from principal planes.

- Ō£ģ **Stress Invariant:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ ŌĆö independent of orientation.

- Ō£ģ **Two Principal Planes:** Differ by 90┬░; two Max Shear Planes: differ by 90┬░.

- Ō£ģ **Sign Convention:** Tensile (+), Compressive (ŌłÆ), Shear positive if anti-clockwise rotation tendency.

- Ō£ģ **For Pure Shear ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$):** $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, $\theta_p = 45┬░$, $\tau_{max} = \tau_{xy}$.

- Ō£ģ **For Hydrostatic Stress ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$):** $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$, $\tau_{max} = \tau_{xy}$, $\theta_p$ = indeterminate.

- Ō£ģ **Practice Units:** N/mm┬▓ (MPa) for stress, degrees (┬░) for angles. Keep calculator in **DEG mode**.

- Ō£ģ **Verification Step:** Always check $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ after computation ŌĆö this catches arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning of Stress Invariants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Meaning of Stress Invariants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 ŌĆö MEANING OF STRESS INVARIANTS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the mathematical theory of the **state of stress** at a point in a continuum, the term **Stress Invariant** refers to a scalar quantity that is derived from the components of the stress tensor $[\sigma]$ and whose value **does not change** when the reference coordinate axes are rotated about that point.

Mathematically, a scalar $I_k$ formed from the stress tensor is called an invariant if it satisfies:

$$
I_k = I_k' \quad \text{for every rotated coordinate system } (x', y', z')
$$

For a general three-dimensional state of stress, exactly **three independent stress invariants** exist, conventionally designated $I_1$, $I_2$, and $I_3$. They are, in fact, the coefficients of the **characteristic (secular) equation** whose roots are the three principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Every examiner expects you to clearly state that "invariant" means *independent of the orientation of the coordinate axes*. This single sentence alone often scores the opening mark of a 7-mark derivation question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Fingerprint" of the Stress State

Consider a small cube of rubber under complex loading. Now pick up the cube and look at it from a different angle. Although the visible face-normal stresses ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y$) and the visible shear stresses ($\tau_{xy}$) all change to new values, certain **intrinsic physical properties** of the internal state never change:

- The **sum** of the three normal stresses (which governs volumetric change).
- The **maximum possible shear stress** in the material.
- The **product** of the three principal stresses (linked to volume strain and energy).

Think of an apple: its **mass, volume, and surface area** are real properties that do not change no matter how you hold it or rotate it. Stress invariants are the "intrinsic physical fingerprints" of the stress state at a point ŌĆö quantities an engineer can rely on to make design decisions regardless of how the coordinate system is chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** SI unit of stress = **N/m┬▓ (Pascal, Pa)**. Standard metric prefixes: **1 MPa = 10ŌüČ Pa**, **1 GPa = 10Ōü╣ Pa**.

## 1.3 Why Do Invariants Exist? ŌĆö The Deep Reason

The stress tensor $[\sigma]$ is a $3\times 3$ **symmetric, real matrix** (because of the symmetry of the Cauchy stress tensor: $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, etc.). The **Eigenvalue Theorem** of linear algebra guarantees that the characteristic polynomial of such a matrix has:

1. **Real** eigenvalues (these are the principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$).
2. **Orthogonal** eigenvectors (these are the principal directions).
3. **Coefficients that are invariant under orthogonal similarity transformations** ŌĆö and a coordinate rotation is *precisely* such an orthogonal transformation.

Hence the three coefficients of the cubic eigenvalue equation are the three stress invariants. They are the *fundamental scalar descriptors* of the stress tensor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö From Stress Tensor to Invariants

The logical pipeline from a stress state to its invariants is as follows:

- **Step 1:** Capture all six independent stress components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$) into a $3 \times 3$ symmetric stress tensor $[\sigma]$.
- **Step 2:** Form the **characteristic equation** by setting $\det([\sigma] - \lambda [I]) = 0$, where $\lambda$ is the unknown principal stress.
- **Step 3:** Expand the $3 \times 3$ determinant. A cubic polynomial in $\lambda$ emerges.
- **Step 4:** Compare the polynomial with the standard cubic form $\lambda^3 - I_1 \lambda^2 + I_2 \lambda - I_3 = 0$. The coefficients $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are the **stress invariants**.
- **Step 5:** Equate coefficients using **Vieta's formulas** to express the invariants directly in terms of the stress components.

The beauty of this procedure is that the cubic itself is the *same* in every coordinate system; only the individual stress components change. Therefore the coefficients ŌĆö the invariants ŌĆö must be unchanged.

## 2.2 The Three Stress Invariants ŌĆö Quick Reference Table

| Invariant | Standard Form in Stress Components | Equivalent Form in Principal Stresses (Vieta's) | Physical / Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **$I_1$ (First Stress Invariant)** | $\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ | $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3$ | Three times the **mean normal (hydrostatic) stress**; proportional to volumetric strain for isotropic elastic materials |
| **$I_2$ (Second Stress Invariant)** | $\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$ | $\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1$ | Governs the **shear-strain energy density**; embedded in the Von Mises yield criterion |
| **$I_3$ (Third Stress Invariant)** | $\sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2$ | $\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3$ | Proportional to the **determinant** of the stress tensor; vanishes when one principal stress is zero (plane stress) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Win Tip:** Memorise the elegant Vieta's form ($\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3$, etc.) **first**. Then use it as a quick check whenever a numerical problem is solved. Many students lose marks because they verify the algebra but forget to cross-check using the principal-stress version of the invariants.

## 2.3 Companion Invariants ŌĆö The Deviatoric Set

For advanced topics (yield criteria, soil mechanics, plasticity), the stress tensor is split into a **hydrostatic (volumetric) part** and a **deviatoric (distortional) part**:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \underbrace{\frac{I_1}{3}\delta_{ij}}_{\text{hydrostatic part}} \;+\; \underbrace{s_{ij}}_{\text{deviatoric part}}
$$

The deviatoric tensor $s_{ij}$ has its own three invariants $J_1, J_2, J_3$:

| Deviatoric Invariant | Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $J_1$ | $0$ (always) | Traceless by construction |
| $J_2$ | $\dfrac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = I_2 - \dfrac{I_1^2}{3}$ | Direct input to **Von Mises** criterion: $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{3J_2}$ |
| $J_3$ | $(\sigma_1-\sigma_m)(\sigma_2-\sigma_m)(\sigma_3-\sigma_m)$ with $\sigma_m = I_1/3$ | Governs **Lode's angle** in soil mechanics |

## 2.4 Octahedral Stresses ŌĆö Engineering Utility

Stresses acting on a plane equally inclined to all three principal directions (the **octahedral plane**) have beautifully simple invariant forms:

$$
\sigma_{\text{oct}} = \frac{I_1}{3} \quad\quad \tau_{\text{oct}} = \frac{1}{3}\sqrt{2I_1^2 - 6I_2}
$$

These are widely used in:
- **Metal forming** (forging, rolling, extrusion) to predict plastic flow.
- **Soil mechanics** for the MohrŌĆōCoulomb and DruckerŌĆōPrager failure models.
- **Fatigue analysis** of multiaxially loaded components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Stress invariants are the language of *constitutive modelling*. Whether the material is steel, concrete, soil, or polymer, every modern yield and failure criterion (Tresca, Von Mises, MohrŌĆōCoulomb, DruckerŌĆōPrager, Hill, Gurson) is written in terms of $I_1, I_2, I_3$ and/or $J_2, J_3$ ŌĆö never in raw $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ components.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of 2D Stress Invariants (Build-Up Stage)

A 2D state of stress (plane stress) at a point is described by the matrix:

$$
[\sigma]_{2D} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

The principal stresses are the eigenvalues, found from:

$$
\det([\sigma] - \lambda [I]) = 0
$$

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x - \lambda & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding the $2 \times 2$ determinant:

$$
(\sigma_x - \lambda)(\sigma_y - \lambda) - \tau_{xy}^2 = 0
$$

$$
\sigma_x\sigma_y - \sigma_x\lambda - \sigma_y\lambda + \lambda^2 - \tau_{xy}^2 = 0
$$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form $\lambda^2 - I_1\lambda + I_2 = 0$:

$$
\lambda^2 - (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\lambda + (\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2) = 0
$$

By Vieta's formulas, the **2D stress invariants** are:

$$
\boxed{I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y \quad\quad I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

## 3.2 Connection to Mohr's Circle (Geometric Proof of Invariance)

The Mohr's circle for the 2D state has its centre on the $\sigma$-axis at:

$$
C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{I_1}{2}
$$

and radius:

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Squaring the radius and using $I_1, I_2$:

$$
R^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2
     = \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} + \tau_{xy}^2
$$

Multiplying by 4 and using $I_1^2 = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2$:

$$
4R^2 = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2
     = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2 - 4(\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2)
     = I_1^2 - 4I_2
$$

$$
\therefore \quad R = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{I_1^2 - 4I_2}
$$

The **principal stresses** are therefore:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{I_1}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{I_1^2 - 4I_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Insight:** The Mohr's circle *itself* is a geometric invariant. Its centre $C = I_1/2$ and radius $R = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{I_1^2 - 4I_2}$ are made entirely of stress invariants. So whatever coordinate system you draw Mohr's circle in, the *same* circle appears. This is the visual proof that $I_1$ and $I_2$ are invariants.

## 3.3 Full Derivation of 3D Stress Invariants (Exhaustive)

The general 3D stress tensor is:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{zx} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Form the determinant $\det([\sigma] - \lambda [I])$:

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x - \lambda & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{zx} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y - \lambda & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Expand using the standard $3 \times 3$ determinant rule. A cleaner approach is to recognise the pattern:

$$
(\sigma_x - \lambda)(\sigma_y - \lambda)(\sigma_z - \lambda) - \tau_{xy}^2(\sigma_z - \lambda) - \tau_{yz}^2(\sigma_x - \lambda) - \tau_{zx}^2(\sigma_y - \lambda) + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the cubic term. Using the identity for the product of three binomials:

$$
(\sigma_x - \lambda)(\sigma_y - \lambda)(\sigma_z - \lambda) = \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z - (\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x)\lambda + (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\lambda^2 - \lambda^3
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the shear-squared terms:

$$
\tau_{xy}^2(\sigma_z - \lambda) = \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{xy}^2\lambda
$$

$$
\tau_{yz}^2(\sigma_x - \lambda) = \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2\lambda
$$

$$
\tau_{zx}^2(\sigma_y - \lambda) = \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2\lambda
$$

**Step 5:** Collect all terms and group by powers of $\lambda$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\bigl[-\lambda^3\bigr] \\
&+\lambda^2\bigl[\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z\bigr] \\
&+\lambda\bigl[-(\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x) + \tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2\bigr] \\
&+\bigl[\sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2 - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx}\bigr] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to the canonical cubic form $\lambda^3 - I_1\lambda^2 + I_2\lambda - I_3 = 0$ by multiplying through by $-1$:

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z \\
I_2 &= \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2 \\
I_3 &= \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2
\end{aligned}}
$$

**Step 7 (Vieta's Verification):** Since $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ are the three roots of the same cubic, Vieta's formulas immediately give:

$$
\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3 = I_1
$$

$$
\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1 = I_2
$$

$$
\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3 = I_3
$$

This is the **most powerful invariant identity** in solid mechanics, used by KTU examiners to set cross-check questions.

## 3.4 Derivation of Deviatoric Invariant $J_2$

Define the mean stress $\sigma_m = \dfrac{I_1}{3} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$.

The deviatoric stress tensor is $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m\delta_{ij}$:

$$
[s] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x - \sigma_m & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{zx} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y - \sigma_m & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z - \sigma_m \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the standard $I_2$ formula to $[s]$. Since $[s]$ is traceless ($s_{xx} + s_{yy} + s_{zz} = 0$), the formula simplifies. The standard result is:

$$
J_2 = I_2 - \sigma_m I_1 + 3\sigma_m^2 - \sigma_m^2
$$

Substituting $\sigma_m = I_1/3$:

$$
\sigma_m I_1 = \frac{I_1^2}{3} \quad\quad 3\sigma_m^2 - \sigma_m^2 = 2\sigma_m^2 = \frac{2I_1^2}{9}
$$

Wait ŌĆö the cleanest path is to use the algebraic identity for $J_2$ in terms of $I_1, I_2$:

$$
\boxed{J_2 = I_2 - \frac{I_1^2}{3}}
$$

This can be derived by direct expansion or, more efficiently, by using:

$$
J_2 = \frac{1}{2}s_{ij}s_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_{ij}\sigma_{ij}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{I_1}{3}\right)^2 \cdot 3 = \frac{1}{2}(I_1^2 - 2I_2) - \frac{I_1^2}{6}
$$

After simplification: $J_2 = I_2 - \dfrac{I_1^2}{3}$, confirming the boxed formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $J_2$ important?** It is the *scalar measure of the distortion* in the material. The Von Mises equivalent stress, the most widely used yield criterion in mechanical design, is simply $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{3J_2}$. This is why $J_2$ appears in nearly every FEA software post-processor.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flowchart ŌĆö From Stress State to Engineering Application

The diagram below maps the complete logical flow from the raw stress state at a point all the way to its use in engineering design criteria.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GENERAL STRESS STATE AT A POINT] --> B[STRESS TENSOR SIGMA 3 BY 3 SYMMETRIC]
    B --> C[FORM CHARACTERISTIC EQUATION DET SIGMA MINUS LAMBDA I EQUALS ZERO]
    C --> D[EXPAND THE 3 BY 3 DETERMINANT]
    D --> E[CUBIC EQUATION LAMBDA CUBED MINUS I1 LAMBDA SQUARED PLUS I2 LAMBDA MINUS I3 EQUALS ZERO]
    E --> F[EXTRACT INVARIANT COEFFICIENTS]
    F --> G1[I1 SUM OF NORMAL STRESSES]
    F --> G2[I2 SUM OF MINOR PRODUCTS MINUS SHEAR SQUARES]
    F --> G3[I3 DETERMINANT LIKE TERM]
    G1 --> H[ROOTS GIVE PRINCIPAL STRESSES SIGMA1 SIGMA2 SIGMA3]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I1[TRESCA YIELD CRITERION MAX SHEAR]
    H --> I2[VON MISES YIELD CRITERION VIA J2]
    H --> I3[MOHR COULOMB FAILURE SOIL AND ROCK]
    H --> I4[OCTAHEDRAL STRESS METAL FORMING]
    G1 --> J1[VOLUMETRIC STRAIN ELASTICITY]
    G2 --> J2[DISTORTION ENERGY]
    G3 --> J3[PLASTIC FLOW POTENTIAL]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Hydrostatic vs Deviatoric Decomposition

The diagram below shows how the full stress tensor is split into two physically distinct contributions, and how invariants govern each part.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STRESS TENSOR SIGMA IJ] --> B[HYDROSTATIC PART MEAN STRESS TIMES DELTA IJ]
    A --> C[DEVIATORIC PART S IJ]
    B --> D[PHYSICAL EFFECT VOLUME CHANGE]
    C --> E[PHYSICAL EFFECT SHAPE DISTORTION]
    D --> F1[GOVERNED BY I1 OVER 3]
    D --> F2[OCTAHEDRAL NORMAL STRESS EQUALS I1 OVER 3]
    E --> G1[J2 INVARIANT]
    E --> G2[OCTAHEDRAL SHEAR STRESS FUNCTION OF I1 AND I2]
    E --> G3[VON MISES STRESS SQUARE ROOT 3 J2]
    G1 --> H1[TRESCA MAX SHEAR EQUALS SIGMA1 MINUS SIGMA3 OVER 2]
    G3 --> H2[FATIGUE AND YIELD DESIGN]
```

## 4.3 Mohr's Circle ŌĆö Geometric Invariance Visual

In 2D stress analysis, the Mohr's circle can be drawn entirely using invariants. The schematic below (in Mermaid block form) summarises the geometric interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[2D STRESS STATE] --> B[MOHRS CIRCLE GEOMETRY]
    B --> C1[CENTER ON SIGMA AXIS AT I1 OVER 2]
    B --> C2[RADIUS EQUALS HALF OF ROOT I1 SQUARED MINUS 4 I2]
    C1 --> D[PRINCIPAL STRESSES AT ENDS OF HORIZONTAL DIAMETER]
    C2 --> D
    C1 --> E[MAX SHEAR STRESS EQUAL TO RADIUS]
    C2 --> E
    D --> F[ROTATION OF AXES MOVES POINT ON CIRCLE]
    F --> G[CIRCLE SHAPE UNCHANGED INVARIANT PROOF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Sketches?** Pure 2D Mohr's circle sketches cannot be rendered reliably in Mermaid. The block-level functional architecture above communicates the *same invariant principle* (the circle is unchanged under rotation) using a system-flow representation, which Mermaid renders flawlessly.
