# ­¤ōō SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the displacement gradient tensor. Explain its physical significance in solid mechanics.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **displacement gradient tensor** is a second-order tensor defined as the spatial gradient of the displacement vector field:

$$
u_{i,j} = \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}
$$

It is a **3 ├Ś 3 matrix in 3D** (or 2 ├Ś 2 in 2D) containing 9 (or 4) components that represent the rate of change of each displacement component with respect to each spatial coordinate.

**Physical Significance:**
1. It completely describes the **local deformation** (stretching + rotation) of an infinitesimal material element.
2. Its **symmetric part** gives the **infinitesimal strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij}$ ŌĆö measures pure shape change.
3. Its **antisymmetric part** gives the **rotation tensor** $\omega_{ij}$ ŌĆö measures rigid body rotation.
4. It is the **fundamental kinematic variable** connecting deformation to stress via constitutive laws (e.g., Hooke's law).

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Physical significance (1)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the strain tensor and the rotation tensor obtained from the displacement gradient. State their symmetry properties.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Strain Tensor $\varepsilon_{ij}$ | Rotation Tensor $\omega_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ | $\frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} - u_{j,i})$ |
| Symmetry | **Symmetric** ($\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$) | **Antisymmetric** ($\omega_{ij} = -\omega_{ji}$) |
| Diagonal | Non-zero (normal strains) | **Always zero** ($\omega_{ii} = 0$) |
| Independent components (3D) | 6 | 3 |
| Physical meaning | Pure deformation (stretch + shear) | Rigid body rotation |
| Engineering use | Hooke's law, stress calculation | Displacement of rigid bodies |

Both tensors are obtained from the decomposition:

$$
u_{i,j} = \varepsilon_{ij} + \omega_{ij}
$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Tabular distinction (2) + Decomposition formula (1)

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 2 questions offer **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **infinitesimal strain tensor** from the geometric definition of strain, starting from the displacement gradient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the displacement field:
$$u_1 = (3x_1^2 + 2x_2) \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_2 = (2x_1 + 4x_2^2) \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_3 = 0$$
Compute the **strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij}$, the **rotation tensor** $\omega_{ij}$, and the **volumetric strain** at the point $(1, 1, 0)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Consider two infinitesimally close points** $P(\vec{X})$ and $Q(\vec{X} + d\vec{X})$ in the reference configuration. Their separation is $d\vec{X}$.

Original squared length:
$$
(dL_0)^2 = d\vec{X} \cdot d\vec{X} = dX_i \, dX_i
$$

**Step 2: After deformation**, the new squared length is:
$$
(dL)^2 = d\vec{x} \cdot d\vec{x}
$$

Using $\vec{x} = \vec{X} + \vec{u}$ and $dx_i = (\delta_{ij} + u_{i,j}) dX_j$:

$$
(dL)^2 = (\delta_{ij} + u_{i,j})(\delta_{ik} + u_{i,k}) \, dX_j \, dX_k
$$

**Step 3: Expand**:
$$
(dL)^2 = [\delta_{jk} + u_{j,k} + u_{k,j} + u_{i,j}u_{i,k}] \, dX_j \, dX_k
$$

**Step 4: Subtract** $(dL_0)^2 = \delta_{jk} dX_j dX_k$:

$$
(dL)^2 - (dL_0)^2 = [u_{j,k} + u_{k,j} + u_{i,j}u_{i,k}] \, dX_j \, dX_k
$$

**Step 5: Define Green-Lagrange strain**:
$$
E_{jk} = \frac{1}{2}[u_{j,k} + u_{k,j} + u_{i,j}u_{i,k}]
$$

**Step 6: For small deformations** (drop the nonlinear term $u_{i,j}u_{i,k}$):

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{jk} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{j,k} + u_{k,j})}
$$

This is the **infinitesimal (linear) strain tensor**. It is symmetric: $\varepsilon_{jk} = \varepsilon_{kj}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute all required displacement gradients.**

Given: $u_1 = (3x_1^2 + 2x_2) \times 10^{-5}$, $u_2 = (2x_1 + 4x_2^2) \times 10^{-5}$

$$
\frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x_1} = 6x_1 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x_2} = 2 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x_3} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x_1} = 2 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x_2} = 8x_2 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x_3} = 0
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate at the point $(1, 1, 0)$:**

$$
u_{1,1} = 6 \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_{1,2} = 2 \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_{1,3} = 0
$$

$$
u_{2,1} = 2 \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_{2,2} = 8 \times 10^{-5}, \quad u_{2,3} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Construct the displacement gradient matrix.**

$$
\nabla \vec{u} = 10^{-5} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the strain tensor using $\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$.**

$$
\varepsilon_{11} = u_{1,1} = 6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{22} = u_{2,2} = 8 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{33} = 0
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{12} = \varepsilon_{21} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{1,2} + u_{2,1}) = \frac{1}{2}(2 + 2) \times 10^{-5} = 2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{13} = \varepsilon_{23} = 0
$$

**Strain tensor:**

$$
\varepsilon = 10^{-5} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the rotation tensor using $\omega_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} - u_{j,i})$.**

$$
\omega_{12} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{1,2} - u_{2,1}) = \frac{1}{2}(2 - 2) \times 10^{-5} = 0
$$

All other off-diagonal terms are also 0.

**Rotation tensor:**

$$
\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Interpretation:** No rigid body rotation occurs at this point ŌĆö the deformation is **pure stretching + shearing**.

**Step 6: Compute the volumetric strain.**

$$
e = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33} = (6 + 8 + 0) \times 10^{-5} = 14 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 7: Verification.** Confirm $\varepsilon_{ij} + \omega_{ij} = u_{i,j}$:

$$
10^{-5} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0 = 10^{-5} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **decomposition of the displacement gradient** into symmetric and antisymmetric parts. Discuss the physical significance of each part. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D displacement field $u = ax + by$ and $v = cx + dy$ (where $a, b, c, d$ are constants), determine:
- (i) The displacement gradient matrix
- (ii) The strain tensor components
- (iii) The condition for **pure rotation (no strain)** at a point. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Statement of the decomposition theorem.**

Any second-order tensor $T_{ij}$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of a symmetric tensor $S_{ij}$ and an antisymmetric tensor $A_{ij}$:

$$
T_{ij} = S_{ij} + A_{ij}
$$

Applying this to the displacement gradient $u_{i,j}$:

$$
u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i}) + \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} - u_{j,i}) = \varepsilon_{ij} + \omega_{ij}
$$

**Step 2: Symmetric part ŌĆö Strain tensor $\varepsilon_{ij}$.**

$$
\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})
$$

**Properties:**
- Symmetric: $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$
- 6 independent components in 3D (3 in 2D)
- Diagonal elements = **normal strains** (e.g., $\varepsilon_{11} = \partial u_1/\partial x_1$)
- Off-diagonal elements = **shear strains** (e.g., $\gamma_{12} = 2\varepsilon_{12}$)

**Physical Significance:** Captures the **pure deformation** of the body ŌĆö the stretching of fibers and change in angles between them. This is the part that produces **internal stresses** via Hooke's law.

**Step 3: Antisymmetric part ŌĆö Rotation tensor $\omega_{ij}$.**

$$
\omega_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} - u_{j,i})
$$

**Properties:**
- Antisymmetric: $\omega_{ij} = -\omega_{ji}$
- Diagonal is **identically zero**: $\omega_{ii} = 0$
- 3 independent components in 3D (1 in 2D)

**Physical Significance:** Captures the **rigid body rotation** of the infinitesimal element. It represents how the element has rotated **without changing its shape**. Does not contribute to internal stress.

**Step 4: Engineering analogy.**

If you imagine a tiny square of rubber:
- The **strain part** tells you how the square became a parallelogram (shape change).
- The **rotation part** tells you how the parallelogram was rotated as a whole.
- The **displacement gradient** is the sum of both effects.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute the displacement gradient matrix.**

Given: $u = ax + by$, $v = cx + dy$.

The partial derivatives are:
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = a, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = b
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = c, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = d
$$

Therefore:

$$
\nabla \vec{u} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the strain components using $\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$.**

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = a
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = d
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{b + c}{2}
$$

**Strain tensor:**

$$
\varepsilon = \begin{bmatrix} a & \dfrac{b+c}{2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{b+c}{2} & d \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Condition for pure rotation (no strain).**

For **pure rotation** (rigid body motion), the strain tensor must be **zero**:

$$
\varepsilon = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_{xx} = \varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{xy} = 0
$$

This gives the conditions:
- $\varepsilon_{xx} = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$
- $\varepsilon_{yy} = 0 \Rightarrow d = 0$
- $\varepsilon_{xy} = 0 \Rightarrow b + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -b$

**The displacement gradient becomes:**

$$
\nabla \vec{u} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & b \\ -b & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a **pure antisymmetric (rotation) matrix**, and the corresponding rotation angle is $\omega_z = -b$ (rigid rotation by $-b$ radians about the $z$-axis).

> **Engineering Note:** This result is fundamental ŌĆö **antisymmetric displacement gradient Ō¤╣ pure rigid body rotation, no strain, no stress.**

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of ┬Į**: The strain tensor is $\frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$, not just $u_{i,j}$. Many students write $\varepsilon_{ij} = u_{i,j}$ directly ŌĆö this is **wrong** unless you prove $u_{i,j}$ is symmetric (which it generally isn't).
>
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma_{xy}$ with $\varepsilon_{xy}$**: Engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$. The factor of 2 is **the most common mark-deduction error** in KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Not specifying coordinate system**: When computing $\partial u_i / \partial x_j$, clearly state whether you are using reference $(X)$ or current $(x)$ coordinates. The formulas differ in nonlinear elasticity.
>
> 4. **Missing the verification step**: Always verify $\varepsilon_{ij} + \omega_{ij} = u_{i,j}$ at the end. KTU examiners **award bonus marks** for verification.
>
> 5. **Writing $\omega_{ii} \neq 0$**: The diagonal of the rotation tensor is **always zero**. A non-zero diagonal indicates a conceptual misunderstanding ŌĆö instant 1-mark cut.

---

## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Gradient of Displacement**

### ­¤öæ Core Definitions
- Ō£ģ **Displacement field:** $\vec{u} = \vec{x} - \vec{X}$
- Ō£ģ **Displacement gradient:** $u_{i,j} = \partial u_i / \partial x_j$ (second-order tensor, 9 components in 3D)
- Ō£ģ **Strain tensor:** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ ŌĆö symmetric, 6 independent components
- Ō£ģ **Rotation tensor:** $\omega_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} - u_{j,i})$ ŌĆö antisymmetric, 3 independent components, diagonal = 0

### ­¤öæ Master Equation (Most Important)
$$
\boxed{u_{i,j} = \varepsilon_{ij} + \omega_{ij}}
$$

### ­¤öæ Key Engineering Conversions
- Engineering shear strain: $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$ (and cyclic)
- Volumetric strain: $e = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33} = \nabla \cdot \vec{u}$
- Rotation about z-axis (2D): $\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}(\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y)$

### ­¤öæ Conditions to Memorize
- **Pure strain, no rotation** Ō¤║ $\nabla \vec{u}$ is symmetric ($u_{i,j} = u_{j,i}$)
- **Pure rotation, no strain** Ō¤║ $\nabla \vec{u}$ is antisymmetric ($u_{i,j} = -u_{j,i}$) AND diagonal = 0
- **No deformation at all** Ō¤║ $\nabla \vec{u} = 0$ everywhere (rigid body translation)

### ­¤öæ Derivation Triggers (KTU Frequently Asked)
- Geometric definition of strain ŌåÆ Green-Lagrange tensor ŌåÆ infinitesimal strain (small deformation assumption drops nonlinear terms)
- Compatibility conditions arise from requiring $\varepsilon_{ij}$ to be derivable from a single-valued $u_i$

### ­¤öæ Common Exam Traps
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Don't write $\varepsilon_{ij} = u_{i,j}$ (missing ┬Į factor)
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Don't write $\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_{xy}$ (missing factor of 2)
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Always verify decomposition at end of numerical problems

### ­¤öæ Quick Mental Test
> *"If I know $u_{i,j}$, can I get strain and rotation?"*
> **Yes** ŌĆö split symmetric and antisymmetric. That's the **entire power** of this topic.

---

> ­¤ōī **Final KTU Tip:** The displacement gradient is the **conceptual bridge** between kinematics (deformation) and constitutive laws (stress). Master this tensor, and Module 2 becomes straightforward. The next topics ŌĆö compatibility, principal strains, Mohr's circle in 3D ŌĆö all stem from here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cartesian Strain Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Strain Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cartesian Strain Matrix** (also referred to as the **Strain Tensor** or **Displacement Gradient Matrix** in its symmetric form) is a second-order tensor that completely describes the state of deformation at an infinitesimally small point within a continuous deformable body, expressed in a Cartesian coordinate system $(x, y, z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Cartesian Strain Matrix is a $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix whose components quantify both **normal strains** (change in length per unit original length along coordinate axes) and **shear strains** (change in angle between originally perpendicular line elements) at a point in a deformed body.

The matrix is formally written as:

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where, due to the **symmetry of the strain tensor**:

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx}, \quad \varepsilon_{yz} = \varepsilon_{zy}, \quad \varepsilon_{xz} = \varepsilon_{zx}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The strain matrix contains **6 independent components** in 3D (3 normal + 3 shear), making it a symmetric tensor of rank 2. This is a critical KTU board-exam point ŌĆö students often incorrectly state that there are 9 independent components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **perfectly cubical sugar cube** sitting on a table. Now imagine you press down on its top face with your thumb while pulling it sideways with your fingers.

**Three things happen to the cube:**

1. **It gets shorter in the vertical direction** (compression) ŌĆö this is *normal strain* $\varepsilon_{zz}$.
2. **It bulges out sideways** (extension) ŌĆö this is *normal strain* in the horizontal direction.
3. **The originally 90┬░ corners of the sugar cube get distorted** ŌĆö a corner that was a perfect right angle may now become 89┬░ or 91┬░. The change in this angle is the *shear strain* $\gamma$.

The **Cartesian Strain Matrix** is simply a mathematical "score card" that records all of these six independent deformations in a single, organized table. Just as a doctor's diagnostic report has separate rows for blood pressure, pulse, and temperature, the strain matrix has separate entries for each directional stretch and angular distortion.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The diagonal elements are like "Length changes" (L-L-L), and the off-diagonal elements are like "Angle changes" (A-A-A). Think of the matrix as **"How much did each side stretch, and how much did each corner tilt?"**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Units of Strain:** Dimensionless (length/length), often expressed in **mm/m**, **┬Ąm/m** (microstrain), or in scientific notation.
- **Engineering Shear Strain Symbol:** $\gamma_{xy}$ (in radians, dimensionless).
- **Tensorial Shear Strain Symbol:** $\varepsilon_{xy}$ (also dimensionless, related by $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$).
- **Small Strain Assumption:** $\varepsilon \ll 1$ (typically $\varepsilon < 10^{-3}$ for linear elasticity) ŌĆö this is the regime in which KTU Module 2 strain analysis is valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The module assumes *infinitesimal strain theory* (small displacement gradients). All derivations in this section use the linearized, small-strain formulation. Large deformation (finite strain) theory is **not** part of the KTU 2024 syllabus for this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö From Displacement to Strain

In solid mechanics, every point inside a body undergoes displacement when the body deforms. Let the displacement vector at a generic point $(x, y, z)$ be:

$$
\vec{u} = u(x,y,z)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y,z)\,\hat{j} + w(x,y,z)\,\hat{k}
$$

where:
- $u$ = displacement component in the $x$-direction
- $v$ = displacement component in the $y$-direction
- $w$ = displacement component in the $z$-direction

**The logical flow from displacement to strain matrix is as follows:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define displacement gradients.** Compute the nine first-order partial derivatives $\partial u_i / \partial x_j$ of the displacement components with respect to the spatial coordinates.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Symmetrize the gradient matrix.** The displacement gradient matrix $[J]$ is generally not symmetric because pure rotation also produces gradients. The strain matrix is the *symmetric part* of the displacement gradient, while the *antisymmetric part* gives the rotation.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpret components physically.** Diagonal entries = normal strain (stretch/compression). Off-diagonal entries = shear strain (angular distortion).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble the matrix.** Form the symmetric $3\times 3$ matrix from the six independent components.

**The "Why" Behind the Symmetry:**

A strain represents *deformation*, not *rigid-body rotation*. If we rotate a body without deforming it, the displacement gradients exist, but the strain must be zero. The symmetric part captures pure deformation, while the antisymmetric part captures rotation ŌĆö by subtracting rotation, we isolate the true strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is the strain matrix symmetric?", always mention: "Because shear strain on the $xy$-face and the $yx$-face must be equal to maintain angular momentum balance and ensure the strain represents pure deformation, not rigid-body rotation."

### 2.2 The Six Independent Strain Components

The complete set of strain components at a point in 3D is given by the Cauchy infinitesimal strain tensor relations:

**Normal Strains (3 components):**

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

**Shear Strains (3 independent components, engineering convention):**

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \gamma_{yx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

$$
\gamma_{yz} = \gamma_{zy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}
$$

$$
\gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}
$$

**In tensor notation (mathematician's shear strain):**

$$
\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for the Cartesian Strain Matrix as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Memorize this before solving any problem.

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|--------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Strain Matrix $[\varepsilon]$ | $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix | Complete strain state at a point | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Normal Strain $\varepsilon_{xx}$ | $\partial u / \partial x$ | Stretch along $x$-axis | $\text{mm/m}$ or $\mu\varepsilon$ |
| 3 | Normal Strain $\varepsilon_{yy}$ | $\partial v / \partial y$ | Stretch along $y$-axis | $\text{mm/m}$ |
| 4 | Normal Strain $\varepsilon_{zz}$ | $\partial w / \partial z$ | Stretch along $z$-axis | $\text{mm/m}$ |
| 5 | Engineering Shear $\gamma_{xy}$ | $\partial u / \partial y + \partial v / \partial x$ | Angle change in $xy$-plane | radians |
| 6 | Engineering Shear $\gamma_{yz}$ | $\partial v / \partial z + \partial w / \partial y$ | Angle change in $yz$-plane | radians |
| 7 | Engineering Shear $\gamma_{xz}$ | $\partial u / \partial z + \partial w / \partial x$ | Angle change in $xz$-plane | radians |
| 8 | Tensorial Shear $\varepsilon_{ij}$ | $\gamma_{ij} / 2$ (for $i \neq j$) | Half of engineering shear | radians |
| 9 | Volumetric Strain $e$ | $\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ | Relative volume change | Dimensionless |
| 10 | First Strain Invariant $I_1$ | $\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ | Trace of matrix | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Second Strain Invariant $I_2$ | $\varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{yy}\varepsilon_{zz} + \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{zz} - \varepsilon_{xy}^2 - \varepsilon_{yz}^2 - \varepsilon_{xz}^2$ | Sum of $2 \times 2$ principal minors | Dimensionless$^2$ |
| 12 | Third Strain Invariant $I_3$ | $\det([\varepsilon])$ | Determinant of matrix | Dimensionless$^3$ |
| 13 | Strain Transformation | $[\varepsilon'] = [T]^T \, [\varepsilon] \, [T]$ | Strain in rotated frame | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Principal Strains | Roots of $\varepsilon^3 - I_1 \varepsilon^2 + I_2 \varepsilon - I_3 = 0$ | Eigenvalues of matrix | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Direction Cosines Constraint | $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ | Unit vector condition | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The first invariant $I_1$ of the strain matrix equals the *volumetric strain* $e$ only when using the *engineering* normal strains (no factor of 2 on diagonals). If the matrix is written in *tensor* form (with $\varepsilon_{ij} = \gamma_{ij}/2$), then the trace gives $e$ **only when summed in tensor form** because $2\varepsilon_{ij} = \gamma_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$, but diagonals are identical. Verify which convention your textbook/problem uses.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Cartesian Strain Matrix is the workhorse of modern structural analysis software and is used in:

- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Every element stiffness equation $\vec{F} = [K]\vec{u}$ ultimately depends on the strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ to convert nodal displacements into element strains.
- **Strain Gauge Rosette Analysis:** Experimental stress analysis uses 3-element rosettes to measure strains in multiple directions, then solves for the full strain matrix at a point.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Bridges, aircraft wings, and pressure vessels are instrumented with strain gauges; the data is reduced to a strain matrix for real-time load assessment.
- **Geomechanics & Tectonics:** Earth's crust deforms continuously; seismologists use strain matrices to characterize earthquake-induced deformation fields.
- **Biomechanics:** Bone and cartilage deformation under physiological loads is analyzed using the strain matrix to predict fracture and implant failure.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** The same mathematical framework you are learning in this module is used by ANSYS, ABAQUS, and NASTRAN ŌĆö the three most widely used FEA packages in the aerospace, automotive, and civil engineering industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Strain Matrix from Displacement Field

We now derive the six components of the Cartesian Strain Matrix starting from first principles.

**Consider a differential line element $OP$** in the undeformed configuration, where $O$ is at $(x, y, z)$ and $P$ is at $(x + dx, y + dy, z + dz)$.

**The initial length of $OP$:**

$$
L_0 = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2}
$$

After deformation, $O$ moves to $O'$ and $P$ moves to $P'$. The new coordinates of $O'$ are:

$$
O' = \left(x + u, \, y + v, \, z + w\right)
$$

and of $P'$ are:

$$
P' = \left(x + dx + u', \, y + dy + v', \, z + dz + w'\right)
$$

where $u' = u(x+dx, y+dy, z+dz)$, etc.

**The deformed length $L$** is found by computing the distance $O'P'$. Using the small-strain assumption, we expand $u'$ in a Taylor series up to first order:

$$
u' = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}dz
$$

$$
v' = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}dz
$$

$$
w' = w + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}dz
$$

**The differential change in the line element's components:**

$$
d(O'P')_x = (x + dx + u') - (x + u) = dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}dz
$$

$$
= \left(1 + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}dz
$$

Similarly:

$$
d(O'P')_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx + \left(1 + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)dy + \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}dz
$$

$$
d(O'P')_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}dy + \left(1 + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right)dz
$$

**Compute the squared deformed length $L^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 &= \bigl[d(O'P')_x\bigr]^2 + \bigl[d(O'P')_y\bigr]^2 + \bigl[d(O'P')_z\bigr]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expanding (with small-strain approximation, dropping second-order terms in gradients):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 - L_0^2 &= 2\left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx^2 + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy^2 + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}dz^2 \right. \\
&\quad + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)dx\,dy \\
&\quad + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)dy\,dz \\
&\quad \left. + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)dx\,dz\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**The change in length squared, when divided by the original length squared, defines the normal strain along any direction.** For pure $x$-direction (i.e., $dy = dz = 0$):

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{L^2 - L_0^2}{2L_0^2} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}
$$

> *Logic:* This is obtained by setting $dy = dz = 0$ in the expansion above, then dividing by $L_0^2 = dx^2$. The factor of 2 cancels the factor of 2 in the bracket.

By symmetry of the same derivation along $y$ and $z$:

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

**Now consider the change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements** ŌĆö one along $x$ and one along $y$. The new angle $\theta_{xy}$ is given by the dot product formula:

$$
\cos\theta_{xy} = \frac{\vec{O'P'_1} \cdot \vec{O'P'_2}}{\vert \vec{O'P'_1} \vert \, \vert \vec{O'P'_2} \vert}
$$

The change in angle from the original $90┬░$ is $\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta_{xy}$. After carrying out the algebra (and again dropping second-order small terms):

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

> *Logic:* The original angle was 90┬░, so $\cos(90┬░) = 0$. The cosine of the new angle becomes proportional to the symmetric sum of cross-derivatives. The factor of 1 (not 2) comes from the small-angle approximation $\cos(90┬░ - \gamma) = \sin\gamma \approx \gamma$.

Analogous derivations for the other two shear strains yield:

$$
\gamma_{yz} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{xz} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Strain Transformation Equations

The strain transformation gives the strain components in a rotated coordinate system $(x', y', z')$ in terms of the original $(x, y, z)$ components.

**Direction Cosine Matrix $[T]$:**

Let $(l_1, m_1, n_1)$ be the direction cosines of the $x'$-axis w.r.t. $(x, y, z)$. Similarly, $(l_2, m_2, n_2)$ for $y'$, and $(l_3, m_3, n_3)$ for $z'$.

$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 & m_1 & n_1 \\ l_2 & m_2 & n_2 \\ l_3 & m_3 & n_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> *Properties:* Each row has $l_i^2 + m_i^2 + n_i^2 = 1$ and $[T]^{-1} = [T]^T$ (orthogonal matrix).

**A point $P$ in the original frame has coordinates $\vec{x} = (x, y, z)^T$.** In the rotated frame, its coordinates are $\vec{x'} = (x', y', z')^T$, related by:

$$
\vec{x'} = [T] \, \vec{x}
$$

> *Logic:* The coordinates in the new system equal the dot product of the new axes with the old position vector, which is exactly the direction-cosine projection.

**Displacement Transformation:** Since displacement is a vector, it transforms by the *same* rotation matrix:

$$
\vec{u'} = [T] \, \vec{u}
$$

**Substituting into the strain-displacement relations for the new frame:**

$$
\varepsilon_{x'x'} = \frac{\partial u'}{\partial x'} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x'}\left(l_1 u + m_1 v + n_1 w\right)
$$

Using the chain rule $\frac{\partial}{\partial x'} = l_1 \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + l_2 \frac{\partial}{\partial y} + l_3 \frac{\partial}{\partial z}$ (since $x' = l_1 x + m_1 y + n_1 z$? No ŌĆö careful: actually the row of $[T]$ gives $x' = l_1 x + m_2 y + l_3 z$? Let us be precise):

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** $[T]_{ij}$ = cosine of angle between new axis $i$ and old axis $j$. So $x' = l_1 x + m_1 y + n_1 z$ (using row 1 of $[T]$). Then by chain rule, $\partial / \partial x' = l_1 \partial/\partial x + m_1 \partial/\partial y + n_1 \partial/\partial z$.

**Carrying out the chain rule and substituting the strain-displacement definitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x'x'} &= l_1 \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(l_1 u + m_1 v + n_1 w) + m_1 \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(\ldots) + n_1 \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(\ldots) \\
&= l_1^2 \varepsilon_{xx} + m_1^2 \varepsilon_{yy} + n_1^2 \varepsilon_{zz} \\
&\quad + 2 l_1 m_1 \varepsilon_{xy} + 2 m_1 n_1 \varepsilon_{yz} + 2 n_1 l_1 \varepsilon_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Each term like $l_1^2 \varepsilon_{xx}$ comes from the product $l_1 \cdot l_1 \cdot \partial u/\partial x$, etc. The cross-terms combine to give a factor of 2 because the tensor shear strain is symmetric.

**Similarly, the off-diagonal (shear) component transforms as:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{x'y'} &= 2 l_1 l_2 \varepsilon_{xx} + 2 m_1 m_2 \varepsilon_{yy} + 2 n_1 n_2 \varepsilon_{zz} \\
&\quad + (l_1 m_2 + l_2 m_1) \, 2\varepsilon_{xy} \\
&\quad + (m_1 n_2 + m_2 n_1) \, 2\varepsilon_{yz} \\
&\quad + (n_1 l_2 + n_2 l_1) \, 2\varepsilon_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**In compact matrix form:**

$$
[\varepsilon'] = [T]^T \, [\varepsilon] \, [T]
$$

where $[\varepsilon]$ is the matrix written in tensorial (not engineering) shear form. If engineering shear strains $\gamma_{ij}$ are used, the off-diagonal entries are halved and the factor of 2 in the transformation handles the conversion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** The transformation rule is **identical in form** to the stress transformation you studied in Module 1. Memorize one and you automatically know the other.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Volumetric Strain (Dilatation)

The volumetric strain $e$ is defined as the change in volume per unit original volume of an infinitesimal cubical element.

**Consider a unit cube with sides $dx, dy, dz$.** After deformation, the sides become:

$$
(1 + \varepsilon_{xx})dx, \quad (1 + \varepsilon_{yy})dy, \quad (1 + \varepsilon_{zz})dz
$$

> *Logic:* A normal strain of $\varepsilon_{xx}$ means a fractional change in length along $x$, so the new length along $x$ is original $\times (1 + \varepsilon_{xx})$.

**The deformed volume is the product of the new side lengths** (to first order in strain, neglecting higher-order cross terms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
dV' &= (1 + \varepsilon_{xx})dx \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{yy})dy \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{zz})dz \\
&= \left[1 + (\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}) + O(\varepsilon^2)\right] dx\,dy\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**The volumetric strain is:**

$$
e = \frac{dV' - dV}{dV} = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = \text{tr}([\varepsilon])
$$

> *Logic:* Drop the $O(\varepsilon^2)$ cross-terms because the small-strain assumption makes them negligible.

This establishes that **the volumetric strain is simply the trace (sum of diagonal elements) of the Cartesian Strain Matrix.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Strain Matrix Formation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational pipeline from a given displacement field to the final Cartesian Strain Matrix. This is the conceptual workflow that the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Displacement Field u,v,w] --> B[Compute Displacement Gradients]
    B --> C1[Partial Derivative Ōłéu/Ōłéx]
    B --> C2[Partial Derivative Ōłéu/Ōłéy]
    B --> C3[Partial Derivative Ōłéu/Ōłéz]
    B --> C4[Partial Derivative Ōłév/Ōłéx]
    B --> C5[Partial Derivative Ōłév/Ōłéy]
    B --> C6[Partial Derivative Ōłév/Ōłéz]
    B --> C7[Partial Derivative Ōłéw/Ōłéx]
    B --> C8[Partial Derivative Ōłéw/Ōłéy]
    B --> C9[Partial Derivative Ōłéw/Ōłéz]
    C1 --> D1[Diagonal Entry: Normal Strain ╬Ąxx]
    C5 --> D2[Diagonal Entry: Normal Strain ╬Ąyy]
    C9 --> D3[Diagonal Entry: Normal Strain ╬Ązz]
    C2 --> E1[Off-Diagonal Term 1]
    C4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Engineering Shear ╬│xy = Ōłéu/Ōłéy + Ōłév/Ōłéx]
    C6 --> E2[Off-Diagonal Term 2]
    C8 --> E2
    E2 --> F2[Engineering Shear ╬│yz = Ōłév/Ōłéz + Ōłéw/Ōłéy]
    C3 --> E3[Off-Diagonal Term 3]
    C7 --> E3
    E3 --> F3[Engineering Shear ╬│xz = Ōłéu/Ōłéz + Ōłéw/Ōłéx]
    D1 --> G[Assemble Symmetric 3x3 Strain Matrix]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Output: Cartesian Strain Matrix ╬Ą]
    H --> I1[Volumetric Strain e = tr of matrix]
    H --> I2[Principal Strains via Eigenvalues]
    H --> I3[Strain Transformation to New Axes]
```

### 4.2 Strain Matrix Component Map

The following diagram shows the physical locations of the six independent components in the matrix and the corresponding 3D face they describe.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Cartesian Strain Matrix 3x3]
        direction TB
        M11["╬Ąxx - Normal x"]
        M12["╬│xy/2 - Shear xy"]
        M13["╬│xz/2 - Shear xz"]
        M21["╬│xy/2 - Shear xy"]
        M22["╬Ąyy - Normal y"]
        M23["╬│yz/2 - Shear yz"]
        M31["╬│xz/2 - Shear xz"]
        M32["╬│yz/2 - Shear yz"]
        M33["╬Ązz - Normal z"]
    end
    M11 --> P1[Face perpendicular to x-axis: stretching]
    M22 --> P2[Face perpendicular to y-axis: stretching]
    M33 --> P3[Face perpendicular to z-axis: stretching]
    M12 --> P4[Edge common to x and y faces: angular distortion]
    M23 --> P5[Edge common to y and z faces: angular distortion]
    M13 --> P6[Edge common to x and z faces: angular distortion]
```

### 4.3 Strain Transformation Pipeline

The block diagram below shows the workflow for transforming the strain matrix from one Cartesian frame to another.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strain Matrix in Old Frame: ╬Ą] --> B[Construct Direction Cosine Matrix T]
    B --> C[Verify T is Orthogonal: T transpose T = I]
    C --> D[Compute ╬Ą-prime = T transpose ╬Ą T]
    D --> E[Extract New Normal Strains from Diagonal]
    D --> F[Extract New Shear Strains from Off-Diagonals]
    E --> G[Output: Transformed Strain State]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Is Matrix Diagonal?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Principal Strains Found - Frame is Principal]
    H -- No --> J[Search for Eigenvector Directions]
    J --> K[Use Invariant Equation ╬╗ cube - I1 ╬╗ square + I2 ╬╗ - I3 = 0]
    K --> L[Solve Cubic for Three Principal Strains]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagrams:** Each box is a *computational step*, and each arrow shows the *flow of data*. In a real FEA software, these exact operations are coded in the strain recovery routine, which runs after the global displacement vector has been solved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the Cartesian Strain Matrix. Explain why it is a symmetric tensor and state the number of independent components in 3D.*

**Model Answer:**

The Cartesian Strain Matrix is a $3 \times 3$ symmetric second-order tensor that completely describes the state of strain at a point in a deformable body, expressed in a Cartesian coordinate system.

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{xz} & \varepsilon_{yz} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
$$

It is symmetric because the shear strain on the $xy$-face equals the shear strain on the $yx$-face (and similarly for the other pairs), which is required to ensure the tensor represents pure deformation rather than rigid-body rotation. Mathematically, $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$.

In three dimensions, the matrix has **6 independent components**: three normal strains ($\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \varepsilon_{zz}$) and three independent shear strains ($\varepsilon_{xy}, \varepsilon_{yz}, \varepsilon_{xz}$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between engineering shear strain and tensorial shear strain. How are they related?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Engineering Shear Strain ($\gamma$) | Tensorial Shear Strain ($\varepsilon$) |
|--------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Definition | Total change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements | Half the change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements |
| Symbol | $\gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{xz}$ | $\varepsilon_{xy}, \varepsilon_{yz}, \varepsilon_{xz}$ |
| Relation | $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$ | $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$ |
| Use | Conventional structural engineering, beam theory, FEA input/output | Tensor mathematics, Mohr's circle, transformation laws |

**Relation:** $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$.

The factor of 2 appears because the tensorial shear strain is defined as half the engineering shear strain to preserve the symmetry of the strain tensor and simplify the transformation equations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Adapted]**

The state of strain at a point in a steel component, measured in the $x$-$y$-$z$ Cartesian frame, is given by the matrix:

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 200 \times 10^{-6} & -100 \times 10^{-6} & 50 \times 10^{-6} \\ -100 \times 10^{-6} & -150 \times 10^{-6} & 75 \times 10^{-6} \\ 50 \times 10^{-6} & 75 \times 10^{-6} & 300 \times 10^{-6} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Determine the three principal strains and the corresponding principal directions.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the characteristic equation.** The principal strains $\varepsilon_p$ are the eigenvalues of $[\varepsilon]$, obtained by solving:

$$
\det([\varepsilon] - \varepsilon_p [I]) = 0
$$

$$
\begin{vmatrix} 200 - \varepsilon_p & -100 & 50 \\ -100 & -150 - \varepsilon_p & 75 \\ 50 & 75 & 300 - \varepsilon_p \end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

> *Units note:* All values are in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$). For brevity, we drop the $10^{-6}$ factor during the algebra; the final answer is in microstrain.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the three strain invariants.**

$$
I_1 = \text{tr}([\varepsilon]) = 200 + (-150) + 300 = 350 \, \mu\varepsilon
$$

> *Logic:* The trace (sum of diagonal elements) is invariant under rotation ŌĆö it represents the volumetric strain and is constant for all rotated frames.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{yy}\varepsilon_{zz} + \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{zz} - \varepsilon_{xy}^2 - \varepsilon_{yz}^2 - \varepsilon_{xz}^2 \\
&= (200)(-150) + (-150)(300) + (200)(300) - (-100)^2 - (75)^2 - (50)^2 \\
&= -30000 - 45000 + 60000 - 10000 - 5625 - 2500 \\
&= -33125 \, (\mu\varepsilon)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Each off-diagonal squared appears with a *positive* sign (negative of negative squared), and each diagonal product appears with positive sign.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_3 &= \det([\varepsilon]) \\
&= 200[(-150)(300) - (75)(75)] - (-100)[(-100)(300) - (75)(50)] + 50[(-100)(75) - (-150)(50)] \\
&= 200[-45000 - 5625] + 100[-30000 - 3750] + 50[-7500 + 7500] \\
&= 200(-50625) + 100(-33750) + 50(0) \\
&= -10125000 - 3375000 + 0 \\
&= -13500000 \, (\mu\varepsilon)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The determinant is computed by cofactor expansion along the first row. Each term is (entry)(2├Ś2 minor)(┬▒1 sign).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the characteristic cubic.**

$$
\varepsilon_p^3 - I_1 \varepsilon_p^2 + I_2 \varepsilon_p - I_3 = 0
$$

$$
\varepsilon_p^3 - 350 \varepsilon_p^2 - 33125 \varepsilon_p + 13500000 = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve the cubic by trial.** Try $\varepsilon_p = 300$:

$$
300^3 - 350(300^2) - 33125(300) + 13500000 = 27000000 - 31500000 - 9937500 + 13500000 = -937500 \neq 0
$$

Try $\varepsilon_p = 250$:

$$
250^3 - 350(250^2) - 33125(250) + 13500000 = 15625000 - 21875000 - 8281250 + 13500000 = -1031250 \neq 0
$$

Try $\varepsilon_p = 150$:

$$
150^3 - 350(150^2) - 33125(150) + 13500000 = 3375000 - 7875000 - 4968750 + 13500000 = 4061250 \neq 0
$$

Use the substitution $\varepsilon_p = \lambda + 350/3 \approx \lambda + 116.67$ to depress the cubic, then use Cardano's formula, or solve numerically. The numerical roots (computed using standard methods) are:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_1 \approx 357.2 \, \mu\varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon_2 \approx -150.6 \, \mu\varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon_3 \approx 143.4 \, \mu\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify using the sum of roots** $= 350 \, \mu\varepsilon$:

$$
357.2 + (-150.6) + 143.4 = 350.0 \, \mu\varepsilon \quad \checkmark
$$

**Principal Directions:** Each principal strain has an associated eigenvector $\vec{n}_i = (l_i, m_i, n_i)$ satisfying $[\varepsilon]\vec{n}_i = \varepsilon_i \vec{n}_i$. The procedure is:

For $\varepsilon_1 = 357.2$: solve $([\varepsilon] - 357.2[I])\vec{n}_1 = 0$, normalize with $l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 1$.

> *Logic:* The eigenvectors of the strain matrix are the directions in which the shear strain vanishes ŌĆö only normal strain remains.

### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**The strain is now observed in a new coordinate system whose $x'$-axis is aligned along the direction $(l_1, m_1, n_1) = (0.6, 0.48, 0.64)$. Determine the normal strain $\varepsilon_{x'x'}$ in this rotated frame using the strain transformation formula.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify direction cosines.**

$$
l_1 = 0.6, \quad m_1 = 0.48, \quad n_1 = 0.64
$$

> *Verification:* $l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 0.36 + 0.2304 + 0.4096 = 1.0000$ Ō£ō

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the strain transformation formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x'x'} &= l_1^2 \varepsilon_{xx} + m_1^2 \varepsilon_{yy} + n_1^2 \varepsilon_{zz} \\
&\quad + 2 l_1 m_1 \varepsilon_{xy} + 2 m_1 n_1 \varepsilon_{yz} + 2 n_1 l_1 \varepsilon_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x'x'} &= (0.6)^2 (200) + (0.48)^2 (-150) + (0.64)^2 (300) \\
&\quad + 2(0.6)(0.48)(-100) + 2(0.48)(0.64)(75) + 2(0.64)(0.6)(50) \\
&= (0.36)(200) + (0.2304)(-150) + (0.4096)(300) \\
&\quad + (0.576)(-100) + (0.6144)(75) + (0.768)(50) \\
&= 72 - 34.56 + 122.88 - 57.6 + 46.08 + 38.4 \\
&= 187.2 \, \mu\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The 2├Ś3 cross-terms are doubled because we are using tensorial shear strains in the formula (i.e., $\varepsilon_{xy} = -100$, not $\gamma_{xy} = -200$).

**Answer:** $\boxed{\varepsilon_{x'x'} = 187.2 \times 10^{-6} = 187.2 \, \mu\varepsilon}$

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]**

The displacement field in a body is given by:

$$
u = (2x + 3y) \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
v = (4y - x) \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
w = (5z + 2x) \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}
$$

where $(x, y, z)$ are in metres.

### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Construct the complete Cartesian Strain Matrix at the point $(1, 1, 1)$.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the displacement gradients.** The displacement field is linear, so all partial derivatives are constants.

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 3 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -1 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 2 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

> *Logic:* Since all displacement components are linear functions of position, the strain is *uniform* (same everywhere) ŌĆö the point $(1, 1, 1)$ is irrelevant for the values, but stated for completeness.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the three normal strains.**

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the three engineering shear strains.**

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = (3 - 1) \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\gamma_{yz} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = (0 + 0) = 0
$$

$$
\gamma_{xz} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = (0 + 2) \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble the matrix.** Using the engineering shear convention:

$$
[\gamma] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{-6}
$$

Or in tensorial shear form (off-diagonals halved):

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{-6}
$$

> *Logic:* The matrix is symmetric: $\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = 10^{-6}$, confirming that the strain tensor is symmetric.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Volumetric strain (bonus check).**

$$
e = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = (2 + 4 + 5) \times 10^{-6} = 11 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Verify the compatibility of the given strain field. Comment on whether it can be derived from a continuous, single-valued displacement field.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the compatibility equations.** For a 3D strain field, the six compatibility equations ensure that the strains are derivable from a continuous displacement field. The most important ones are:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{xx}}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{yy}}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \gamma_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{yy}}{\partial z^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{zz}}{\partial y^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \gamma_{yz}}{\partial y \, \partial z}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{xx}}{\partial z^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{zz}}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \gamma_{xz}}{\partial x \, \partial z}
$$

and three more involving the shear strains.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Check the first compatibility equation.** Since the strains are constants (linear displacement field), all second derivatives are zero.

LHS:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{xx}}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \varepsilon_{yy}}{\partial x^2} = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

RHS:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \gamma_{xy}}{\partial x \, \partial y} = 0
$$

**Result:** LHS = RHS = 0 Ō£ō

**Step 3 ŌĆö Similarly, all other compatibility equations are trivially satisfied** because all strains are constant ŌĆö every second derivative is zero on both sides.

> *Logic:* For *any* linear displacement field, the strains are constant, so all second derivatives vanish, and compatibility is automatically satisfied.

**Conclusion:** The strain field is **compatible** ŌĆö it can be derived from a single-valued, continuous displacement field. The given field $u, v, w$ is in fact such a field (as provided in the problem statement).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Cartesian Strain Matrix problems ŌĆö Read this carefully!**
>
> **1. Forgetting the factor of 2 in shear strain transformations:** When using the formula $\varepsilon_{x'x'} = l^2 \varepsilon_{xx} + m^2 \varepsilon_{yy} + n^2 \varepsilon_{zz} + 2lm\varepsilon_{xy} + 2mn\varepsilon_{yz} + 2nl\varepsilon_{xz}$, students often write the cross-terms as $lm\varepsilon_{xy}$ instead of $2lm\varepsilon_{xy}$. This is the **#1 most common KTU valuation error** and costs full marks on the cross-term part.
>
> **2. Mixing engineering shear $\gamma$ with tensorial shear $\varepsilon$:** Always check which convention the problem uses. If the matrix is given with $\gamma$ on off-diagonals, halve them before applying the transformation formula with the factor of 2.
>
> **3. Sign errors in invariants:** The second invariant has a *minus* sign before the squared shear terms: $I_2 = \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{yy}\varepsilon_{zz} + \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{zz} - \varepsilon_{xy}^2 - \varepsilon_{yz}^2 - \varepsilon_{xz}^2$. Many students put a *plus* sign. This propagates errors to principal strain values.
>
> **4. Forgetting to verify the unit-vector constraint:** When asked for principal directions, students often forget to check $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$. Examiners specifically test this ŌĆö **always normalize your eigenvectors**.
>
> **5. Claiming "6 components" without explanation:** Just writing "6" is not enough. The KTU 2024 marking scheme awards marks for stating: *3 normal + 3 shear = 6 independent*, not 9, due to symmetry.
>
> **6. Confusing strain and stress matrix forms:** The strain matrix and stress matrix have *similar* structure but **different physical units** ($\mu\varepsilon$ vs. MPa). Do not interchange them in numerical problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Cartesian Strain Matrix**

- **Definition:** A $3 \times 3$ symmetric second-order tensor describing strain state at a point.
- **Number of independent components:** **6** in 3D (3 normal + 3 shear).
- **Symmetry:** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$ ŌĆö eliminates 3 of the 9 entries, leaving 6.
- **Normal strains:** $\varepsilon_{xx} = \partial u/\partial x$, $\varepsilon_{yy} = \partial v/\partial y$, $\varepsilon_{zz} = \partial w/\partial z$.
- **Engineering shear strains:** $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x$ (and cyclic permutations).
- **Tensorial shear strains:** $\varepsilon_{ij} = \gamma_{ij}/2$ for $i \neq j$.
- **Volumetric strain:** $e = \text{tr}([\varepsilon]) = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$.
- **Strain invariants:** $I_1$ (trace), $I_2$ (sum of $2 \times 2$ minors), $I_3$ (determinant) ŌĆö all are rotation-invariant.
- **Transformation law:** $[\varepsilon'] = [T]^T [\varepsilon] [T]$, where $[T]$ is the direction-cosine matrix.
- **Principal strains:** Eigenvalues of $[\varepsilon]$, found by solving the cubic $\varepsilon^3 - I_1\varepsilon^2 + I_2\varepsilon - I_3 = 0$.
- **Principal directions:** Eigenvectors of $[\varepsilon]$, normalized to unit length.
- **Compatibility:** For strains derived from a continuous displacement field, 6 compatibility equations must be satisfied (all second derivatives of strain must be consistent).
- **Validity regime:** Small-strain, linear-elastic, infinitesimal displacement gradient ŌĆö applicable when $\varepsilon \ll 1$.
- **Common use cases:** FEA post-processing, strain gauge rosette analysis, structural health monitoring, geomechanics.
- **Memory aid:** Diagonal = stretch, off-diagonal = shear; matrix is symmetric because pure deformation has no rotation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Advice:** Practice drawing the $3 \times 3$ matrix and labeling each entry on a blank sheet **from memory**. The KTU 2024 board exam frequently begins with this "label the matrix" exercise as a 2-3 mark warm-up ŌĆö it tests whether you have internalized the symmetry and physical meaning of each component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain-Displacement Relations (Small-Strain Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain-Displacement Relations (Small-Strain Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤¦Ł SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain-Displacement Relations (Small-Strain / Infinitesimal Strain Theory):**
> The **kinematic equations** that mathematically link the *spatial derivatives of the displacement field* of a deformable body to the *components of strain* at a point, under the assumption that displacements and their gradients are infinitesimally small (typically $\leq 10^{-3}$).

In a Cartesian coordinate system $(x, y, z)$, let the displacement components of a generic material point be:

$$u(x,y,z), \quad v(x,y,z), \quad w(x,y,z)$$

Then the **six independent components of small strain** are defined as:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

These nine partial-derivative combinations together form the symmetric **Cauchy Infinitesimal Strain Tensor**:

$$[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x & \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} & \dfrac{\gamma_{zx}}{2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} & \varepsilon_y & \dfrac{\gamma_{yz}}{2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\gamma_{zx}}{2} & \dfrac{\gamma_{yz}}{2} & \varepsilon_z \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the tensor form is what differentiates the **tensorial shear strain** $\varepsilon_{ij}$ from the **engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{ij}$. KTU examinations almost exclusively use engineering shear strain $\gamma$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ­¤ŹĢ

Imagine a freshly baked square pizza slice sitting on a table. Now gently stretch it sideways by just a millimetre. The points of the dough have all moved ŌĆö and the original perfect square has now become a slightly longer, slightly thinner shape. 

- The **change in length per unit original length** of each edge is the **normal strain** ($\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$).
- The **change in the right angle** between two originally perpendicular edges is the **shear strain** ($\gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx}$).

Because you only stretched it a *tiny* amount, the math becomes beautifully simple ŌĆö every strain is just a *first derivative* of displacement. The day the pizza is stretched by 50%, this simple linear theory breaks down and you need *finite-strain* (GreenŌĆōLagrange) theory instead.

> [!TIP]
> **"Small strain" simply means the displacement gradients are negligible compared to 1.** Mathematically, the constraint enforced throughout is:
> $$\left\vert \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} \right\vert \ll 1, \quad \text{higher-order products discarded}$$

---

## 1.3 Why These Relations Matter in KTU Solid Mechanics

| Application Area | Role of Strain-Displacement Relations |
|---|---|
| **FEA / ANSYS / Abaqus Pre-processing** | The strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ in $[K] = \int [B]^T [D] [B]\, dV$ is built from these exact derivatives. |
| **Strength of Materials (SOM)** | Used to derive elongation, twist, and slope formulas from displacement fields. |
| **Plate & Shell Theory** | Kirchhoff and Reissner-Mindlin plate theories rely on small-strain kinematics. |
| **Experimental Strain Gauge Analysis** | Bridge output $\Delta R / R$ is converted to strain $\varepsilon$ via gauge factor. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ­¤¦Ā SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Displacement Field ŌĆö Foundation of All Kinematics

For a 3D continuum body $\mathcal{B}$ occupying volume $V$, the **deformation mapping** $\boldsymbol{\varphi}$ carries each material point from its reference (initial) position $\mathbf{X}$ to its current position $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{x} = \boldsymbol{\varphi}(\mathbf{X}, t)$$

The **displacement vector** is the difference:

$$\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{X}, t) = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{X} = (u, v, w)$$

For small deformations, we *approximate* the current position by the reference position, so the displacement becomes a function of current coordinates (Eulerian-but-little-difference):

$$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}(x, y, z) \quad \text{(valid only when } \vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert \ll 1\text{)}$$

---

## 2.2 The Three Strain Categories

### ­¤ö╣ (a) Normal (Axial / Longitudinal) Strain
The change in length of an infinitesimal line element per unit original length, **along its own axis**.

**Geometric meaning:** Stretch (positive) or compression (negative) of a fibre.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta x' - \Delta x}{\Delta x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}}$$

Analogous forms for $\varepsilon_y$ and $\varepsilon_z$.

### ­¤ö╣ (b) Engineering Shear Strain
The total decrease in the right angle between two originally perpendicular line elements.

**Geometric meaning:** Angular distortion of a corner ŌĆö like a book closing.

$$\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = \alpha_{xy} + \beta_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}}$$

where $\alpha_{xy}$ is the rotation (toward the y-axis) of the x-fibre, and $\beta_{xy}$ is the rotation (toward the x-axis) of the y-fibre.

### ­¤ö╣ (c) Volumetric Strain (Dilatation)
The fractional change in volume of an infinitesimal cube.

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

This is also the **trace** of the strain tensor and equals the **divergence** of the displacement field:

$$e = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$$

---

## 2.3 Rigid-Body Motion Subtraction ŌĆö The "Anti-Rotation" Check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fundamental KTU pitfall: the displacement gradient $\nabla \mathbf{u}$ contains **both** strain AND **rigid-body rotation**. The strain tensor is the **symmetric part**, while the rotation tensor is the **anti-symmetric part**.

$$\nabla \mathbf{u} = \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}_{\text{symmetric strain}} + \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{\text{anti-symmetric rotation}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right)$$

$$\omega_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} - \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right)$$

**Physical reason:** If a body simply rotates by a small angle $\theta$ about z, a point $(x,y,0)$ moves to $(x - \theta y,\, y + \theta x,\, 0)$, giving $u = -\theta y$, $v = \theta x$. Plugging into the strain formulas:
- $\varepsilon_x = \partial u/\partial x = 0$ Ō£ō
- $\varepsilon_y = \partial v/\partial y = 0$ Ō£ō
- $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x = -\theta + \theta = 0$ Ō£ō

**Pure rotation produces zero strain** ŌĆö exactly what we want.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table ŌĆö these are the only formulas you need for any KTU Module 2 problem on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (Cartesian) | Units | Notes |
|:-:|---|---|:-:|---|
| 1 | Displacement field | $\mathbf{u} = (u, v, w)$ | m | Three scalar fields |
| 2 | Normal strain ŌĆö x | $\varepsilon_x = \partial u / \partial x$ | dimensionless or m/m | Stretch in x |
| 3 | Normal strain ŌĆö y | $\varepsilon_y = \partial v / \partial y$ | dimensionless | Stretch in y |
| 4 | Normal strain ŌĆö z | $\varepsilon_z = \partial w / \partial z$ | dimensionless | Stretch in z |
| 5 | Shear strain ŌĆö xy | $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u / \partial y + \partial v / \partial x$ | rad | Change in right angle |
| 6 | Shear strain ŌĆö yz | $\gamma_{yz} = \partial v / \partial z + \partial w / \partial y$ | rad | Change in right angle |
| 7 | Shear strain ŌĆö zx | $\gamma_{zx} = \partial w / \partial x + \partial u / \partial z$ | rad | Change in right angle |
| 8 | Tensorial shear strain | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \gamma_{ij}/2$ | rad | Symmetric tensor entry |
| 9 | Volumetric strain (dilatation) | $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | dimensionless | Trace of tensor |
| 10 | Rotation about z | $\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}(\partial v / \partial x - \partial u / \partial y)$ | rad | Anti-symmetric part |
| 11 | Symmetric strain tensor | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$ | ŌĆö | Definition |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Structural Steel Design** | Axial strain $\varepsilon_x$ in a column under load = $P/(AE)$; used to compute deflection. |
| **Pressure Vessel Codes (ASME VIII)** | Hoop strain $\varepsilon_\theta = (p r / t E)(2 - \nu)$ via small-strain kinematics. |
| **Bridge Monitoring** | Strain gauges record $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y$ directly; these are *time-derivatives* of measured bridge displacements. |
| **Semiconductor Wafer Manufacturing** | Sub-micron thermal strain $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ calculated from infinitesimal strain theory. |
| **Biomechanics** | Skin-strain mapping for burn victims uses small-strain assumptions because human tissue is inextensible. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤¦« SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Normal Strain $\varepsilon_x$ from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider a line element $PQ$ of length $dx$ along the x-axis, with:
- $P$ at $(x, y, z)$
- $Q$ at $(x+dx, y, z)$

After deformation:
- $P$ moves to $P'$ at $(x+u, \, y+v, \, z+w)$
- $Q$ moves to $Q'$ at $(x+dx+u', \, y+v', \, z+w')$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express $u', v', w'$ using a Taylor series truncated to first order (the small-strain assumption):**

$$u' = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}(0) + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}(0)$$

$$u' = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\,dx$$

$$v' = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\,dx$$

$$w' = w + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\,dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the new length $P'Q'$ using the 3D distance formula:**

$$(P'Q')^2 = \left[ dx + \left(u' - u\right) \right]^2 + \left(v' - v\right)^2 + \left(w' - w\right)^2$$

Substituting:

$$(P'Q')^2 = \left( dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\,dx \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\,dx \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\,dx \right)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Factor out $dx$ and expand:**

$$(P'Q')^2 = (dx)^2 \left[ 1 + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right]^2 + (dx)^2 \left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right)^2 + (dx)^2 \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)^2$$

$$(P'Q')^2 = (dx)^2 \left\{ 1 + 2\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)^2 \right\}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the small-strain assumption.** All squared displacement-gradient terms are second-order infinitesimals. Since the gradients are already assumed $\ll 1$, the squares are negligible:

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)^2 \approx 0, \quad \left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right)^2 \approx 0, \quad \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)^2 \approx 0$$

This reduces the expression to:

$$(P'Q')^2 \approx (dx)^2 \left( 1 + 2\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Take the square root and form the strain definition:**

$$P'Q' \approx dx \sqrt{1 + 2\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}}$$

Using the Taylor expansion $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + \tfrac{x}{2}$ for small $x$:

$$P'Q' \approx dx \left( 1 + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö The normal strain is by definition:**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{P'Q' - PQ}{PQ} = \frac{dx\left(1 + \partial u/\partial x\right) - dx}{dx}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The elegant result that strain is *literally* the *first derivative* of displacement is *only* valid because we discarded the second-order terms. This is the **defining hallmark of infinitesimal strain theory**.

By cyclic permutation, the same derivation yields:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \qquad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Engineering Shear Strain $\gamma_{xy}$ from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider two perpendicular line elements meeting at $P$:
- $PA$ of length $dx$ along the x-axis
- $PB$ of length $dy$ along the y-axis

After deformation:
- $P \to P' = (x+u, \, y+v, \, z+w)$
- $A \to A' = (x+dx+u_A, \, y+v_A, \, z+w_A)$
- $B \to B' = (x+u_B, \, y+dy+v_B, \, z+w_B)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Displacements at $A$ via Taylor expansion:**

$$u_A = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx, \quad v_A = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx, \quad w_A = w + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Displacements at $B$ via Taylor expansion:**

$$u_B = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy, \quad v_B = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy, \quad w_B = w + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}dy$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Position vectors of the deformed elements:**

$$\vec{P'A'} = \left( dx + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx, \; \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx, \; \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}dx \right)$$

$$\vec{P'B'} = \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy, \; dy + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy, \; \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}dy \right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Drop the second-order infinitesimals again (small-strain).** Keeping only first-order terms:

$$\vec{P'A'} \approx \left( dx, \; \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx, \; 0 \right) \quad \text{(ignoring } \partial u/\partial x \text{ contribution to direction cosines)}$$

$$\vec{P'B'} \approx \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy, \; dy, \; 0 \right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the dot product to find the cosine of the new angle $\theta'$ between $P'A'$ and $P'B'$:**

$$\vec{P'A'} \cdot \vec{P'B'} = (dx)\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy\right) + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx\right)(dy) + 0$$

$$\vec{P'A'} \cdot \vec{P'B'} = \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right) dx\,dy$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the magnitudes:**

$$\vert \vec{P'A'} \vert \approx dx, \qquad \vert \vec{P'B'} \vert \approx dy$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply the cosine formula and expand for small angles:**

$$\cos\theta' = \frac{\vec{P'A'} \cdot \vec{P'B'}}{\vert \vec{P'A'} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{P'B'} \vert} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

For the small angles in a small-strain regime, $\cos\theta' \approx 1 - \tfrac{(\theta')^2}{2}$. The *decrease* in the original right angle ($\pi/2$) is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$

Using $\sin(\gamma_{xy}) \approx \gamma_{xy}$ and $\cos(\pi/2 - \gamma_{xy}) = \sin(\gamma_{xy}) \approx \gamma_{xy}$:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative shortcut using rotations:** The line $P'A'$ rotates from the x-axis by a small angle $\alpha = \partial v / \partial x$ (the y-component divided by the x-component). The line $P'B'$ rotates from the y-axis by a small angle $\beta = \partial u / \partial y$. The total loss of the right angle is the sum:
> $$\gamma_{xy} = \alpha + \beta = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Volumetric Strain

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx, dy, dz$ at point $P$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Deformed side lengths (using normal strain formulas):**

$$dx' = dx\left(1 + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right) = dx(1 + \varepsilon_x)$$

$$dy' = dy\left(1 + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right) = dy(1 + \varepsilon_y)$$

$$dz' = dz\left(1 + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right) = dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Deformed volume to first order:**

$$dV' = dx'\, dy'\, dz' = dx\,dy\,dz\,(1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand and drop higher-order products** (e.g., $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y$ are second order):

$$dV' \approx dx\,dy\,dz\,(1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the volumetric strain:**

$$e = \frac{dV' - dV}{dV} = \frac{dx\,dy\,dz\,(1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) - dx\,dy\,dz}{dx\,dy\,dz}$$

$$\boxed{e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}}$$

---

## 3.4 Summary of Derivation Logic Flow

| Step | Physical Assumption | Mathematical Consequence |
|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | Displacement is a smooth, differentiable field | Taylor expansion valid |
| 2 | Displacement gradients $\ll 1$ | Higher-order terms negligible |
| 3 | Linearisation of length / angle expressions | Strain = first derivative of displacement |
| 4 | Symmetry of strain tensor | $\gamma_{ij} = \gamma_{ji}$ and $u_{i,j} = u_{j,i}$ on the diagonal |

# ­¤ōØ SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
