## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define normal strain and shear strain at a point in a deformable body. Write the displacement field representation and the six strain components in Cartesian coordinates for small-strain theory.**

#### ­¤¤ó Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition ŌĆö Normal Strain (1 Mark):**
Normal strain $\varepsilon_n$ along a direction is the ratio of the change in length of an infinitesimal line element to its original length, in the limit as the length approaches zero. For the x-direction:

$$\varepsilon_x = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta x' - \Delta x}{\Delta x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

**Definition ŌĆö Shear Strain (1 Mark):**
Engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is the total decrease in the right angle between two originally perpendicular line elements, lying in the xy-plane, meeting at a point:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

**Six Strain Components (1 Mark):**
With displacement field $\mathbf{u} = (u, v, w)$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of volumetric strain (dilatation). How is it expressed in terms of the displacement field for small deformations?**

#### ­¤¤ó Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Volumetric strain $e$ (also called dilatation or cubic dilatation) is defined as the ratio of the change in volume of an infinitesimal material element to its original volume:

$$e = \lim_{dV \to 0} \frac{dV' - dV}{dV}$$

**Derivation via Side Lengths (1 Mark):**
For an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx, dy, dz$, after deformation the new sides are $dx(1+\partial u/\partial x)$, $dy(1+\partial v/\partial y)$, $dz(1+\partial w/\partial z)$. The new volume is the product. Expanding to first order and subtracting the original volume:

$$dV' - dV = dx\,dy\,dz\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right)$$

**Final Expression (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{e = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}}$$

Equivalently, $e$ equals the trace of the small-strain tensor:

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$$

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-strain linearisation step** in derivations will cost you **2 marks immediately** ŌĆö KTU examiners look for the explicit statement *"neglecting second-order terms, since displacement gradients are much less than 1."*
> 2. **Confusing engineering shear strain $\gamma$ with tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$**: KTU problems always want $\gamma$ (the full value), **NOT** $\gamma/2$.
> 3. **Mixing up $\partial v/\partial x$ and $\partial u/\partial y$** in shear-strain formulas is the single most common 1-mark error in this module.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply cyclic permutation** when generalising from x to y and z loses a mark.

---

### **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) Derive the expression for normal strain $\varepsilon_x$ along the x-direction from first principles using the small-strain assumption.** (7 Marks)

**(b) The displacement field in a 2D body is given by $u = 0.001\,x + 0.002\,y$, $v = 0.003\,x - 0.001\,y$ (units in mm, with x and y in mm). Compute all strain components at the point (100, 50).** (7 Marks)

#### ­¤¤ó Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of $\varepsilon_x$ (7 Marks):**

*Refer to Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. The model answer must include:*

1. **Diagram/Sketch description** (1 Mark): Line element $PQ$ of length $dx$ along x-axis, with $P$ at $(x,y,z)$ and $Q$ at $(x+dx, y, z)$.

2. **Taylor expansion at $Q$** (2 Marks):
$$u' = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\,dx, \quad v' = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\,dx, \quad w' = w + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\,dx$$

3. **New length calculation** (2 Marks):
$$(P'Q')^2 = (dx)^2\left[1 + 2\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^2\right]$$

4. **Small-strain linearisation + square-root expansion** (1 Mark):
$$P'Q' \approx dx\left(1 + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right) \quad \text{(dropping } \mathcal{O}(\nabla \mathbf{u})^2 \text{ terms)}$$

5. **Final result and cyclic extension** (1 Mark):
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the partial derivatives (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0.001, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0.002$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0.003, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -0.001$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute normal strains (1 Mark):**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0.001 \; (\text{i.e., } 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon)$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -0.001 \; (\text{i.e., } -1000 \, \mu\varepsilon)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute shear strain (1 Mark):**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.005 \; \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute rotation (1 Mark):**

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.003 - 0.002) = 0.0005 \; \text{rad}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute volumetric strain (1 Mark):**

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y = 0.001 + (-0.001) = 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification via direct length change (1 Mark):**
For an element $dx = 1$ mm along x:
$$\Delta x \approx 0.001 \times 1 = 0.001 \text{ mm}$$
$$\varepsilon_x = 0.001/1 = 0.001 \;\; \checkmark$$

#### Ō£ģ Final Answers:
- $\varepsilon_x = 0.001$, $\varepsilon_y = -0.001$
- $\gamma_{xy} = 0.005$ rad
- $\omega_z = 0.0005$ rad
- $e = 0$ (isochoric deformation in the plane)
- Point coordinates $(100, 50)$ are irrelevant for these *constant-gradient* strains, as expected.

---

### **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper ŌĆö Alternative Choice]**
**(a) Derive the engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ at a point in a deformable body using the small-strain kinematic assumption. Clearly show all intermediate steps.** (7 Marks)

**(b) The displacement components in a 3D body are: $u = (3x^2 + 2yz)\times 10^{-6}$, $v = (2y^2 + 3xz)\times 10^{-6}$, $w = (4z^2 + 5xy)\times 10^{-6}$ (all lengths in metres). Determine: (i) the normal strain at point (1, 0, 0), (ii) the shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ at the same point, and (iii) the volumetric strain at the origin.** (7 Marks)

#### ­¤¤ó Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of $\gamma_{xy}$ (7 Marks):**

*Refer to Section 3.2 for the complete derivation. The model answer must include:*

1. **Two perpendicular elements $PA$ and $PB$** (1 Mark): $PA$ along x-axis of length $dx$, $PB$ along y-axis of length $dy$.

2. **Displacements at $A$ and $B$ via Taylor expansion** (2 Marks):
$$u_A = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx, \quad v_A = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx$$
$$u_B = u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy, \quad v_B = v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy$$

3. **Deformed vectors $\vec{P'A'}$ and $\vec{P'B'}$** (1 Mark):
$$\vec{P'A'} = \left(dx\left(1+\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right), \; dx\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}, \; dx\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)$$
$$\vec{P'B'} = \left(dy\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}, \; dy\left(1+\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right), \; dy\frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$

4. **Dot product to find new angle $\theta'$** (1 Mark):
$$\vec{P'A'}\cdot\vec{P'B'} = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)dx\,dy + \mathcal{O}(2)$$

5. **Small-angle cosine expansion and shear-strain definition** (1 Mark):
$$\cos\theta' = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta' \approx \frac{\pi}{2} - \gamma_{xy}$$

6. **Final boxed result** (1 Mark):
$$\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks):**

Given:
$$u = (3x^2 + 2yz) \times 10^{-6}$$
$$v = (2y^2 + 3xz) \times 10^{-6}$$
$$w = (4z^2 + 5xy) \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the nine partial derivatives (3 Marks):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 6x \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2z \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 2y \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 3z \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4y \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 3x \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 5y \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 5x \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 8z \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate at $(1, 0, 0)$ for (i) and (ii) (1 Mark):**

At $(x, y, z) = (1, 0, 0)$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 6 \times 10^{-6}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 2a ŌĆö (i) Normal strain at $(1, 0, 0)$ (1 Mark):**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(1,0,0)} = 6 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 2b ŌĆö (ii) Shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ at $(1, 0, 0)$ (1 Mark):**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö (iii) Volumetric strain at the origin (1 Mark):**

At $(0, 0, 0)$, all partial derivatives containing x, y, or z factors vanish:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

$$e = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$

#### Ō£ģ Final Answers:
- (i) $\varepsilon_x = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ at $(1, 0, 0)$
- (ii) $\gamma_{xy} = 0$ at $(1, 0, 0)$
- (iii) $e = 0$ at the origin (isochoric ŌĆö no volume change)

# ­¤¦Š Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Strain-Displacement Relations (Small Strain)**

### ­¤Ä» Core Results (Memorise These 6 Lines)

- $\varepsilon_x = \partial u / \partial x$
- $\varepsilon_y = \partial v / \partial y$
- $\varepsilon_z = \partial w / \partial z$
- $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u / \partial y + \partial v / \partial x$
- $\gamma_{yz} = \partial v / \partial z + \partial w / \partial y$
- $\gamma_{zx} = \partial w / \partial x + \partial u / \partial z$

### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual High-Yield Points

1. **Small-strain (infinitesimal) assumption** = displacement gradients $\ll 1$ ŌćÆ second-order terms in the derivation are dropped.
2. **Engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{ij}$ is **twice** the tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$.
3. **Rigid-body motion produces zero strain** ŌĆö strain tensor is the *symmetric* part of $\nabla \mathbf{u}$.
4. **Volumetric strain** $e = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})$ ŌĆö measures local volume change.
5. **Cyclic permutation** $x \to y \to z \to x$ with $u \to v \to w \to u$ generalises any formula.
6. **Pure shear vs simple shear**: pure shear has $\varepsilon_x = -\varepsilon_y$ with $\varepsilon_z = 0$ and $e = 0$; simple shear has $\gamma_{xy} \neq 0$ with all normal strains zero.
7. **Plane strain condition** ($\varepsilon_z = 0$, $\gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$) is the standard assumption in 2D problems like long dams and tunnels.

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid

- ŌØī Using $\gamma/2$ instead of $\gamma$ in final answers.
- ŌØī Forgetting the **linearisation step** when deriving strain from a displacement field.
- ŌØī Confusing $\partial v/\partial x$ with $\partial u/\partial y$ in $\gamma_{xy}$.
- ŌØī Treating displacement as a *function of time* in static problems (it isn't).
- ŌØī Reporting strain with **units** ŌĆö it is **dimensionless** (or m/m, sometimes written as $\mu\varepsilon$ for microstrain).

### ­¤öü Bridge to Next Topics

- **Strain transformation** (Module 2 continuation): $2\varepsilon_{ij}$ transforms like stress components, but with a factor of 2.
- **Compatibility equations** (Module 3): 6 PDEs that ensure single-valued continuous displacement field exists.
- **Constitutive law (Hooke's law)** (Module 4): $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\varepsilon_{kl}$ ŌĆö connects these strains to stresses.
- **Plane stress vs plane strain** (Module 4): Specialisations of the 3D relations to thin plates and long prisms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Problems to Find Strain Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems to Find Strain Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Strain Matrix** (also called the **Strain Tensor** in 3D Cartesian coordinates) is a **symmetric second-order tensor** that completely describes the state of deformation at a point in a deformed continuum. For a body point with Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and displacement components $(u, v, w)$, the strain matrix is defined as:

$$[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

where the normal and shear strain components are obtained from the kinematic relations (Cauchy strain-displacement equations for small deformations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis**: The strain matrix is ALWAYS symmetric: $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$. This means it has only **6 independent components** out of 9 total entries. Students often lose marks for incorrectly populating the off-diagonal terms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small **Rubber Cube** placed on a table. Now gently stretch it horizontally, squish it vertically, and slightly shear it sideways (like pushing the top face while holding the bottom). The **Strain Matrix** is like a **3├Ś3 scorecard** that records exactly *how much* and *in which direction* the cube has been deformed at a specific point.

- The **diagonal entries** (top-left, middle, bottom-right) = "How much did the cube stretch or compress along each axis?"
- The **off-diagonal entries** = "How much did the faces of the cube tilt/skew relative to each other?"

Just as a single photograph captures the shape of the deformed cube, the strain matrix captures the *complete deformation state* in a compact mathematical object.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Strain** is a **dimensionless** quantity (length/length).
> - It is typically expressed in **mm/mm**, **m/m**, or **microstrain** ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).
> - In engineering practice, strains often range from **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$**.
> - Shear strains $\gamma_{ij}$ are technically the **engineering shear strains** (twice the tensor shear component: $\gamma_{ij} = 2\varepsilon_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Independent Strain Components ŌĆö Logic Breakdown

For a 3D body undergoing small deformations, the displacement field is given by:
$$\vec{u} = u(x,y,z)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y,z)\,\hat{j} + w(x,y,z)\,\hat{k}$$

**Why these particular derivatives?** Each strain component represents a *change in geometry*. For example:

- **$\varepsilon_{xx}$** = fractional change in length along x-direction ŌåÆ derivative of u with respect to x
- **$\gamma_{xy}$** = total change in angle between originally perpendicular x and y faces ŌåÆ sum of two cross-derivatives

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Identify** the displacement field $u(x,y,z)$, $v(x,y,z)$, $w(x,y,z)$.
2. **Compute three normal strains** by partial differentiation along the same axis:
   - $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$
   - $\varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$
   - $\varepsilon_{zz} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$
3. **Compute three shear strains** by summing cross-partial derivatives:
   - $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$
   - $\gamma_{yz} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y}$
   - $\gamma_{zx} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$
4. **Assemble** the symmetric $3 \times 3$ strain matrix (with off-diagonal terms as half of $\gamma$).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip**: Many KTU students place $\gamma_{xy}$ directly in the off-diagonal. The CORRECT off-diagonal entry is $\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}$. The diagonal-stored strain matrix is the **tensor** form, NOT the engineering form.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Engineering Form (used in KTU boards)** | **Symbol** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Normal strain along x | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \partial u \,/\, \partial x$ | $\varepsilon_{x}$ | dimensionless |
| Normal strain along y | $\varepsilon_{yy} = \partial v \,/\, \partial y$ | $\varepsilon_{y}$ | dimensionless |
| Normal strain along z | $\varepsilon_{zz} = \partial w \,/\, \partial z$ | $\varepsilon_{z}$ | dimensionless |
| Shear strain in xy-plane | $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u \,/\, \partial y + \partial v \,/\, \partial x$ | $\gamma_{xy}$ | rad (dimensionless) |
| Shear strain in yz-plane | $\gamma_{yz} = \partial v \,/\, \partial z + \partial w \,/\, \partial y$ | $\gamma_{yz}$ | rad (dimensionless) |
| Shear strain in zx-plane | $\gamma_{zx} = \partial w \,/\, \partial x + \partial u \,/\, \partial z$ | $\gamma_{zx}$ | rad (dimensionless) |

**Strain Matrix (Tensor Form):**

$$[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} & \dfrac{\gamma_{zx}}{2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \dfrac{\gamma_{yz}}{2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\gamma_{zx}}{2} & \dfrac{\gamma_{yz}}{2} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)**: Strain matrices form the basis of stress-strain transformation in ANSYS, ABAQUS, and COMSOL.
- **Structural Health Monitoring**: Strain gauges measure local strains which are then assembled into a strain matrix for damage assessment.
- **Metal Forming & Forging**: Predicting shape change during rolling/extrusion requires the strain matrix at every material point.
- **Geotechnical Engineering**: Soil deformation analysis uses these tensors to model settlements and slope stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Below are **three canonical KTU-style problems** solved exhaustively. Each follows the **Module 2 standard answer pattern** taught in KTU valuation workshops.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Problem 3.1 ŌĆö Uniform Linear Strain (Foundational Problem)

**Statement:** The displacement field in a body is given by:
$$u(x,y,z) = 0.001\,x, \quad v(x,y,z) = -0.002\,y, \quad w(x,y,z) = 0.0005\,z$$

Find the strain matrix. Comment on the type of deformation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify displacement components** at an arbitrary point $(x,y,z)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(x,y,z) &= 0.001\,x \\
v(x,y,z) &= -0.002\,y \\
w(x,y,z) &= 0.0005\,z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute normal strains:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx} &= \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(0.001\,x) = 0.001 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{yy} &= \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(-0.002\,y) = -0.002 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{zz} &= \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}(0.0005\,z) = 0.0005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute shear strains** (each cross-derivative is constant; sum is zero):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{xy} &= \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0 + 0 = 0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{yz} &= \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0 + 0 = 0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{zx} &= \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble the strain matrix:**

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.001 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.002 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0005 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since all off-diagonal terms vanish, this represents **pure normal strain** (uniaxial/biaxial/triaxial elongation/compression) with NO shear. The body experiences uniform stretching in x and z, and uniform compression in y.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Problem 3.2 ŌĆö Pure Shear Deformation (Module 2 Classic)

**Statement:** A body undergoes simple shear in the x-y plane with displacement field:
$$u = \gamma_0\,y, \quad v = 0, \quad w = 0$$

where $\gamma_0$ is a constant. Determine the strain matrix.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Displacement components:**

$$
u(x,y,z) = \gamma_0\,y, \quad v(x,y,z) = 0, \quad w(x,y,z) = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Normal strains:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx} &= \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(\gamma_0\,y) = 0 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{yy} &= \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(0) = 0 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{zz} &= \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}(0) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Shear strains:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{xy} &= \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \gamma_0 + 0 = \gamma_0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{yz} &= \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0 + 0 = 0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{zx} &= \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Strain matrix:**

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dfrac{\gamma_0}{2} & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{\gamma_0}{2} & 0 & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the textbook case of **pure shear**. The deformation angle equals $\gamma_0$ (in radians). Examples: shear loading in riveted joints, torsion of thin-walled tubes (locally).

---

### ­¤ö╣ Problem 3.3 ŌĆö Mixed Quadratic Displacement (Higher-Order)

**Statement:** The displacement field at a point is given by:
$$u = 2xy^2, \quad v = 3x^2y, \quad w = 0$$

Determine the strain matrix at point $(x=1, y=1, z=0)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute each required partial derivative symbolically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(2xy^2) = 2y^2 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(2xy^2) = 4xy \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2y) = 6xy \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2y) = 3x^2 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} &= 0, \quad \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate each strain component symbolically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx} &= 2y^2 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{yy} &= 3x^2 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{zz} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{xy} &= 4xy + 6xy = 10xy \\[4pt]
\gamma_{yz} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{zx} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate at the point $(1, 1, 0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx} &= 2(1)^2 = 2 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{yy} &= 3(1)^2 = 3 \\[4pt]
\gamma_{xy} &= 10(1)(1) = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the strain matrix at $(1,1,0)$:**

$$
[\varepsilon]_{(1,1,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 0 \\ 5 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Note**: When the problem asks strain "at a point", you MUST substitute coordinates BEFORE writing the final matrix. Students often leave it as a symbolic matrix ŌĆö this loses 1-2 marks.

---

### 3.4 Master Procedure Summary (Follow this order for full marks)

| **Step** | **Action** | **Marks Weight** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Write displacement components $u, v, w$ explicitly | 1 mark |
| 2 | Differentiate to get six strain components | 4 marks |
| 3 | Substitute point coordinates (if asked "at a point") | 2 marks |
| 4 | Write the symmetric $3\times 3$ matrix | 2 marks |
| 5 | Comment on deformation type (pure / shear / mixed) | 1 mark |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flowchart: From Displacement Field ŌåÆ Strain Matrix

This Mermaid block renders the **canonical KTU workflow** for solving strain matrix problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given displacement field u x y z, v x y z, w x y z] --> B[Step 1: Identify u, v, w expressions]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute normal strains]
    C --> C1[eps_xx = partial u over partial x]
    C --> C2[eps_yy = partial v over partial y]
    C --> C3[eps_zz = partial w over partial z]
    C1 --> D[Step 3: Compute shear strains]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[gamma_xy = partial u over partial y plus partial v over partial x]
    D --> D2[gamma_yz = partial v over partial z plus partial w over partial y]
    D --> D3[gamma_zx = partial w over partial x plus partial u over partial z]
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Substitute point coordinates if needed]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Step 5: Assemble symmetric 3x3 strain matrix]
    F --> G[Step 6: Interpret deformation type]
    G --> H[End: Final strain matrix complete]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture of the Strain Matrix

This Mermaid block visualizes the **structural layout** of the symmetric strain matrix and what each region physically represents.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M[3x3 Strain Matrix Structure]
        direction TB
        subgraph DIAG[Diagonal Block: Normal Strains]
            D1[Position 1,1: eps_xx]
            D2[Position 2,2: eps_yy]
            D3[Position 3,3: eps_zz]
        end
        subgraph OFFD[Off-Diagonal Block: Shear Tensor Components]
            O1[Position 1,2 and 2,1: gamma_xy divided by 2]
            O2[Position 2,3 and 3,2: gamma_yz divided by 2]
            O3[Position 3,1 and 1,3: gamma_zx divided by 2]
        end
    end
    DIAG --> NOTE[Physical Meaning: Stretching or Compression along axes]
    OFFD --> NOTE2[Physical Meaning: Angular distortion between planes]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Identifying Deformation Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Strain Matrix Computed] --> Q{All off-diagonal terms zero?}
    Q -->|Yes| R{All diagonal terms non-zero?}
    Q -->|No| S{Pairwise off-diagonals equal and opposite diagonals zero?}
    R -->|Yes| T[Pure Normal Strain Triaxial Stretching]
    R -->|No| U[Uniaxial or Biaxial Strain]
    S -->|Yes| V[Pure Shear Deformation]
    S -->|No| W[General Mixed Deformation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Question]**
Define the strain matrix. State the relationship between the engineering shear strain and the tensor shear strain component.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **strain matrix** (or **Cauchy strain tensor**) is a $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix that completely characterizes the state of deformation at a point in a continuous medium under small deformation theory.

For small strains, the **engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{ij}$ is related to the tensor shear strain component $\varepsilon_{ij}$ by:

$$\gamma_{ij} = 2\,\varepsilon_{ij} \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

Equivalently, the off-diagonal entries of the strain matrix are: $\varepsilon_{ij} = \dfrac{\gamma_{ij}}{2}$.

The strain matrix is symmetric: $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$, hence only **6 independent components** exist. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question]**
For a 2D displacement field $u(x,y) = ax + by$ and $v(x,y) = cx + dy$, where $a, b, c, d$ are constants, find the four non-zero components of the strain matrix.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given $u = ax + by$ and $v = cx + dy$:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = a, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = d
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = b + c
$$

The 2D strain matrix is:

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} a & \dfrac{b+c}{2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{b+c}{2} & d \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

---

### ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Note:** ESE Module 2 Part B typically offers an internal choice. Both choices below are full 14-mark answers.

---

### ­¤¤” QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper]**

**(a)** The displacement field in a body is given by:
$$u = 2x + 3y, \quad v = x + 5y, \quad w = 2z$$

Determine the complete strain matrix. Identify the type of deformation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A body undergoes deformation with the following displacement field:
$$u = 3x^2y, \quad v = 2xy^2, \quad w = 0$$

Find the strain matrix at the point $(2, 1, 0)$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(a) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$u = 2x + 3y, \quad v = x + 5y, \quad w = 2z$$

**Step 1: Compute normal strains** (1 mark)

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 5
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{zz} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 2
$$

**Step 2: Compute shear strains** (3 marks)

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

$$
\gamma_{yz} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

$$
\gamma_{zx} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

**Step 3: Assemble strain matrix** (2 marks)

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret deformation** (1 mark)

> The off-diagonal term $\varepsilon_{xy} = 2$ indicates simultaneous presence of **shear in xy-plane along with normal strain** ŌĆö a general plane deformation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$u = 3x^2y, \quad v = 2xy^2, \quad w = 0$$

**Step 1: Compute partial derivatives** (2 marks)

$$
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 6xy, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 3x^2
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2y^2, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4xy
$$

**Step 2: Compute strain components** (2 marks)

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = 6xy, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = 4xy, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = 0
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = 3x^2 + 2y^2, \quad \gamma_{yz} = 0, \quad \gamma_{zx} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Substitute point $(2, 1, 0)$** (2 marks)

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = 6(2)(1) = 12
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = 4(2)(1) = 8
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = 3(2)^2 + 2(1)^2 = 12 + 2 = 14
$$

**Step 4: Strain matrix at $(2, 1, 0)$** (1 mark)

$$
[\varepsilon]_{(2,1,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 7 & 0 \\ 7 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

---

### ­¤¤ź QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper]**

**(a)** A body undergoes simple shear deformation with displacement field:
$$u = 0.0005\,y, \quad v = 0.0005\,x, \quad w = 0$$

Determine the strain matrix. What type of deformation does this represent? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For a body with displacement field:
$$u = x^2 + yz, \quad v = y^2 + xz, \quad w = z^2 + xy$$

Find the strain matrix at the origin $(0, 0, 0)$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(a) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $u = 0.0005\,y$, $v = 0.0005\,x$, $w = 0$

**Step 1: Compute partial derivatives** (1 mark)

$$
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0.0005
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0.0005, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

**Step 2: Compute strains** (3 marks)

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = 0, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = 0, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = 0
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = 0.0005 + 0.0005 = 0.001
$$

$$
\gamma_{yz} = 0, \quad \gamma_{zx} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Strain matrix** (2 marks)

$$
[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.0005 & 0 \\ 0.0005 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation** (1 mark)

> All normal strains are zero and the only non-zero component is the off-diagonal $\varepsilon_{xy} = 0.0005$. This is a **pure shear deformation** in the xy-plane with shear angle $= 0.001$ rad. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$u = x^2 + yz, \quad v = y^2 + xz, \quad w = z^2 + xy$$

**Step 1: Compute all required partial derivatives** (3 marks)

$$
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = z, \quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} = y
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = z, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2y, \quad \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} = x
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} = y, \quad \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} = x, \quad \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 2z
$$

**Step 2: Form strain expressions** (2 marks)

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = 2x, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = 2y, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = 2z
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = z + z = 2z
$$

$$
\gamma_{yz} = x + x = 2x
$$

$$
\gamma_{zx} = y + y = 2y
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate at origin $(0,0,0)$** (1 mark)

All six components evaluate to **zero** at the origin.

**Step 4: Strain matrix at origin** (1 mark)

$$
[\varepsilon]_{(0,0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

> This indicates that the origin experiences **no strain** ŌĆö it acts as a strain-free reference point.

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 Strain Matrix Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$**: Placing $\gamma_{xy}$ directly in the off-diagonal instead of $\gamma_{xy}/2$ ŌĆö **loses 1-2 marks**.
> 2. **Asymmetric matrix**: Writing $\varepsilon_{xy} \neq \varepsilon_{yx}$ ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct heavily for violating symmetry.
> 3. **Skipping the point substitution**: When the problem says "at a point", leaving symbolic expressions ŌĆö **loses 1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing the interpretation step**: Not identifying pure / shear / mixed deformation ŌĆö **loses 1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong partial derivative order**: Mixing up $\partial u/\partial y$ vs $\partial u/\partial z$ ŌĆö common under exam pressure.
> 6. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_{zz}$**: In 3D problems, students sometimes compute only xy components and leave the matrix incomplete.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ Strain matrix is a **symmetric** $3 \times 3$ second-order tensor with **6 independent components**.
- Ō£ģ Diagonal entries = **normal strains** ($\varepsilon_{xx}$, $\varepsilon_{yy}$, $\varepsilon_{zz}$).
- Ō£ģ Off-diagonal entries = **half of engineering shear strains** ($\gamma_{ij}/2$).
- Ō£ģ Engineering shear strain: $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$ (and cyclic permutations).
- Ō£ģ Strain is **dimensionless** ŌĆö typical magnitudes are $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- Ō£ģ Standard procedure: **(1)** Identify $u, v, w$ ŌåÆ **(2)** Differentiate ŌåÆ **(3)** Substitute point ŌåÆ **(4)** Assemble matrix ŌåÆ **(5)** Interpret.
- Ō£ģ **Pure shear**: all diagonals zero, only off-diagonal non-zero.
- Ō£ģ **Pure normal strain**: all off-diagonals zero, only diagonal non-zero.
- Ō£ģ Always **state symmetry** explicitly in the answer for full marks.
- Ō£ģ Use $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}$, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}$, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ notation ŌĆö never abbreviate with $u_x$ in KTU boards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Deformation of Circular Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Torsional Deformation of Circular Shafts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite torques (couples) acting in planes perpendicular to the axis. When a **circular shaft** is subjected to such twisting moments, it undergoes **torsional deformation**, characterized by the **angle of twist (Žå)** and the development of **shear stresses (Žä)** that vary linearly from zero at the centroidal axis to a maximum at the outer surface. In the context of KTU 2024 Solid Mechanics (PCRUT302, Module 3), the analysis of torsional deformation of circular shafts is restricted to **linearly elastic**, **homogeneous**, **isotropic** materials operating within the **proportional elastic limit** ŌĆö i.e., the shaft obeys **Hooke's Law in shear** (Žä = G╬│), where **G** is the **Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus)**, measured in **GPa** or **N/mm┬▓**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module covers derivation of the torsion equation, shear stress distribution across the cross-section, angle of twist for shafts of uniform and varying cross-section, torsional rigidity, and the design of shafts for power transmission. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a **chocolate bar** at both ends and twisting your hands in opposite directions. The chocolate bar resists the twist ŌĆö that's torsion in action. Now picture a **drill bit** rotating inside a wall: the electric motor applies torque at one end, the drill bit experiences resistance at the cutting tip, and the bit twists slightly along its length. This angular distortion is the **angle of twist (Žå)**. **Real-world engineering examples:** | Application | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | Automobiles | Drive shaft (propeller shaft) | Transmits engine torque to differential | | Turbomachinery | Rotor shaft of steam/gas turbine | Carries torque from blades to generator | | Machine tools | Lathe spindle | Holds workpiece and transmits cutting torque | | Civil structures | Helicoidal staircases | Resists torsional moments from occupants | > [!TIP] > The key physical insight: **every cross-section rotates as a rigid disc** about the longitudinal axis. The cross-sections do NOT warp; they simply rotate relative to each other by an angle proportional to the distance along the shaft. ### 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions in Classical Torsion Theory The standard torsion formula is derived under these **five assumptions**, all examinable in KTU: 1. The shaft material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**. 2. The cross-section is **circular** (solid or hollow) and remains **plane** after twisting. 3. **Plane sections remain plane** and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis (no warping). 4. **Radius remains straight** during deformation (no cross-section distortion). 5. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** in shear: Žä = G╬│. The **polar moment of inertia (J)** has units of **mmŌü┤** and represents the cross-section's resistance to twisting. For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter **d**: $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ For a **hollow circular shaft** of outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d**: $$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Torsion Equation ŌĆö The Heart of the Module The **torsion equation** is the fundamental relationship governing the elastic behavior of circular shafts under torque. It is to torsion what the bending equation (M/I = Žā/y = E/R) is to flexure. **Statement of the Torsion Equation:** $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$ where: - **T** = Applied torque (N┬Ęmm) - **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (mmŌü┤) - **Žä** = Shear stress at radius **r** (N/mm┬▓ or MPa) - **r** = Radial distance from the centroidal axis (mm) - **G** = Modulus of rigidity of the material (N/mm┬▓) - **╬Ė** = Angle of twist over length **L** (radians) - **L** = Length of the shaft segment (mm) ### 2.2 Logical Derivation Outline (Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow) 1. **Consider an element** on the outer surface of the shaft at radius r. Under torque T, it experiences shear stress Žä. 2. **Shear strain** ╬│ on this element equals the angle through which the radius line rotates: ╬│ = (r┬ĘŽå)/L, where Žå is the angle of twist over length L. 3. **Apply Hooke's Law in shear**: Žä = G╬│ Ō¤╣ Žä = G┬Ę(rŽå/L). 4. **Equilibrium of moments**: The internal torque T equals the integral of (Žä ┬Ę r) over the cross-sectional area: T = Ōł½(Žär)dA. 5. **Substitute** Žä from step 3 into step 4: T = Ōł½(G┬Ęr┬▓Žå/L)dA = (GŽå/L)┬ĘŌł½r┬▓dA. 6. **Recognize** that Ōł½r┬▓dA = J (polar moment of inertia). 7. **Rearrange** to obtain: T/J = Žä/r = GŽå/L. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** All three ratios (T/J), (Žä/r), and (GŽå/L) are **equal in magnitude**. This is the form most often tested in KTU problems ŌĆö the "triple equality" of the torsion equation. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units (SI)** | **Units (mm basis)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Polar moment of inertia (solid) | J | ŽĆdŌü┤/32 | mŌü┤ | mmŌü┤ | | Polar moment of inertia (hollow) | J | ŽĆ(DŌü┤ ŌłÆ dŌü┤)/32 | mŌü┤ | mmŌü┤ | | Maximum shear stress (solid) | Žä_max | 16T/(ŽĆd┬│) | Pa | N/mm┬▓ | | Maximum shear stress (hollow) | Žä_max | 16T┬ĘD / [ŽĆ(DŌü┤ ŌłÆ dŌü┤)] | Pa | N/mm┬▓ | | Angle of twist | Žå | TL / (GJ) | rad | rad | | Torsional rigidity | k_t | GJ | N┬Ęm┬▓/rad | N┬Ęmm┬▓/rad | | Shear stress at radius r | Žä | Tr/J | Pa | N/mm┬▓ | | Power transmitted | P | 2ŽĆNT / 60 | W | W | | Equivalent torque from power | T | 60P / (2ŽĆN) | N┬Ęm | N┬Ęmm | | Strain energy in torsion | U | T┬▓L / (2GJ) | J (joules) | N┬Ęmm | | Composite shaft torque split | TŌéü, TŌéé | TŌéü/TŌéé = GŌéüJŌéü/(GŌééJŌéé) | ŌĆō | ŌĆō | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU:** The **"TJG" triangle** ŌĆö T/J = Žä/r = GŽå/L. Memorize this triangle; it is the single most important relationship in this module. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive driveline design**: Propeller shafts are designed hollow to maximize J (and hence torsional capacity) while minimizing weight. The J of a hollow shaft with D = 2d is approximately **15 times** the J of a solid shaft of diameter d, while the cross-sectional area is only 4 times larger. - **Turbogenerator shafts**: Forged from high-grade alloy steel (G Ōēł 80 GPa), these shafts transmit torques on the order of **MN┬Ęm** while limiting twist to fractions of a degree over their entire length. - **Seismic design**: Tall buildings and chimneys are analyzed for torsional response of their cores under lateral (earthquake/wind) loads. ### 2.5 Important Geometric Ratios for Hollow Shafts - **Ratio of polar moments**: J_hollow / J_solid = 1 ŌłÆ (d/D)Ōü┤ - **Mass ratio** (for same length and material): m_hollow / m_solid = 1 ŌłÆ (d/D)┬▓ - **Strength-to-weight ratio advantage**: A hollow shaft with D = 2d transmits **~15├Ś more torque** for the **same weight** as a solid shaft of diameter d. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **polar moment of inertia J** (torsion) with **area moment of inertia I** (bending). Remember: **J = Ix + Iy** for a circular section, and J = 2I for a solid circular section since Ix = Iy = ŽĆdŌü┤/64. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torsion Equation **Setup:** Consider a circular shaft of length **L**, fixed at one end, and subjected to a torque **T** at the free end. **Step 1: Geometric Kinematics (Deformation Pattern)** Let the free end rotate through a small angle **Žå** relative to the fixed end. A point originally on the outer surface at radius **R** (and at axial distance x from the fixed end) moves to a new angular position. The arc length traversed by this point is: $$s = R \cdot \phi_x$$ where Žå_x is the angle of twist over the length x. **Step 2: Shear Strain on the Surface** By definition, shear strain ╬│ is the change in right angle at a material element. For an element on the outer surface: $$\gamma = \frac{\text{arc length}}{\text{axial length}} = \frac{R \cdot \phi_x}{x}$$ **Step 3: Generalization to an Arbitrary Radius r** Since plane sections remain plane and radial lines remain straight, the angle of twist Žå_x is the same for all points on the cross-section at position x. A point at an arbitrary radius r (where 0 Ōēż r Ōēż R) experiences an arc length of r┬ĘŽå_x, and thus: $$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot \phi_x}{x}$$ **Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear** $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \phi_x}{x}$$ **Step 5: Equilibrium of the Cross-Section** The internal torque T(x) at section x must equilibrate the external torque. For a differential area dA at radius r, the shear stress Žä acts tangentially and produces a moment Žä┬Ęr┬ĘdA. Summing over the entire cross-section: $$T(x) = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA$$ **Step 6: Substitute the Expression for Žä** $$T(x) = \int_A \left( \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \phi_x}{x} \right) \cdot r \, dA = \frac{G \cdot \phi_x}{x} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$ **Step 7: Identify the Polar Moment of Inertia** By definition: $$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$ For a solid circular cross-section of diameter d, evaluating in polar coordinates (with dA = r┬Ędr┬Ęd╬Ė): $$J = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{d/2} r^2 \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta = 2\pi \cdot \frac{r^4}{4} \Big|_0^{d/2} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{(d/2)^4}{4} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ **Step 8: Combine to Form the Torsion Equation** Substituting J back into Step 6: $$T(x) = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \phi_x}{x}$$ Rearranging for a segment of length L over which the twist is Žå: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \phi}{L}$$ And since Žä = G┬Ęr┬ĘŽå/L, we can also write Žä/r = GŽå/L. Combining all three: $$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \phi}{L}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Shaft Starting from Žä = Tr/J, maximum shear stress occurs at r = R = d/2 (the outer surface): $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{32 T}{2 \pi d^3} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress in a Hollow Shaft For a hollow shaft with outer radius R = D/2 and inner radius r_i = d/2: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)/32} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$ > [!NOTE] > Notice that the inner fibers of a hollow shaft carry **less shear stress** than the outer fibers. This is precisely why hollow shafts are more **material-efficient** ŌĆö the inner material in a solid shaft is "underutilized." ### 3.4 Derivation of the Angle of Twist From the torsion equation, the angle of twist over a shaft of length L under constant torque T is: $$\phi = \frac{T L}{G J}$$ For a shaft with **stepped diameters** or **varying torque**, the total angle of twist is the sum of contributions from each segment: $$\phi_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i}$$ **Sign Convention:** Use the **right-hand rule**. A torque vector pointing outward (away from the section) is positive. This allows algebraic summation when multiple torques are applied. ### 3.5 Power Transmission and Torque Relationship When a shaft rotates at **N revolutions per minute (rpm)**, the power transmitted is: $$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$ where T is in **N┬Ęm** and P is in **watts (W)**. Solving for the required torque: $$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$$ ### 3.6 Composite (Compound) Shafts When two or more shafts of different materials or cross-sections are connected **in parallel** (rigidly bonded along their length, twisting through the same angle Žå): $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{G_1 J_1}{G_2 J_2}, \quad T = T_1 + T_2$$ $$\phi = \frac{T_1 L}{G_1 J_1} = \frac{T_2 L}{G_2 J_2}$$ For shafts connected **in series** (end-to-end, transmitting the same torque T): $$\phi_{total} = \frac{T L_1}{G_1 J_1} + \frac{T L_2}{G_2 J_2}$$ ### 3.7 Statically Indeterminate Torsion Problems When a shaft is fixed at both ends (or has redundant supports), apply: 1. **Equilibrium:** Sum of torques = 0. 2. **Compatibility:** Total angle of twist between the fixed ends = 0. Example setup: A shaft fixed at both ends A and C, with an applied torque T_B at intermediate point B. - Let T_A and T_C be reaction torques. - Equilibrium: T_A + T_C = T_B. - Compatibility: Žå_AB + Žå_BC = 0. - Solve simultaneously for T_A and T_C. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The sign convention in compatibility is critical. If T_A causes twist in the opposite sense to T_C, the compatibility equation becomes T_A┬ĘL_1/(GJ)_1 = T_C┬ĘL_2/(GJ)_2. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Torsion Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Problem Input:
Applied Torque, Geometry, Material] --> B{Identify Shaft Type} B -->|Solid Circular| C1[J = pi d^4 / 32] B -->|Hollow Circular| C2[J = pi D^4 minus d^4 / 32] B -->|Composite / Stepped| C3[Decompose into Segments] C1 --> D[Compute Torsional Rigidity GJ] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E{Required Output} E -->|Shear Stress| F1[tau = T times r over J] E -->|Angle of Twist| F2[phi = T times L over GJ] E -->|Shaft Diameter| F3[d = 16T over pi tau_max power 1/3] E -->|Power Capacity| F4[P = 2 pi N T over 60] F1 --> G[Cross-Check via T/J = tau/r = G phi/L] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H[Final Engineering Solution] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Torsion in a Circular Shaft ŌĆö Stress and Strain States ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Loading] T1[Applied Torque T] end subgraph DEFORMATION[Deformation Field] D1[Angle of Twist phi] D2[Shear Strain gamma = r phi / L] end subgraph STRESS[Stress Field] S1[Shear Stress tau = G gamma] S2[Linear Distribution: tau = 0 at center, tau max at surface] end subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Quantities] O1[Torsional Rigidity GJ] O2[Strain Energy U = T squared L over 2GJ] O3[Design Diameter d] end T1 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> S1 S1 --> S2 T1 --> O1 S1 --> O2 S1 --> O3 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Composite Shaft Loading Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Applied Torque T] --> B{Connection Type} B -->|Series| C1[Same T in Each Segment] B -->|Parallel Bonded| C2[Same phi, T split by GJ ratio] C1 --> D1[phi_total = sum of T Li over Gi Ji] C2 --> D2[T1 over T2 = G1J1 over G2J2] D1 --> E[Total Angle of Twist] D2 --> E ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: Shear Stress Variation Across Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Center: r = 0] -->|tau = 0| B[Mid Radius: r = R/2] B -->|tau = tau_max / 2| C[Outer Surface: r = R] C -->|tau = tau_max| D[Linear Variation from 0 to tau_max] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Schematic: Statically Indeterminate Shaft Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fixed Support A] -->|Length L1, GJ1| B[Applied Torque TB at Point B] B -->|Length L2, GJ2| C[Fixed Support C] A -.->|Reaction TA| B C -.->|Reaction TC| B B --> D[Equilibrium: TA plus TC equals TB] B --> E[Compatibility: phi_AB plus phi_BC equals 0] D --> F[Solve Simultaneous Equations] E --> F F --> G[Reactions TA and TC] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** **State the torsion equation and explain the meaning of each term involved.** **Model Answer:** The torsion equation for a circular shaft is: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \phi}{L}$$ where: - **T** = Torque applied at the section (N┬Ęmm) - **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis (mmŌü┤) - **Žä** = Shear stress at a point located at radial distance **r** from the axis (N/mm┬▓) - **r** = Radial distance of the point from the centroidal axis (mm) - **G** = Modulus of rigidity of the shaft material (N/mm┬▓) - **Žå** = Angle of twist over length **L** (radians) - **L** = Length of the shaft under consideration (mm) This equation indicates that the shear stress varies **linearly** with the radial distance r, and is **zero at the axis** and **maximum at the outer surface**. It is valid for circular cross-sections within the elastic limit, with the assumption that plane sections remain plane and the material obeys Hooke's law in shear. **[3 Marks: 1 for statement, 1 for each term explanation cluster, 1 for assumption]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm transmits 50 kW at 200 rpm. Calculate the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** d = 60 mm, P = 50 kW = 50 ├Ś 10┬│ W, N = 200 rpm **Step 1: Calculate the torque transmitted.** $$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 50 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 200} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{400 \pi} = \frac{7500}{\pi} \approx 2387.3 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$ Converting to N┬Ęmm: T = 2387.3 ├Ś 10┬│ = 2.3873 ├Ś 10ŌüČ N┬Ęmm **Step 2: Calculate the maximum shear stress.** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2.3873 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{38.197 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000}$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{38.197 \times 10^6}{678584.0} \approx 56.29 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Answer:** Žä_max Ōēł **56.29 MPa** **[3 Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for torque, 1 for final stress]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** A solid steel shaft of diameter **80 mm** is subjected to a torque of **12 kN┬Ęm**. Calculate: - (i) The maximum shear stress in the shaft. **[3 marks]** - (ii) The angle of twist per metre length, if G = 80 GPa. **[4 marks]** **(b)** A composite shaft consists of a steel core (diameter 50 mm) surrounded by a brass tube (outer diameter 80 mm). The shaft is subjected to a total torque of **6 kN┬Ęm**. If G_steel = 80 GPa and G_brass = 40 GPa, determine the torque carried by each material and the maximum shear stress in each. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a)(i): Maximum Shear Stress** Given: d = 80 mm, T = 12 kN┬Ęm = 12 ├Ś 10ŌüČ N┬Ęmm $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 12 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (80)^3} = \frac{192 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 512000}$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{192 \times 10^6}{1.6085 \times 10^6} \approx 119.37 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Answer:** Žä_max Ōēł **119.37 MPa** **[3 marks]** --- **Part (a)(ii): Angle of Twist per Metre Length** Given: L = 1 m = 1000 mm, G = 80 GPa = 80 ├Ś 10┬│ N/mm┬▓ First, compute the polar moment of inertia: $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40.96 \times 10^6}{32} = \frac{128.68 \times 10^6}{32} \approx 4.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ Now compute angle of twist: $$\phi = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80 \times 10^3 \times 4.021 \times 10^6}$$ $$\phi = \frac{12 \times 10^9}{3.217 \times 10^{11}} = 0.03730 \text{ rad}$$ Converting to degrees: Žå = 0.03730 ├Ś (180/ŽĆ) Ōēł **2.137┬░** per metre **Answer:** Žå Ōēł **0.0373 rad/m or 2.137┬░/m** **[4 marks]** --- **Part (b): Composite Shaft Analysis** Given: - Steel core: d_s = 50 mm, G_s = 80 GPa - Brass tube: D = 80 mm, d_i = 50 mm (inner dia = steel outer dia), G_b = 40 GPa - Total torque T = 6 kN┬Ęm = 6 ├Ś 10ŌüČ N┬Ęmm **Step 1: Compute polar moments of inertia.** Steel core: $$J_s = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{32} \approx 0.6136 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ Brass tube: $$J_b = \frac{\pi [(80)^4 - (50)^4]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times [40.96 \times 10^6 - 6.25 \times 10^6]}{32}$$ $$J_b = \frac{\pi \times 34.71 \times 10^6}{32} \approx 3.408 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Step 2: Apply compatibility and equilibrium.** For a bonded composite shaft, both materials twist through the **same angle**: $$\frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b L}{G_b J_b}$$ $$\frac{T_s}{T_b} = \frac{G_s J_s}{G_b J_b} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 0.6136 \times 10^6}{40 \times 10^3 \times 3.408 \times 10^6}$$ $$\frac{T_s}{T_b} = \frac{49.088 \times 10^9}{136.32 \times 10^9} = 0.3602$$ Equilibrium: T_s + T_b = 6 ├Ś 10ŌüČ N┬Ęmm Substituting T_s = 0.3602 T_b: $$0.3602 T_b + T_b = 6 \times 10^6 \implies 1.3602 T_b = 6 \times 10^6$$ $$T_b = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{1.3602} \approx 4.411 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm} \approx 4.411 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$$ $$T_s = 0.3602 \times 4.411 \times 10^6 \approx 1.589 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm} \approx 1.589 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$$ **Step 3: Compute maximum shear stresses.** For steel core (max at r = 25 mm): $$\tau_{s,max} = \frac{T_s \cdot r_s}{J_s} = \frac{1.589 \times 10^6 \times 25}{0.6136 \times 10^6} = \frac{39.725 \times 10^6}{0.6136 \times 10^6} \approx 64.74 \text{ MPa}$$ For brass tube (max at r = 40 mm): $$\tau_{b,max} = \frac{T_b \cdot r_b}{J_b} = \frac{4.411 \times 10^6 \times 40}{3.408 \times 10^6} = \frac{176.44 \times 10^6}{3.408 \times 10^6} \approx 51.77 \text{ MPa}$$ **Answers:** - Torque in steel: T_s Ōēł **1.589 kN┬Ęm** - Torque in brass: T_b Ōēł **4.411 kN┬Ęm** - Žä_max in steel: Ōēł **64.74 MPa** - Žä_max in brass: Ōēł **51.77 MPa** **[7 marks: 2 for J, 2 for torques, 3 for shear stresses]** --- **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the torsion equation **T/J = Žä/r = GŽå/L** starting from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A shaft fixed at both ends A and C carries an applied torque of **10 kN┬Ęm** at the intermediate point B. The segment AB has length 1.5 m, diameter 60 mm, and G = 80 GPa. The segment BC has length 1.0 m, diameter 50 mm, and G = 80 GPa. Determine the reaction torques at A and C. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a): Derivation** The full derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** above. The model answer for KTU should include: 1. **Statement of assumptions** (5 standard assumptions). **[2 marks]** 2. **Kinematic relation** for shear strain: ╬│ = r┬ĘŽå/L. **[1 mark]** 3. **Hooke's Law in shear**: Žä = G╬│ = G┬Ęr┬ĘŽå/L. **[1 mark]** 4. **Equilibrium integral**: T = Ōł½Žä┬Ęr┬ĘdA. **[1 mark]** 5. **Recognition of J** = Ōł½r┬▓┬ĘdA, leading to final torsion equation. **[1 mark]** 6. **Statement of result** with units. **[1 mark]** --- **Part (b): Statically Indeterminate Shaft** Given: - T_B = 10 kN┬Ęm applied at B - Segment AB: L_1 = 1.5 m, d_1 = 60 mm, G = 80 GPa - Segment BC: L_2 = 1.0 m, d_2 = 50 mm, G = 80 GPa **Step 1: Compute polar moments of inertia.** $$J_1 = \frac{\pi (60)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 12.96 \times 10^6}{32} \approx 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ $$J_2 = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{32} \approx 0.6136 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Step 2: Set up equations.** Let T_A = reaction at A, T_C = reaction at C. **Equilibrium:** $$T_A + T_C = T_B = 10 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$$ **Compatibility** (total twist between A and C is zero, since both are fixed): The internal torque in AB is T_A (twisting it from A to B), and the internal torque in BC is T_C (twisting it from B to C). Both segments twist, but the net rotation of C relative to A is zero. $$\phi_{AB} + \phi_{BC} = 0$$ $$\frac{T_A L_1}{G J_1} + \frac{T_C L_2}{G J_2} = 0$$ (The sign convention: T_A and T_C act in opposite senses on the shaft ŌĆö the torque in BC is ŌłÆT_C when measured in the same rotational sense as T_A.) $$T_A = -T_C \cdot \frac{L_2 J_1}{L_1 J_2}$$ Computing the ratio: $$\frac{L_2 J_1}{L_1 J_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 1.272 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 0.6136 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.272}{0.9204} \approx 1.382$$ So: T_A = ŌłÆ1.382 T_C **Step 3: Solve simultaneously.** From equilibrium: T_A = 10 ŌłÆ T_C (in kN┬Ęm) Substituting: 10 ŌłÆ T_C = ŌłÆ1.382 T_C $$10 = T_C - 1.382 T_C = -0.382 T_C$$ $$T_C = \frac{10}{-0.382} \approx -26.18 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$$ The negative sign indicates T_C acts in the **opposite sense** to assumed direction. The magnitude is 26.18 kN┬Ęm. $$T_A = 10 - (-26.18) = 36.18 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$$ **Verification of compatibility:** $$\phi_{AB} = \frac{36.18 \times 10^6 \times 1500}{80 \times 10^3 \times 1.272 \times 10^6} = \frac{54.27 \times 10^9}{101.76 \times 10^9} = 0.5333 \text{ rad}$$ $$\phi_{BC} = \frac{-26.18 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80 \times 10^3 \times 0.6136 \times 10^6} = \frac{-26.18 \times 10^9}{49.088 \times 10^9} = -0.5333 \text{ rad}$$ Sum = 0 Ō£ō **Answers:** - T_A Ōēł **36.18 kN┬Ęm** - T_C Ōēł **26.18 kN┬Ęm** (opposite sense) **[7 marks: 2 for J, 2 for equilibrium and compatibility, 2 for solving, 1 for verification]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Unit Consistency:** Always convert all lengths to **mm** and torques to **N┬Ęmm** before substituting into the torsion equation. Mixing units (e.g., d in mm and L in m) is the **#1 source of calculation errors** in KTU answer scripts. > 2. **Sign Convention in Statically Indeterminate Problems:** Markers deduct heavily for incorrect sign handling in the compatibility equation. Always draw a free-body diagram and label the assumed positive direction of each reaction torque before writing the compatibility condition. > 3. **Solid vs Hollow Confusion:** When asked for "shaft diameter," check if the problem implies a solid or hollow cross-section. Using J_solid when a hollow section is required (or vice versa) leads to **wrong design values** and loss of 2ŌĆō3 marks. > 4. **Power vs Torque:** Power P (W) and Torque T (N┬Ęm) are **NOT** the same. The relationship T = 60P/(2ŽĆN) must be invoked to convert between them; students often skip this step. > 5. **Composite Shafts:** Remember that for a **bonded parallel composite shaft**, the angle of twist is the **same** in both materials, but the torques and stresses are different. For a **series composite shaft**, the torque is the **same**, but the angles of twist add. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 ŌĆö Torsional Deformation of Circular Shafts** - ŌśÉ **Torsion Equation:** T/J = Žä/r = GŽå/L ŌĆö the master relationship. - ŌśÉ **Polar Moment of Inertia (J):** Solid: ŽĆdŌü┤/32. Hollow: ŽĆ(DŌü┤ ŌłÆ dŌü┤)/32. - ŌśÉ **Maximum Shear Stress:** Solid: 16T/(ŽĆd┬│). Hollow: 16TD/[ŽĆ(DŌü┤ ŌłÆ dŌü┤)]. - ŌśÉ **Angle of Twist:** Žå = TL/(GJ). For stepped shafts, sum over each segment. - ŌśÉ **Torsional Rigidity:** k_t = GJ (resistance to twisting). - ŌśÉ **Five Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, circular cross-section, plane sections remain plane, Hooke's law in shear. - ŌśÉ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** Žä = G╬│. - ŌśÉ **Power Equation:** P = 2ŽĆNT/60, hence T = 60P/(2ŽĆN). - ŌśÉ **Strain Energy in Torsion:** U = T┬▓L/(2GJ). - ŌśÉ **Composite Shafts (Parallel/Bonded):** Same Žå, T split as TŌéü/TŌéé = GŌéüJŌéü/(GŌééJŌéé). - ŌśÉ **Composite Shafts (Series):** Same T, Žå_total = ╬Ż T_iL_i/(G_iJ_i). - ŌśÉ **Statically Indeterminate:** Use Equilibrium + Compatibility (Žå_total = 0 between fixed ends). - ŌśÉ **Units (SI / mm):** Force in N, lengths in mm, Žä in N/mm┬▓ (= MPa), G in N/mm┬▓, T in N┬Ęmm, Žå in rad. - ŌśÉ **Hollow vs Solid Efficiency:** A hollow shaft with D = 2d carries ~15├Ś more torque than a solid shaft of the same mass. - ŌśÉ **Sign Convention:** Right-hand rule; torques causing twist in the same sense have the same sign. - ŌśÉ **Angle Conversion:** Multiply radians by (180/ŽĆ) to convert to degrees. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always write the **assumptions** before solving, draw a **clear free-body diagram** showing the internal torque in each segment, and **box the final answer** with correct units. Most marks in KTU valuations are awarded for systematic presentation, not just the final number.

topics.title: Assumptions for Shafts Subjected to Torsion Within Elastic Deformation Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions for Shafts Subjected to Torsion Within Elastic Deformation Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Theory of Elastic Torsion of Circular Shafts**, a set of simplifying hypotheses are adopted to make the otherwise complex three-dimensional elasticity problem tractable through elementary strength-of-materials analysis. These assumptions collectively define the classical **CoulombŌĆōSaint-Venant Torsion Theory**, which forms the basis of every KTU board-level torsion problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The assumptions for shafts subjected to torsion within the elastic deformation range are the foundational hypothesis upon which the **Torsion Equation**
> $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$
> is derived. Any violation of these assumptions renders the formula invalid for that particular shaft.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Twisted Towel Intuition

Imagine holding a dry towel at both ends and twisting it in opposite directions. What you observe:

1. The **fibers of the towel** (representing longitudinal generators on the shaft surface) that were originally straight, become **helical spirals**.
2. The **circular ends** of the towel (representing cross-sections of the shaft) do **not** get crushed, distorted, or bulge out ŌĆö they simply **rotate as rigid discs** about the central axis.
3. The towel is **not pulled apart or compressed** along its length ŌĆö its length remains constant.
4. The outermost fibers (farthest from the center) experience the **most distortion** ŌĆö they stretch and tilt the most, while the central fiber doesn't deform at all.

This towel-twisting model is **exactly** the physical picture the KTU assumptions describe for a circular shaft. The assumptions are simply the engineering translation of "what a perfect shaft does when you twist it gently."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Topic:**
> - **G** = Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus) ŌĆö units: **N/m┬▓** or **Pa** (typical steel: **G Ōēł 80 GPa**)
> - **E** = Young's Modulus ŌĆö units: **N/m┬▓** (typical steel: **E Ōēł 200 GPa**)
> - **╬Į** = Poisson's Ratio ŌĆö dimensionless (typical steel: **╬Į Ōēł 0.30**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twelve Pillars of Elastic Torsion Theory

The classical theory rests on **twelve distinct assumptions**. KTU examiners frequently test these individually, so each must be understood separately.

#### A. Material Assumptions (The Substance)

1. **Homogeneity** ŌĆö The material has identical mechanical properties at every point throughout the volume of the shaft.
   - *Why?* So that a single shear modulus **G** describes the entire shaft.

2. **Isotropy** ŌĆö The material has identical properties in all directions at any point.
   - *Why?* So that the shear modulus is the same regardless of the orientation of the element.

3. **Continuity** ŌĆö The material is free from voids, cracks, or internal flaws.
   - *Why?* The mathematical formulation assumes a continuous stress field.

4. **Linear Elasticity (Hooke's Law in Shear)** ŌĆö Shear stress is proportional to shear strain.
   $$\tau = G\gamma$$
   - *Why?* This is the **defining condition** for being "within the elastic range."

#### B. Geometric Assumptions (The Shape)

5. **Straight Axis** ŌĆö The longitudinal axis of the shaft is a perfectly straight line in its undeformed state.

6. **Uniform Cross-Section** ŌĆö The cross-section is constant along the entire length (prismatic shaft).

7. **Circular Cross-Section (Solid or Hollow)** ŌĆö The section is either a solid circle or a concentric circular annulus.
   - *Why?* Only for circular sections do the assumptions of rigid cross-sectional rotation hold exactly. Non-circular sections warp and require **St. Venant's warping function** theory.

#### C. Kinematic Assumptions (The Motion)

8. **Plane Sections Remain Plane** ŌĆö Cross-sections that were plane and perpendicular to the axis **before loading remain plane and perpendicular to the axis after loading**.
   - *Why?* This means there is no **warping** (out-of-plane distortion) of the cross-section.

9. **Cross-Sections Rotate as Rigid Bodies** ŌĆö Each cross-section rotates about the longitudinal axis as if it were a rigid disc, with no in-plane distortion.

10. **Radius Remains Straight and Unchanged in Length** ŌĆö A line drawn on the cross-section from the center to the surface does not change in length; it only rotates.
    - *Why?* This leads directly to the **linear stress distribution**: $\tau \propto r$.

11. **Distance Between Cross-Sections Remains Unchanged** ŌĆö The longitudinal fibers do not stretch or compress; the shaft does not "neck down" or "bulge."
    - *Why?* This rules out axial or radial deformation.

#### D. Loading Assumptions (The Force)

12. **Pure Torsion (No Other Loading)** ŌĆö The shaft is loaded **only** by torques applied at its ends (or sections). There is **no bending moment, no axial force, no transverse shear** acting on the shaft.

### 2.2 Consequences of These Assumptions ŌĆö The Derivative Facts

From the above assumptions, the following KTU-board-tested facts follow:

- **Linear shear stress distribution**: $\tau$ varies **linearly** from **zero at the center** to a **maximum at the outer surface**.
- **Pure shear stress state**: The only non-zero stress component is $\tau_{xy}$ (or $\tau_{xz}$ in 3D). Principal stresses are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign: $\sigma_1 = +\tau,\ \sigma_2 = -\tau$ at $45┬░$ to the axis.
- **Principal stress angle**: The maximum principal stress acts on planes inclined at **45┬░** to the shaft axis ŌĆö the classic "torsion fracture on a 45┬░ helix" observed in ductile shafts.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Torsion Equation (Main) | ŌĆö | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | ŌĆö | **THE** master equation |
| 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J$ | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}}{2}$ | mŌü┤ | $D$ = diameter, $r$ = radius |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow) | $J$ | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D_{o}^{4} - D_{i}^{4}\right)$ | mŌü┤ | $D_o, D_i$ = outer/inner diameter |
| 4 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi D^{3}}$ | Pa or N/m┬▓ | For solid shaft, at $r = D/2$ |
| 5 | Maximum Shear Stress (Hollow) | $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16\,T\,D_{o}}{\pi\left(D_{o}^{4} - D_{i}^{4}\right)}$ | Pa | At outer surface |
| 6 | Angle of Twist | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ | rad | Total twist over length $L$ |
| 7 | Angle of Twist per Unit Length | $\theta/L$ | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{GJ}$ | rad/m | |
| 8 | Shear Strain at Radius $r$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{r\,\theta}{L}$ | rad | |
| 9 | Shear Modulus Relation | $G$ | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Pa | Links $G, E, \nu$ |
| 10 | Power Transmitted by Shaft | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W (Watts) | $N$ = rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These assumptions are used in the design of **drive shafts, axles, propeller shafts, drill pipes, turbine rotors, and machine tool spindles**. Almost every rotating power-transmission member in mechanical engineering relies on this theory. KTU frequently frames numerical problems in the context of automotive drive shafts and motor couplings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation from the Twelve Assumptions

This is the KTU 14-mark derivation pattern. The proof proceeds in seven logical steps.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Setting Up the Geometry (Assumptions 5ŌĆō7)

Consider a straight circular shaft of length $L$ and diameter $D$, fixed at one end and free at the other. A torque $T$ is applied at the free end about the longitudinal $x$-axis.

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Surface Fiber Deformation (Assumption 8)

Consider a surface generator $AB$ on the shaft, originally parallel to the axis. After twisting, the free end rotates through a small angle $\theta$, and $B$ moves to a new position $B'$ on a circular arc of radius $R = D/2$.

The arc length traversed is:
$$\widehat{BB'} = R \cdot \theta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \theta$$

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Shear Strain (Assumption 8 ŌĆö Plane Sections Remain Plane)

Since the cross-section has only rotated (no warping), the shear strain $\gamma$ at the outer surface equals the angular deformation of the fiber $AB$:

$$\gamma = \frac{\widehat{BB'}}{L} = \frac{R\,\theta}{L} = \frac{D\,\theta}{2L}$$

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Hooke's Law in Shear (Assumption 4)

Within the elastic range, the shear stress $\tau$ at the surface is:

$$\tau = G\,\gamma = G \cdot \frac{R\,\theta}{L} = \frac{G R \theta}{L}$$

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Linear Stress Distribution (Assumption 10)

Because the radius remains straight and unchanged in length, and the deformation is small, the shear strain at any interior radius $r$ is obtained by similar triangles (linear interpolation from zero at center to maximum at the surface):

$$\gamma_r = \gamma \cdot \frac{r}{R} = \frac{r\,\theta}{L}$$

Hence the shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$\tau_r = G\,\gamma_r = \frac{G\,r\,\theta}{L}$$

This confirms the **linear variation** $\tau \propto r$.

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Equilibrium of Torque (Force Balance)

The shear stress on an elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r\, dr$ acts tangentially. The elemental torque contributed is:

$$dT = \tau_r \cdot dA \cdot r = \frac{G\,r\,\theta}{L} \cdot (2\pi r\, dr) \cdot r = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^{3}\, dr$$

The total resisting torque equals the applied torque $T$:

$$T = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} r^{3}\, dr = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{2}$$

Simplifying:

$$T = \frac{\pi G \theta R^{4}}{2L} = \frac{G \theta J}{L}$$

where the polar moment of inertia is $J = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$.

#### Step 7 ŌĆö Combining to Get the Torsion Equation

From Step 6, $T = \dfrac{G \theta J}{L}$ gives:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

From Step 5, $\tau_r = \dfrac{G\,r\,\theta}{L}$ gives:

$$\frac{\tau_r}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

Equating the two right-hand sides:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}\;}$$

This is the **Torsion Equation** of KTU Module 3.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Validity Conditions

The following matrix tells you **when the formula works** and **when it fails** ŌĆö a common KTU Part-A question:

| Condition | Formula Valid? | Why / Why Not |
|-----------|:--------------:|---------------|
| Circular solid shaft, elastic range, pure torsion | Ō£ģ YES | All 12 assumptions satisfied |
| Hollow circular shaft, elastic range, pure torsion | Ō£ģ YES | Polar moment formula changes, theory unchanged |
| Circular shaft, torque exceeds yield torque | ŌØī NO | Assumption 4 (linear elasticity) violated ŌåÆ use **plastic torsion** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3T}{2\pi R^{3}}$ |
| Square / rectangular / non-circular section | ŌØī NO | Assumption 7 violated; **warping** occurs ŌåÆ use St. Venant's $J$ from series solutions |
| Tapered (conical) circular shaft | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ PARTIAL | Assumption 6 violated; use **variable $J(x)$** integration $\theta = \int \dfrac{T}{G J(x)} dx$ |
| Shaft with keyway or hole | ŌØī NO | Assumptions 7 and 8 violated near the discontinuity (use St. Venant's principle & stress concentration factor) |
| Thin-walled open section (e.g., C-channel) | ŌØī NO | Assumption 7 violated; uses **thin-walled open section theory** |
| Combined torsion + bending | ŌØī NO | Assumption 12 violated; use **superposition of stresses** |
| High speed (centrifugal effects) | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ PARTIAL | Additional stresses must be superimposed |

### 3.3 Proof of the Power Equation $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$

Although KTU tests this less frequently, it is a natural consequence of the assumptions. If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Power transmitted is torque times angular velocity:

$$P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts, when } T \text{ in N┬Ęm]}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Assumption Dependency Flow

This diagram maps how the twelve assumptions are interconnected and feed into the master torsion equation. It is rendered in compliance with the alphanumeric-node safeguard rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Shaft Setup] --> A1[1 Homogeneity]
    A1 --> A2[2 Isotropy]
    A2 --> A3[3 Continuity]
    A3 --> A4[4 Linear Elasticity Hookes Law tau = G gamma]
    A4 --> K1[Material Block Established]

    A0 --> B1[5 Straight Axis]
    B1 --> B2[6 Uniform Cross Section]
    B2 --> B3[7 Circular Section Solid or Hollow]
    B3 --> K2[Geometric Block Established]

    B3 --> C1[8 Plane Sections Remain Plane]
    C1 --> C2[9 Cross Sections Rotate as Rigid Discs]
    C2 --> C3[10 Radius Unchanged in Length]
    C3 --> C4[11 Distance Between Sections Unchanged]
    C4 --> K3[Kinematic Block Established]

    A0 --> D1[12 Pure Torsion Only]
    D1 --> K4[Loading Block Established]

    K1 --> FINAL[Combine All Four Blocks]
    K2 --> FINAL
    K3 --> FINAL
    K4 --> FINAL

    FINAL --> EQ[Torsion Equation T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
    EQ --> OUT1[Max Shear Stress tau_max at r = R]
    EQ --> OUT2[Angle of Twist theta equals TL over GJ]
    EQ --> OUT3[Polar Moment J for solid and hollow sections]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Deformation Geometry of a Twisted Shaft

This block illustrates how a surface fiber deforms from straight $AB$ to helical $AB'$, supporting the derivation in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Original Fiber AB] --> S1[Apply Torque T at Free End]
    S1 --> S2[Cross Section Rotates by theta radians]
    S2 --> S3[Point B moves to B-prime on arc radius R]
    S3 --> S4[Arc Length BB-prime equals R theta]
    S4 --> S5[Shear Strain gamma equals R theta over L]
    S5 --> S6[Hookes Law tau equals G gamma]
    S6 --> S7[Linear Interpolation tau at r equals tau_max times r over R]
    S7 --> S8[Integrate to get T equals G theta J over L]
    S8 --> S9[Final Torsion Equation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Torsion Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Given a Torsion Problem] --> Q1{Is Cross Section Circular?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2[Use Saint Venant Warping Theory or Thin Walled Approximation]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q3{Is Torque within Elastic Limit?}
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4[Use Plastic Torsion Theory tau_max equals 3T over 2 pi R cubed]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q5{Is Shaft Prismatic?}
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6[Integrate theta equals integral T over G J of x dx]
    Q5 -- YES --> Q7[Solid or Hollow?]
    Q7 -- SOLID --> Q8[Use J equals pi D to the 4 over 32]
    Q7 -- HOLLOW --> Q9[Use J equals pi over 32 times D outer to the 4 minus D inner to the 4]
    Q8 --> Q10[Apply Torsion Equation]
    Q9 --> Q10
    Q6 --> Q10
    Q10 --> Q11[Solve for T tau or theta]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** State the assumptions made in the derivation of the torsion equation for a circular shaft subjected to torsion within the elastic range.
