# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define Polar Moment of Inertia. State its units and write the formula relating it to the planar moments of inertia.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The polar moment of inertia of a plane area about a point (pole) is defined as the integral of the product of the elementary area and the square of its perpendicular distance from that point:
$$J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

**Units (1 mark):** SI: $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$; FPS: $\text{in}^4$.

**Relation (1 mark):** By the Perpendicular Axis Theorem, $J_O = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$, where $I_{xx}$ and $I_{yy}$ are the planar moments of inertia about two mutually perpendicular axes through the pole, lying in the plane of the area.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**For a solid circular shaft of diameter $60 \text{ mm}$, compute the polar moment of inertia and the polar section modulus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $D = 60 \text{ mm}$.

**Polar Moment of Inertia (2 marks):**
$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (60)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32} = 1{,}272{,}345 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Polar Section Modulus (1 mark):**
$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 216{,}000}{16} = 42{,}411.5 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 4.241 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module 3 pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students choose **one** of two alternatives.

---

### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter $80 \text{ mm}$ transmits a torque of $4 \text{ kN┬Ęm}$. Determine:
   (i) The polar moment of inertia of the cross-section.
   (ii) The maximum shear stress induced in the shaft.
   (iii) The polar section modulus.

**(b)** For the same shaft, calculate the polar radius of gyration. If a hollow shaft of the same material must carry the same torque with the **same maximum shear stress**, find the inner diameter when the outer diameter is $100 \text{ mm}$. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$ (not needed for stress equality ŌĆö only for context).

#### Model Solution to Q-A:

**(a) (i) Polar Moment of Inertia (3 marks):**
$$D = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad R = 40 \text{ mm}$$
$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 80^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40{,}960{,}000}{32} = 4{,}021{,}238.6 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 4.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**(a) (ii) Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks):**
$T = 4 \text{ kN┬Ęm} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}$
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 40}{4.021 \times 10^6} = 39.79 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 39.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**(a) (iii) Polar Section Modulus (2 marks):**
$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{4.021 \times 10^6}{40} = 1{,}00{,}530.97 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 1.005 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**(b) Polar Radius of Gyration (3 marks):**
Area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2$
$$k_p = \sqrt{\frac{J}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.021 \times 10^6}{5026.55}} = \sqrt{800} = 28.28 \text{ mm}$$

(Equivalent: $k_p = D/4 = 80/4 = 20 \text{ mm}$ ŌĆö wait, this is wrong; let me re-check. For solid circular, $k_p = D/4$? Actually:
$$k_p^2 = D^2/8 \Rightarrow k_p = D/\sqrt{8} = D/2\sqrt{2} = 80/2.828 = 28.28 \text{ mm} \;\checkmark$$
The earlier formula $k_p = D/4$ was a typo ŌĆö corrected value is $k_p = D/(2\sqrt{2})$.

**Hollow shaft inner diameter (4 marks):**
Equating $\tau_{\max}$ for same torque:
$$\frac{T}{Z_p} = \text{const} \Rightarrow Z_p \text{ must be equal}$$
For solid: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16} = \dfrac{\pi (80)^3}{16} = 1.005 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$

For hollow with $D_o = 100 \text{ mm}$:
$$Z_p = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \frac{D_o^4 - d_i^4}{D_o} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \frac{100^4 - d_i^4}{100} = \frac{\pi}{1600}(10^8 - d_i^4)$$

Setting equal:
$$\frac{\pi}{1600}(10^8 - d_i^4) = 1.005 \times 10^5$$
$$10^8 - d_i^4 = \frac{1.005 \times 10^5 \times 1600}{\pi} = 5.12 \times 10^7$$
$$d_i^4 = 10^8 - 5.12 \times 10^7 = 4.88 \times 10^7$$
$$d_i = (4.88 \times 10^7)^{1/4} = 83.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** Inner diameter $\approx 83.55 \text{ mm}$. Note that $d_i < D_o$ Ō£ō (valid hollow section).

---

### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** The moment of inertia of a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$ about its horizontal centroidal axis is $I_{xx} = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$. Using the perpendicular axis theorem, derive an expression for the polar moment of inertia of the rectangle about the corner where the centroidal axes meet.

**(b)** A T-section has flange dimensions $120 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ and web dimensions $20 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}$. Compute the polar moment of inertia about the centroid of the entire section. Assume the centroidal axes are aligned with the symmetry axes of the T.

#### Model Solution to Q-B:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the rectangle have width $b$ (horizontal) and depth $d$ (vertical), with the origin at the centroid $O$.

**Step 1:** Write the known planar moments about the centroid.
$$I_{xx} = \frac{bd^3}{12}, \quad I_{yy} = \frac{db^3}{12}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Perpendicular Axis Theorem at the centroid:
$$J_O = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = \frac{bd^3}{12} + \frac{db^3}{12} = \frac{bd(d^2 + b^2)}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Use parallel axis theorem to shift from centroid $O$ to corner $C$.

The horizontal distance from $O$ to corner $C$ is $b/2$; the vertical distance is $d/2$.
$$J_C = J_O + A \cdot (\text{distance from } O \text{ to } C)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the perpendicular distance from $O$ to corner $C$:
$$r_{OC} = \sqrt{(b/2)^2 + (d/2)^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{b^2 + d^2}$$

**Step 5:** Square it:
$$r_{OC}^2 = \frac{b^2 + d^2}{4}$$

**Step 6:** Apply parallel axis theorem:
$$J_C = J_O + A \cdot r_{OC}^2 = \frac{bd(d^2 + b^2)}{12} + (bd) \cdot \frac{b^2 + d^2}{4}$$

**Step 7:** Factor $\dfrac{bd(b^2 + d^2)}{1}$ and combine:
$$J_C = bd(b^2 + d^2)\left[\frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{4}\right] = bd(b^2 + d^2)\left[\frac{1 + 3}{12}\right] = bd(b^2 + d^2) \cdot \frac{4}{12}$$

$$\boxed{J_C = \frac{bd(b^2 + d^2)}{3}}$$

**(b) T-Section Computation (7 marks):**

Divide T-section into two rectangles:
- **Flange (Rectangle 1):** $b_1 = 120 \text{ mm}$, $d_1 = 20 \text{ mm}$, Area $A_1 = 2400 \text{ mm}^2$
- **Web (Rectangle 2):** $b_2 = 20 \text{ mm}$, $d_2 = 100 \text{ mm}$, Area $A_2 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 1:** Locate centroid (measuring $y$ from bottom of web).
$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_1 \bar{y}_1 + A_2 \bar{y}_2}{A_1 + A_2} = \frac{2400 \times 110 + 2000 \times 50}{4400} = \frac{264000 + 100000}{4400} = \frac{364000}{4400} = 82.73 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $I_{xx}$ about the centroidal $x$-axis (using parallel axis theorem).
For Flange: $I_{xx,1} = \dfrac{120 \times 20^3}{12} + 2400 \times (110 - 82.73)^2 = 80{,}000 + 2400 \times (27.27)^2$
$= 80{,}000 + 2400 \times 743.65 = 80{,}000 + 1{,}784{,}768 = 1{,}864{,}768 \text{ mm}^4$

For Web: $I_{xx,2} = \dfrac{20 \times 100^3}{12} + 2000 \times (82.73 - 50)^2 = 1{,}666{,}667 + 2000 \times (32.73)^2$
$= 1{,}666{,}667 + 2000 \times 1071.25 = 1{,}666{,}667 + 2{,}142{,}500 = 3{,}809{,}167 \text{ mm}^4$

$$I_{xx,\text{total}} = 1{,}864{,}768 + 3{,}809{,}167 = 5{,}673{,}935 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3:** Compute $I_{yy}$ about the centroidal $y$-axis (by symmetry, $y$-centroid lies on the symmetry axis, no horizontal shift for the web; flange extends on both sides of web, so $I_{yy}$ uses parallel axis theorem horizontally).

For Flange: $I_{yy,1} = \dfrac{20 \times 120^3}{12} + 2400 \times (60 - 10)^2 = 2{,}880{,}000 + 2400 \times 2500 = 2{,}880{,}000 + 6{,}000{,}000 = 8{,}880{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$
*(Flange centroid is 60 mm from origin in y; web centre is 10 mm from origin in y.)*

For Web: $I_{yy,2} = \dfrac{100 \times 20^3}{12} + 2000 \times 0^2 = 66{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$
*(Web is centered on the y-axis.)*

$$I_{yy,\text{total}} = 8{,}880{,}000 + 66{,}667 = 8{,}946{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4:** Apply Perpendicular Axis Theorem.
$$J = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = 5{,}673{,}935 + 8{,}946{,}667 = 14{,}620{,}602 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{J \approx 1.462 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $J$ in $\text{m}^4$ when all dimensions are in $\text{mm}$. Internal consistency is mandatory. If you start in $\text{mm}$, keep $J$ in $\text{mm}^4$.
>
> 2. **Formula misidentification:** Using $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$ for a **rectangular** or **T-section** ŌĆö this formula applies **only** to circular geometry. For non-circular sections, you **must** use $J = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ via the Perpendicular Axis Theorem.
>
> 3. **Inner diameter oversight:** For hollow shafts, students forget to subtract $d^4$ and only write the outer term. Always show the full expression $\dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}$.
>
> 4. **Parallel Axis Theorem sign error:** The term $A \cdot d^2$ is **always positive** (added, not subtracted). Markers deduct 1 full mark if sign convention is violated.
>
> 5. **Final answer without units:** In KTU answer sheets, omitting units for $J$, $Z_p$, or $k_p$ attracts a **mandatory ┬Į mark deduction**.
>
> 6. **Confusing polar $J$ with mass moment of inertia $I$:** Mentioning "$I = mr^2$" or units of $\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$ in a Solid Mechanics problem will be marked **wrong** even if numerically close.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Polar Moment of Inertia (PCRUT302 ŌĆō Module 3):**

- **Definition:** $J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA$ ŌĆö second moment of area about a **point** (pole).
- **Unit:** $\text{mm}^4$ (NOT $\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$ ŌĆö that's mass moment of inertia).
- **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** $J = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ ŌĆö valid only for **plane laminas**, only about **axes in the plane through the same point**.
- **Solid Circular:** $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$; $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}$; $k_p = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{2}}$.
- **Hollow Circular:** $J = \dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}$; $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$; $k_p = \dfrac{\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}}{2\sqrt{2}}$.
- **Torsion Linkage:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{TR}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$; $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ ŌĆö both formulas depend critically on $J$.
- **Rectangle about corner:** $J_C = \dfrac{bd(b^2 + d^2)}{3}$ ŌĆö high-yield formula for short questions.
- **Radius of gyration link:** $J = A \cdot k_p^2$ ŌĆö useful when $J$ is unknown but $k_p$ is given.
- **Always** show unit consistency throughout the solution; **never** confuse $\pi D^4/32$ with $\pi D^4/64$ (the latter is $I_x$ for solid circular, not $J$).
- **Hollow shafts save weight:** For the same $J$, a hollow circular section weighs significantly less than a solid circular section ŌĆö a frequent design question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Design of Transmission Shafts. Types of Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Design of Transmission Shafts. Types of Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Basic Design of Transmission Shafts

### ­¤Ä» Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Shaft:** A transmission shaft is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, whose primary function is to transmit **power (torque)** from one location to another within a mechanical power transmission system. It also carries transverse loads (bending moments) imposed by gears, pulleys, sprockets, or other power-transmitting components mounted on it.

> **Power Transmitted Equation:**
> $$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$
> where $P$ = power in kW, $N$ = speed in rpm, $T$ = torque in N┬Ęm.

### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ŌĆō The Spinning Rod of a Bicycle:**
> Imagine the long metal rod that connects the pedals to the rear wheel of a bicycle. When you push the pedal, you apply a *twisting force* (torque). The rod then "transmits" this twist to the rear wheel, making it spin. Now, what happens if the cyclist leans sideways or stands up? The rod experiences a *bending force* along with the twist. So a real shaft, like the bicycle rod, almost always carries **combined torsion and bending**. The job of shaft design is to make the rod thick enough to safely handle both effects simultaneously ŌĆö just like a bicycle's chainstay must be strong in both twist and bend.

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Standard Engineering Constants & Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Allowable Shear Stress (mild steel) | $\tau_{allow}$ | **40 ŌĆō 80 MPa** | Used in basic design |
| Allowable Shear Stress (alloy steel) | $\tau_{allow}$ | **60 ŌĆō 120 MPa** | After heat treatment |
| Modulus of Rigidity | $G$ | **80 GPa (steel)** | For rigidity check |
| Bending/Torsion Stress Concentration Factor | $K_t, K_m$ | **1.5 ŌĆō 3.0** | For fatigue / fluctuating loads |
| Design Factor of Safety | $n$ | **1.5 ŌĆō 4.0** | Application dependent |

> **Types of Shafts (brief):**
> - **Machine Shafts** ŌĆō mounted on bearings; transmit power between components in a machine.
> - **Transmission Shafts** ŌĆō long shafts connecting two distant units (e.g., line shafts, countershafts). They carry only torsion and self-weight bending.
> - **Axle / Spindles** ŌĆō non-rotating or rotating shafts carrying transverse loads (no torque).

---

## 1.2 Types of Beams

### ­¤Ä» Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beam:** A structural member subjected to transverse loads (forces or moments acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis) is called a **beam**. Beams transfer these loads to the supports through **bending**, **shear**, and sometimes **torsion**.

### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆō The Diving Board:**
> Picture a diver standing at the free end of a diving board. One end is firmly clamped to the pool deck (fixed end), the other end is in the air (free end). The diver's weight makes the board bend downward. This is a **cantilever beam** ŌĆö fixed at one end, free at the other. Now imagine placing a heavy weight in the middle of a plank supported on two bricks. The plank bends between the two supports. This is a **simply supported beam**. Every beam in real life falls into one of these two categories, or a combination of them.

### ­¤ōŗ Quick Classification Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Classification ŌĆō Based on Support Conditions:**
> 1. **Simply Supported Beam** ŌĆō pin at one end, roller at the other.
> 2. **Overhanging Beam** ŌĆō simply supported span extended beyond one or both supports.
> 3. **Cantilever Beam** ŌĆō fixed at one end, completely free at the other.
> 4. **Fixed Beam (Built-in / Encastered)** ŌĆō fixed at both ends.
> 5. **Continuous Beam** ŌĆō more than two supports (rest is continuous).
> 6. **Propped Cantilever** ŌĆō cantilever with an additional simple support somewhere in the span.

> **Secondary Classification ŌĆō Based on Geometry / Loading:**
> - Straight, Curved, Tapered beams.
> - Determinate vs Indeterminate beams.
> - Statically determinate (3 equilibrium equations sufficient) vs statically indeterminate (extra compatibility equations required).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆō Shaft Design

A transmission shaft in service is subjected to:
- **Torsional moment ($T$)** ŌĆō from power transmission.
- **Bending moment ($M$)** ŌĆō from transverse forces of gears/pulleys (radial loads).
- Sometimes **axial thrust** ŌĆō from helical gears (negligible in basic design).

### ­¤ö¼ Why a "Combined Loading" Approach?

When a shaft carries both $M$ and $T$, the surface fibers experience:
- **Bending stress (normal):** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^{3}}$
- **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau_t = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$

At the **critical surface point** (typically at 45┬░ to the shaft axis on the outer fiber), the stress state is **biaxial** ŌĆö a normal stress $\sigma_b$ and a shear stress $\tau_t$ act simultaneously.

The **principal stresses** are:

$$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^{\,2}}$$

The **maximum shear stress** in the element is:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^{\,2}}$$

### ­¤Ä» The "Equivalent Torque" Concept

The KTU board examiner's favourite trick ŌĆö converting a combined loading problem into a "pure torsion" problem using a fictitious **equivalent torque** $T_e$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Torque ŌĆō Static / Steady Loading (Maximum Shear Stress Theory):**
> $$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$
> This comes from setting $\tau_{\max} = \tau_{allow}$.

> **Equivalent Torque ŌĆō Fluctuating / Fatigue Loading (ASME Code):**
> $$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{(K_m \cdot M)^2 + (K_t \cdot T)^2}}$$
> where $K_m$ = fatigue stress concentration factor for bending, $K_t$ = fatigue stress concentration factor for torsion.

### ­¤ōÉ Shaft Diameter Equation (Derivation in Section 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Design Equation for Solid Circular Shaft:**
> $$\boxed{d = \left(\frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}}$$
> For a **hollow shaft** (outer dia $D$, inner dia $d$):
> $$\boxed{D = \left(\frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow} \, (1 - k^4)}\right)^{1/3}}, \quad k = d/D$$

### ­¤øĪ’ĖÅ Rigidity Check (Angular Twist Criterion)

For shafts requiring **limited twist** (e.g., line shafts in textile mills), diameter is governed by stiffness:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} \leq \theta_{allow}, \quad J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{d = \left(\frac{32 \, T \, L}{\pi^{2} \, G \, \theta_{allow}}\right)^{1/4}}$$

where $\theta$ is in radians per unit length, and $G$ is the modulus of rigidity.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆō Types of Beams

### ­¤¦® Statically Determinate vs Indeterminate Beams

| Feature | Determinate Beams | Indeterminate Beams |
|---|---|---|
| Unknown reactions | 3 | > 3 |
| Equations available | 3 (equilibrium only) | 3 + compatibility |
| Solution method | Equilibrium equations alone | Equilibrium + compatibility / Castigliano / Moment distribution |
| Examples | Simply supported, cantilever, simple overhang | Fixed beam, continuous beam, propped cantilever |
| Bending moment at supports | Zero for simply supported | Non-zero (depends on fixity) |

> **Reactive Components at Common Supports:**
> - **Pin / Hinge support:** provides $R_x$ and $R_y$ (2 reactions)
> - **Roller support:** provides only $R_y$ perpendicular to roller (1 reaction)
> - **Fixed support:** provides $R_x, R_y, M$ (3 reactions)

### ­¤öü Why is this in Solid Mechanics? The Bridge to Bending Stresses

> [!NOTE]
> The classification of beams matters because **the bending moment diagram (BMD) shape** ŌĆö and hence the **maximum bending stress** $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ ŌĆö depends directly on the support type. A fixed beam carries a smaller mid-span moment than a simply supported beam, but it carries a non-zero **hogging moment at the supports**. This is why the moment of resistance formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ is **always paired with the correct BMD** for the given support type.

---

## 2.3 ­¤ōŖ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print-and-Pin Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power transmitted | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW when $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N┬Ęm |
| 2 | Torque from power | $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \, P}{2\pi N}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 3 | Bending stress (solid shaft) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Pa |
| 4 | Torsional shear stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Pa |
| 5 | Max shear stress (combined) | $\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^{\,2}}$ | Pa |
| 6 | Equivalent torque (static) | $T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | Equivalent torque (fatigue) | $T_e = \sqrt{(K_m M)^{2} + (K_t T)^{2}}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 8 | Shaft diameter (strength) | $d = \left(\dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}$ | m (convert to mm for practical) |
| 9 | Shaft diameter (rigidity) | $d = \left(\dfrac{32 T L}{\pi^{2} G \theta_{allow}}\right)^{1/4}$ | m |
| 10 | Section modulus (solid circular) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ | m┬│ |
| 11 | Polar section modulus | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}$ | m┬│ |
| 12 | Polar moment of inertia | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | mŌü┤ |
| 13 | Hollow shaft diameter | $D = \left(\dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow} (1 - k^{4})}\right)^{1/3}$ | m |
| 14 | Angle of twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | rad |
| 15 | Simply supported ŌĆō max BM (UDL $w$) | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{8}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 16 | Cantilever ŌĆō max BM (point load $P$ at end) | $M_{\max} = P L$ | N┬Ęm |
| 17 | Fixed beam ŌĆō support moment (UDL) | $M_{\text{support}} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{12}$ | N┬Ęm (hogging) |
| 18 | Fixed beam ŌĆō mid-span moment (UDL) | $M_{\text{mid}} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{24}$ | N┬Ęm (sagging) |
| 19 | Reactions ŌĆō simply supported (UDL) | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w L}{2}$ | N |
| 20 | Reactions ŌĆō simply supported (point load $P$ at centre) | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **In all formulas above, use SI units throughout.** Common KTU pitfall: mixing $N$ with $mm$ or $MPa$ with $N/m^{2}$. Always do the **dimensional audit** before submitting your answer script.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Automotive Industry:** Drive shafts, propeller shafts, and half-shafts in cars are designed using $T_e$ = $\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$.
> - **Industrial Machinery:** Line shafts in textile mills, rolling mills, and power plants must satisfy **both strength and rigidity** criteria.
> - **Civil/Structural:** Beam-type classification drives the choice of construction ŌĆö bridges are mostly continuous beams, canopies are cantilevers, slabs in buildings are fixed beams.
> - **Aerospace:** Hollow transmission shafts are preferred in aircraft (high strength-to-weight), governed by ASME-style fatigue design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Equivalent Torque Formula (Strength Basis)

We will derive $T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ rigorously from the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca criterion)**.

### ­¤¦« Step 0 ŌĆö Setup

Consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ subjected to:
- A **bending moment** $M$ (causing $\sigma_b$ at the extreme fiber)
- A **torque** $T$ (causing $\tau_t$ at the outer surface)

At the most stressed point on the outer surface, we have a stress element with:
- Normal stress $\sigma_b$ (along the shaft axis)
- Shear stress $\tau_t$ (on the cross-section)

### ­¤¦« Step 1 ŌĆö Express Each Stress in Terms of $M$ and $T$

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M}{\pi d^{3}/32} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$$

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{T}{\pi d^{3}/16} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$

> **Reasoning:** $Z$ is the elastic section modulus for bending and $Z_p = 2Z$ is the polar section modulus. These follow from $I = \pi d^{4}/64$ and $J = \pi d^{4}/32$.

### ­¤¦« Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory

The maximum shear stress in a 2-D stress element with $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$ and $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t$ is:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_t^{\,2}}$$

Substitute the expressions from Step 1:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}$$

### ­¤¦« Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$$

### ­¤¦« Step 4 ŌĆö Equate to Allowable Shear Stress

For safe design, $\tau_{\max} \leq \tau_{allow}$:

$$\frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \tau_{allow}$$

$$\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{\pi d^{3} \, \tau_{allow}}{16}$$

### ­¤¦« Step 5 ŌĆö Define Equivalent Torque

By definition, the **equivalent torque** $T_e$ is the fictitious pure-torsion torque that would produce the same maximum shear stress:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$$

(Units: N┬Ęm, consistent with $M$ and $T$.)

### ­¤¦« Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for Diameter

Substituting $T_e$:

$$\frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi d^{3}} = \tau_{allow} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^{3} = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}$$

$$\boxed{\,d = \left(\frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}\,}$$

> **Key Takeaway:** The "equivalent torque" trick lets you treat a combined-bending-and-torsion problem as a simple torsion problem. This is the bedrock of KTU's shaft design module.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Rigidity-Based Shaft Diameter

When the **angular twist per unit length** $\theta$ (in rad/m or ┬░/m) is the design constraint:

### ­¤¦« Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the Torsion Formula

$$\theta = \frac{T}{G J} \quad \text{(radians per unit length)}$$

For a solid shaft, $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$.

### ­¤¦« Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $J$

$$\theta = \frac{32 \, T}{G \, \pi \, d^{4}}$$

### ­¤¦« Step 3 ŌĆö Set $\theta \leq \theta_{allow}$ and Solve for $d$

$$\frac{32 \, T}{G \, \pi \, d^{4}} = \theta_{allow}$$

$$d^{4} = \frac{32 \, T}{G \, \pi \, \theta_{allow}}$$

$$\boxed{\,d = \left(\frac{32 \, T}{G \, \pi \, \theta_{allow}}\right)^{1/4}\,}$$

> **Note:** In some textbooks the term $L$ appears when the total twist $\phi$ is constrained ($\phi = TL/(GJ)$). KTU's basic-design module typically uses the **per-unit-length** form. Read the question carefully!

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (Valuation Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 pattern]:**
> A solid circular transmission shaft is required to transmit **75 kW at 144 rpm**. The shaft also carries a **bending moment of 2 kN┬Ęm** at the critical section due to gear loading. Design the shaft diameter using maximum shear stress theory, taking $\tau_{allow} = 50\ \text{MPa}$.

### ­¤¦« Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the torque from power:**

$$T = \frac{60{,}000 \, P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 75}{2\pi \times 144}$$

$$T = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{904.78} \approx 4{,}973.6\ \text{N┬Ęm} \approx 4.974\ \text{kN┬Ęm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify bending moment:**

$$M = 2{,}000\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute equivalent torque (static loading):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \sqrt{(2{,}000)^{2} + (4{,}973.6)^{2}}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{6} + 24.74 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{28.74 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_e \approx 5{,}361.0\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute shaft diameter:**

$$d^{3} = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} = \frac{16 \times 5{,}361.0}{\pi \times 50 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{85{,}776}{1.5708 \times 10^{8}} = 5.461 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$d = (5.461 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} \approx 0.0816\ \text{m} \approx 81.6\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Adopt a standard diameter (next higher standard):**

$$\boxed{d_{standard} = 85\ \text{mm}}$$

> **Check (informational):** $\tau_{actual} = \dfrac{16 \times 5{,}361}{\pi \times (0.085)^{3}} = \dfrac{85{,}776}{1.928 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 44.5\ \text{MPa} < 50\ \text{MPa}$ Ō£ģ

---

## 3.4 Beam Support Reaction Analysis (Reference Table for KTU)

| Beam Type | Support Reactions (UDL $w$ over full span $L$) | Max BM Location | Max BM Value | Deflection Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simply Supported** | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | Mid-span | $wL^{2}/8$ (sagging) | Sagging only |
| **Cantilever (Fixed-Free)** | $R = wL$, $M_f = wL^{2}/2$ | Fixed end | $wL^{2}/2$ (hogging) | Hogging only |
| **Propped Cantilever** | $R_A = 3wL/8$, $R_B = 5wL/8$ | Fixed end | $wL^{2}/8$ (hogging) | Both signs |
| **Fixed-Fixed Beam** | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | Supports and mid | $wL^{2}/12$ (hogging at supports), $wL^{2}/24$ (sagging at mid) | Both signs, smaller magnitude |
| **Overhanging (one side)** | $R_A = wL_{1}/2 + wL_{2}^{2}/(2L_{1})$ | Support and overhang root | Depends on lengths | Both signs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactive counts (for determinacy check):**
> - **Simply supported beam** = 2 supports (1 pin + 1 roller) = 3 reactions ŌåÆ **statically determinate** Ō£ģ
> - **Cantilever** = 1 fixed support = 3 reactions, 3 equilibrium equations ŌåÆ **determinate** Ō£ģ
> - **Fixed-Fixed Beam** = 2 fixed supports = 6 reactions > 3 ŌåÆ **indeterminate to degree 3** ŌØī
> - **Continuous beam with $n$ spans** = $n+1$ supports (each contributing 2 reactions in 2-D) ŌåÆ **highly indeterminate**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shaft Design Procedure (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Identify Service Conditions] --> B[Step2: Compute Torque T from Power and Speed]
    B --> C[Step3: Compute Bending Moment M from Radial Loads]
    C --> D{Step4: Loading Nature?}
    D -->|Static or Steady| E[Use Te equals sqrt M squared plus T squared]
    D -->|Fluctuating or Fatigue| F[Use Te equals sqrt KmM squared plus KtT squared]
    E --> G[Step5: Select Allowable Shear Stress from Material Data]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step6: Compute d equals cube root of 16Te divided by pi tau]
    H --> I[Step7: Apply Rigidity Check if Required]
    I --> J{Rigidity Satisfied?}
    J -->|No| K[Revise d upward]
    K --> I
    J -->|Yes| L[Step8: Select Next Higher Standard Diameter]
    L --> M[Step9: Verify with Bearing and Keyway Design]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a sequential step a designer follows. The "feedback arrow" between Step 7 and Step 8 is critical ŌĆö the rigidity check may force you to increase $d$ above what the strength criterion demands. **Always check both criteria** before finalizing the diameter.

---

## 4.2 Beam Classification (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BEAMS] --> B[By Support Conditions]
    A --> C[By Geometry]
    A --> D[By Static Determinacy]

    B --> B1[Simply Supported Pin and Roller]
    B --> B2[Overhanging Extended beyond supports]
    B --> B3[Fixed Beam Fixed at both ends]
    B --> B4[Continuous Beam More than two supports]
    B --> B5[Propped Cantilever Fixed plus simple support]
    B --> B6[Pure Cantilever Fixed plus free end]

    C --> C1[Straight Beam]
    C --> C2[Curved Beam]
    C --> C3[Tapered Beam]

    D --> D1[Determinate Solvable by 3 equations]
    D --> D2[Indeterminate Requires compatibility equations]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Beam classification is a *tree* with three independent root branches: support type, geometry, and determinacy. **One physical beam** can simultaneously belong to multiple categories ŌĆö e.g., a "straight, indeterminate, fixed beam" is a real engineering object.

---

## 4.3 Combined Loading on Shaft (Block-Level Stress Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Loads: Torque T and Bending M] --> B[Section Moduli Z and Zp]
    B --> C[Stresses sigma b and tau t]
    C --> D[Maximum Shear Stress Criterion]
    D --> E[Equivalent Torque Te]
    E --> F[Master Diameter Equation]
    F --> G[Selected Standard Diameter d]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** External loads $\rightarrow$ cross-sectional geometry $\rightarrow$ element stresses $\rightarrow$ failure criterion $\rightarrow$ equivalent single-torque problem $\rightarrow$ sizing equation $\rightarrow$ final design.

---

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic of a Transmission Shaft with Pulley

