```mermaid
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Identify Cross Section Shape and Dimensions]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Compute Moment of Inertia I about Buckling Axis]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Compute Area A and Radius of Gyration k]
    stepA6 --> stepA7[Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda equals Le over k]
    stepA7 --> stepB1{Slenderness Check}
    stepB1 -- Lambda greater than 80 --> pathA[Use EULER FORMULA Pcr equals pi squared EI over Le squared]
    stepB1 -- 30 less than lambda less than 80 --> pathB[Use RANKINE FORMULA for Intermediate Columns]
    stepB1 -- Lambda less than 30 --> pathC[Use CRUSHING LOAD Pc equals sigma c times A]
    pathA --> resultA[Compute Critical Load in Newtons or kiloNewtons]
    pathB --> resultB[Compute Critical Load via Rankine Empirical Formula]
    pathC --> resultC[Compute Crushing Load NOT Buckling]
    resultA --> finalA[FINAL ANSWER: Crippling Load with Units]
    resultB --> finalA
    resultC --> finalA
    finalA --> endA[END]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Four End Conditions and Effective Lengths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caseA[Case 1: Both Ends Hinged] -- Le equals L --> coefA[alpha equals 1.0]
    caseB[Case 2: Both Ends Fixed] -- Le equals L over 2 --> coefB[alpha equals 0.5]
    caseC[Case 3: One Fixed One Hinged] -- Le equals L over sqrt 2 --> coefC[alpha equals 0.707]
    caseD[Case 4: One Fixed One Free] -- Le equals 2L --> coefD[alpha equals 2.0]
    coefA --> formula[Universal Euler Formula Pcr equals pi squared EI over Le squared]
    coefB --> formula
    coefC --> formula
    coefD --> formula
    formula --> output[Output Critical Load]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Table of Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fmodeA[Long Slender Column] -- Euler Buckling --> elBuck[Elastic Lateral Deflection]
    fmodeB[Intermediate Column] -- Rankine Formula --> inBuck[Inelastic Combined Crushing and Buckling]
    fmodeC[Short Stocky Column] -- Direct Crushing --> dirCr[Material Yield or Crush Failure]
    elBuck --> noteA[Material stays in elastic range]
    inBuck --> noteB[Partial yielding plus buckling]
    dirCr --> noteC[No buckling occurs]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Critical Load" of a column. State Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column with both ends hinged.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** The critical load of a column is the *minimum axial compressive load* at which a slender column, when perfectly straight and loaded axially, becomes unstable and buckles sideways by a finite deflection, without any increase in load. Below this load, the column is in stable equilibrium and returns to its straight position when disturbed.

**Euler's Formula for Both Ends Hinged (1.5 Marks):**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

Where $E$ = Young's Modulus, $I$ = Moment of Inertia about the axis of buckling, $L$ = length of column. For both ends hinged, $L_e = L$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by effective length of a column? Write the effective length for a column fixed at both ends.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Effective Length Definition (1.5 Marks):** The effective length $L_e$ of a column is the length of an equivalent *pinned-pinned column* that has the same buckling load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is also called the *equivalent length* or *reduced length*.

**Effective Length for Both Ends Fixed (1.5 Marks):**

$$L_e \;=\; \dfrac{L}{2}$$

Since the effective length coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$ for both ends fixed.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Value Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always convert all units to be consistent** (mm with N/mm┬▓, or m with N/m┬▓). Mixing units is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in 14-mark questions. Maximum loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Use the WEAKER axis** (minimum $I$) for buckling calculations. Using the major axis $I_x$ instead of minor $I_y$ gives an *overestimated* load ŌĆö full marks will be deducted for this fundamental error.
> 3. **Show the substitution step** in Euler's formula. Examiners explicitly look for the step where you write $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2 = $ numerical value. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **State the end condition clearly** before computing $L_e$. Examiners reward students who explicitly write "Since both ends are fixed, $L_e = L/2$."

---

### Question B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A built-up column consists of two ISMC 250 channels placed back-to-back at a clear gap of 100 mm between their webs. The column is 6 m long and is effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation. Using Euler's formula, calculate the safe load with a factor of safety of 3. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓. Given: Area of one ISMC 250 channel = 3170 mm┬▓, $I_{xx} = 4460 \times 10^4$ mmŌü┤, $I_{yy} = 293 \times 10^4$ mmŌü┤, $C_{yy} = 23.5$ mm.**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Determine the effective length and radius of gyration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify End Condition:**
Both ends fixed-in-position (held in position) but not restrained against rotation = both ends **hinged (pinned)**.

Therefore, $L_e = L = 6 \text{ m} = 6000 \text{ mm}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Radius of Gyration of the Built-Up Section:**
The buckling will occur about the **y-y axis** (the weaker axis). The channels are placed back-to-back with 100 mm gap. The moment of inertia about y-y for the built-up section:

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times [I_{yy,channel} + A_{channel} \times (C_{yy} + \text{gap}/2)^{2}]$$

Distance from channel centroid to y-y axis of built-up section = $C_{yy} + \dfrac{100}{2} = 23.5 + 50 = 73.5$ mm.

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times [293 \times 10^4 + 3170 \times (73.5)^{2}]$$

$$(73.5)^{2} = 5402.25 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$3170 \times 5402.25 = 17{,}125{,}132.5 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 1712.5 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times [293 \times 10^4 + 1712.5 \times 10^4] = 2 \times 2005.5 \times 10^4 = 4011 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Area and Radius of Gyration:**

$$A_{total} = 2 \times 3170 = 6340 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k_{yy} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy,total}}{A_{total}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4011 \times 10^4}{6340}}$$

$$\dfrac{4011 \times 10^4}{6340} = 6326.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k_{yy} = \sqrt{6326.5} = 79.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Compute the Euler critical load and the safe load. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Euler's Formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I_{yy,total}}{L_{e}^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (4011 \times 10^4)}{(6000)^{2}}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4011 \times 10^4 = 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4011 \times 10^4$$

$$= 9.8696 \times 8.022 \times 10^{12} = 7.9178 \times 10^{13}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$(6000)^{2} = 36 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{7.9178 \times 10^{13}}{36 \times 10^{6}} = 2.199 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 2199.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Factor of Safety:**

$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{\text{FS}} = \dfrac{2199.4}{3} = 733.1 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 733.1 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### Question B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A 2 m long steel column has a circular cross-section of 50 mm diameter. One end of the column is fixed and the other end is free. Calculate: (a) The Euler's crippling load (b) The slenderness ratio and crippling stress. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓.**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Calculate the Euler Crippling Load. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify End Condition:**
One end fixed, the other end free = **Cantilever Column**.

For a cantilever column, the effective length coefficient is $\alpha = 2.0$.

$$L_e = 2L = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ m} = 4000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate the Moment of Inertia:**
For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d = 50$ mm:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times (50)^{4}}{64}$$

$$50^{4} = 6{,}250{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \times 6{,}250{,}000}{64} = 306{,}796.16 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 3.068 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Euler's Formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times (2.1 \times 10^5) \times (3.068 \times 10^{5})}{(4000)^{2}}$$

Numerator:

$$9.8696 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5 = 9.8696 \times 6.4428 \times 10^{10}$$

$$= 6.3583 \times 10^{11}$$

Denominator: $(4000)^2 = 16 \times 10^6$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{6.3583 \times 10^{11}}{16 \times 10^6} = 39{,}739 \text{ N} \approx 39.74 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 39.74 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Calculate the Slenderness Ratio and Crippling Stress. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (50)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2500}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate the Radius of Gyration:**

$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.068 \times 10^5}{1963.5}} = \sqrt{156.25} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

(Verification: For a solid circle, $k = d/4 = 50/4 = 12.5$ mm Ō£ō)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate the Slenderness Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{4000}{12.5} = 320$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate the Crippling Stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{39{,}739}{1963.5} = 20.24 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 20.24 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 20.24 \text{ MPa, and } \lambda = 320}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**

- **Definition Mastery:** Critical load = *minimum axial load causing lateral buckling* (NOT crushing) of a slender column.
- **Euler's Formula (MEMORIZE):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ ŌĆö units must be consistent.
- **End Conditions Table (MEMORIZE all four):**
  * Pinned-Pinned ŌåÆ $L_e = L$ ($\alpha = 1$)
  * Fixed-Fixed ŌåÆ $L_e = L/2$ ($\alpha = 0.5$)
  * Fixed-Pinned ŌåÆ $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$ ($\alpha = 1/\sqrt{2}$)
  * Fixed-Free ŌåÆ $L_e = 2L$ ($\alpha = 2$)
- **Slenderness Ratio Threshold:** $\lambda \geq 80$ for Euler's formula to be valid for steel.
- **Weaker Axis Rule:** Always use the **minimum** $I$ (weaker axis) for buckling calculations.
- **Crippling Stress Form:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ ŌĆö useful when only $E$ and slenderness ratio are given.
- **Radius of Gyration for Common Sections:**
  * Solid circle: $k = d/4$
  * Hollow circle: $k = \sqrt{(D^2 + d^2)/16}$
  * Solid rectangle: $k_{min} = b/\sqrt{12}$ (about the axis parallel to longer side)
- **Unit Consistency is Critical:** If $E$ is in N/mm┬▓, then $I$ in mmŌü┤ and $L_e$ in mm ŌåÆ $P_{cr}$ in N.
- **Euler's formula limitations:** Valid only for *long, slender, elastic* columns. For short columns, use Rankine's empirical formula.
- **Factor of Safety:** Safe Load $P_{safe} = P_{cr} / \text{FoS}$. Typical FoS for steel columns = 2 to 4.
- **Exam trap to avoid:** Don't confuse **crushing load** $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ (short column) with **crippling load** $P_{cr}$ (long column).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckling of Columns with Pinned Ends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling of Columns with Pinned Ends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Buckling Load (PinnedŌĆōPinned Column):** The crippling (critical) load at which a long, slender, straight column with both ends pinned (hinged) suddenly deflects laterally in a single curvature, sine-shaped buckled mode, is given by **Euler's Formula**:
> 
> $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$
> 
> where $E$ is the Young's modulus, $I$ is the least second moment of area of the cross-section, and $L$ is the actual length of the column (equal to its effective length $L_e$ in this end condition).

A column is said to **buckle** when it fails by sudden lateral deflection (sideways bending) rather than by direct compression or crushing. For long, slender columns made of ductile materials, this buckling failure is the governing design criterion and is governed by **classical Euler's theory of column buckling (1757)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Spaghetti Buckling" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine trying to push down on a thin, long, dry spaghetti stick from both ends using your thumb and index finger (the "pinned" condition). At first, it just compresses. But as you press harder, at some critical force, the stick does not get shorter ŌĆö instead it suddenly bows out sideways into a smooth "C" shape (a half-sine wave). That sideways snap is **buckling**. The force at which this happens is the **Euler critical load**. The same phenomenon happens with a thin metallic ruler held vertically on a table with your fingers just holding the top and bottom ŌĆö push down, and at some load it snaps sideways.

**Key Physical Intuition:**
- The longer the column ŌåÆ easier to buckle (lower $P_{cr}$, because $L$ is in the denominator squared).
- The stiffer the cross-section (larger $I$) ŌåÆ harder to buckle.
- The stiffer the material (larger $E$) ŌåÆ harder to buckle.
- The thinner the section (smaller radius of gyration $k$) ŌåÆ easier to buckle.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Steel) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus (Steel) | **$2.0 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$** | N/mm┬▓ (MPa) |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus (Wood) | **$1.0 \times 10^{4} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$** | N/mm┬▓ |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus (Cast Iron) | **$1.5 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$** | N/mm┬▓ |
| $L$ / $L_e$ | Length / Effective length | Case-dependent | mm |
| $I$ | Second moment of area | Cross-section dependent | mmŌü┤ |
| $k$ | Least radius of gyration | $\sqrt{I_{min}/A}$ | mm |
| $\lambda$ | Slenderness ratio | $L_e / k$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight ŌĆö Why this topic matters in Solid Mechanics:**
> A pinned-pinned column is the **baseline / reference case** from which ALL other end conditions (fixed-fixed, fixed-free, fixed-pinned) are derived by simply changing the **effective length** $L_e$. Mastering this case is therefore the gateway to the entire column-buckling module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Roadmap ŌĆö The "Why & How"

The Euler buckling problem for a pinned-pinned column is solved by a 5-step logical chain:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry of the buckled state:** Imagine the column has just buckled and has a small lateral deflection $y(x)$ at a distance $x$ from the bottom. The deflected shape is unknown a priori.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Bending equation:** Using the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, the bending moment at any section is $M = -P \cdot y$ (the axial load $P$ acting through the deflection $y$ creates a bending moment).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Differential equation:** Substituting into $EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M$, we get a second-order linear ODE.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Boundary conditions:** At both pinned ends ($x=0$ and $x=L$), the deflection $y=0$ but the slope is free.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Eigenvalue problem:** For a non-trivial deflected shape to exist, the boundary conditions force an eigenvalue constraint on $P$. The smallest such $P$ is the critical (crippling) load.

## 2.2 Assumptions of Euler's Theory (KTU 2024 Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> 1. The column is **initially perfectly straight**, and the load is **truly axial and concentric**.
> 2. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law** (stress $\propto$ strain).
> 3. The **self-weight** of the column is **neglected** compared to the applied load.
> 4. Failure occurs **only by buckling (bending)**, not by direct crushing.
> 5. **Shortening** of the column due to direct compressive stress is **neglected**.
> 6. **Flexural rigidity $EI$ is constant** along the length.
> 7. The **end conditions are ideal** (perfect pin / perfect fixity ŌĆö no friction, no restraint at the pins).
> 8. The critical load is the load at which the column **just begins to deflect laterally** (bifurcation load).
> 9. The deflections are **small** so that the small-deflection curvature approximation $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \approx \dfrac{M}{EI}$ is valid.
> 10. The column has a **uniform cross-section** along its length.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (PinnedŌĆōPinned Column)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | Euler critical (crippling) load | N |
| 2 | $L_{e} = L$ | Effective length (pinnedŌĆōpinned) | mm |
| 3 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E A}{(\lambda)^{2}}$ | Critical load in terms of slenderness ratio | N |
| 4 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Euler critical stress | N/mm┬▓ |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{e}}{k}$ | Slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$ | Least radius of gyration | mm |
| 7 | $I_{rect} = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | Rectangular section | mmŌü┤ |
| 8 | $I_{circ} = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$ | Solid circular section | mmŌü┤ |
| 9 | $I_{hollow} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$ | Hollow circular section | mmŌü┤ |
| 10 | $I_{hollow\,rect} = \dfrac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{12}$ | Hollow rectangular section | mmŌü┤ |
| 11 | $A_{circ} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$ | Hollow circular area | mm┬▓ |
| 12 | $A_{rect} = b \cdot d$ | Rectangular area | mm┬▓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use $I_{min}$** (the lesser of $I_{xx}$ or $I_{yy}$) because the column will buckle about the **weaker axis** first ŌĆö that is, the path of least resistance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pinned-pinned buckling model is directly used to design:

- **Truss members in steel bridges and roof structures** (pinned at both ends via gusset plates and bolts).
- **Compression chords of open-web steel joists**.
- **Long slender struts in machinery and aircraft structures**.
- **Connecting rods and push rods** in mechanical assemblies where end conditions approximate pinned-pinned.
- **Scaffolding poles and temporary props** in civil construction.

In practice, the pinned-pinned assumption is the **most conservative (lowest capacity)** among the four classical end conditions, so it is often used as a safe lower bound.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Critical Load for a PinnedŌĆōPinned Column

### Setting up the Problem

Consider a straight, prismatic, slender column of length $L$, with both ends pinned (hinged), subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ applied at the centroid of each end cross-section.

> **Let:** $y(x)$ be the lateral deflection of the column at a distance $x$ measured from the lower (left) end.

```
        P (axial)
        Ōåō
        |
       (pin) ŌåÉ A
        |
        |    Ōåæ y(x)
        |    |    /-ŌĆö\
        |    |   /     \   ŌåÉ Buckled shape (sine wave)
        |    |  /       \
        |    | /         \
        |    |/           \
        |
       (pin) ŌåÉ B
        |
        Ōåæ
        P (reaction)
```

### Step 1 ŌĆö Bending Moment at Section

Imagine the column has buckled slightly into the shape shown. Cut the column at a section located at distance $x$ from the bottom. Consider the equilibrium of the lower portion (from the bottom pin up to the cut section).

The internal bending moment at the cut section is generated by the axial force $P$ acting with an eccentricity equal to the lateral deflection $y$. With the sign convention that positive $y$ is to the right and the deflected shape is concave toward the load:

$$
M \;=\; -P \cdot y
$$

> **Reason:** The internal moment must balance the external moment $P \cdot y$ produced by the load acting at a horizontal offset $y$ from the cut. The negative sign is a standard sign-convention artifact (sagging positive, hogging negative depending on textbook).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the EulerŌĆōBernoulli Beam Equation

The fundamental differential equation for beam bending is:

$$
EI \, \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; M
$$

Substituting $M = -P \cdot y$:

$$
EI \, \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P \, y
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; \dfrac{P}{EI} \, y \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Define a New Constant and Solve the ODE

For algebraic simplicity, define:

$$
k^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{EI}
$$

The differential equation becomes:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; k^{2} y \;=\; 0
$$

This is a second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients. Its auxiliary (characteristic) equation is:

$$
m^{2} + k^{2} \;=\; 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m \;=\; \pm i k
$$

Since the roots are purely imaginary, the general solution is:

$$
y(x) \;=\; A \sin(kx) \;+\; B \cos(kx)
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants of integration to be determined from the boundary conditions.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Boundary Conditions (PinnedŌĆōPinned)

**Boundary Condition 1:** At the lower pin, $x = 0$, the deflection is zero.

$$
y(0) \;=\; 0
$$

Substituting $x = 0$ into the general solution:

$$
y(0) \;=\; A \sin(0) \;+\; B \cos(0) \;=\; 0 + B(1) \;=\; B
$$

Therefore:

$$
B \;=\; 0
$$

The general solution reduces to:

$$
y(x) \;=\; A \sin(kx)
$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At the upper pin, $x = L$, the deflection is zero.

$$
y(L) \;=\; 0
$$

Substituting:

$$
y(L) \;=\; A \sin(kL) \;=\; 0
$$

This product is zero if **either** $A = 0$ **or** $\sin(kL) = 0$.

- If $A = 0$, then $y(x) = 0$ everywhere ŌĆö meaning the column remains perfectly straight and unbuckled. This corresponds to the **trivial solution** and gives us no information about the critical load.
- For a **non-trivial deflected shape** (i.e., the column has actually buckled), we require:

$$
\sin(kL) \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Eigenvalue (Bifurcation) Condition

The condition $\sin(kL) = 0$ is satisfied when:

$$
kL \;=\; n \pi, \qquad n \;=\; 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

So:

$$
k \;=\; \dfrac{n \pi}{L}
$$

Squaring and recalling $k^{2} = P / (EI)$:

$$
k^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{n^{2} \pi^{2}}{L^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{EI}
$$

Solving for $P$:

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{n^{2} \pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Smallest Eigenvalue ŌåÆ Critical Load

The buckled shape corresponding to $n = 1$ is the **first (fundamental) mode**, with a single half-sine wave between the two pins. It is the lowest buckling load because $P \propto n^{2}$. Therefore, the **critical (crippling) load** is:

$$
P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Crippling Load (PinnedŌĆōPinned):** $\quad P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$
> 
> For pinned-pinned, the effective length $L_{e} = L$, so $L_{e}^{2} = L^{2}$.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Deflected Shape of the First Mode

Substituting $k = \pi / L$ back into $y(x) = A \sin(kx)$:

$$
y(x) \;=\; A \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)
$$

This is a **half-sine wave** of amplitude $A$ (which is theoretically indeterminate ŌĆö it is governed by initial imperfections, and at the exact bifurcation point, $A$ can take any value; in practice, the column collapses as $A$ grows unstably).

The **maximum deflection** (at mid-height, $x = L/2$) is:

$$
y_{max} \;=\; A \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \;=\; A
$$

## 3.2 Alternative Form in Terms of Slenderness Ratio

Divide both sides of Euler's formula by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$
\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{A L_{e}^{2}}
$$

Recognizing that $I / A = k^{2}$ where $k$ is the radius of gyration:

$$
\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E k^{2}}{L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\left(L_{e}/k\right)^{2}}
$$

Define the **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{e} / k$. Then:

$$
\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A column with a **large** $\lambda$ (long, thin) buckles at a **low** stress ŌĆö it is a "long column." A column with a **small** $\lambda$ (short, fat) does not buckle but fails by crushing ŌĆö it is a "short column." Euler's theory is valid only for long columns where $\lambda \geq \lambda_{c}$ (a critical slenderness ratio that depends on the material's yield stress).

## 3.3 Summary of the Derivation Logic

| Step | What we did | Why we did it |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Expressed $M = -P y$ | The axial load times the lateral lever arm equals the bending moment at any cut section. |
| 2 | Substituted into $EI y'' = M$ | This is the fundamental beam-bending differential equation. |
| 3 | Defined $k^{2} = P / EI$ and solved the ODE | Reduced the equation to the standard simple harmonic form $y'' + k^{2} y = 0$. |
| 4 | Applied BCs: $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$ | Both pinned ends cannot translate laterally. |
| 5 | Set $\sin(kL) = 0$ for non-trivial solution | Forced the existence of a buckled mode (eigenvalue problem). |
| 6 | Chose smallest $n = 1$ | Smallest $P$ corresponds to the first buckling mode (most easily excited). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Analytical Architecture of the Buckling Problem

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the pinned-pinned column buckling analysis: inputs, governing equation, mathematical operator chain, and final outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Data: Length L, Modulus E, Moment of Inertia I, End Conditions"] --> B["Define Differential Element dx of Column"]
    B --> C["Cut Section at Distance x: Internal Bending Moment M"]
    C --> D["Apply Static Equilibrium: M = -P times y"]
    D --> E["Substitute into Euler-Bernoulli: EI d2y/dx2 = M"]
    E --> F["Form Second-Order Linear ODE: d2y/dx2 + k2 y = 0"]
    F --> G["General Solution: y = A sin kx + B cos kx"]
    G --> H["Apply BC1: y at x=0 is 0  Ō¤╣  B = 0"]
    H --> I["Apply BC2: y at x=L is 0  Ō¤╣  A sin kL = 0"]
    I --> J{"Non-trivial Solution?"}
    J -- "Yes: sin kL = 0" --> K["Eigenvalue: kL = n pi  Ō¤╣  k = n pi / L"]
    J -- "No: A = 0" --> Z["Trivial Case: Column Remains Straight"]
    K --> L["Smallest n = 1  Ō¤╣  k = pi / L"]
    L --> M["Critical Load: P_cr = pi squared E I over L squared"]
    M --> N["Critical Stress: sigma_cr = pi squared E over lambda squared"]
    M --> O["Buckled Shape: y = A sin pi x / L  (Half-Sine Wave)"]
    N --> P["Engineering Design Output: Safe Buckling Capacity"]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Effect of Each Parameter on $P_{cr}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INCREASE_BUCKLING ["Factors that INCREASE P_cr (Stronger Column)"]
        D1["Increase E (Stiffer Material)"] --> RESULT1["P_cr increases"]
        D2["Increase I (Larger / Better Distributed Section)"] --> RESULT1
        D3["Use Hollow Section (I up, mass up only slightly)"] --> RESULT1
        D4["Add Lateral Restraints (Reduce Le)"] --> RESULT1
    end

    subgraph DECREASE_BUCKLING ["Factors that DECREASE P_cr (Weaker Column)"]
        D5["Increase L (Longer Column)"] --> RESULT2["P_cr decreases as 1 over L squared"]
        D6["Use Thin Slender Section (Small I)"] --> RESULT2
        D7["Use Soft Material (Low E)"] --> RESULT2
        D8["No Lateral Restraints (Full Le = L)"] --> RESULT2
    end

    RESULT1 --> OUT["P_cr = pi squared E I over L_e squared"]
    RESULT2 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic ŌĆö Buckled Mode Shape

```
       PŌåō                                          PŌåō
       |                                            |
      (ŌĆó) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ pin A                                (ŌĆó) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ pin A
       |                                            |
       |        y(x) = A sin(ŽĆx/L)                  |         /
       |       ŌĢ▒ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌĢ▓                          |        /
       |      ŌĢ▒           ŌĢ▓                         |       /
       |     ŌĢ▒             ŌĢ▓                        |      /
       |    ŌĢ▒               ŌĢ▓                       |     /
       |   ŌĢ▒                 ŌĢ▓                      |    /
       |  ŌĢ▒                   ŌĢ▓                     |   /
       | ŌĢ▒                     ŌĢ▓                    |  /
       |ŌĢ▒                       ŌĢ▓                   | /
      (ŌĆó) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ pin B                                (ŌĆó) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ pin B
                                                    Ōåæ
                                                Single curvature
                                                (one inflection
                                                 point ŌĆö none, in fact,
                                                 for half-sine wave)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The buckled shape is a **single half-sine wave** with zero slope at mid-height (the curvature changes sign at the midpoint? ŌĆö actually, the half-sine wave has the **maximum curvature at mid-height** and the slope is **non-zero** at mid-height, just like $\sin$ at $\pi/2$). The pinned-pinned column buckles into a **single lobe**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023 (Model)]

**Define the following terms in the context of column buckling:**
**(i) Crippling load, (ii) Effective length, (iii) Slenderness ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Crippling Load (Critical Load, $P_{cr}$):** The axial compressive load at which a long, slender column suddenly fails by buckling (i.e., lateral deflection), as predicted by Euler's formula. For a pinned-pinned column, $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L^{2}}$.
>
> **(ii) Effective Length ($L_{e}$):** The length of an equivalent pinned-pinned column that has the same buckling load as the actual column with its real end conditions. For a pinned-pinned column, $L_{e} = L$. For other end conditions, $L_e$ is obtained by multiplying the actual length by a factor (e.g., $2L$ for fixed-free, $0.7L$ for fixed-pinned, $0.5L$ for fixed-fixed).
>
> **(iii) Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** The ratio of the effective length of the column to the least radius of gyration of its cross-section, $\lambda = L_{e}/k$, where $k = \sqrt{I_{min}/A}$. It is a dimensionless measure of how "slender" the column is ŌĆö large $\lambda$ means long and thin, prone to buckling.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 (Model)]

**State Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column with both ends hinged. List any four assumptions of Euler's theory.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Euler's Formula for a pinned-pinned (hinged-hinged) column:**
> 
> $$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$
> 
> where $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = least second moment of area, $L$ = actual length = effective length for this end condition.
>
> **Four Assumptions of Euler's Theory:**
> 1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** and the load is **truly axial** (no eccentricity).
> 2. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law** throughout.
> 3. **Self-weight** of the column is **neglected** in comparison to the applied load.
> 4. Failure is **only by buckling** (bending), and the **shortening** of the column under direct compression is negligible.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** Derive Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column with both ends pinned. State the assumptions clearly. **    (7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel column is **4 m long** with **both ends pinned**. The cross-section is a **hollow circular tube of outer diameter 100 mm and inner diameter 80 mm**. Find the crippling (Euler) load. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$. **    (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup:** Consider a column of length $L$, both ends pinned, subjected to axial compressive load $P$. Let $y(x)$ be the lateral deflection at distance $x$ from the lower end.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bending moment at section $x$:** Taking the equilibrium of the lower portion,

$$M \;=\; -P \cdot y$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Euler-Bernoulli equation:**

$$EI \, \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P y$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange and define:**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y \;=\; 0, \qquad \text{where } k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö General solution:**

$$y(x) \;=\; A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply BCs:**
- At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ ŌåÆ $B = 0$
- At $x = L$, $y = 0$ ŌåÆ $A \sin(kL) = 0$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Eigenvalue condition:** For a non-trivial deflected shape,

$$\sin(kL) \;=\; 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Smallest eigenvalue ($n=1$):**

$$k \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{L} \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{P}{EI} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Euler's crippling load:**

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}}$$

**Assumptions:** (Write any 4ŌĆō5 from the list in ┬¦2.2 ŌĆö initial straightness, axial load, Hooke's law, no self-weight, no crushing, constant $EI$, ideal end conditions, small deflections.)

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**Given:** $L = 4 \; \text{m} = 4000 \; \text{mm}$, $D = 100 \; \text{mm}$, $d = 80 \; \text{mm}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$, both ends pinned.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Moment of inertia of the hollow circular section:**

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

$$D^{4} \;=\; (100)^{4} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{8} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$d^{4} \;=\; (80)^{4} \;=\; 4.096 \times 10^{7} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$D^{4} - d^{4} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{8} - 4.096 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 5.904 \times 10^{7} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 5.904 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 0.04909 \times 5.904 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 2.898 \times 10^{6} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Effective length (pinned-pinned):**

$$L_{e} \;=\; L \;=\; 4000 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Euler's crippling load:**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{(9.8696) \times (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.898 \times 10^{6})}{(4000)^{2}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 2.898 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 9.8696 \times 5.796 \times 10^{11} \;=\; 5.7202 \times 10^{12}$$

**Denominator:**

$$(4000)^{2} \;=\; 1.6 \times 10^{7}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{5.7202 \times 10^{12}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; 3.5751 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \;\approx\; 357.51 \; \text{kN}}$$

---

### **Question B-B.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 (Model) ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the concept of slenderness ratio. A long column is said to fail by buckling if its slenderness ratio is greater than a certain value. State the expression for this critical slenderness ratio. **    (7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3 m long** pin-ended column has a **rectangular cross-section of 80 mm ├Ś 60 mm**. Find the Euler's critical load. Take $E = 200 \; \text{GPa}$. **    (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** It is the ratio of the effective length $L_e$ of the column to the least radius of gyration $k$ of its cross-section:

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{e}}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{e}}{\sqrt{I_{min}/A}}$$

**Significance:** It is a dimensionless number that classifies columns into long, medium, and short categories.

- **Long column** ŌĆö $\lambda \geq \lambda_{c}$: fails by **buckling** (Euler theory valid).
- **Short column** ŌĆö $\lambda < \lambda_{c}$: fails by **crushing** (Euler theory NOT valid).
- **Medium column** ŌĆö empirical formulas (Rankine, Johnson's) are used.

**Critical slenderness ratio:** The value of $\lambda$ at which the Euler critical stress equals the yield stress $\sigma_{y}$ of the material (the transition from elastic buckling to crushing failure). Equating:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \sigma_{y} \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda_{c}^{2}} \;=\; \sigma_{y}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{c} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_{y}}}}$$

For mild steel, $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{y} = 320 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$ gives $\lambda_{c} \approx 80$.

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**Given:** $L = 3 \; \text{m} = 3000 \; \text{mm}$, cross-section $b \times d = 60 \; \text{mm} \times 80 \; \text{mm}$, $E = 200 \; \text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}$, both ends pinned.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the buckling axis (least $I$):**

$$I_{xx} \;=\; \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times (80)^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 512000}{12} \;=\; 2.56 \times 10^{6} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

$$I_{yy} \;=\; \dfrac{d b^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{80 \times (60)^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{80 \times 216000}{12} \;=\; 1.44 \times 10^{6} \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

Minimum: $I_{min} = I_{yy} = 1.44 \times 10^{6} \; \text{mm}^{4}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Effective length (pinned-pinned):**

$$L_{e} \;=\; L \;=\; 3000 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Euler's critical load:**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{(9.8696) \times (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.44 \times 10^{6})}{(3000)^{2}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.44 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 9.8696 \times 2.88 \times 10^{11} \;=\; 2.8424 \times 10^{12}$$

**Denominator:**

$$(3000)^{2} \;=\; 9 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{2.8424 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 3.1582 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \;\approx\; 315.82 \; \text{kN}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $I_{min}$:** Students often use $I_{xx}$ even when $I_{yy} < I_{xx}$. Buckling occurs about the **weaker axis** ŌĆö always pick the **smaller** moment of inertia. ŌåÆ **Lose up to 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm and m, or forgetting to convert $E$ from GPa to N/mm┬▓ (multiply by $10^{3}$). 1 GPa = $10^{3}$ N/mm┬▓. ŌåÆ **Lose up to 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Using $L$ when $L_e$ should be used:** For pinned-pinned, $L_e = L$ (safe to substitute directly), but in **other end conditions** the student MUST convert. ŌåÆ **Lose up to 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps in Part (a):** KTU examiners allocate 1 mark each for the differential equation, general solution, boundary conditions, eigenvalue condition, and final result. Skipping intermediate steps ŌåÆ **lose 3ŌĆō4 marks.**
>
> 5. **Not stating the assumptions:** A question asking to "derive and state assumptions" requires **at least 4ŌĆō5 assumptions explicitly listed**. ŌåÆ **Lose up to 2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Confusing "crippling load" with "crushing load":** Crippling = buckling (governed by Euler); Crushing = direct compression failure. ŌåÆ **Conceptual mark deduction.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Buckling of Pinned-Pinned Columns**

- Ō£ģ **Euler's crippling load (pinned-pinned):** $\quad P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$

- Ō£ģ **Effective length for pinned-pinned:** $\quad L_{e} = L$

- Ō£ģ **Deflected shape (first mode):** $\quad y(x) = A \sin(\pi x / L)$ ŌĆö a half-sine wave.

- Ō£ģ **Always use the minimum** $I$ ($I_{min}$) and the corresponding **least radius of gyration** $k_{min} = \sqrt{I_{min}/A}$.

- Ō£ģ **Critical stress in terms of slenderness ratio:** $\quad \sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$

- Ō£ģ **Critical slenderness ratio** (transition from buckling to crushing): $\quad \lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{E / \sigma_{y}}$

- Ō£ģ **Euler's theory is valid only when** $\lambda \geq \lambda_{c}$ ŌĆö i.e., for **long columns**.

- Ō£ģ **Key formula-mnemonic:** "**P**oor **E**ngineers **I**diotically **L**ove **L**arge $L$" ŌåÆ $P = \pi^{2} E I / L^{2}$ ŌĆö but remember, **larger $L$** actually **reduces** $P_{cr}$!

- Ō£ģ **The 4 standard end conditions and their effective lengths** (for cross-comparison in later modules):
  - PinnedŌĆōPinned: $L_e = L$
  - FixedŌĆōFixed: $L_e = L/2$
  - FixedŌĆōPinned: $L_e = 0.7L \approx 0.699L$
  - FixedŌĆōFree: $L_e = 2L$

- Ō£ģ **Practical design rule of thumb:** A column is "long" if its actual length $L > 20 \times$ (least lateral dimension) for steel, and a slenderness ratio $\lambda > 80$ŌĆō$100$ for mild steel.

- Ō£ģ **Units check before plugging into** $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$: $E$ in N/mm┬▓, $I$ in mmŌü┤, $L$ in mm ŌåÆ $P_{cr}$ in N. Convert everything to mm first to avoid errors.

- Ō£ģ **Common KTU exam trap:** Do not confuse Euler's formula with the **secant formula** (used for eccentric loading) or with **empirical formulas** like Rankine's or Johnson's straight-line formula (used for intermediate columns). Those belong to other sub-topics in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Buckling Theory for Long Columns. Critical Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Buckling Theory for Long Columns. Critical Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Euler's Buckling Theory** is a classical stability analysis developed by the Swiss mathematician **Leonhard Euler (1757)** that determines the **critical (buckling) load** at which a slender, initially straight, axially loaded compression member (column) suddenly deflects laterally and fails by instability rather than by direct material crushing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A long, slender column subjected to an axial compressive load **P** will fail by **lateral deflection (buckling)** when the load reaches a critical value, even if the induced compressive stress is well below the yield stress of the material. This load is called the **Euler Critical Load (P_cr)** or **Crippling Load (P_c)**.

Mathematically, for a column with both ends pinned (hinged), Euler's critical load is expressed as:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

where:
- **E** = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the material
- **I** = Minimum moment of inertia of the cross-section (about the axis of buckling)
- **L** = Actual length of the column
- **ŽĆ┬▓** Ōēł 9.8696 (dimensionless constant)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Ruler Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Take a thin plastic ruler and hold it vertically on a table. Press down gently on the top with your thumb. At small forces, the ruler remains perfectly straight ŌĆö the load is carried by direct **compression** of the material. Suddenly, at a particular load, the ruler snaps sideways with a "pop." This is **buckling** ŌĆö failure by *shape instability*, not by crushing.
>
> The thinner and taller the ruler, the *less* force is needed to make it buckle. A short, fat stub of the same ruler can withstand a huge load without buckling. This shows that **length (L)** and **flexural rigidity (EI)** are the controlling parameters ŌĆö not the cross-sectional *area* (which governs direct crushing).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (Steel) | **E** | N/m┬▓ (Pa) | 2.0 ├Ś 10┬╣┬╣ Pa = 200 GPa |
| Young's Modulus (Timber) | E | N/m┬▓ | 1.0 ├Ś 10┬╣Ōü░ Pa Ōēł 10 GPa |
| Young's Modulus (Aluminium) | E | N/m┬▓ | 7.0 ├Ś 10┬╣Ōü░ Pa = 70 GPa |
| Slenderness Ratio | **╬╗** | dimensionless | > 80 (long) < 30 (short) |
| Euler's constant factor | ŽĆ┬▓ | dimensionless | 9.8696 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this in your KTU syllabus?** Buckling governs the design of every compression member in civil engineering ŌĆö building columns, bridge piers, truss chords, and even the legs of a chair. A column is *not* designed by crushing strength; it is designed by **stability** (Euler's formula) to prevent sudden lateral collapse.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Euler's Theory ŌĆö Step-by-Step Thought Chain

Euler's analysis is built on **four foundational assumptions** that determine when the formula is valid:

1. **The column is initially perfectly straight** ŌĆö no initial crookedness.
2. **The load is perfectly axial (concentric)** ŌĆö no eccentricity.
3. **The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** ŌĆö the column never yields or crushes before buckling.
4. **Self-weight is neglected** compared to the axial load.
5. **The cross-section is uniform along the length** ŌĆö prismatic member.
6. **End joints are frictionless and ideal** ŌĆö e.g., perfectly pinned, perfectly fixed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Criterion ŌĆö The Slenderness Ratio:**
> Euler's theory is valid **only for long columns** where the slenderness ratio exceeds a critical value.
> $$\lambda \;=\; \frac{L_{e}}{k} \;>\; \lambda_{critical}$$
> where $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the **radius of gyration**. For long columns, **buckling** governs; for short columns, **crushing** governs.

### 2.2 The Underlying Differential Equation

The entire theory stems from one elegant differential equation. Consider a column pinned at both ends, axially loaded with **P**. When the column deflects laterally by **y(x)**, the bending moment at any section is:

$$M(x) \;=\; -P \cdot y(x)$$

Applying the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation**:

$$E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; M(x) \;=\; -P \cdot y(x)$$

Rearranging into the canonical second-order linear ODE:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; \frac{P}{E I}\,y \;=\; 0$$

Setting $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, this becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; k^{2} y \;=\; 0$$

whose general solution is **y = A sin(kx) + B cos(kx)**. Applying the boundary conditions **y(0) = 0** and **y(L) = 0** gives the **eigenvalue problem** whose lowest non-trivial solution yields the critical load.

### 2.3 Effective Length Concept (KTU Favourite!)

Different end conditions produce different buckling shapes. To use **one universal formula**, Euler introduced the **Effective Length (L_e)**:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E A}{\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

The effective length is the distance between **consecutive inflection points (points of zero moment)** of the buckled shape.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet ŌĆö End Conditions Table

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE ŌĆö It appears in almost every KTU question on buckling.**

| End Condition | Effective Length $L_{e}$ | Sketch / Buckled Shape | KTU Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both ends **Hinged (Pinned-Pinned)** | $L_{e} = 1.0\,L$ | Single half sine wave | $\pi^{2}EI / L^{2}$ |
| One end **Fixed, Other Free** (cantilever) | $L_{e} = 2.0\,L$ | Quarter sine wave (longest) | $\pi^{2}EI / (2L)^{2}$ |
| Both ends **Fixed** | $L_{e} = 0.5\,L$ | Two half sine waves | $\pi^{2}EI / (0.5L)^{2}$ |
| One end **Fixed, Other Hinged** | $L_{e} = 0.7\,L \approx \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Asymmetric half wave | $\pi^{2}EI / (0.7L)^{2}$ |

### 2.5 Critical Stress Formula

The **Euler Critical Stress** $\sigma_{cr}$ is the critical load divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{A\, L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

where $\lambda = L_{e}/k$ is the **slenderness ratio**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation for KTU:**
> Notice that $\sigma_{cr}$ depends **only on E and ╬╗**, not on the yield stress $\sigma_{y}$. This is why long, slender steel columns can fail at stresses far below their yield strength ŌĆö buckling is a *stability* phenomenon, not a *strength* phenomenon.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of building columns, bridge piers, scaffolding poles.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Push-rods of hydraulic presses, piston rods, gun barrels.
- **Aerospace:** Struts in aircraft landing gear (where weight must be minimized).
- **Structural Steel Design (IS 800:2007):** Mandates Euler's formula for $\lambda > \sqrt{\pi^{2}E/\sigma_{y}}$ and an empirical Rankine-type formula for intermediate slenderness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Critical Load (Pinned-Pinned Column)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup the Geometry**

Consider a column AB of length **L**, pinned at both ends A and B, subjected to an axial compressive load **P** at the centroidal axis. When P reaches a critical value, the column buckles into a slight lateral deflected shape **y(x)**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Free-Body Diagram of the Lower Segment**

Cut the column at section X-X (at distance x from A). The lower portion experiences:
- Axial compressive force **P** along its original axis.
- A lateral reaction at the cut, plus a bending moment **M**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Bending Moment at Section X-X**

Taking moments about the cut section:

$$M(x) \;=\; P \cdot y(x)$$

(The negative sign is omitted since we are dealing with magnitudes and the deflected shape is sinusoidal.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Euler-Bernoulli Equation**

The differential equation of the elastic curve is:

$$E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; M(x) \;=\; -P \cdot y$$

The negative sign indicates that the curvature opposes the deflection (concave toward the original axis). Rearranging:

$$E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; P\,y \;=\; 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Define the Constant and Reduce to Standard Form**

Let

$$k^{2} \;=\; \frac{P}{E I}$$

The equation becomes the **standard second-order linear homogeneous ODE**:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; k^{2} y \;=\; 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Write the General Solution**

The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is $m^{2} + k^{2} = 0$, giving $m = \pm ik$. Hence:

$$y(x) \;=\; A \sin(kx) \;+\; B \cos(kx)$$

where **A** and **B** are constants of integration.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply the Boundary Conditions**

- **At x = 0 (pin A):** Deflection y = 0
- **At x = L (pin B):** Deflection y = 0

From **x = 0**:

$$0 \;=\; A \sin(0) \;+\; B \cos(0) \;\Rightarrow\; B \;=\; 0$$

From **x = L**:

$$0 \;=\; A \sin(kL) \;+\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A \sin(kL) \;=\; 0$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö The Eigenvalue Condition**

For a non-trivial (non-zero) deflection, $A \neq 0$. Therefore:

$$\sin(kL) \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL \;=\; n\pi, \quad n \;=\; 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **smallest** buckling load corresponds to the **lowest mode**, **n = 1** (the first buckling mode):

$$kL \;=\; \pi \;\Rightarrow\; k \;=\; \frac{\pi}{L}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Back-Substitute to Obtain P_cr**

$$k^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{E I}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}\,}$$

The corresponding **buckled shape** is $y(x) = A \sin(\pi x / L)$ ŌĆö a half sine wave.

### 3.2 Derivation for a Column Fixed at One End and Free at the Other (Cantilever Column)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry and Boundary Conditions**

Length = L. Fixed at bottom (A): no deflection, no rotation. Free at top (B): no moment, deflected by ╬┤.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Build the Deflection Function**

The buckled shape is a quarter sine wave. Measuring **x from the free end B**:

$$y(x) \;=\; \delta \left[ 1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2L}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Curvature Formula at the Base (x = L)**

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; \delta \cdot \frac{\pi^{2}}{4L^{2}} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2L}\right)$$

At $x = L$: $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$ ŌĆö which is consistent with the **zero moment at the free end** but does not give the equation. We need the curvature at the **fixed end**, which from the known exact solution of the differential equation $EI y'' = -P(L - x) \cdot y'(x)$ (no wait ŌĆö for cantilever, $M(x) = P(\delta - y)$ and the boundary-condition method yields $kL = \pi/2$).

**Step 4 ŌĆö The Eigenvalue**

For a cantilever column, the transcendental equation reduces to:

$$kL \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Back-Substitute**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; k^{2} E I \;=\; \left(\frac{\pi}{2L}\right)^{2} E I$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{(2L)^{2}}\,}$$

This shows that the **effective length** of a cantilever is $L_e = 2L$ (the column behaves like a pinned-pinned column of length 2L).

### 3.3 Derivation of Critical Stress

Starting from:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

Divide both sides by the cross-sectional area **A**:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{A\, L_{e}^{2}}$$

Recognize that $I/A = k^{2}$, where **k** is the **least radius of gyration**:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E k^{2}}{L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

Let $\lambda = L_e / k$:

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}\,}$$

### 3.4 Slenderness Ratio Classification (KTU Board Convention)

| Class | Slenderness Range | Governing Failure Mode | Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long Column** | $\lambda > \lambda_{c}$ | Elastic Buckling (Euler) | $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ |
| **Intermediate Column** | $\lambda_{c} > \lambda > 30$ | Inelastic Buckling (Empirical) | Rankine / Johnson / IS 800 |
| **Short Column (Stub)** | $\lambda < 30$ | Direct Crushing / Yielding | $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_{y}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit of Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda_{c}$):**
> Euler's formula is valid only when $\sigma_{cr} \leq \sigma_{y}$ (elastic regime). Equating:
> $$\frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda_{c}^{2}} \;=\; \sigma_{y} \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_{c} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_{y}}}$$
> For mild steel (E = 200 GPa, $\sigma_{y}$ = 250 MPa): $\lambda_{c} \approx 89$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow ŌĆö Euler Buckling Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Column Geometry L, A, I, k"] --> B["SELECT End Condition"]
    B --> C["DETERMINE Effective Length Le"]
    C --> D["COMPUTE Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le divided by k"]
    D --> E{"Is lambda greater than lambda_c?"}
    E -->|YES| F["APPLY Euler Buckling Pcr = pi squared E I divided by Le squared"]
    E -->|NO| G["APPLY Rankine or IS 800 Empirical Formula"]
    F --> H["COMPUTE Critical Stress sigma_cr = Pcr divided by A"]
    G --> I["COMPUTE Critical Stress from Empirical Relation"]
    H --> J["OUTPUT: Pcr and sigma_cr"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: Pcr and sigma_cr"]
```

### 4.2 End-Condition Schematic (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SC1["PINNED-PINNED: Le = 1.0L"]
        A1["Pin Support"] --- B1["Column Body Half Sine Wave"] --- C1["Pin Support"]
    end
    subgraph SC2["FIXED-FREE: Le = 2.0L"]
        A2["Fixed Base"] --- B2["Column Body Quarter Sine"] --- C2["Free Top Deflected"]
    end
    subgraph SC3["FIXED-FIXED: Le = 0.5L"]
        A3["Fixed Base"] --- B3["Column Body Two Half Sines"] --- C3["Fixed Top"]
    end
    subgraph SC4["FIXED-PINNED: Le = 0.707L"]
        A4["Fixed Base"] --- B4["Column Body Asymmetric Wave"] --- C4["Pin Top"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Eigenvalue / Mode Diagram (KTU Visualization Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["MODE 1 n=1: kL = pi, Pcr minimum CRITICAL"] --> M2["MODE 2 n=2: kL = 2 pi, Pcr = 4 times Pcr_min"]
    M2 --> M3["MODE 3 n=3: kL = 3 pi, Pcr = 9 times Pcr_min"]
    M3 --> Note["Higher modes are physically irrelevant: column buckles at the LOWEST critical load"]
```

### 4.4 Stress vs. Slenderness Ratio Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H["High Slenderness: Euler Elastic Buckling Zone: sigma_cr = pi squared E divided by lambda squared"] --> M["Medium Slenderness: Empirical Transition Zone: Rankine or IS 800"]
    M --> L["Low Slenderness: Crushing Zone: sigma_cr = sigma_y yield"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid for Section 4:** Each block represents a *stage* in the analytical decision-tree, not a physical drawing. The shapes (sine waves) described in the text are captured symbolically in the labels ŌĆö actual deflection curves can be drawn in your exam script as required.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*State Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column with both ends hinged. Define each term and state its unit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}\,}$$

- **P_cr** = Euler's critical (crippling) load in **N** (Newton)
- **E** = Young's Modulus of the column material in **N/m┬▓** or **Pa**
- **I** = Minimum moment of inertia of the cross-section in **mŌü┤**
- **L** = Actual (unsupported) length of the column in **m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for term definitions, 1 mark for units.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Define "effective length" of a column. What is its value for a column fixed at one end and hinged at the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **effective length (L_e)** of a column is the distance between two successive points of zero bending moment (inflection points) on the deflected column, used as the equivalent length of a pin-ended column for the purpose of applying Euler's formula.

For a column **fixed at one end and hinged at the other**:

$$\boxed{\,L_{e} \;=\; \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 0.7\,L\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for numerical value with reasoning.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jul 2024]**

A solid cylindrical steel column of diameter **100 mm** and length **3 m** is pinned at both ends. Take **E = 2.0 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ N/mm┬▓**. Calculate:
(i) The Euler critical load.
(ii) The Euler critical stress.
(iii) The slenderness ratio, and comment on whether Euler's formula is valid. Use yield stress $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm┬▓.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 50$ mm
- Length $L = 3$ m $= 3000$ mm
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm┬▓
- Both ends pinned: $L_e = L = 3000$ mm
- $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm┬▓

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-sectional properties**

$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 100^{2}}{4} \;=\; 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 100^{4}}{64} \;=\; 4.9087 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$k \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4.9087 \times 10^{6}}{7853.98}} \;=\; 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Euler critical load (1+1+1 = 3 marks for the formula, substitution, and result)**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4.9087 \times 10^{6}}{3000^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4.9087 \times 10^{6}}{9 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{9.6876 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 1.0764 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1076.4 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Euler critical stress (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{1.0764 \times 10^{6}}{7853.98} \;\approx\; 137.04 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Slenderness ratio and validity (2 marks)**

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{L_{e}}{k} \;=\; \frac{3000}{25} \;=\; 120$$

Critical slenderness:

$$\lambda_{c} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_{y}}} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{\frac{2.0 \times 10^{5}}{250}} \;=\; \pi \sqrt{800} \;\approx\; 88.86$$

Since $\lambda = 120 > \lambda_{c} = 88.86$, **Euler's formula is valid** and the column fails by **elastic buckling**.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jul 2024]**
*A 2 m long steel column has a hollow circular cross-section with outer diameter 80 mm and inner diameter 60 mm. It is fixed at one end and free at the other. Find the Euler crippling load. Take E = 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ N/mm┬▓.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Effective length for fixed-free end condition**

$$L_{e} \;=\; 2L \;=\; 2 \times 2 \;=\; 4 \text{ m} \;=\; 4000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment of inertia of hollow circular section**

$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\left(80^{4} - 60^{4}\right)$$

$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\left(4.096 \times 10^{7} - 1.296 \times 10^{7}\right)$$

$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\left(2.8 \times 10^{7}\right) \;=\; 1.3744 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Euler's formula**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.3744 \times 10^{6}}{4000^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{9.8696 \times 2.747 \times 10^{11}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{cr} \;\approx\; 1.695 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \;\approx\; 169.5 \text{ kN}\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Most common mistake (ŌĆō2 marks):** Students *forget* to apply the effective length factor. For fixed-free columns, always use $L_e = 2L$, not L. For fixed-fixed, use $L_e = L/2$. For fixed-hinged, use $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$. The examiner will deduct marks even if the rest of the math is correct.
>
> 2. **Second most common mistake (ŌĆō1 to 2 marks):** Computing **I** incorrectly. For a *hollow* circular section, use $I = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$ ŌĆö do **NOT** use $\pi d^4/64$ with the inner diameter alone.
>
> 3. **Validity check omitted (ŌĆō1 mark):** Always state $\lambda$ and $\lambda_c$ and comment on whether Euler's formula is applicable. The examiner specifically looks for this statement.
>
> 4. **Unit mismatch (ŌĆō1 mark):** If E is given in N/mm┬▓, then I must be in mmŌü┤, A in mm┬▓, and length in mm. Do **not** mix mm and m in the same calculation.
>
> 5. **Axis of buckling (ŌĆō1 mark):** If a rectangular column is given, students often use $I_{xx}$ when the column buckles about the axis with the *minimum* I (i.e., the weaker axis). Always pick **I_min** unless told otherwise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Summary ŌĆö Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Core formula (memorize):** $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L_{e}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$
- **Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = L_{e}/k$ where $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ ŌĆö use the **minimum I** (weakest axis).
- **Effective lengths to remember by heart:**
  * Pinned-Pinned ŌåÆ $L_e = 1.0L$
  * Fixed-Free ŌåÆ $L_e = 2.0L$
  * Fixed-Fixed ŌåÆ $L_e = 0.5L$
  * Fixed-Hinged ŌåÆ $L_e = L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7L$
- **Validity of Euler's formula:** valid only when $\lambda > \lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ (elastic regime).
- **For mild steel:** $\lambda_c \approx 89$; column is "long" if $\lambda > 89$.
- **Critical stress is independent of the yield stress** (for long columns) ŌĆö this is a classic KTU conceptual question.
- **Buckling is a stability problem, not a strength problem** ŌĆö even a small load can buckle a long column.
- **End-condition table is the most-asked** KTU question ŌĆö draw neat sketches with deflection curves.
- **First-mode (n=1) buckling is always the critical mode** ŌĆö higher modes give 4├Ś, 9├ŚŌĆ” the load and never occur in practice.
- **Assumptions of Euler's theory** ŌĆö straight, axially loaded, prismatic, elastic, ideal joints. Loss of 1 mark if even one is missed in a 7-mark derivation.
- **SI Unit of P_cr:** Newton (N) or kilo-Newton (kN).
- **SI Unit of E:** N/m┬▓ (Pa); N/mm┬▓ if working in mm-scale.
- **SI Unit of I:** mŌü┤ (or mmŌü┤ for mm-scale).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slenderness Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Solid Mechanics** and structural stability, the **Slenderness Ratio** (denoted by the Greek letter lambda, $\lambda$) is a fundamental dimensionless geometric parameter that quantifies the tendency of a structural member (typically a column or strut) to fail by **elastic buckling** rather than by direct compressive yielding or crushing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Slenderness Ratio is the ratio of the effective length ($L_{eff}$) of a compression member to its least radius of gyration ($k_{min}$).*
> $$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}}$$

It is purely a **geometric property** of the cross-section and the end-support conditions; it does **not** depend on the magnitude of the applied load.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **short, fat eraser** between your thumb and forefinger and squeezing it. The eraser simply gets crushed (squashed). This is a **strength failure** (crushing failure).

Now, take a **long, thin wooden stick** (like a matchstick) and apply the same gentle axial push along its length. Instead of crushing, the stick suddenly **bows sideways and snaps**. This is a **stability failure** (buckling failure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The "slender" stick has a **high slenderness ratio**, meaning its length is much greater than its cross-sectional dimensions. Engineers use the slenderness ratio as a single number that tells them *how likely* a column is to bend and buckle before it crushes.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following table highlights the standard metrics associated with the slenderness ratio:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective Length | $L_{eff}$ | mm | Depends on end conditions |
| Least Radius of Gyration | $k_{min}$ | mm | $k_{min} = \sqrt{I_{min}/A}$ |
| Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda$ | **Dimensionless** | Ratio of two lengths |
| End Condition Factor | $\alpha$ | ŌĆō | Multiplies actual length L |

The three primary reference values used universally (IS 800, IS 456, and KTU examinations) are:
- $\lambda = 0$ ŌåÆ Pure compression/cushing (theoretical)
- $\lambda \le \lambda_c$ ŌåÆ **Short columns** (crushing governs)
- $\lambda > \lambda_c$ ŌåÆ **Long columns** (buckling governs)

---

### 1.4 Why is it "Slenderness" and not "Thickness"?

The word *slenderness* inherently captures the **length-to-width proportion**. A **larger $\lambda$** means the member is geometrically *more slender*, i.e., longer compared to its cross-section size, and therefore more prone to lateral instability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition

The slenderness ratio is built from two distinct sub-concepts:

**(a) Effective Length ($L_{eff}$):**
The actual length $L$ of a column rarely reflects its true buckling behavior because the end supports may be fixed, pinned, or free. To standardize analysis, we use an **effective length** concept:

$$L_{eff} = \alpha \cdot L$$

where $\alpha$ is a coefficient that depends on the rotational and translational restraint at the column ends.

**(b) Least Radius of Gyration ($k_{min}$):**
A column buckles about the **weakest principal axis** of its cross-section. Therefore, we use the *minimum* moment of inertia:

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$$

Combining these:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot L}{\sqrt{I_{min}/A}} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{I_{min}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Buckling is a directional phenomenon. An I-section column will buckle about its weaker web axis long before its stronger flange axis ŌĆö this is why $I_{min}$ is selected, not $I_{max}$.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{eff} / k_{min}$ | $L_{eff}$, $k_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Radius of Gyration | $k_{min} = \sqrt{I_{min}/A}$ | $I_{min}$, $A$ | mm |
| 3 | Effective Length | $L_{eff} = \alpha L$ | $\alpha$ (end factor), $L$ | mm |
| 4 | End Factor (Both Ends Hinged) | $\alpha = 1$ | ŌĆō | ŌĆō |
| 5 | End Factor (Both Ends Fixed) | $\alpha = 0.5$ | ŌĆō | ŌĆō |
| 6 | End Factor (One Fixed, One Free) | $\alpha = 2$ | ŌĆō | ŌĆō |
| 7 | End Factor (One Fixed, One Hinged) | $\alpha = 0.7$ (Ōēł 0.6992) | ŌĆō | ŌĆō |
| 8 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I_{min} / L_{eff}^2$ | $E$, $I_{min}$ | N |
| 9 | Euler Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ | $E$, $\lambda$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 10 | Critical Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_c}$ | $E$, $\sigma_c$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Rankine Load | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a(\lambda^2)}$ | $\sigma_c$, $a$, $\lambda$ | N |
| 12 | Johnson's Parabolic Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_c - \left(\sigma_c^2 / 4\pi^2 E\right)\lambda^2$ | ŌĆō | N/mm┬▓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Slenderness Ratio $\lambda_c$** is the boundary value. For a given material (with known $E$ and crushing stress $\sigma_c$), any column with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ will fail by Euler buckling; any with $\lambda \le \lambda_c$ will fail by crushing.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Designing RCC columns in multi-storey buildings (IS 456 mandates $\lambda \le 60$ for compression members to avoid slenderness reduction factors).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Strut design in machine tool frames, hydraulic press columns, crane booms, and telescopic jack supports.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Aircraft fuselage stringers and wing spars ŌĆö buckling here is catastrophic.
- **Steel Structures (IS 800:2007):** Classification of compression members as *short*, *medium*, or *long* columns based on $\lambda$.
- **Biomedical:** Even human long bones (femur) exhibit buckling failure modes under excessive axial load.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In ESE problems, always compute $\lambda$ *first*, then compare it with $\lambda_c$. This decides which formula (Euler vs. Rankine vs. Johnson) to apply. Marks are awarded for this logical flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slenderness Ratio from First Principles

We begin from the Euler buckling theory for a pin-ended column of length $L$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L^2}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express $I_{min}$ in terms of radius of gyration:**

By definition of radius of gyration:

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{min} = A \cdot k_{min}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into Euler's formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E \cdot (A \cdot k_{min}^2)}{L^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate the slenderness ratio:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E \cdot A}{\left(\dfrac{L}{k_{min}}\right)^2}$$

Recognizing that $\lambda = L / k_{min}$ for pin-ended columns (i.e., $L_{eff} = L$):

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derive the average critical buckling stress:**

Dividing both sides by cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

This is the famous **Euler hyperbola** in the stress-vs-slenderness plane.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda_c$)

The critical slenderness ratio marks the boundary between **crushing failure** (governed by $\sigma_c$) and **buckling failure** (governed by Euler's stress).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Equate Euler buckling stress to crushing stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_c$$

$$\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \sigma_c$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $\lambda$ (renamed $\lambda_c$ at the transition):**

$$\lambda^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_c}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $\lambda_c$:** It is the slenderness ratio at which the column is equally likely to fail by crushing or by buckling. For mild steel, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓ and $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm┬▓, giving $\lambda_c \approx 78.6$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A hollow circular cast-iron column has outer diameter $60$ mm, inner diameter $40$ mm, and length $4$ m. It is fixed at both ends. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓ and crushing stress $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm┬▓. Find the Euler crippling load using slenderness ratio approach.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-sectional properties:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(60^2 - 40^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(3600 - 1600) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(2000)$$

$$A = 500\pi \approx 1570.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment of inertia (hollow circle):**

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(60^4 - 40^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(12{,}960{,}000 - 2{,}560{,}000)$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(10{,}400{,}000) \approx 510{,}508.8 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Radius of gyration:**

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{510{,}508.8}{1570.8}} = \sqrt{325} \approx 18.028 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Effective length (both ends fixed, $\alpha = 0.5$):**

$$L_{eff} = 0.5 \times 4000 = 2000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}} = \dfrac{2000}{18.028} \approx 110.94$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compare with critical slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5}{550}} = \pi \sqrt{218.18} \approx \pi \times 14.77 \approx 46.4$$

Since $\lambda = 110.94 > \lambda_c = 46.4$, Euler's formula is **valid**.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Euler crippling load:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 510{,}508.8}{2000^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 510{,}508.8}{4 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{6.0475 \times 10^{11}}{4 \times 10^6} \approx 151{,}188 \text{ N} \approx 151.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{cr} \approx 151.2$ kN.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Column Classification

| Region | Slenderness Condition | Failure Mode | Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short Column | $\lambda \le \lambda_c$ | Crushing (Yielding) | $\sigma_c \cdot A$ (Johnson or direct) |
| Medium Column | $\lambda$ near $\lambda_c$ | Combined | Rankine's formula |
| Long Column | $\lambda > \lambda_c$ | Elastic Buckling | Euler's formula |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Column Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Compression Member Given"] --> B["Compute Cross-Section Properties: A and I_min"]
    B --> C["Compute Radius of Gyration: k_min = sqrt(I_min / A)"]
    C --> D["Identify End Conditions and Effective Length: L_eff = alpha times L"]
    D --> E["Calculate Slenderness Ratio: lambda = L_eff divided by k_min"]
    E --> F{"Compare lambda with lambda_c = pi times sqrt(E divided by sigma_c)"}
    F -- "lambda GREATER than lambda_c" --> G["LONG COLUMN: Apply Euler Buckling Formula"]
    F -- "lambda LESS than or EQUAL to lambda_c" --> H["SHORT or MEDIUM COLUMN: Apply Rankine or Johnson Formula"]
    G --> I["Compute P_cr = pi squared E I_min divided by L_eff squared"]
    H --> J["Compute P_R using Rankine Empirical Relation"]
    I --> K["Final Answer: Crippling Load in Newtons or Kilonewtons"]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Effective Length Factors for Different End Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Both Ends Hinged: alpha equals 1.0"] --> Q1["L_eff equals L"]
    P2["Both Ends Fixed: alpha equals 0.5"] --> Q2["L_eff equals 0.5 L"]
    P3["One End Fixed Other Free: alpha equals 2.0"] --> Q3["L_eff equals 2 L"]
    P4["One End Fixed Other Hinged: alpha equals 0.7"] --> Q4["L_eff equals 0.7 L"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Stress versus Slenderness Curve (Euler Hyperbola)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Vertical Axis: Critical Stress sigma_cr"] --> S2["Horizontal Axis: Slenderness Ratio lambda"]
    S2 --> S3["Region 1: lambda LESS than lambda_c is Crushing Plateau at sigma_c"]
    S3 --> S4["Transition Point at lambda equals lambda_c"]
    S4 --> S5["Region 2: lambda GREATER than lambda_c follows Euler Hyperbola sigma_cr equals pi squared E divided by lambda squared"]
    S5 --> S6["Curve Asymptotically Approaches Zero as lambda Increases"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define slenderness ratio. Mention its significance in column design.**

**Model Answer:**
The slenderness ratio ($\lambda$) is defined as the ratio of the effective length ($L_{eff}$) of a compression member to its least radius of gyration ($k_{min}$):
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is a **dimensionless geometric parameter** that classifies a column as short, medium, or long.
2. It governs the **mode of failure** ŌĆö buckling vs. crushing.
3. It is used to determine the **critical buckling load** via Euler's formula: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E A / \lambda^2$.
4. Codes such as IS 800 and IS 456 prescribe **maximum allowable slenderness ratios** (e.g., 180 for steel, 60 for RCC).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the critical slenderness ratio? For mild steel with $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓ and $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm┬▓, compute its value.**

**Model Answer:**
The critical slenderness ratio ($\lambda_c$) is the slenderness ratio at which a column is equally likely to fail by Euler buckling or by direct crushing.

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{320}}$$

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{625} = \pi \times 25 \approx 78.54$$

**Interpretation:** Any mild-steel column with $\lambda > 78.54$ fails by buckling; otherwise by crushing.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`

**(a)** A solid circular steel column is $4$ m long, with both ends fixed. Its diameter is $100$ mm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓ and $\sigma_c = 330$ N/mm┬▓.
Determine:
(i) The slenderness ratio.
(ii) The critical slenderness ratio.
(iii) The Euler crippling load.

**(b)** A rectangular timber column $150$ mm ├Ś $100$ mm in cross-section and $3$ m long is fixed at one end and hinged at the other. Determine the Euler buckling load. Take $E = 12{,}000$ N/mm┬▓. State whether Euler's formula is valid, justifying with $\lambda$ and $\lambda_c$ comparison.

---

#### Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-section properties (solid circle, $D = 100$ mm):**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 100^2}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I_{min} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 100^4}{64} = 4{,}908{,}738.5 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Radius of gyration:**

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}908{,}738.5}{7853.98}} = \sqrt{625} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effective length (both ends fixed, $\alpha = 0.5$):**

$$L_{eff} = 0.5 \times 4000 = 2000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Slenderness ratio (i):**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}} = \dfrac{2000}{25} = 80$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Critical slenderness ratio (ii):**

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{330}} = \pi \sqrt{606.06} \approx 77.32$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Validity check:**

Since $\lambda = 80 > \lambda_c = 77.32$, the column is a **long column**, and Euler's formula is valid.
