**Step 7 ŌĆö Euler crippling load (iii):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4{,}908{,}738.5}{2000^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.694 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^6} \approx 2{,}423{,}605 \text{ N} \approx 2423.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Answers:** $\lambda = 80$, $\lambda_c \approx 77.32$, $P_{cr} \approx 2423.6$ kN.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-section properties (rectangular $b = 100$ mm weak axis, $d = 150$ mm strong axis):**

$$A = 150 \times 100 = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I_{min} = \dfrac{d \cdot b^3}{12} = \dfrac{150 \times 100^3}{12} = 12{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Radius of gyration:**

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{12{,}500{,}000}{15{,}000}} = \sqrt{833.33} \approx 28.87 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effective length (fixed-hinged, $\alpha = 0.7$):**

$$L_{eff} = 0.7 \times 3000 = 2100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}} = \dfrac{2100}{28.87} \approx 72.74$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Validity (using $\sigma_c \approx 50$ N/mm┬▓ for timber):**

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_c}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{12{,}000}{50}} = \pi \sqrt{240} \approx 48.67$$

Since $\lambda = 72.74 > \lambda_c = 48.67$, Euler's formula **is valid**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Euler buckling load:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 12{,}000 \times 12{,}500{,}000}{2100^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{1.4805 \times 10^{12}}{4.41 \times 10^6} \approx 335{,}714 \text{ N} \approx 335.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $P_{cr} \approx 335.7$ kN. Euler's formula is valid because $\lambda > \lambda_c$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the four standard end conditions used to compute effective length of a column. Tabulate the effective-length factor $\alpha$ and the buckling mode shape for each.

**(b)** A hollow rectangular cast-iron column has outer dimensions $120 \times 80$ mm and inner dimensions $80 \times 40$ mm. Length is $5$ m, both ends pinned. Take $E = 1.5 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓, $\sigma_c = 560$ N/mm┬▓, and Rankine constant $a = 1/7500$. Compute:
(i) Slenderness ratio.
(ii) Euler crippling load.
(iii) Rankine crippling load.
(iv) Which value should be adopted for safe design and why?

---

#### Solution to Question B (a):

**Sketch and Tabulation of End Conditions:**

| End Condition | Effective Length Factor $\alpha$ | Buckled Shape Description |
|---|---|---|
| Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned) | $\alpha = 1.00$ | Single half-sine wave, deflection at mid-height |
| Both Ends Fixed (Fixed-Fixed) | $\alpha = 0.50$ | Two half-sine waves, points of contraflexure at $L/4$ and $3L/4$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Free (Fixed-Free) | $\alpha = 2.00$ | Quarter sine wave, max deflection at free end |
| One End Fixed, Other Hinged (Fixed-Pinned) | $\alpha \approx 0.70$ | Asymmetric curve, point of contraflexure between fixed end and mid-length |

**Explanation:**
The effective length factor $\alpha$ captures the *rotational and translational restraint* offered by the end supports. Higher restraint (fixed supports) reduces the effective buckling length, while absence of restraint (free end) doubles or quadruples it. This factor is the bridge between an *idealized mathematical column* and the *real-world boundary condition* in design.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-section properties (hollow rectangle, $D_1 = 120$, $D_2 = 80$, $d_1 = 80$, $d_2 = 40$):**

$$A = (D_1 \cdot D_2) - (d_1 \cdot d_2) = (120 \times 80) - (80 \times 40) = 9600 - 3200 = 6400 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moments of inertia about both axes:**

$$I_{xx} = \dfrac{D_1 \cdot D_2^3 - d_1 \cdot d_2^3}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 80^3 - 80 \times 40^3}{12}$$

$$I_{xx} = \dfrac{120 \times 512{,}000 - 80 \times 64{,}000}{12} = \dfrac{61{,}440{,}000 - 5{,}120{,}000}{12} = \dfrac{56{,}320{,}000}{12} \approx 4{,}693{,}333.3 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_{yy} = \dfrac{D_2 \cdot D_1^3 - d_2 \cdot d_1^3}{12} = \dfrac{80 \times 120^3 - 40 \times 80^3}{12}$$

$$I_{yy} = \dfrac{80 \times 1{,}728{,}000 - 40 \times 512{,}000}{12} = \dfrac{138{,}240{,}000 - 20{,}480{,}000}{12} = \dfrac{117{,}760{,}000}{12} \approx 9{,}813{,}333.3 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify minimum I and compute $k_{min}$:**

$$I_{min} = I_{xx} \approx 4{,}693{,}333.3 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$k_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}693{,}333.3}{6400}} = \sqrt{733.33} \approx 27.08 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Slenderness ratio (both ends pinned, $\alpha = 1$):**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}} = \dfrac{5000}{27.08} \approx 184.6$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Euler crippling load (ii):**

$$P_{Euler} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 4{,}693{,}333.3}{5000^2}$$

$$P_{Euler} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 7.04 \times 10^{11}}{2.5 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{6.948 \times 10^{12}}{2.5 \times 10^7} \approx 277{,}930 \text{ N} \approx 277.93 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rankine crippling load (iii):**

$$P_{Rankine} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a(\lambda^2)} = \dfrac{560 \times 6400}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500}(184.6)^2}$$

$$\lambda^2 = 34{,}077.16$$

$$a\lambda^2 = \dfrac{34{,}077.16}{7500} \approx 4.544$$

$$P_{Rankine} = \dfrac{3{,}584{,}000}{1 + 4.544} = \dfrac{3{,}584{,}000}{5.544} \approx 646{,}465 \text{ N} \approx 646.47 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Safe design choice (iv):**

The **lower of the two loads** governs the safe design, i.e., **$P_{Euler} \approx 277.93$ kN**. The Rankine formula gives a higher value because the column is highly slender; for such columns, Euler buckling is the controlling failure mode.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Lose Marks For):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to identify $I_{min}$:** In Question B(b), many students use $I_{yy}$ (the larger one) and get a *wrong* slenderness ratio. Always use the **weak axis** inertia.
> 2. **Using actual length instead of effective length:** Always check the end conditions and multiply by $\alpha$ before computing $\lambda$.
> 3. **Not validating Euler's formula:** A common 3-mark deduction occurs when students apply $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/L^2$ even for short columns where Euler's formula is *not* valid. **Always compare $\lambda$ with $\lambda_c$ first.**
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and mm in area and inertia calculations. Stick to **mm throughout**.
> 5. **Incorrect end-condition factor:** The value $\alpha = 0.7$ (fixed-hinged) is often misquoted as $0.65$ or $0.6992$. Use $\alpha \approx 0.7$ unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> 6. **Skipping the slenderness ratio entirely:** Many students jump to Euler's formula directly. The KTU examiner expects you to *show* the slenderness ratio calculation as a discrete step ŌĆö this is a guaranteed 2-mark reward.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Definition:** $\lambda = L_{eff} / k_{min}$, dimensionless.
> - **Geometry Source:** Depends on cross-section ($A$, $I_{min}$) and end conditions ($\alpha$).
> - **End Condition Factors:** $\alpha = 1.0$, $0.5$, $2.0$, $0.7$ for hinged-hinged, fixed-fixed, fixed-free, fixed-hinged respectively.
> - **Critical Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E / \sigma_c}$ ŌĆö the boundary between crushing and buckling failures.
> - **Euler's Formula in Slenderness Form:** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$.
> - **Euler's Formula in Inertia Form:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I_{min} / L_{eff}^2$.
> - **Validity:** Euler's formula applies **only when $\lambda > \lambda_c$**. Otherwise use Rankine or Johnson.
> - **Always use $I_{min}$ (least moment of inertia)** because columns buckle about their weakest axis.
> - **Units discipline:** Use mm for all dimensions; $E$ in N/mm┬▓ (MPa); $P$ in N or kN.
> - **Code limits:** IS 800 caps $\lambda \le 180$ for steel compression members; IS 456 caps $\lambda \le 60$ for RCC columns.
> - **Engineering intuition:** A slender column (high $\lambda$) is buckling-prone; a stocky column (low $\lambda$) is crushing-prone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Formula for Short Columns. Theories of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Formula for Short Columns. Theories of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rankine's Formula for Short Columns ŌĆö Definition

**Rankine's Formula** is the most widely used empirical relationship in KTU-endorsed Solid Mechanics for determining the **Crippling Load (Critical Load)** of a short column. A short column (slenderness ratio $\lambda \le 80$ for mild steel, or slenderness ratio typically less than the critical slenderness ratio $\lambda_c$) is one that fails primarily by **crushing** (direct compressive yielding), but whose strength is reduced by the presence of initial curvature, eccentric loading, and the resulting secondary bending stresses.

The formula is a rational combination of two failure limits:
- The **crushing strength** of the material (governs stubby members).
- The **Euler's buckling strength** of the same member treated as a long column (governs slender members).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** "The crippling load $P_R$ of a short column according to Rankine is the load at which the total shortening due to direct compression plus that due to bending is equal to the shortening of a short block of the same material under direct compression equal to its crushing stress."

The empirical Rankine-Gordon formula is:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$

where every term is precisely defined in the KTU Formula Sheet below.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 ŌĆö The Bamboo vs. The Brick:** Imagine supporting a heavy roof. A *short fat brick pillar* will fail by simply **crushing** under the load (squashed flat, no sideways motion). A *long thin bamboo stick* will fail by **buckling** (sudden sideways snap). Most real engineering columns are *in between* ŌĆö they have a finite slenderness, so they have *some* crushing and *some* buckling happening simultaneously. **Rankine's formula is the mathematical "weighted average"** of these two competing failure modes.

**Analogy 2 ŌĆö The Strained Rope:** Picture trying to break a rope by pulling. You could pull it axially (tension ŌĆö rope snaps cleanly), twist it (shear ŌĆö rope fibers slide past each other), or compress it (the fibers spread sideways). *Theories of Failure* are essentially different **"yardsticks"** for predicting at what point the rope (or your machine part) will give up, depending on what kind of stress is most dangerous to the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Material Constants (to memorize):**
> - **Rankine's Constant $a$** for Mild Steel = $\dfrac{1}{7500}$
> - **Rankine's Constant $a$** for Cast Iron = $\dfrac{1}{1600}$
> - **Crushing Stress $\sigma_c$** for Mild Steel $\approx \textbf{320 N/mm}^2$
> - **Young's Modulus $E$** for Mild Steel = $\textbf{2 \times 10^5 N/mm}^2$

## 1.3 Theories of Failure ŌĆö Overview

A machine component in service rarely experiences only uniaxial stress. The state of stress at a critical point is a complex 3-D combination of normal and shear stresses. **Theories of Failure** are mathematical hypotheses that predict *when* a complex 3-D state of stress will cause the material to fail, by *equating* it to a known, simple uniaxial test (tension or shear) on the same material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Theories of Failure (to be studied in detail):**
> 1. **Maximum Principal Stress Theory** (W. J. M. Rankine, 1857) ŌĆö also called *Normal Stress Theory*.
> 2. **Maximum Principal Strain Theory** (Saint-Venant, 1837).
> 3. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** (Tresca, 1864) ŌĆö also called *Guest's Theory*.
> 4. **Total Strain Energy Theory** (Haigh, 1918).
> 5. **Distortion Energy Theory / Shear Strain Energy Theory** (von Mises, 1913; Hencky, 1924) ŌĆö the most widely used for ductile metals.
> 6. **Internal Friction Theory** (Mohr-Coulomb) ŌĆö used for brittle materials like cast iron, concrete.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Formula ŌĆö The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Origin of the Formula

A "perfectly straight" short column should fail by pure crushing at $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$. In reality, columns have:
- **Initial curvature** (slight crookedness from manufacturing).
- **Load eccentricity** (load never truly passes through the centroid).
- **Material inhomogeneities.**

These cause additional **bending stresses** even under a nominally axial load. Hence, the average stress under the load is:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\text{Direct compressive stress}}{1 + \text{Deflection due to bending / Direct deflection}}$$

Rankine, building on the work of **Hodgkinson** and **Gordon**, set this average stress equal to the crushing stress and obtained the formula.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation

1. The column has an **initial bow** (assumed sinusoidal or similar).
2. The total deflection $y$ at the mid-height under load $P$ is the **sum** of the initial bow $y_0$ and the bending deflection $y_1$.
3. The **direct compressive stress** is $P/A$.
4. The **bending stress** at mid-height is $M \cdot c / I = P \cdot y \cdot c / I$.
5. The **maximum compressive stress** at the concave face is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot y_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{P}{A} \left[ 1 + \frac{y_{max} \cdot c}{r^2} \right]$$

where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the radius of gyration.
6. The **total deflection** $y_{max} = y_0 + y_1$, where $y_1 = \dfrac{P y_{max}}{P_e}$ (using the Euler relationship for deflection under eccentric/curved column).
7. Substituting and equating $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_c$ (crushing stress), and after algebra:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_e}$$

where $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ and $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$.

8. Replacing $I = A k^2$ and defining **Rankine's Constant** $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance of $a$:** A larger $a$ means the material is more prone to buckling (e.g., cast iron, $a=1/1600$, vs. mild steel, $a=1/7500$). The constant $a$ has units of $\textbf{(strain)}$ or $\textbf{mm/mm}$, but is dimensionless when SI is consistently used.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

Rankine's formula is used in:
- **Design of short and intermediate struts** in buildings and bridges.
- **Design of connecting rods** in engines.
- **Pedestals** for industrial machinery.
- **Punch press frames** and **stanchions**.
- **Spindles of machine tools** (especially those with low slenderness).

## 2.2 Theories of Failure ŌĆö Detailed Logical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the *maximum* principal stress $\sigma_1$ in a complex stress state reaches the ultimate tensile stress $\sigma_u$ (or elastic limit $\sigma_y$ for ductile materials) of the material as determined from a simple tension test.

**Condition for NO failure:**

$$\sigma_1 \le \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \le \sigma_{uc}$$

where $\sigma_{ut}$ and $\sigma_{uc}$ are ultimate stresses in tension and compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** This theory ignores the effect of the *other* principal stress. It is generally **inconsistent** (gives different results depending on which axis you label as "x") and is best suited for **brittle materials** like cast iron, concrete, and ceramics.

### 2.2.2 Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the *maximum* principal strain in the complex state equals the strain at the elastic limit in a simple tension test.

**Condition for NO failure:**

$$\varepsilon_1 \le \varepsilon_e$$

Using Hooke's law for 3-D stress state:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \right]$$

And setting $\varepsilon_1 = \sigma_e / E$ (the strain at elastic limit in uniaxial tension):

$$\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \sigma_e$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** This theory gives **unconservative (unsafe) results** for ductile materials in cases of compressive principal stress. It is rarely used in modern design.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca / Guest)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the *maximum shear stress* in the complex state equals the maximum shear stress in a simple tension test at yield.

The maximum shear stress in 3-D is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

The maximum shear stress in uniaxial tension at yield is:

$$\tau_{max,tension} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

**Condition for NO failure:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \sigma_y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** This theory is **independent of the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$**. It is widely used for *ductile materials* and gives **safe (conservative)** results compared to experiments.

### 2.2.4 Total Strain Energy Theory (Haigh)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the *total strain energy per unit volume* in the complex state equals the strain energy at the elastic limit per unit volume in a simple tension test.

Total strain energy per unit volume in 3-D:

$$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1) \right]$$

Strain energy at elastic limit in uniaxial tension:

$$U_t = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{2E}$$

**Condition for NO failure:**

$$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1) \le \sigma_e^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Includes the *dilatational* (volumetric) part of strain energy, which is harmless in ductile metals. Hence, it gives **overly conservative results** for ductile materials and is not commonly used.

### 2.2.5 Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises / Hencky / Shear Strain Energy)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the *distortion strain energy* (the part responsible for shape change, *not* volume change) reaches the value at yield in a simple tension test.

The **distortion energy per unit volume** is:

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \mu}{3E} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right]$$

The **yield condition** is $U_d = U_{d,tension}$ at $\sigma_y$:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \le 2 \sigma_y^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most accurate theory for ductile materials** like mild steel, aluminium alloys, and copper. It is the basis of the von Mises stress used in FEA software (ANSYS, Abaqus, SolidWorks Simulation).

### 2.2.6 Internal Friction Theory (Mohr-Coulomb / Mohr's Theory)

**Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the shear stress on any plane reaches a value that depends on the normal stress on that plane, with the relationship:

$$\vert \tau \vert = S_0 - \mu_f \sigma_n$$

where $S_0$ is the cohesion (shear strength when $\sigma_n = 0$) and $\mu_f$ is the coefficient of internal friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Used for **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, soil, rock) and for materials with different strengths in tension and compression. The Mohr's circle diagram is used for graphical representation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_R$ (Rankine Crippling Load) | $\dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e / k)^2}$ | $\textbf{N}$ or $\textbf{kN}$ | Empirical crippling load |
| $P_c$ (Crushing Load) | $\sigma_c \cdot A$ | $\textbf{N}$ | Load causing pure crushing |
| $P_e$ (Euler Buckling Load) | $\dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | $\textbf{N}$ | Load causing elastic buckling |
| $1/P_R$ (Reciprocal Relation) | $\dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_e}$ | $\textbf{N}^{-1}$ | Rankine's fundamental relation |
| $a$ (Rankine's Constant) | $\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ | $\textbf{dimensionless}$ | Material property |
| $k$ (Least Radius of Gyration) | $\sqrt{I_{min}/A}$ | $\textbf{mm}$ | Geometric property |
| $\lambda$ (Slenderness Ratio) | $L_e / k$ | $\textbf{dimensionless}$ | Indicates column type |
| $\sigma_c$ (Crushing Stress) | Material property | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Tabulated in KTU data book |
| $E$ (Young's Modulus) | Material property | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Tabulated in KTU data book |
| $\mu$ (Poisson's Ratio) | $\in [0.25, 0.33]$ for steel | $\textbf{dimensionless}$ | Used in strain energy theories |
| Max. Principal Stress | $\sigma_1 \le \sigma_{ut}$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Brittle materials |
| Max. Principal Strain | $\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \sigma_e$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Rarely used |
| Max. Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \sigma_y$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Conservative for ductile |
| Total Strain Energy | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \le \sigma_e^2$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Overly conservative |
| Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \le 2\sigma_y^2$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Most accurate for ductile |
| Equivalent von Mises Stress | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Used in FEA |
| Internal Friction (Mohr) | $\vert \tau \vert = S_0 - \mu_f \sigma_n$ | $\textbf{N/mm}^2$ | Brittle / frictional materials |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Formula (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Assumptions:**
1. The column has an **initial curvature** (not perfectly straight).
2. The deflection is small (first-order theory).
3. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law up to crushing.
4. The bending moment is $M = P \cdot y$ at every cross-section.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total Deflection at Mid-Height**

The initial bow of the column is approximately:

$$y_0 = y_{initial}$$

Under the load $P$, the additional deflection is $y_1$. By the principle of superposition:

$$y_{max} = y_0 + y_1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relating $y_1$ to the Euler Load**

The differential equation of the deflected shape for a pin-ended column with an initial bow is:

$$E I \frac{d^2 y_1}{dx^2} = -P (y_0 + y_1)$$

The solution for a sinusoidal initial bow $y_0 = y_0^{max} \sin(\pi x / L)$ gives:

$$y_1 = \frac{P \cdot y_0}{P_e - P}$$

Therefore:

$$y_{max} = y_0 + y_1 = y_0 + \frac{P y_0}{P_e - P} = y_0 \left[ \frac{P_e - P + P}{P_e - P} \right] = \frac{y_0 \cdot P_e}{P_e - P}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum Compressive Stress**

The maximum bending moment occurs at mid-height where the deflection is maximum:

$$M_{max} = P \cdot y_{max} = \frac{P \cdot y_0 \cdot P_e}{P_e - P}$$

The maximum compressive stress at the concave face:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot y_0 \cdot P_e \cdot c}{I (P_e - P)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Failure Criterion**

The column is on the verge of crushing when $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot y_0 \cdot P_e \cdot c}{I (P_e - P)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognizing Rankine's Constant**

Define $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ and use $I = A k^2$, with the standard initial-bow parameter $c \cdot y_0 / k^2$ being a small constant. After algebraic manipulation, the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{P} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_e}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Rankine Formula**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e / k)^2}$$

**Verification of Limiting Cases:**
- If $L_e / k \to 0$ (very short column): $P_R \to \sigma_c \cdot A = P_c$ (crushing controls). Ō£ō
- If $L_e / k \to \infty$ (very long column): $P_R \to \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{a (L_e/k)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = P_e$ (Euler controls). Ō£ō

## 3.2 Derivation of Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose Total Strain Energy**

The total strain energy density is:

$$U = U_d + U_v$$

where $U_d$ is the **distortion** (shape-changing) part and $U_v$ is the **volumetric** (volume-changing) part.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Volumetric Strain Energy**

The mean stress is $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$. The volumetric energy is:

$$U_v = \frac{3 (1 - 2\mu)}{2E} \sigma_m^2 = \frac{1 - 2\mu}{6E} (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Strain Energy**

$$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Subtract Volumetric from Total**

$$U_d = U - U_v$$

After expanding and simplifying:

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \mu}{6E} \left[ 2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2) - 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \right]$$

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \mu}{3E} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Failure Criterion**

In a uniaxial tension test at yield: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$, so:

$$U_{d,tension} = \frac{1 + \mu}{3E} \left[ \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_y^2 \right] = \frac{1 + \mu}{E} \sigma_y^2$$

Equating $U_d = U_{d,tension}$:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2 \sigma_y^2$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example ŌĆö Rankine's Formula

**Problem:** A hollow circular cast iron column has outer diameter 200 mm and inner diameter 150 mm. It is 4 m long and is fixed at both ends. Find the safe load using Rankine's formula with a factor of safety of 5. Take $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm┬▓, $a = 1/1600$, and $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Area of Cross-Section**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(200^2 - 150^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = \frac{\pi}{4}(17500) = 13744.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment of Inertia (Minimum)**

For a hollow circle, $I_{min} = I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(200^4 - 150^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.09375 \times 10^9) = 5.367 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effective Length (Fixed-Fixed)**

For fixed-fixed ends: $L_e = L/2 = 4000/2 = 2000$ mm.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Radius of Gyration**

$$k^2 = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{5.367 \times 10^7}{13744.5} = 3904.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k = 62.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{2000}{62.49} = 32.0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Crushing Load**

$$P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A = 550 \times 13744.5 = 7.559 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 7559.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Rankine Crippling Load**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e/k)^2} = \frac{7.559 \times 10^6}{1 + \dfrac{1}{1600} \times (32)^2}$$

$$P_R = \frac{7.559 \times 10^6}{1 + 0.64} = \frac{7.559 \times 10^6}{1.64} = 4.609 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Safe Load**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_R}{FOS} = \frac{4609}{5} = 921.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** Safe Load $\approx \textbf{922 kN}$.

## 3.4 Numerical Example ŌĆö Theories of Failure

**Problem:** At a point in a machine component, the stresses are: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. The elastic limit in simple tension is $\sigma_e = 200$ MPa and Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.3$. Check the safety using all five theories (Take FOS = 2).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Principal Stresses**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{400 + 900} = 60 \pm 36.06$$

$$\sigma_1 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum Principal Stress Theory**

$\sigma_1 = 96.06$ MPa. Compare with $\sigma_e / FOS = 200/2 = 100$ MPa.

Since $96.06 < 100$, the design is **SAFE**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum Principal Strain Theory**

$$\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = 96.06 - 0.3(23.94 + 0) = 96.06 - 7.18 = 88.88 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $88.88 < 100$, the design is **SAFE**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 96.06 - 0 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare with $\sigma_e / FOS = 100$ MPa. Since $96.06 < 100$, design is **SAFE**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total Strain Energy Theory**

LHS = $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)$

$$= 96.06^2 + 23.94^2 + 0 - 2(0.3)(96.06 \times 23.94 + 0 + 0)$$

$$= 9227.5 + 573.1 - 2(0.3)(2300.0) = 9800.6 - 1380.0 = 8420.6$$

RHS = $\sigma_e^2 / (FOS)^2 = 200^2 / 4 = 10000$ (MPa┬▓)

Since $8420.6 < 10000$, design is **SAFE**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2$$

$$= (96.06 - 23.94)^2 + (23.94 - 0)^2 + (0 - 96.06)^2$$

$$= 72.12^2 + 23.94^2 + 96.06^2 = 5201.3 + 573.1 + 9227.5 = 15001.9$$

RHS = $2 \sigma_e^2 / (FOS)^2 = 2 \times 40000 / 4 = 20000$

Since $15001.9 < 20000$, design is **SAFE**.

**Conclusion:** The component is **safe** by all five theories.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Rankine's Formula Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Input Data: L, Le, k, A, E, sigma_c, a] --> node2[Compute Crushing Load Pc = sigma_c x A]
    node1 --> node3[Compute Euler Load Pe = pi^2 x E x I / Le^2]
    node2 --> node4[Apply Rankine Equation: 1/PR = 1/Pc + 1/Pe]
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5[Compute Crippling Load PR]
    node5 --> node6[Check Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le / k]
    node6 --> node7{Is lambda < 80?}
    node7 -- YES --> node8[Short Column: Rankine Governs]
    node7 -- NO --> node9[Long Column: Euler Governs]
    node8 --> node10[Compute Safe Load: P_safe = PR / FOS]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Output: Final Design Load]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart ŌĆö Theories of Failure Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Identify Material Type] --> nodeB{Is material DUCTILE?}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC[Use von Mises or Tresca]
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD[Use Rankine or Mohr]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Compute Principal Stresses sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Apply Tresca: sigma1 - sigma3 less than sigma_y / FOS]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Apply von Mises: 2sigma_y^2 check]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Apply Max Principal Stress: sigma1 less than sigma_ut / FOS]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Apply Mohr Internal Friction: tau = S0 - mu x sigma_n]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Conclusion: SAFE or UNSAFE]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Two Failure Modes of a Short Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Axial Load P] --> nodeQ[Short Column]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Direct Crushing Component]
    nodeQ --> nodeS[Buckling Component]
    nodeR --> nodeR1[Stress: sigma = P / A]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[Stress: pi^2 E / lambda^2]
    nodeR1 --> nodeT[Total Combined Stress]
    nodeS1 --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeU[Rankine Formula Combines Both]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Stress State on a Differential Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    elem[2D Stress Element] --> sX[sigma_x on left and right faces]
    elem --> sY[sigma_y on top and bottom faces]
    elem --> tau[tau_xy on all four faces]
    sX --> princ[Compute Principal Stresses]
    sY --> princ
    tau --> princ
    princ --> out[sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    out --> theory[Apply Chosen Theory of Failure]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**

**Q: What is Rankine's formula? Why is it used for short columns instead of Euler's formula?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rankine's formula gives the **empirical crippling load** of a short column, combining the crushing strength and Euler's buckling strength:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^2}$$

Euler's formula $P_e = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ is derived assuming a *perfectly straight, axially-loaded, slender* column and is **valid only for long columns** (slenderness ratio above the critical value). Short columns fail by a combination of direct crushing and buckling due to initial curvature and load eccentricity. **Rankine's formula** accounts for both effects empirically and is valid for all slenderness ratios (it reduces to $P_c$ for stubby columns and $P_e$ for very slender ones), making it the preferred choice for **intermediate and short columns** in KTU-endorsed design practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark question):** Students often confuse Rankine's *crushing stress* $\sigma_c$ with Euler's *yield stress*. **Never** use $\sigma_y$ in Rankine's formula ŌĆö use $\sigma_c$ only. Also, $\sigma_c$ and $E$ must be in *consistent* units (both N/mm┬▓ or both MPa).

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Dec 2022]**

**Q: List the important theories of failure for ductile and brittle materials. Why is distortion energy theory preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**For Ductile Materials** (e.g., mild steel, aluminium, copper):
1. Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)
2. Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises / Hencky)
3. Total Strain Energy Theory (Haigh)

**For Brittle Materials** (e.g., cast iron, concrete, ceramics):
1. Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine)
2. Internal Friction Theory (Mohr-Coulomb)
3. Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant)

**Why von Mises is preferred:** The **Distortion Energy Theory** is most accurate because it considers only the energy that causes *shape change* (which is what causes yielding in ductile metals), discarding the harmless *volumetric* part of strain energy. Experimental results for ductile materials under combined stress match the von Mises yield locus much better than Tresca or the other theories. It is the **basis of modern FEA codes** (ANSYS, ABAQUS, NASTRAN).

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Full Question]**

**Q: A) Define Rankine's constant. A hollow rectangular column of cross-section 200 mm ├Ś 150 mm outside and 160 mm ├Ś 110 mm inside carries an axial load of 900 kN. The column is 6 m long and is effectively fixed at one end and hinged at the other end. Determine the factor of safety of the column using Rankine's formula. Take $a = 1/7500$, $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm┬▓, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm┬▓.**

**B) State and explain the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and Distortion Energy Theory of failure with the necessary expressions.**

---

### **Part (a) Solution ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cross-sectional area**

$$A = 200 \times 150 - 160 \times 110 = 30000 - 17600 = 12400 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moments of Inertia**

$$I_x = \frac{200 \times 150^3}{12} - \frac{160 \times 110^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3.375 \times 10^6}{1} - \frac{1.6 \times 1.331 \times 10^6}{1}$$

$$I_x = 5.625 \times 10^7 - 2.1296 \times 10^7 = 3.4954 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_y = \frac{150 \times 200^3}{12} - \frac{110 \times 160^3}{12} = 1.0 \times 10^8 - 3.7547 \times 10^7 = 6.2453 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

Minimum $I = I_x = 3.4954 \times 10^7$ mmŌü┤.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Radius of Gyration (Minimum)**

$$k^2 = \frac{I_{min}}{A} = \frac{3.4954 \times 10^7}{12400} = 2819 \text{ mm}^2, \quad k = 53.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Effective Length (Fixed-Hinged)**

For fixed-hinged condition: $L_e = 0.7 L = 0.7 \times 6000 = 4200$ mm.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Crushing Load**

$$P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A = 320 \times 12400 = 3.968 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3968 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rankine Crippling Load**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e/k)^2} = \frac{3.968 \times 10^6}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500} \times (4200/53.1)^2}$$

$$\frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{4200}{53.1} = 79.1$$

$$P_R = \frac{3.968 \times 10^6}{1 + (1/7500) \times 6256.8} = \frac{3.968 \times 10^6}{1 + 0.8342} = \frac{3.968 \times 10^6}{1.8342}$$

$$P_R = 2.164 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2164 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Factor of Safety**

$$FOS = \frac{P_R}{P_{applied}} = \frac{2164}{900} = \textbf{2.4}$$

### **Part (b) Solution ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's Theory):**

According to this theory, failure occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in the complex state of stress equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test.

For a 3-D stress state with principal stresses $\sigma_1 > \sigma_2 > \sigma_3$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

In uniaxial tension at yield, $\tau_{max,tension} = \sigma_y / 2$.

**Design equation:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS}$$

This theory is **independent of the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$** and gives **conservative (safe) results** for ductile materials.

**Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises / Hencky):**

According to this theory, failure occurs when the **distortion strain energy** per unit volume (the energy responsible for shape change, excluding volumetric change) equals the distortion energy at yield in a simple tension test.

For a 3-D stress state:

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \mu}{3E} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right]$$

**Design equation:**

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \le \frac{2 \sigma_y^2}{(FOS)^2}$$

**Equivalent von Mises stress:**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS}$$

This theory is the **most accurate** for ductile materials and forms the basis of modern FEA software.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Full Question]**

**Q: A) Derive Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a short column. Explain the significance of Rankine's constant.**

**B) At a point in a structural member, the principal stresses are: $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -60$ MPa, and $\sigma_3 = 30$ MPa. Find the factor of safety according to: (i) Maximum Principal Stress Theory, (ii) Maximum Shear Stress Theory, and (iii) Distortion Energy Theory. Take elastic limit $\sigma_e = 240$ MPa.**

---

### **Part (a) Solution ŌĆö 7 marks**

Refer to **Section 3.1 above** for the complete step-by-step derivation of Rankine's formula. The final result is:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e/k)^2}, \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$$

**Significance of Rankine's Constant $a$:**
1. It is a **material property** that quantifies the *bending* contribution to failure of a column.
2. Larger $a$ ŌåÆ material is more **prone to buckling** (e.g., cast iron has $a = 1/1600$, mild steel $a = 1/7500$).
3. It is the ratio of the crushing stress to a measure of elastic stiffness ($\pi^2 E$).
4. It governs the transition from a crushing-dominated failure (short columns) to a buckling-dominated failure (long columns).

### **Part (b) Solution ŌĆö 7 marks**

Given: $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -60$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = 30$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 240$ MPa.

Note: Re-arrange in order ŌĆö $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 30$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -60$ MPa (largest to smallest, algebraic).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Maximum Principal Stress Theory**

$$FOS_1 = \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{240}{120} = \textbf{2.0}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 120 - (-60) = 180 \text{ MPa}$$

$$FOS_2 = \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{240}{180} = \textbf{1.333}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2$$

$$= (120 - 30)^2 + (30 - (-60))^2 + ((-60) - 120)^2$$

$$= 90^2 + 90^2 + (-180)^2 = 8100 + 8100 + 32400 = 48600 \text{ MPa}^2$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{48600}{2}} = \sqrt{24300} = 155.88 \text{ MPa}$$

$$FOS_3 = \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{240}{155.88} = \textbf{1.54}$$

**Comparative Result Table:**

| Theory | FOS | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Max. Principal Stress | 2.00 | Most liberal (unconservative) |
| Max. Shear Stress (Tresca) | 1.33 | Most conservative (safe) |
| Distortion Energy (von Mises) | 1.54 | Most accurate ŌĆö between the two |

**Note:** The von Mises value lies *between* Tresca and Max Principal Stress, which is the classic KTU textbook pattern.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Critical!):**
> 1. **Units consistency**: Convert all stresses to N/mm┬▓ (MPa) and lengths to mm **before** substituting. Many students lose 2 marks per question for unit mismatch.
> 2. **Ordering of principal stresses**: Always label $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ (algebraically, not in absolute value). For Tresca, *always* use $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$, **never** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$.
> 3. **Rankine's constant units**: When using $a = 1/7500$ directly, the formula gives the correct unitless ratio. But if $L_e$ is in *m*, first convert to *mm*; otherwise the slenderness ratio term is wrong by a factor of $10^6$.
> 4. **Effective length $L_e$**: For KTU, memorize the four cases:
>    - Both ends hinged: $L_e = L$
>    - Both ends fixed: $L_e = L/2$
>    - One end fixed, one end free: $L_e = 2L$
>    - One end fixed, one end hinged: $L_e = 0.7L \approx L/\sqrt{2}$
> 5. **FOS sign convention**: FOS < 1 ŌåÆ UNSAFE; FOS > 1 ŌåÆ SAFE. Always state the conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### **Rankine's Formula ŌĆö Key Points**
- Ō£ģ **Definition**: $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e/k)^2}$
- Ō£ģ **Fundamental relation**: $\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_e}$
- Ō£ģ **Rankine's constant**: $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ (dimensionless)
- Ō£ģ **Crushing load**: $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$
- Ō£ģ **Euler's load**: $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$
- Ō£ģ **Limiting cases**: $P_R \to P_c$ for stubby columns; $P_R \to P_e$ for slender columns.
- Ō£ģ **Validity**: Slenderness ratio $\lambda < 80$ (for mild steel); above this, Euler's formula is used.
- Ō£ģ **Why Rankine over Euler?** Rankine accounts for *initial curvature* and *eccentricity* ŌĆö real columns are never perfect.

### **Theories of Failure ŌĆö Key Points**
- Ō£ģ **Max. Principal Stress Theory (Rankine)**: Use for **brittle materials**. Equation: $\sigma_1 \le \sigma_u$.
- Ō£ģ **Max. Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant)**: $\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \sigma_e$. **Rarely used**, gives unsafe results.
- Ō£ģ **Max. Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \sigma_y$. **Conservative** for ductile.
- Ō£ģ **Total Strain Energy Theory (Haigh)**: Overly conservative. Includes volumetric energy (which is harmless).
- Ō£ģ **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**: **Most accurate** for ductile metals. Basis of FEA.
- Ō£ģ **Internal Friction Theory (Mohr)**: For **brittle** and frictional materials. $\tau = S_0 - \mu_f \sigma_n$.
- Ō£ģ **Ordering of principal stresses**: ALWAYS $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ algebraically.
- Ō£ģ **For plane stress**, $\sigma_3 = 0$ always.

### **Material Constants (Memorize)**
| Material | $a$ (Rankine) | $\sigma_c$ (N/mm┬▓) | $E$ (N/mm┬▓) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | $1/7500$ | 320 | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Cast Iron | $1/1600$ | 550 | $1.2 \times 10^5$ |
| Wrought Iron | $1/9000$ | 250 | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Timber | $1/750$ | 50 | $1.2 \times 10^4$ |

### **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tips**
- ŌÅ▒ Always show the **unit check** explicitly in numerical problems.
- ŌÅ▒ Mention the **theory assumption** at the start of every solution.
- ŌÅ▒ Use **consistent significant figures** (typically 3ŌĆō4 decimals in FOS answers).
- ŌÅ▒ In choice questions, attempt the *easier* sub-part first to bank easy marks.
- ŌÅ▒ Always **state a conclusion** at the end of every problem (SAFE/UNSAFE).
- ŌÅ▒ For KTU Module 4 specifically, focus more numerical practice on **Rankine + combined stress problems**, which appear almost every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Theory for Maximum Normal Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Theory for Maximum Normal Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rankine's Theory of Maximum Normal Stress** (also known as the *Maximum Principal Stress Theory* or *Normal Stress Theory of Failure*) is a classical **strength hypothesis** used in Solid Mechanics to predict the failure of **brittle materials** under complex states of stress. 

According to the formal KTU 2024 syllabus statement, the theory postulates:

> *"Failure of a brittle material occurs when the **maximum principal stress** (ŽāŌéü) developed at a point in the stressed material reaches the ultimate strength of the material (Žā_u), irrespective of the values of the other principal stresses (ŽāŌéé and ŽāŌéā)."*

In mathematical form, the failure criterion is expressed as:

$$
\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{(for tensile loading)}
$$

$$
\vert \sigma_2 \vert \geq \sigma_{uc} \quad \text{(for compressive loading)}
$$

Where:
- **ŽāŌéü** = Major (maximum) principal stress in **N/mm┬▓** (MPa)
- **ŽāŌéé** = Minor (minimum) principal stress in **N/mm┬▓** (MPa)
- **Žā_ut** = Ultimate tensile strength of the material in **N/mm┬▓** (MPa)
- **Žā_uc** = Ultimate compressive strength of the material in **N/mm┬▓** (MPa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** This theory was historically proposed by **William John Macquorn Rankine** (1858) and is classified under the **Classical Failure Theories** group. It is **NOT recommended for ductile materials** (where shear/distortion theories like Tresca and Von-Mises are preferred).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **glass rod** being bent slowly between your hands. The glass will suddenly snap the moment the pulling force along its outermost fiber exceeds the molecular bonding strength of glass. Notice that:

- The break happens **suddenly**, without any prior "necking" or visible bending.
- It snaps **perpendicular** to the direction of the maximum pulling force.
- The other stresses (shear, lateral compression) almost seem **irrelevant** to the breaking event.

This real-world observation is the **physical intuition** behind Rankine's Theory:
> The material "doesn't care" about the intermediate stresses ŌĆö it fails the instant the **single largest tensile pull** exceeds the material's molecular grip.

### ­¤Ä» The "Cracker Barrel" Analogy

Consider three walnuts being squeezed in your palm from different directions:
- Walnut 1: Pushed lightly (ŽāŌéé = small)
- Walnut 2: Pushed moderately (ŽāŌéé = medium)  
- Walnut 3: Pushed VERY HARD (ŽāŌéü = large)

**Rankine's logic:** Only Walnut 3 matters! The shell cracks the moment the **single hardest squeeze** exceeds the breaking strength of the shell ŌĆö regardless of how the other two walnuts are being pressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Rankine's theory assumes principal stresses act **independently**. The material is treated as if ŽāŌéü, ŽāŌéé, and ŽāŌéā are **three separate uniaxial tests** running in parallel, and failure is triggered by whichever one fails first.

---

## 1.3 Why This Theory Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Typical Application of Rankine's Theory |
|------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Brittle cast iron end-caps under internal pressure |
| **Cement & Concrete Structures** | Columns, beams, retaining walls |
| **Ceramic Components** | Cutting tools, insulators, refractories |
| **Geotechnical Engineering** | Rock mechanics, soil failure analysis |
| **Cast Iron Machine Parts** | Pulleys, gears, brake drums, machine tool beds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Data Needed:** To apply Rankine's theory, you typically need **Žā_ut** (Ultimate Tensile Strength) and **Žā_uc** (Ultimate Compressive Strength) from a standard uniaxial tension/compression test as per **IS 1608 / ASTM E8** testing standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The theory rests on **three core assumptions** that define its applicability:

### Assumption 1: Brittle Material Behavior
The material must exhibit **brittle fracture characteristics** ŌĆö meaning fracture occurs with negligible plastic deformation. The stress-strain curve is essentially **linear up to failure**.

### Assumption 2: Principal Stress Dominance
Only the **algebraically maximum principal stress** controls failure. The other principal stresses are assumed to have **no influence** on the fracture process.

### Assumption 3: Isotropic Homogeneity
The material is treated as **isotropic** (same strength in all directions) and **homogeneous** (uniform composition throughout).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

To apply Rankine's Theory in any 2D stress problem, follow this structured logic:

**Step 1:** Identify the **general state of stress** at the critical point:
- Normal stresses: ŽāŌéō, Žā_y
- Shear stress: ŽäŌéōßĄ¦

**Step 2:** Compute the **principal stresses** using the standard 2D transformation equation:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **sign convention** (tensile positive, compressive negative) to identify:
- ŽāŌéü = Major (maximum algebraic) principal stress
- ŽāŌéé = Minor (minimum algebraic) principal stress

**Step 4:** Compare ŽāŌéü with **Žā_ut** and ŽāŌéé with **Žā_uc**:
- If ŽāŌéü (tensile) Ōēź Žā_ut ŌåÆ **TENSILE FAILURE** predicted
- If ŽāŌéé (compressive) Ōēż -Žā_uc ŌåÆ **COMPRESSIVE FAILURE** predicted

**Step 5:** Compute the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** using the governing formula:
- For tensile condition: $\quad FOS_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$
- For compressive condition: $\quad FOS_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert}$

**Step 6:** Report the **minimum** of (FOS_t, FOS_c) as the **governing factor of safety**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** The theory is **independent** of ŽāŌéé in tension cases and **independent** of ŽāŌéü in compression cases. This independence is both its **simplicity advantage** and its **major inaccuracy limitation**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas** ŌĆö they form the core of every numerical question in KTU University Exams on this topic.

| # | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|-------|
| 1 | 2D Principal Stress (Major) | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 2 | 2D Principal Stress (Minor) | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 4 | Principal Plane Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | radians |
| 5 | Tensile Failure Criterion | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 6 | Compressive Failure Criterion | $\vert \sigma_2 \vert \geq \sigma_{uc}$ | N/mm┬▓ |
| 7 | Factor of Safety (Tensile) | $FOS_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Factor of Safety (Compressive) | $FOS_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Governing FoS | $FOS = \min(FOS_t, FOS_c)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Octahedral Stress Component | $\sigma_{oct} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ | N/mm┬▓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU boards, **tensile stresses are POSITIVE** and **compressive stresses are NEGATIVE**. Always retain signs when computing ŽāŌéü and ŽāŌéé.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

Rankine's theory provides the **most conservative** failure prediction among all classical theories. This makes it:

- **Useful as a preliminary design check** for brittle components
- **Reliable for uniaxial tension-dominated scenarios** (e.g., brittle beams in pure bending)
- **Appropriate for materials with Žā_ut Ōē¬ Žā_uc** (typical of concrete, cast iron)
- **Often used as a "first-cut" screening tool** before applying more sophisticated theories

### Comparative Behavior with Other Theories

| Theory | Applicable Material | Failure Mode Predicted |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Rankine (Max Normal Stress)** | Brittle | Cracking (tensile) / Crushing (compressive) |
| **Tresca (Max Shear Stress)** | Ductile | Yielding |
| **Von-Mises (Distortion Energy)** | Ductile | Yielding |
| **Mohr's Theory** | Brittle & Ductile | Combined shear/normal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Whenever a problem specifies a **brittle material** (cast iron, concrete, plaster, glass, ceramic) with **both Žā_ut and Žā_uc given**, Rankine's Theory is **always the first preferred choice** unless the question explicitly asks for Mohr's or another theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Principal Stress Equation (Foundation Recap)

Starting from a 2D stress element with ŽāŌéō, Žā_y, ŽäŌéōßĄ¦, the normal stress on an inclined plane at angle ╬Ė is:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
$$

For the **principal plane**, the shear stress component vanishes. Differentiating Žā_╬Ė with respect to ╬Ė and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin(2\theta) + 2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

Substituting back into Žā_╬Ė expression and simplifying (using sin and cos of the angle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **principal stress equation** that forms the bedrock of Rankine's theory application.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 ŌĆö Pure Bending of a Brittle Beam (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style):**
> A simply supported cast iron beam of rectangular cross-section **80 mm ├Ś 120 mm** (depth) carries a central point load of **P = 30 kN**. The span is **L = 2 m**. The ultimate tensile and compressive strengths of cast iron are **Žā_ut = 200 N/mm┬▓** and **Žā_uc = 600 N/mm┬▓**. Using **Rankine's Theory**, determine the **factor of safety** at the critical section. (Self-weight neglected)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Critical Section
Maximum bending moment occurs at the **mid-span** for a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{PL}{4}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 2000}{4} = 15{,}000{,}000 \text{ N┬Ęmm} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Section Properties

The section modulus for a rectangular cross-section:

$$
Z = \frac{bd^2}{6}
$$

Where **b = 80 mm** (width) and **d = 120 mm** (depth).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{80 \times 120^2}{6} \\
  &= \frac{80 \times 14{,}400}{6} \\
  &= \frac{1{,}152{,}000}{6} \\
  &= 192{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Extreme Fibre Stresses

For pure bending, the **bending stress** at distance y from neutral axis is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

At the **extreme fibre** (y = d/2 = 60 mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z} \\
            &= \frac{15 \times 10^6}{192{,}000} \\
            &= 78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (Tensile at bottom, Compressive at top)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Identify Principal Stresses at Top Fibre (Compression Zone)

At the top fibre of the mid-span, **Žā_y = 0**, **Žä = 0** (pure bending assumption at extreme fibres):

$$
\sigma_x = -78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{compressive}), \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = 0
$$

Applying principal stress formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{-78.125 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{-78.125 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + 0^2} \\
            &= -39.0625 \pm 39.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = -78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Identify Principal Stresses at Bottom Fibre (Tension Zone)

At the bottom fibre:

$$
\sigma_x = +78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{tensile}), \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{78.125 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{78.125 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + 0^2} \\
            &= 39.0625 \pm 39.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 = +78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Rankine's Failure Criterion

**Tensile Check (Bottom Fibre):**

$$
FOS_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{78.125} = 2.56
$$

**Compressive Check (Top Fibre):**

$$
FOS_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert} = \frac{600}{78.125} = 7.68
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Determine Governing Factor of Safety

$$
\boxed{FOS_{governing} = \min(2.56, 7.68) = 2.56}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The bottom fibre (tension side) governs the design with FoS = **2.56**. This is consistent with brittle cast iron behavior ŌĆö cast iron is **always weaker in tension than compression**, hence the tensile face governs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 ŌĆö Combined Loading on a Brittle Shaft (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style):**
> A circular shaft of diameter **d = 60 mm** is simultaneously subjected to:
> - An axial tensile load **P = 50 kN**
> - A bending moment **M = 2 kN┬Ęm**
> - A torque **T = 1.5 kN┬Ęm**
> 
> The material is **brittle** with Žā_ut = **150 N/mm┬▓** and Žā_uc = **450 N/mm┬▓**.
> Determine the **factor of safety** using Rankine's Theory.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Section Properties

For circular cross-section:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}, \quad Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}, \quad J = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi \times 60^2}{4} = 2827.43 \text{ mm}^2 \\
Z &= \frac{\pi \times 60^3}{32} = 21{,}205.75 \text{ mm}^3 \\
J &= \frac{\pi \times 60^3}{16} = 42{,}411.50 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Individual Stresses

**Direct Tensile Stress (Axial):**

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{2827.43} = 17.68 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Bending Stress (Maximum at extreme fibre):**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{21{,}205.75} = 94.31 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Torsional Shear Stress (Maximum at surface):**

$$
\tau = \frac{T}{J/R} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = 35.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Determine Stresses at the Critical Point

The **critical point** is at the top (or bottom) of the shaft on the tensile side where bending stress adds to axial stress:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_d + \sigma_b = 17.68 + 94.31 = 111.99 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 0 \text{ (free surface)}
$$

$$
\tau_{xy} = 35.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Principal Stresses

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{111.99 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{111.99 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (35.37)^2} \\
            &= 55.995 \pm \sqrt{(55.995)^2 + (35.37)^2} \\
            &= 55.995 \pm \sqrt{3135.44 + 1251.04} \\
            &= 55.995 \pm \sqrt{4386.48} \\
            &= 55.995 \pm 66.23
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 = 55.995 + 66.23 = 122.225 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{tensile})
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 55.995 - 66.23 = -10.235 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{compressive})
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Apply Rankine's Criterion

**Tensile Check:**

$$
FOS_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{150}{122.225} = 1.227
$$

**Compressive Check:**

$$
FOS_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert} = \frac{450}{10.235} = 43.97
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Governing Factor of Safety

$$
\boxed{FOS_{governing} = \min(1.227, 43.97) = 1.227}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** With FoS = 1.227 < 1.5 (typical design minimum), the shaft is **UNSAFE**. The student should recommend either: (a) increase shaft diameter to ~70 mm, or (b) upgrade to a higher-strength material.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | Žā_ut / Žā_uc Ratio | Rankine Prediction Accuracy | Recommended Theory |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| Cast Iron (Žā_ut Ōēł 200, Žā_uc Ōēł 600) | 1 : 3 | **Excellent** | Rankine |
| Concrete (Žā_ut Ōēł 30, Žā_uc Ōēł 5) | 1 : 6 to 1 : 10 | **Excellent** | Rankine |
| Mild Steel (Žā_ut = 410, Žā_uc = 410) | 1 : 1 | **Poor** (over-conservative) | Tresca / Von-Mises |
| Brittle Glass (Žā_ut Ōēł 30, Žā_uc Ōēł 1000) | 1 : 30+ | **Moderate** | Mohr's Theory |
| Timber (anisotropic) | Highly variable | **Not applicable** | Hankinson / Norris |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rankine's Theory Application

The diagram below illustrates the **decision flow** when applying Rankine's Theory to a given stress state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: General 2D Stress State sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Step 1: Identify Material Properties sigma_ut, sigma_uc]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Sign Convention Tensile positive, Compressive negative]
    D --> E{Major Principal Stress sigma_1}
    E -->|Tensile positive| F[Compute FOS_t equals sigma_ut divided by sigma_1]
    E -->|Compressive or zero| G[Compute FOS_c equals sigma_uc divided by absolute sigma_2]
    F --> H{Is FOS_t greater than 1}
    G --> I{Is FOS_c greater than 1}
    H -->|Yes| J[Step 4: Determine Governing FoS as minimum of FOS_t and FOS_c]
    H -->|No| K[FAILURE PREDICTED in TENSION]
    I -->|Yes| J
    I -->|No| L[FAILURE PREDICTED in COMPRESSION]
    J --> M[Output: Factor of Safety and Failure Mode]
    K --> N[Output: Redesign Required]
    L --> N
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Map ŌĆö Rankine Theory Failure Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Classical Failure Theories Universe]
        direction TB
        B[Brittle Materials Group]
        C[Ductile Materials Group]
        B --> D[Rankine Theory - Max Normal Stress]
        B --> E[Modified Mohr Theory]
        B --> F[Brittle Coulomb Mohr]
        C --> G[Tresca Theory - Max Shear]
        C --> H[Von Mises Theory - Distortion Energy]
    end

    subgraph I[Rankine Theory Characteristics]
        direction TB
        J[Material: Brittle Only]
        K[Failure Mode: Sudden Fracture]
        L[Input: sigma_ut and sigma_uc]
        M[Output: Factor of Safety]
        N[Limitation: Ignores Intermediate Principal Stress]
    end

    D -. Links to .-> I
```

---

## 4.3 Stress Element Visual Representation (ASCII Schematic)

The schematic below represents the **stress state** on a 2D element and the corresponding principal stress directions as per Rankine's theory:

```
        Normal Stress Distribution on 2D Element

        Top Fiber (Compression Zone)

        |                                |
        |    Žä_xy  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║              |
        |                                |
        |  Žā_x (horizontal normal)       |
        |                                |
        ----------------------------------
        Bottom Fiber (Tension Zone)

        Principal Stress Directions (Rankine):
        ŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉ

                     ŽāŌéü (Major)
                       Ōåæ

               ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
               Ōöé       Ōöé       Ōöé
        ŽāŌéé ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Ōöé   X   Ōöé   X   Ōöé ŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ ŽāŌéé (Minor)
               Ōöé (Critical Point) Ōöé
               ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś

        Note: ŽāŌéü controls failure if tensile
              ŽāŌéé controls failure if compressive
```

## 4.4 Comparative Failure Envelope ŌĆö 2D Mohr's Circle Perspective

```mermaid

    A[Material Point Under Biaxial Stress] --> B[Plot Mohr Circle]
    B --> C{Circle Touches Sigma_ut Line}

```
