<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"State Rankine's Theory of failure. Mention its applicability and limitations."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** Rankine's Theory of Maximum Normal Stress states that *"failure of a brittle material occurs when the maximum principal stress developed in the material equals or exceeds the ultimate strength of the material in a simple uniaxial test."*

**Applicability (1 Mark):** 
- Applicable to **brittle materials** like cast iron, concrete, plaster, ceramics, and stone.
- Suitable where fracture occurs **without prior plastic deformation**.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- It **ignores the influence of the other principal stresses**.
- Does not account for **shear stress effects** on failure.
- Inaccurate for **ductile materials**.
- Cannot handle **biaxial stress reversal** cases reliably.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between Rankine's Theory and Tresca's Theory of failure."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Rankine's Theory | Tresca's Theory |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Material** | Brittle | Ductile |
| **Failure Criterion** | ŽāŌéü = Žā_ut | Žä_max = Žä_y |
| **Failure Mode** | Fracture | Yielding |
| **Stress Considered** | Maximum principal stress only | Maximum shear stress |
| **Strength Parameter** | Žā_ut, Žā_uc | Shear yield strength |
| **Application** | Cast iron, concrete | Mild steel, aluminium |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Rankine's theory is **simpler but conservative**, while Tresca's is more accurate for ductile materials.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Module 4]`

> A circular shaft of diameter **100 mm** is subjected to a **bending moment of 5 kN┬Ęm** and a **torque of 4 kN┬Ęm**. The material is brittle with Žā_ut = **120 MPa** and Žā_uc = **360 MPa**. Determine the **factor of safety** using **Rankine's Theory**. If the FoS is less than 2, suggest the required diameter.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Principal Stress Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Section Properties:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 100^2}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 100^3}{32} = 98{,}174.77 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = 196{,}349.54 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Individual Stresses at Extreme Fibre (Tensile Side):**

Bending stress:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{98{,}174.77} = 50.93 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Shear stress (torsion):

$$
\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 100^3} = 20.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Principal Stresses:**

$$
\sigma_x = 50.93 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = 20.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{50.93 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{50.93 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (20.37)^2} \\
            &= 25.465 \pm \sqrt{648.79 + 414.94} \\
            &= 25.465 \pm \sqrt{1063.73} \\
            &= 25.465 \pm 32.61
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 25.465 + 32.61 = 58.08 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{tensile})
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 25.465 - 32.61 = -7.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{compressive})
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Factor of Safety and Redesign (7 Marks)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Rankine's Criterion:**

Tensile Check:

$$
FOS_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{120}{58.08} = 2.07
$$

Compressive Check:

$$
FOS_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert} = \frac{360}{7.15} = 50.35
$$

**Governing FoS:**

$$
\boxed{FOS = \min(2.07, 50.35) = 2.07}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verification:**

Since FoS = 2.07 > 2, **no redesign is required**. The shaft is safe under Rankine's theory.

If the required FoS were higher (say 3), then:

$$
\sigma_{1,allowable} = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{3} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Solving $\sigma_1 = 40$ yields a new diameter of approximately **112 mm** (computed by iteration).

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Module 4]`

> A simply supported cast iron beam of **I-section** (flange width 120 mm, flange thickness 15 mm, web thickness 10 mm, overall depth 200 mm) carries a **uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m** over a span of **4 m**. The ultimate tensile and compressive strengths are **Žā_ut = 150 N/mm┬▓** and **Žā_uc = 450 N/mm┬▓**. Using Rankine's Theory, determine the **factor of safety** at the critical section.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Section Properties & Bending Stresses (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Maximum Bending Moment:**

For simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times 4000^2}{8} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moment of Inertia (I-section):**

For the I-section, treating it as a composite rectangle (full 120 ├Ś 200 minus the cut-outs):

$$
I = \frac{BD^3}{12} - \frac{(B-b)d^3}{12}
$$

Where B = 120 mm, D = 200 mm, b = 10 mm, d = 200 - 2(15) = 170 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{120 \times 200^3}{12} - \frac{(120-10) \times 170^3}{12} \\
  &= \frac{120 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} - \frac{110 \times 4{,}913{,}000}{12} \\
  &= 80 \times 10^6 - 45.03 \times 10^6 \\
  &= 34.97 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Section Modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{34.97 \times 10^6}{100} = 349{,}700 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Extreme Fibre Stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{349{,}700} = 57.19 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Factor of Safety Using Rankine's Theory (7 Marks)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Principal Stresses at Extreme Fibres:**

At top fibre (compressive side): ŽāŌéü = 0, ŽāŌéé = -57.19 N/mm┬▓

At bottom fibre (tensile side): ŽāŌéü = +57.19 N/mm┬▓, ŽāŌéé = 0

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Rankine's Criterion:**

Tensile Check (Bottom Fibre):

$$
FOS_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{150}{57.19} = 2.62
$$

Compressive Check (Top Fibre):

$$
FOS_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert} = \frac{450}{57.19} = 7.87
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Governing Factor of Safety:**

$$
\boxed{FOS_{governing} = \min(2.62, 7.87) = 2.62}
$$

**Conclusion:** The beam is safe with a governing FoS of **2.62**. The tensile side governs the design, consistent with brittle cast iron behavior where tensile strength is significantly lower than compressive strength.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks):** Forgetting that compressive stresses are negative. Always write $\sigma_2 = -78.125 \text{ N/mm}^2$, **NOT** $\sigma_2 = 78.125$.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the Compressive Check (Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks):** Many students only compute the tensile FoS and stop. KTU examiners **always expect both checks** ŌĆö tension AND compression.
>
> 3. **Wrong Section Modulus Formula (Loss: 1 mark):** Using $Z = bd^2/6$ for circular sections ŌĆö incorrect! Use $Z = \pi d^3/32$ for circular and $Z = \pi(d^4 - d_i^4)/(32d)$ for hollow circular.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units (Loss: 0.5ŌĆō1 mark):** Mixing N/mm┬▓ with N/m┬▓ or kN with N. Always convert to **consistent SI units (N, mm, N/mm┬▓)** before computation.
>
> 5. **Reporting Maximum FoS instead of Minimum (Loss: 2 marks):** The governing FoS is the **MINIMUM** of tensile and compressive factors. Reporting the maximum shows conceptual misunderstanding.
>
> 6. **Not Identifying the Critical Point in Combined Loading (Loss: 2 marks):** In combined loading problems, identify the point where bending adds to axial stress (tensile side) ŌĆö this is usually the critical point. Don't blindly apply to top or bottom without checking.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Principal Angle Calculation (Loss: 1 mark):** For full marks in 14-mark problems, KTU expects **╬Ė_p calculation** as a sub-step. Always compute: $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
>
> 8. **Applying Rankine to Ductile Materials (Loss: Conceptual):** If the material has **equal Žā_ut and Žā_uc** (like mild steel), Rankine's theory is **not appropriate**. Use Tresca or Von-Mises.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤Ä» Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Key Concepts to Memorize
- Ō£ģ Rankine's Theory applies **only to brittle materials**.
- Ō£ģ Failure criterion: **ŽāŌéü Ōēź Žā_ut** (tension) **or** **|ŽāŌéé| Ōēź Žā_uc** (compression).
- Ō£ģ Always check **BOTH** tension and compression conditions.
- Ō£ģ Governing FoS = **MINIMUM** of FoS_t and FoS_c.
- Ō£ģ Material must have **Žā_ut ŌēĀ Žā_uc** for Rankine to be meaningful.
- Ō£ģ Principal stresses must be computed using the **2D transformation equation**.
- Ō£ģ For pure bending on brittle beams, **tensile side governs** (since Žā_ut < Žā_uc).

### Critical Formulas (Quick Recall)
- Ō£ģ $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
- Ō£ģ $FOS_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$
- Ō£ģ $FOS_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_2 \vert}$
- Ō£ģ $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$

### Materials Where Rankine is BEST Applied
- Ō£ģ Cast iron (Žā_ut Ōēł 200, Žā_uc Ōēł 600 MPa)
- Ō£ģ Concrete (Žā_ut Ōēł 3ŌĆō5, Žā_uc Ōēł 25ŌĆō40 MPa)
- Ō£ģ Ceramics and refractories
- Ō£ģ Plaster and stone
- Ō£ģ Tool steels in hardened state

### Materials Where Rankine is **NOT** Recommended
- ŌØī Mild steel and ductile metals
- ŌØī Aluminium alloys
- ŌØī Polymers (in ductile temperature range)

### KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Summary
| Question Type | Marks | Time to Allocate | Key Focus |
|---------------|-------|------------------|-----------|
| Part A (Definition) | 3 | 6ŌĆō8 minutes | Theory statement + applicability |
| Part A (Comparison) | 3 | 6ŌĆō8 minutes | Tabular comparison with other theories |
| Part B (Numerical) | 14 | 25ŌĆō30 minutes | Principal stress ŌåÆ FoS ŌåÆ Redesign if needed |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guest's Theory for Maximum Shear Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Guest's Theory for Maximum Shear Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Guest's Theory of Maximum Shear Stress** is a classical **Strength Theory (Failure Theory)** in Solid Mechanics that postulates the yielding of ductile materials under complex states of stress. The theory was formally proposed by **James Guest in the year 1850** and is mathematically equivalent to **Tresca's Yield Criterion (1864)**, hence it is frequently referred to as the **GuestŌĆōTresca Criterion** in modern literature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4 ŌĆō Theories of Failure):**
> *"Failure by yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress in the combined stress system reaches a value equal to the maximum shear stress at the yield point in a simple tensile test on the same material."*

Mathematically, the failure/yield condition for a general **tri-axial stress state** is expressed as:

$$
\tau_{\max}^{complex} = \tau_{\max}^{tension}
$$

When the three principal stresses are ordered as $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ (algebraically), the maximum shear stress in a 3D element is governed by the **extreme principal stresses only**:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}
$$

The corresponding yield condition becomes:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt}
$$

where $\sigma_{yt}$ is the **uniaxial yield stress** obtained from a standard tensile test on the same material.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆō The Crushed Cardboard Box:**
> Imagine pushing a heavy cardboard box from opposite sides along its top surface. The cardboard itself does not stretch or compress along the push direction, but it eventually fails by a **sliding/shearing action** along an inclined plane at **45┬░** to the applied force. The fibers of the cardboard slip past one another along that inclined slip-line.

**Connection to the theory:**
- The cardboard is the ductile material.
- The "slip" represents the microscopic slip along the plane of **maximum shear stress**.
- Guest's theory says: *a material will begin to yield exactly when such internal slipping becomes critical, which mathematically corresponds to the difference between the largest and smallest principal stresses reaching the yield strength in simple tension.*

The plane of maximum shear stress in a stressed element always occurs at **45┬░** to the directions of the major and minor principal stresses ŌĆö which is why in a uniaxial tension test, the slip bands appear at 45┬░ to the loading axis (an easily observable phenomenon in mild steel specimens).

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principal Stress (Major) | $\sigma_1$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) | Algebraically largest normal stress |
| Principal Stress (Intermediate) | $\sigma_2$ | MPa | Algebraically middle normal stress |
| Principal Stress (Minor) | $\sigma_3$ | MPa | Algebraically smallest normal stress |
| Yield Stress in Tension | $\sigma_{yt}$ | MPa | Stress at onset of yielding in simple tension |
| Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max}$ | MPa | Half the difference of extreme principal stresses |
| Factor of Safety | $F.O.S$ or $N$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of yield strength to induced maximum shear |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumption

The theory rests on the hypothesis that **ductile materials yield due to slippage along crystallographic planes**, where the resolved shear stress becomes critical. The plane carrying the **maximum shear stress** is therefore the plane of incipient yielding, regardless of the normal stress acting on it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** The hydrostatic (mean) stress component $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$ has **no influence** on yielding. Only the **deviatoric (shear-related) portion** matters.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The theory can be decomposed into the following logical steps:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the stress state:** Determine whether the problem is 2D (plane stress/plane strain) or 3D.
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute principal stresses:** Apply either analytical 2D formula or eigenvalue approach for 3D Mohr's circle.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Order the principal stresses:** Arrange them algebraically as $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\tau_{\max}$:** Use $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ (3D) or $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ (2D, where $\sigma_3 = 0$).
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Compare with simple-tension yield shear:** $\tau_{y} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}$.
6. **Step 6 ŌĆö Yield / No-Yield Decision:** If $\tau_{\max} \geq \tau_y$, the material yields; otherwise it is safe.
7. **Step 7 ŌĆö Factor of Safety (FOS):** $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ (3D) or $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}$ (2D).

## 2.3 Why the Difference of Extreme Principal Stresses?

For a general 3D stress state, the **three Mohr's circles** represent combinations of normal and shear stress on all possible planes. The largest Mohr's circle ŌĆö with diameter $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ ŌĆö represents the **maximum possible shear stress** in the entire stress system. The intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ lies between the two extreme values and does **not** enlarge the maximum shear stress; therefore it drops out of the governing equation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every KTU-tested formula** for Guest's Theory. Memorize these for direct application in numericals.

| # | Stress State | Maximum Shear Stress | Yield Condition (Failure) | Factor of Safety (FOS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniaxial Tension** $\sigma_x$ | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}$ | $\sigma_x = \sigma_{yt}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_x}$ |
| 2 | **Pure Shear** $\tau_{xy}$ | $\tau_{\max} = \tau_{xy}$ | $\tau_{xy} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2\tau_{xy}}$ |
| 3 | **2D / Plane Stress** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_{yt}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}$ |
| 4 | **3D General** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ |

| # | Special Loading Scenarios | Guest's Theory Result |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | Uniaxial Compression | $\sigma_x = -\sigma_{yc}$, FOS = $\sigma_{yt}/\sigma_x$ (numerically) |
| 6 | Biaxial Equal Tension $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$ | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 0$, FOS = $\infty$ (safe for any $\sigma \leq \sigma_{yt}$) |
| 7 | Hydrostatic Pressure $\sigma_x=\sigma_y=\sigma_z$ | $\sigma_1-\sigma_3=0$, FOS = $\infty$ (no yielding) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessel design:** Used for thin-walled cylindrical vessels under internal pressure where 2D biaxial state exists (longitudinal + hoop stress).
- **Shaft design:** Calculating FOS for rotating shafts subjected to combined bending and torsion (the latter is a pure-shear case yielding $\tau = 16T/\pi d^3$).
- **Ductile material screening (Mild Steel, Copper, Aluminium):** Provides **conservative (safe-side) estimates** because it predicts yielding earlier than von Mises for most non-proportional loading paths.
- **Civil/mechanical code check:** Many design codes (especially older ones) still adopt Tresca/Guest for ductile metals in pressure-vessel calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Guest's Theory from a Simple Tension Test

Let us consider a **cylindrical specimen** of a ductile material subjected to uniaxial tension. At the instant of yielding in the simple tension test:

The applied normal stress is $\sigma_x = \sigma_{yt}$, and all other normal and shear stresses are zero. The principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_{yt}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = 0
$$

The **maximum shear stress** at this point is:

$$
\tau_{\max}^{tension} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt} - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}
$$

Now consider a **complex tri-axial stress state** acting on the same material. The principal stresses are $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$. The maximum shear stress in this complex state is:

$$
\tau_{\max}^{complex} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}
$$

**Failure hypothesis (Guest's postulate):** Yielding will commence when the maximum shear stress in the complex state equals the maximum shear stress at yield in the simple tension test:

$$
\tau_{\max}^{complex} = \tau_{\max}^{tension}
$$

Substituting the two expressions:

$$
\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}
$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt}}
$$

This is the **principal form of Guest's yield criterion** for general 3D states of stress.

## 3.2 Derivation of Factor of Safety (General)

By the **linear scaling principle**, the factor of safety is the ratio of the yield stress in simple tension to the equivalent stress measure under complex loading. Since Guest's equivalent stress is the difference $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$, we can write:

$$
FOS = \frac{\text{Strength Property}}{\text{Applied Equivalent Stress}} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Worked Examples (Sub-Concept Demonstrations)

### Sub-Concept 1: Pure Shear Loading

A shaft is subjected to pure torsion producing a shear stress $\tau_{xy} = 90$ MPa. The material has $\sigma_{yt} = 240$ MPa. Check safety using Guest's theory.

For pure shear, the principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -\tau_{xy}
$$

Hence:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \tau_{xy} - (-\tau_{xy}) = 2\tau_{xy}
$$

The yield condition requires:

$$
2\tau_{xy} \leq \sigma_{yt}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
2(90) = 180 \text{ MPa} \leq 240 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe}
$$

The factor of safety is:

$$
FOS = \frac{240}{180} = 1.333
$$

### Sub-Concept 2: 2D Biaxial Stress

A steel plate has $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Given $\sigma_{yt} = 250$ MPa, find FOS.

Compute principal stresses for 2D plane stress:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80+40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\left(\frac{80-40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400+900} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 \approx 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 \approx 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0
$$

Ordered: $\sigma_1 = 96.06$, $\sigma_2 = 23.94$, $\sigma_3 = 0$. Compute:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 96.06 - 0 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}
$$

The FOS is:

$$
FOS = \frac{250}{96.06} \approx 2.60
$$

### Sub-Concept 3: Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

For a thin-walled cylinder of diameter $D$, thickness $t$, internal pressure $p$:

- Hoop (circumferential) stress: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$
- Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$
- Radial stress $\approx 0$ for thin walls.

Ordering: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l$, $\sigma_3 = 0$. Apply Guest's criterion:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt} \Rightarrow \frac{pD}{2t} = \sigma_{yt}
$$

Solving for the safe pressure:

$$
p = \frac{2t\sigma_{yt}}{D}
$$

### Sub-Concept 4: Combined Bending and Torsion in a Shaft

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ with bending moment $M$ and torque $T$:

- Maximum bending stress (tension side): $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}$
- Maximum torsional shear stress: $\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$

The principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{1,3} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}
$$

The middle principal stress is $\sigma_2 = 0$. So:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}
$$

The Guest's yield condition gives:

$$
\sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2} = \sigma_{yt}
$$

Squaring:

$$
\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2 = \sigma_{yt}^2
$$

This is the famous **Guest's equation for combined bending and torsion**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Yield Locus in 2D (Hexagonal Form)

For plane stress with principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ and $\sigma_3 = 0$, the yield condition becomes:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_{yt}
$$

This single equation, however, is not sufficient when $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ could be **either positive or negative**. The complete set of possible yield conditions is obtained by considering all permutations of which two principal stresses are extreme:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \pm \sigma_{yt}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 - \sigma_3 = \pm \sigma_{yt}
$$

$$
\sigma_3 - \sigma_1 = \pm \sigma_{yt}
$$

In the $\sigma_1$ŌĆō$\sigma_2$ plane (with $\sigma_3 = 0$), these six lines enclose a **regular hexagon** symmetric about the equibiaxial line $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. The vertices of the hexagon are at:

- $(\sigma_{yt}, 0)$, $(0, \sigma_{yt})$ ŌĆö uniaxial tension
- $(-\sigma_{yt}, 0)$, $(0, -\sigma_{yt})$ ŌĆö uniaxial compression
- $(\sigma_{yt}, \sigma_{yt})$, $(-\sigma_{yt}, -\sigma_{yt})$ ŌĆö equibiaxial (these are not on the actual yield locus since $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 0$ for these points)

The actual safe region is the **interior of the hexagon**. The corners of the hexagon correspond to states of pure shear: e.g., $\sigma_1 = \sigma_{yt}/2$, $\sigma_2 = -\sigma_{yt}/2$ gives $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_{yt}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The hexagonal yield locus in the $\sigma_1$-$\sigma_2$ plane is the **fingerprint** of Guest's theory. Whenever you are asked to "draw the yield locus," this hexagon is the required answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow ŌĆö Application Procedure for Guest's Theory

The Mermaid diagram below maps the decision workflow a student must follow when applying Guest's theory to a numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Stress State and sigma_yt] --> B[Step 1: Identify Stress Dimensionality 2D or 3D]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Principal Stresses via Mohr Circle or Eigenvalue Method]
    C --> D[Step 3: Algebraically Order Them sigma1 greater than or equal to sigma2 greater than or equal to sigma3]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute tau_max equals sigma1 minus sigma3 divided by 2]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Reference Shear tau_y equals sigma_yt divided by 2]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compare tau_max with tau_y]
    G --> H{Is tau_max less than or equal to tau_y}
    H -- YES --> I[SAFE - Compute FOS equals sigma_yt divided by sigma1 minus sigma3]
    H -- NO --> J[UNSAFE - Material has Yielded]
    I --> K[END: Report FOS Value and Conclusion]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture ŌĆö Relationship between Guest's Theory and Material Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data]
        A1[Principal Stresses sigma1 sigma2 sigma3]
        A2[Yield Stress sigma_yt]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Guest Theory Engine]
        B1[Order Principal Stresses]
        B2[Compute Difference sigma1 minus sigma3]
        B3[Form FOS Ratio]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Decision]
        C1{FOS greater than or equal to 1}
        C2[Component Safe]
        C3[Component Fails]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- YES --> C2
    C1 -- NO --> C3
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation ŌĆö Hexagonal Yield Locus (Textual)

The hexagonal yield locus in the $\sigma_1$ (horizontal) vs $\sigma_2$ (vertical) plane can be characterized as follows:

```
                sigma_2
                  |
        YIELD     |    YIELD
        REGION    |    REGION
        (SAFE)    |    (SAFE)
                  |
   (-sigyt,0)----+----(sigyt,0)----> sigma_1
                  |
        YIELD     |    YIELD
        REGION    |    REGION
                  |
                  v

     YIELD LOCI (Failure Boundaries) - Six Lines:
     1. sigma1 minus sigma2  =  +sigyt   (Top-Right edge)
     2. sigma1 minus sigma2  =  -sigyt   (Bottom-Left edge)
     3. sigma2              =  +sigyt   (Vertical, where sigma1=0)
     4. sigma2              =  -sigyt   (Vertical, where sigma1=0)
     5. sigma1              =  +sigyt   (Horizontal, where sigma2=0)
     6. sigma1              =  -sigyt   (Horizontal, where sigma2=0)

     Vertices (Pure Shear States):
     A = ( sigyt/2,  sigyt/2)   -> tau = 0
     B = ( sigyt,   0     )   -> Uniaxial Tension
     C = ( sigyt/2, -sigyt/2)   -> Pure Shear (Corner)
     D = ( 0,       -sigyt )   -> Uniaxial Compression
     E = (-sigyt/2, -sigyt/2)   -> tau = 0
     F = (-sigyt,   0     )   -> Uniaxial Compression
```

> [!NOTE]
> The six-sided polygon (hexagon) is the geometric signature of Guest's theory. A point lying **inside** the hexagon ŌåÆ **safe**; on the boundary ŌåÆ **at yield**; outside ŌåÆ **failure has occurred**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**"State Guest's theory of failure. Comment on its applicability for ductile materials."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** Guest's theory of maximum shear stress states that *"failure by yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress in a complex stress system reaches a value equal to the maximum shear stress at the yield point in a simple tensile test."*

**Mathematical Form:** For principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, the yield condition is $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt}$, where $\sigma_{yt}$ is the uniaxial tensile yield stress.

**Applicability (1 mark):** The theory is best suited for **ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel, copper, aluminium) which fail by shear slippage along planes of maximum shear stress. It is **conservative** (predicts failure earlier than experiments) for combined loading, hence widely used as a safe-side design criterion.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**"The principal stresses at a point in a machine component are $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -40$ MPa. The yield stress of the material in simple tension is $\sigma_{yt} = 250$ MPa. Check the safety of the component using Guest's theory."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Ordering (1 mark): $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -40$ MPa.

Maximum shear stress (1 mark):

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{120 - (-40)}{2} = \frac{160}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

Reference shear at yield (0.5 mark):

$$
\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2} = \frac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ MPa}
$$

Conclusion (0.5 mark): Since $\tau_{\max} = 80$ MPa $< \tau_y = 125$ MPa, the component is **safe**.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full-Format 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō ESE Pattern]**

**A solid circular shaft of diameter $60$ mm is subjected to a bending moment of $4$ kN┬Ęm and a torque of $6$ kN┬Ęm. The yield stress of the shaft material in simple tension is $\sigma_{yt} = 350$ MPa.**

**(a)** *Determine the principal stresses at the critical point of the shaft using elementary formulas. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Using Guest's theory of maximum shear stress, find the factor of safety of the shaft. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Bending stress computation (2 marks):**

The maximum bending stress in a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ is given by:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}
$$

Substituting $M = 4 \text{ kN┬Ęm} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}$ and $d = 60$ mm:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{32 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{128 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{128 \times 10^6}{678584.0} \approx 188.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Torsional shear stress computation (2 marks):**

$$
\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}
$$

Substituting $T = 6 \text{ kN┬Ęm} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{16 \times 6 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{678584.0} \approx 141.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Principal stress computation (3 marks):**

At the critical point (top fibre), the stress state is $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = 188.6$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = 141.5$ MPa. The principal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{1,3} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{188.6}{2} = 94.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\left(\frac{188.6}{2}\right)^2 + 141.5^2} = \sqrt{94.3^2 + 141.5^2} = \sqrt{8892.49 + 20022.25} = \sqrt{28914.74} \approx 170.04 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 94.3 + 170.04 = 264.34 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 94.3 - 170.04 = -75.74 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0
$$

Ordering: $\sigma_1 = 264.34$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -75.74$ MPa.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Guest's yield criterion (3 marks):**

The Guest equivalent stress is the difference between the extreme principal stresses:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 264.34 - (-75.74) = 340.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Factor of Safety (3 marks):**

$$
FOS = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{350}{340.08} \approx 1.029
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion (1 mark):**

The factor of safety is approximately **1.03**, which is **barely above unity**, indicating the shaft is **critically loaded** and would benefit from a larger diameter or stronger material.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common error is to **omit the sign of $\sigma_3$** when computing $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$. Since $\sigma_3$ is negative in this problem, the actual difference is much larger than $\sigma_1$ alone. Many students lose 1ŌĆō2 marks by writing $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 264.34 - 0 = 264.34$ MPa, which is **wrong**. Always **order** the principal stresses and use the **algebraic values**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): **[Alternative Choice for Internal Option]**

**A steel component is subjected to a biaxial state of stress with $\sigma_x = 90$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 45$ MPa. The yield stress of the material in simple tension is $\sigma_{yt} = 280$ MPa.**

**(a)** *Determine the principal stresses at the point and identify the plane of maximum shear stress. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Evaluate the factor of safety using Guest's theory. Plot the stress point on the yield hexagon and comment on the safety. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Principal stress formula for 2D plane stress (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substituting values (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{90 + 30}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\left(\frac{90 - 30}{2}\right)^2 + 45^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 45^2} = \sqrt{900 + 2025} = \sqrt{2925} \approx 54.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 60 + 54.08 = 114.08 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 60 - 54.08 = 5.92 \text{ MPa}
$$

For 2D plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$. Ordering: $\sigma_1 = 114.08$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 5.92$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Plane of maximum shear stress (2 marks):**

The plane of maximum shear stress is at **45┬░** to the principal stress directions. The maximum shear stress magnitude is:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{114.08 - 0}{2} = 57.04 \text{ MPa}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Guest's equivalent stress (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{eq}^{Guest} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 114.08 - 0 = 114.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor of safety (2 marks):**

$$
FOS = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{280}{114.08} \approx 2.454
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Plotting on the yield hexagon (2 marks):**

The point $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2) = (114.08, 5.92)$ MPa is plotted on the $\sigma_1$-$\sigma_2$ plane. The yield hexagon has vertices at $(\pm 280, 0)$, $(0, \pm 280)$, and $(\pm 140, \pm 140)$. The plotted point lies **well within** the interior of the hexagon, confirming that the component is **safe** with a comfortable margin.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion (1 mark):**

The factor of safety is approximately **2.45**, and the stress point lies inside the yield locus, so the component is **safe under the given biaxial loading**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Sign Convention Trap:** In 2D problems, students often forget that $\sigma_3 = 0$ is a valid principal stress. When **either $\sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$ comes out negative**, the ordering $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq 0$ must be **re-checked carefully**. Using $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ when one of them is negative gives a **wrong** FOS. Always order first, then apply $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Marks Deduction in Guest's Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to order** the principal stresses algebraically before applying $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$.
> 2. **Using $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$** in 2D problems when one stress is tensile and the other is compressive ŌĆö this is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 3. **Mixing up units** (MPa vs N/mm┬▓) ŌĆö both are numerically equal but must be written clearly.
> 4. **Not drawing the yield hexagon** when explicitly asked ŌĆö partial marks are lost in graphical questions.
> 5. **Omitting the plane of maximum shear stress** at 45┬░ orientation in the answer.
> 6. **Failing to identify hydrostatic stress correctly** ŌĆö many students wrongly include $\sigma_m$ in their yield check.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Guest's Theory of Maximum Shear Stress**
>
> - ŌŁÉ Guest's Theory (1850) is also called the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** or **Tresca Criterion**.
> - ŌŁÉ It applies to **ductile materials** that fail by **shear slip** (e.g., mild steel, copper).
> - ŌŁÉ **Governing equation (3D):** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_{yt}$
> - ŌŁÉ **Governing equation (2D):** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_{yt}$ (only if both principal stresses are positive AND $\sigma_3 = 0$ is between them; otherwise fall back to 3D form).
> - ŌŁÉ **FOS (3D):** $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$
> - ŌŁÉ **FOS (2D):** $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}$
> - ŌŁÉ **Plane of max shear stress** is at **45┬░** to the major and minor principal stress directions.
> - ŌŁÉ **Yield locus in $\sigma_1$ŌĆō$\sigma_2$ plane** is a **regular hexagon** with vertices at $(\pm \sigma_{yt}, 0)$, $(0, \pm \sigma_{yt})$, and $(\pm \sigma_{yt}/2, \pm \sigma_{yt}/2)$.
> - ŌŁÉ The theory is **conservative** (safe-side) compared to **von Mises** and **experimental results** for most non-proportional loading paths.
> - ŌŁÉ For **pure shear** of magnitude $\tau$: yield occurs when $\tau = \sigma_{yt}/2$.
> - ŌŁÉ For **combined bending + torsion** in a shaft: $\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2 = \sigma_{yt}^2$ at the onset of yield.
> - ŌŁÉ For **thin cylindrical pressure vessels** (longitudinal + hoop): yield occurs when $\sigma_h = \sigma_{yt}$ (i.e., $pD/2t = \sigma_{yt}$).
> - ŌŁÉ Always **order principal stresses** algebraically before applying the criterion ŌĆö the **intermediate** principal stress $\sigma_2$ does **not** appear in the final equation.
> - ŌŁÉ The theory **ignores hydrostatic stress** ŌĆö only the **deviatoric** part causes yielding.
> - ŌŁÉ KTU-recommended use: **shaft design, pressure vessels, ductile metal structures**.
> - ŌŁÉ Numerical trick: When $\sigma_3 = 0$ is between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ algebraically, the 3D formula reduces to $\sigma_1 - 0 = \sigma_1$ (NOT $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saint-Venant's Theory for Maximum Normal Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT302 - SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Saint-Venant's Theory for Maximum Normal Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Saint-Venant's Theory of Maximum Normal Strain** (also called the *Maximum Principal Strain Theory* or *Normal Strain Theory of Failure*) is a classical phenomenological failure hypothesis in Strength of Materials, originally proposed by the French mechanician **Barr├® de Saint-Venant** in 1837. The theory postulates that:

> *A ductile (or brittle) material element subjected to a complex multi-axial stress state is considered to have failed when the maximum principal strain at any point in the element reaches the limiting normal strain that the same material can sustain in a simple uniaxial tension (or compression) test at the elastic limit.*

In quantitative terms, if $\varepsilon_1$ is the algebraically largest (or maximum magnitude) principal strain under combined loading, failure is predicted when:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \pm \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
$$

where $E$ is **Young's Modulus** and $\sigma_y$ is the **Yield Strength** in simple tension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (2024 Scheme):** Saint-Venant's strain theory is treated as the *first* of the advanced failure theories (after Rankine and Guest theories). It must be studied in conjunction with Generalized Hooke's Law because the stress-form of the criterion is obtained by substituting principal strains expressed through principal stresses.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are stretching a **rubber band** with both hands. Now, instead of just pulling the two ends apart, suppose you also slightly **pinch the sides** together with your fingers. The rubber band will snap at a *lower* applied axial force than it would have if you had just pulled it straight ŌĆö because the *total stretching deformation* in the fibre direction is no longer the only contributor. The lateral pinch causes a *contractile* effect that adds to the longitudinal strain.

Saint-Venant's theory captures exactly this phenomenon: the failure is **strain-controlled** rather than purely stress-controlled. It says *"breakage happens when the material is stretched (or compressed) by a certain limiting amount, irrespective of whether the stretching is direct or accompanied by sideways squeezing."*

A more rigorous engineering analogy: consider a **brittle cast-iron test specimen** loaded in combined tension and internal pressure. The hoop stress and axial stress both contribute to the longitudinal strain (through Poisson's effect), and the cast iron cracks when the largest fibre strain reaches the rupture strain of a separately tested tensile coupon. Saint-Venant's theory predicts this correctly, while the older Rankine (Maximum Normal Stress) theory over- or under-predicts the strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope Reminder for KTU 2024:** Saint-Venant's theory is generally considered more accurate than Rankine's theory for **brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, and ceramics ŌĆö particularly in 2-D plane-stress situations where Poisson's lateral strain effects dominate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following material/elastic constants appear explicitly in the theory:

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** measured in **GPa** (typically 70 GPa for aluminium, 200 GPa for steel, 100ŌĆō200 GPa for cast iron).
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** dimensionless, generally in the range $0.20$ to $0.35$ for metals; $\approx 0.10$ for concrete; $\approx 0.20$ for cast iron.
- **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$):** in **MPa**.
- **Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$):** in **MPa** (used in place of $\sigma_y$ for brittle materials).
- **Factor of Safety ($n$ or $FOS$):** dimensionless ratio.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions and Logic Steps

Saint-Venant's theory is built upon a layered logical structure. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate each of the following:

1. **Assumption of Continuous, Homogeneous, Isotropic (CHI) material** ŌĆö same as for Hooke's Law.
2. **Assumption of Linear Elasticity** ŌĆö the material obeys Generalized Hooke's Law up to the failure point.
3. **Strain-based failure criterion** ŌĆö the controlling variable is *strain*, not stress.
4. **Reference state:** a simple uniaxial tension test on a specimen of the *same* material provides the limiting strain.
5. **Maximum principal strain governs:** failure initiates along the direction of the largest principal strain (tensile) or largest compressive strain (compressive).
6. **Superposition of lateral effects:** under multi-axial stress, the principal strain in a given direction is the *net* result of the axial extension AND the Poisson contractions from the other two principal stresses.

### 2.2 Generalized Hooke's Law ŌĆö The Foundation

For a 3-D stress state with principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$), the principal strains are obtained from Generalized Hooke's Law:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_3 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]
$$

These are the **three fundamental strain equations** that must be mastered for the KTU board exam.

### 2.3 Failure Criterion Derivation (Logic Flow)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the controlling principal strain.** Take the algebraically largest principal strain $\varepsilon_{\max}$. In a tension-dominated problem, this is usually $\varepsilon_1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compare with uniaxial limit.** In a uniaxial tension test, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$, and so $\varepsilon_{\text{uniaxial}} = \sigma_y / E$. The uniaxial compressive limit is $-\sigma_y / E$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equate.** For safety (no failure):
$$
\varepsilon_{\max} \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}
$$
Substituting from Hooke's Law:
$$
\dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}
$$
Multiplying through by $E$:
$$
\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \sigma_y
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Define equivalent stress.** Saint-Venant's equivalent (or *effective*) stress is therefore:

$$
\sigma_{eq,\,SV} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö State the design inequality.** The structure is safe if:

$$
\sigma_{eq,\,SV} \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma_{eq,\,SV} \le \sigma_{\text{allowable}}
$$

### 2.4 The Factor of Safety (FOS)

The Factor of Safety according to Saint-Venant's theory is:

$$
n_{SV} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)}
$$

For 2-D plane stress ($\sigma_3 = 0$):

$$
n_{SV} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu\,\sigma_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Warning:** The denominator $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ must be **positive** for the formula to be physically meaningful when used with a tensile $\sigma_y$. If the expression is negative (e.g., pure hydrostatic compression), the theory reduces to checking the most compressive principal strain against $-\sigma_y/E$, and the FOS formula uses absolute values.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Quantity Name | Formula | Units | Remarks / Special Cases |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Principal strain $\varepsilon_1$ (3-D) | $\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$ | dimensionless | Hooke's Law (general) |
| 2 | Principal strain $\varepsilon_2$ (3-D) | $\varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$ | dimensionless | Hooke's Law (general) |
| 3 | Principal strain $\varepsilon_3$ (3-D) | $\varepsilon_3 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]$ | dimensionless | Hooke's Law (general) |
| 4 | Uniaxial yield strain | $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ | dimensionless | Reference limit |
| 5 | Saint-Venant failure criterion (tension) | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \sigma_y$ | MPa | At failure |
| 6 | Saint-Venant equivalent stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ | MPa | Effective scalar |
| 7 | FOS ŌĆö Saint-Venant (3-D) | $n = \sigma_y \,/\, \left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$ | dimensionless | Ductile, $\sigma_u$ for brittle |
| 8 | FOS ŌĆö Saint-Venant (2-D plane stress) | $n = \sigma_y \,/\, \left[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right]$ | dimensionless | $\sigma_3 = 0$ |
| 9 | Safety condition | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \sigma_y / n$ | MPa | Design check |
| 10 | Hydrostatic stress invariant | $\sigma_H = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ | MPa | Volume change $\sigma_H / K$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Board Tip:** For brittle materials, replace $\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_u$ (ultimate tensile strength) in **every** formula. Failure is then deemed to occur at fracture, not at yield.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure-vessel design** for brittle materials (cast iron, glass-lined reactors) ŌĆö Saint-Venant gives more realistic burst-pressure predictions than Rankine.
- **Concrete columns and beams** ŌĆö the lateral confinement from stirrups reduces the axial compressive strain, and Saint-Venant accounts for this through the $\nu \sigma_2$ term.
- **Composite laminates** ŌĆö fibre-direction strains are predicted, and Saint-Venant's criterion is used as a first-mode failure check.
- **Geotechnical engineering** ŌĆö soil and rock masses under triaxial stress are evaluated using modified forms of strain-based criteria that descend from Saint-Venant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Saint-Venant Failure Criterion from First Principles

We begin with the standard 3-D principal-stress notation $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (all along the principal directions $1, 2, 3$). The principal strains are decoupled from shear strains because, in the principal frame, all shear stresses vanish.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from Generalized Hooke's Law (volumetric + deviatoric form).**

For an isotropic, linearly elastic solid, the strain-stress relation along any principal axis $i$ is:

$$
\varepsilon_i = \dfrac{\sigma_i}{E} - \dfrac{\nu}{E}\sum_{j \ne i} \sigma_j
$$

Explicitly, for $i = 1$:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E} - \dfrac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor out the common denominator.**

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]
$$

This is the equation shown earlier in the formula sheet; it is equation **(1)**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the uniaxial reference strain.**

In a simple uniaxial tension test along axis 1, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$, so:

$$
\varepsilon_1^{(\text{uniaxial})} = \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \quad \text{at yield}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Saint-Venant hypothesis.**

The hypothesis states that the maximum principal strain at failure equals the uniaxial yield strain (in magnitude):

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \pm \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute (1) into the hypothesis.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \pm \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $E$ to obtain the stress form.**

$$
\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \pm \sigma_y
$$

The **positive** sign applies to a tensile-failure scenario; the **negative** sign applies to compressive failure. Most KTU numerical problems use the tensile form (positive sign), unless otherwise specified. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Factor of Safety Expression

**Step 1 ŌĆö Begin with the safety inequality.**

For the structure to be safe, the maximum equivalent stress must be less than or equal to the allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{all}} = \sigma_y / n$.

$$
\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate $n$.**

Multiplying both sides by $n$:

$$
n \cdot \left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] \le \sigma_y
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Divide both sides by the bracketed term (assumed positive).**

$$
n \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)}
$$

The **limiting (design) value** of $n$ is:

$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)} \qquad \text{(3-D)}
$$

For 2-D plane stress ($\sigma_3 = 0$):

$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2} \qquad \text{(2-D)}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Derivation ŌĆö Effect of Biaxial Stress on Critical Tensile Load

This is a frequently tested KTU problem archetype. We derive the relationship between biaxial loading and the equivalent uniaxial load for a brittle bar.

**Problem Set-up (Generic):** A brittle rectangular bar carries axial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ and transverse compressive stress $\sigma_y$ (both principal). Determine the axial stress $\sigma_x$ at which failure occurs, given material properties $\sigma_u$ (ultimate tensile strength) and $\nu$ (Poisson's ratio).

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the principal stresses.**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_x, \quad \sigma_2 = \sigma_y, \quad \sigma_3 = 0
$$

with the convention $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Saint-Venant's strain-based failure criterion.**

For a brittle material, replace $\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_u$:

$$
\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = \sigma_u
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $\sigma_1$ (the critical axial stress).**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_u + \nu \sigma_2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpret the result.**

- If $\sigma_2$ is **compressive** (negative), the term $\nu \sigma_2$ is *negative*, and so $\sigma_1 < \sigma_u$ ŌĆö the bar fails at a *lower* axial load because the lateral compression adds tensile strain (via Poisson expansion) to the longitudinal fibre.
- If $\sigma_2$ is **tensile** (positive), $\sigma_1 > \sigma_u$ ŌĆö lateral tension *reduces* longitudinal strain, and the bar can carry *more* axial load.
- If $\sigma_2 = 0$, we recover the uniaxial case $\sigma_1 = \sigma_u$.

This behaviour matches physical intuition and is the principal reason Saint-Venant's theory is preferred over Rankine's for brittle materials.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Derivation ŌĆö FOS for a Pressure Vessel

A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, is made of a brittle material with ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. The closed ends produce a 2-D biaxial stress state. Derive the FOS according to Saint-Venant.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the principal stresses.**

The hoop (circumferential) and longitudinal stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \sigma_1 = \dfrac{pD}{2t}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{long}} = \sigma_2 = \dfrac{pD}{4t}
$$

$$
\sigma_3 = 0 \quad \text{(plane stress, thin wall)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Verify the order.**

Clearly $\sigma_1 > \sigma_2 > 0 = \sigma_3$, so $\sigma_1$ is the maximum principal stress.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Saint-Venant FOS formula for a brittle material.**

$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the stress expressions.**

$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\dfrac{pD}{2t} - \nu \cdot \dfrac{pD}{4t}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Factor out $\dfrac{pD}{4t}$ from the denominator.**

$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\dfrac{pD}{4t}\left(2 - \nu\right)}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rearrange to give the burst pressure in terms of the design FOS.**

$$
n = \dfrac{4t\,\sigma_u}{pD\,(2 - \nu)}
$$

Hence the **allowable internal pressure** is:

$$
p_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{4t\,\sigma_u}{n\,D\,(2 - \nu)}
$$

This formula is the canonical KTU board answer for "pressure vessel + Saint-Venant" problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with Rankine:** The Maximum Normal Stress theory gives $p_{\text{allow, Rankine}} = 2t\sigma_u / (nD)$, which is *more conservative* for brittle vessels because it ignores the beneficial Poisson-strain-relief provided by the longitudinal stress.

---

### 3.5 Master Summary Table ŌĆö When to Use Which Failure Theory (for Quick Revision)

| Loading / Material Scenario | Preferred Saint-Venant Usage? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Ductile steel, static load, $\sigma_2 \approx 0$ | Yes (matches von Mises closely) | Lateral strain is small |
| Brittle cast iron, biaxial stress | **Yes ŌĆö strongly preferred** | Poisson effects dominate |
| Concrete, triaxial compression | Yes (with $\sigma_u$) | Strain control is realistic |
| Pure hydrostatic pressure | Not applicable | All principal strains equal, no failure predicted |
| Ductile material, large $\sigma_2$ | Use Tresca / von Mises instead | Strain theory can over-predict strength |
| Fatigue / cyclic loading | Not used in original form | Needs modified strain-life approach |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Saint-Venant's Decision Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the operational decision tree that an engineer follows when applying Saint-Venant's theory. It traces from the raw stress input all the way to the final FOS calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given 2D or 3D Stress State] --> B[Identify Principal Stresses sigma1 sigma2 sigma3]
    B --> C[Order Them: sigma1 >= sigma2 >= sigma3]
    C --> D{Material Type?}
    D -->|Ductile| E[Use Yield Stress sigma_y]
    D -->|Brittle| F[Use Ultimate Stress sigma_u]
    E --> G[Compute Max Principal Strain epsilon1]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Generalized Hooke: epsilon1 = sigma1 - nu sigma2 + sigma3 / E]
    H --> I{Compute Equivalent Stress}
    I --> J[sigma_eq_SV = sigma1 - nu sigma2 + sigma3]
    J --> K{Is sigma_eq_SV positive?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Check against Tensile Limit sigma_y / sigma_u]
    K -->|No| M[Check against Compressive Limit sigma_y / sigma_u in absolute]
    L --> N[Compute FOS: n = sigma_y / sigma_eq_SV]
    M --> O[Compute FOS using |sigma_eq_SV|]
    N --> P[Compare n with Design FOS]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{n greater than Design FOS?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[SAFE: Design Acceptable]
    Q -->|No| S[UNSAFE: Redesign Required]
    R --> T[End]
    S --> T
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of the Saint-Venant Criterion

The next diagram shows the *inputs*, *computational modules*, and *outputs* of the Saint-Venant design calculation as a function-block architecture. This block schematic is a Mermaid-safe alternative to physical 3-D drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        H3[Compute epsilon3]
    end

    subgraph CRITERION[Saint Venant Criterion Module]
        C1[Form sigma_eq = sigma1 - nu sigma2 + sigma3]
        C2[Compare with limit sigma_y or sigma_u]
        C3[Form Factor of Safety n]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        O1[FOS Numerical Value]

    end

    I1 --> H1
    I2 --> H1
    I3 --> H1
    I4 --> H1
    I5 --> H1
    I2 --> H2

    I3 --> H3

    I2 --> H3

    H1 --> C1

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RT[Rankine Theory: Max Normal Stress]
    GT[Guest or Tresca Theory: Max Shear Stress]
    SV[Saint Venant Theory: Max Normal Strain]
    VM[Von Mises Theory: Distortion Energy]
    MH[Mohr Theory: Modified Mohr]

    RT -->|Adds Poisson Effect| SV
    GT -->|Strain Energy Form| VM

    GT -->|Envelope of Mohr Circles| MH
    RT -->|Envelope of Mohr Circles| MH

    SV -.->|Best For Brittle Materials| BR[Cast Iron, Concrete, Ceramics]

```
